persona.occupation: Owner & Head Chef, Luna Latina Kitchen, LLC
persona.education: B.A. in Hospitality Management, University of Illinois at Chicago (UIC), Class of 2013
family_members: Rosa Martinez – mother, age 61, retired home health aide, lives in Cicero, IL
Javier Rodriguez – father, age 63, warehouse supervisor, lives in Cicero, IL
Ana Rodriguez – younger sister, age 29, CPS bilingual elementary school teacher, lives in Berwyn, IL
Diego Rodriguez – younger brother, age 26, HVAC technician, lives in Little Village, Chicago, IL
Lucia Elena Rodriguez – niece (Ana’s daughter), age 4, preschooler
Mateo Javier Rodriguez – nephew (Diego’s son), age 2
Carmen “Carmelita” Torres – maternal grandmother, age 84, lives with Marisol’s parents in Cicero, IL
Sofia Morales – cousin and close friend, age 33, freelance graphic designer, lives in Logan Square, Chicago, IL
Miguel Alvarez – long-term boyfriend, age 36, union electrician, lives in Humboldt Park, Chicago, IL
persona.background_context:
This is Marisol Elena Rodriguez, a 34-year-old Mexican-American woman who splits her time between Chicago’s West Side, where she lives, and Pilsen, where she runs her growing food business, Luna Latina Kitchen, LLC, at 1632 W 18th Street. She lives alone in a modest third-floor walk-up on W Monroe Street in the Austin neighborhood, about a 20-minute drive from her shared commercial kitchen. Her days start early—usually around 5:30 a.m.—with coffee, a quick scroll through catering inquiries on her phone, and a check-in on inventory and prep lists she keeps in a color-coded Google Sheet. On Thursdays and Sundays, her busiest days, she loads her hatchback with coolers, hotel pans, and boxes of packaging before heading to the kitchen by 7:00 a.m.

Lately, her life has revolved around two intertwined challenges: managing steady growth at Luna Latina Kitchen and trying to secure a small brick-and-mortar space before the end of 2025. She’s been touring potential locations in Pilsen and Little Village with a local commercial realtor since late summer and has her eye on an 1,000-square-foot former bakery space on 18th Street that became available in October. The rent and security deposit are a stretch, so she’s been running numbers with her cousin Sofia, who helps her with branding and social media, and meeting once a month with a small business mentor from a neighborhood nonprofit. At the same time, she’s juggling day-to-day operations: coordinating a part-time line cook’s schedule, keeping up with orders from two independent grocery stores that carry her bottled salsas, and testing frozen empanada recipes in the evenings to see which hold up best after baking at home.

Family is a constant presence in her routine, even if they don’t live under the same roof. Most Tuesday nights, she drives to Cicero to have dinner with her parents, Rosa and Javier, and her abuela Carmelita, who moved in with them after a minor fall in early 2023. Marisol still worries about her grandmother’s balance and quietly checks that throw rugs are taped down and that the grab bars she insisted on installing in the bathroom are secure. Weekends are a mix of work and family: on Saturdays, if she’s not catering a baby shower or a nonprofit board retreat, she might meet her boyfriend Miguel in Humboldt Park for a walk and a late lunch, or swing by Berwyn to see her sister Ana and four-year-old niece Lucia, who proudly tells everyone that “Tía Mari makes the best salsa in the world.”

Her daily life has a rhythm but also a constant sense of improvisation. On a typical weekday without a big catering job, she’ll spend the morning in the kitchen prepping for the next meal pickup cycle—marinating chipotle-lime chicken, roasting trays of vegetables, and blending batches of salsa macha. Midday, she often runs deliveries to the two grocery stores that stock her products, chatting briefly with the owners about what’s selling and which flavors customers are asking for. Afternoons are reserved for the less glamorous but essential side of running a business: updating QuickBooks, answering emails from office managers planning staff lunches, and tinkering with her website’s online ordering page. She usually eats a late lunch standing at the stainless-steel prep table, tasting and adjusting recipes as she goes.

Over the past year, she’s been feeling the strain of long hours on her body—sore feet, tight shoulders, and the occasional stress headache. In September 2024, after a scare where she felt dizzy during a large catering event, she scheduled a long-overdue physical at a community clinic near her apartment. Her blood pressure was slightly elevated, and the doctor gently urged her to take her own health as seriously as she takes her customers’ nutrition. Since then, she’s made small but real changes: trying to fit in a 20-minute walk around the neighborhood before bed at least a few nights a week, drinking more water instead of her habitual second and third coffees, and blocking off one Sunday evening a month as non-negotiable rest time—no events, no email, just a movie or a book and maybe takeout she didn’t cook herself.

Despite the fatigue, she feels proud of how far she’s come since 2021, when she started cooking out of a cramped shared kitchen after losing her restaurant management job during the pandemic. Some of her earliest customers—teachers from a nearby elementary school and nurses from a community clinic—still order from her for staff meetings, and she knows many of them by name. She donates leftover trays of food twice a month to a local mutual aid group, something she coordinates through a WhatsApp chat, and she’s in the early stages of planning a small paid summer internship program for two high school students from Pilsen in 2026. In quiet moments, usually late at night when she’s labeling bottles of pineapple-habanero salsa, she thinks about what it would mean to have her own storefront, with her logo in the window and a small counter where neighborhood kids stop in for empanadas after school. That vision keeps her moving through the long days, even as she balances spreadsheets, family obligations, and the everyday uncertainty of running a small, community-rooted business in Chicago.

secrets.secret_content: After the dizzy spell at a large catering event in early September 2024, Marisol had a follow‑up appointment on October 3, 2024 with Nurse Practitioner Elena Ruiz at the Austin Community Health Center on W Chicago Ave, where she was told she has stage 1 hypertension and was given a prescription for low‑dose lisinopril that she has been inconsistently taking because she worries it will affect her ability to work long kitchen shifts.
secrets.why_sensitive: A formal hypertension diagnosis and medication use are protected health information. Disclosure could lead to stigma, higher insurance premiums, or doubts from potential landlords, investors, or partners about her reliability and physical capacity to run a demanding food business.
secrets.how_disclosed: She might mention this in an email to her cousin Sofia like, “I have to leave the kitchen by 3:30 on Thursday for my blood pressure check with Elena at the Austin clinic,” or in a draft grant narrative explaining why she’s prioritizing equipment that reduces physical strain in the business.

secrets.secret_content: In November 2024, Marisol had a private consultation with physical therapist Daniel Kim at West Side Rehab & Wellness on Madison Street about chronic foot and shoulder pain from standing 10–12 hours a day in the kitchen, and he recommended custom orthotics and limiting double shifts, which she has not implemented because of cost and fear of losing business.
secrets.why_sensitive: Revealing ongoing musculoskeletal issues could make potential lenders, partners, or even employees question the long‑term sustainability of her role as head chef. It is also detailed medical information that could be misused in employment or insurance contexts.
secrets.how_disclosed: She might reference this in a late‑night text to her boyfriend Miguel (“Daniel at West Side Rehab said I really shouldn’t be doing back‑to‑back 12‑hour days anymore”) or in notes to herself about why she needs to hire another line cook to handle prep work.

secrets.secret_content: In March 2023, after her grandmother Carmen “Carmelita” Torres fell in the bathroom at her parents’ house in Cicero, Marisol had a tense family meeting in her parents’ kitchen where she pushed for installing grab bars and removing throw rugs, and her father Javier accused her of “acting like a social worker” and trying to take over decisions about her abuela’s care.
secrets.why_sensitive: This exposes private family conflict and her grandmother’s declining mobility, which could be embarrassing to relatives and might affect how community members perceive her family’s stability and ability to provide care.
secrets.how_disclosed: She might allude to this in a voice note to her cousin Sofia explaining why she can’t take certain weekend catering jobs (“Papá’s still mad about the grab bars talk after Abuela’s fall last March”) or when scheduling business commitments around Tuesday dinners in Cicero.

secrets.secret_content: In late October 2024, Marisol had a closed‑door meeting at the Pilsen branch of First Community Credit Union with loan officer Patrick O’Neill, where he declined her request for a $45,000 small business loan for the 18th Street storefront because her 2022 tax return showed a net loss and she has a maxed‑out $9,500 business credit card with Chase Ink Business Cash.
secrets.why_sensitive: Detailed information about loan denials, specific debt amounts, and credit utilization can harm her perceived financial stability with future lenders, landlords, or partners and could damage her professional reputation if widely known.
secrets.how_disclosed: She might describe this in a frustrated email to her business mentor (“Patrick at First Community said no because of the 2022 loss and my Chase balance”) or in notes while revising a grant application to explain why non‑debt funding is critical.

secrets.secret_content: Since June 2023, Marisol has occasionally paid her part‑time line cook, Carla Mendoza, in cash at the end of busy catering weekends at the shared kitchen on 18th Street without always logging the hours in QuickBooks, especially for last‑minute Sunday events, to avoid overtime and because she’s behind on payroll tax filings.
secrets.why_sensitive: Informal cash payments and incomplete payroll records pose tax and labor law compliance risks. Disclosure could expose her and her business to audits, penalties, or legal action and damage her credibility with funders or partners.
secrets.how_disclosed: She might mention this in a private planning call with her small business mentor (“I need help cleaning up my books—sometimes I just pay Carla cash after Sunday events”) or in a draft internal checklist about formalizing HR practices before opening a storefront.

secrets.secret_content: In February 2021, when she was laid off from her restaurant management job at “El Corazón Grill” in the Loop, Marisol signed a severance agreement in the office of HR manager Linda Patterson that included a non‑disparagement clause after she informally complained about unsafe COVID kitchen practices and being asked to work while mildly symptomatic in December 2020.
secrets.why_sensitive: This involves potential workplace safety violations and a legal agreement. Publicly discussing the circumstances could risk breaching the non‑disparagement clause and exposing her to legal action or reputational conflict within the local restaurant community.
secrets.how_disclosed: She might reference this in a one‑on‑one conversation with her business mentor to explain her motivation for starting Luna Latina (“After what happened at El Corazón with Linda and that severance paper, I knew I wanted my own kitchen”) or in a private email to a friend about why she’s strict on health protocols.

secrets.secret_content: In August 2024, Marisol received an email from Chicago Department of Public Health inspector Laura Chen with the subject line “Follow‑Up: Temperature Log and Labeling Corrections – Luna Latina Kitchen” after a routine shared‑kitchen inspection noted incomplete cooling logs for her salsas and two unlabeled containers, which she corrected within a week but has been anxious might affect future licensing.
secrets.why_sensitive: Even minor inspection findings can be misinterpreted as serious food safety problems, potentially harming her business’s reputation with customers, partners, landlords, or grant committees.
secrets.how_disclosed: She might mention this in a Slack message to her cousin Sofia about tightening procedures (“We have to keep perfect temp logs after Laura from CDPH flagged us in August”) or in internal notes when justifying the need for better labeling and refrigeration equipment.

secrets.secret_content: In January 2024, during a late‑night argument at Miguel’s apartment in Humboldt Park, Miguel told Marisol he was unsure about ever getting married or having kids because of his own parents’ divorce, and she left in tears after saying, “I can’t keep building this business alone forever,” a conflict they have not shared with their families.
secrets.why_sensitive: This reveals intimate relationship tensions and differing long‑term goals, which could invite judgment or pressure from family and friends and strain her relationship if discussed publicly.
secrets.how_disclosed: She might confide this in a WhatsApp voice message to her cousin Sofia (“After that January fight at Miguel’s, I’m scared to factor him into my plans for the storefront”) or in a private journal entry about whether to expand the business assuming she’ll stay in Chicago long‑term.

secrets.secret_content: During her final semester at UIC in Spring 2013, Marisol failed an upper‑level accounting course and had to retake it in Summer 2013, meeting twice a week in BSB 140 with Professor Robert Klein, who warned her in office hours that her difficulty with spreadsheets could “really hurt you if you ever run your own place,” a comment that still makes her self‑conscious about her bookkeeping skills.
secrets.why_sensitive: Academic struggles and a repeated course can make her feel less credible when presenting herself as a capable business owner, especially around financial management, and she may fear funders or partners will question her competence if they knew.
secrets.how_disclosed: She might mention this history in a candid conversation with her small business mentor (“I bombed accounting with Professor Klein back at UIC, so I still double‑guess my QuickBooks entries”) or in a private note about why she wants to hire a bookkeeper once revenue grows.

secrets.secret_content: Since early 2024, Marisol has been quietly transferring $150 on the first of every month from her Luna Latina Kitchen business checking account at Chase to her mother Rosa’s personal account to help cover Carmelita’s prescription costs at the Walgreens on Cermak Road, without formally recording it as an owner draw or family support expense in her business books.
secrets.why_sensitive: Mixing business funds with undocumented family support can create tax and accounting problems, complicate loan or grant applications, and reveal private details about her family’s financial and health situation.
secrets.how_disclosed: She might explain this in a worried email to her mentor (“I know I shouldn’t be paying Abuela’s meds straight from the business account, but Rosa needs the $150 every month”) or in a late‑night conversation with Miguel about why she feels constant financial pressure despite growing revenue.

artifacts.content:
Hi Sofi,

I’m finally sitting down with my laptop and a mug of manzanilla after labeling what feels like a million bottles of salsa macha.

I wanted to catch you up on a few things and also pick your brain.

First, health stuff: remember that dizzy spell I had during that big catering event in early September? I went back to the Austin Community Health Center on W Chicago Ave on October 3rd and saw Elena Ruiz again (she’s actually a nurse practitioner, not a doctor). She officially called it “stage 1 hypertension” and put me on a low-dose lisinopril. I’ve been… let’s say, not 100% consistent taking it because I keep worrying it’ll make it harder to get through those 10–12 hour kitchen days. She wants me in again this Thursday afternoon for a blood pressure check, so I have to leave the kitchen by 3:30.

On top of that, I snuck over to West Side Rehab & Wellness on Madison in November to see this physical therapist, Daniel Kim, about the foot and shoulder pain. He basically said, “custom orthotics and no more back-to-back 12-hour shifts.” I nodded like a good patient and then came back to the kitchen and did exactly that the next weekend. Orthotics = $$$ and cutting shifts = maybe losing clients, so here we are.

Money-wise, I’m still salty about that late October meeting at the Pilsen branch of First Community Credit Union. Patrick O’Neill shut down the $45k loan for the 18th Street storefront because of the 2022 net loss and the fact that my Chase Ink Business Cash card is sitting at like $9,500, totally maxed. I walked out of that office wanting to scream.

I’m trying not to panic, but it’s a lot: my body complaining, Elena and Daniel telling me to slow down, and Patrick basically saying “come back when you’re richer.”

Can we maybe sit down this weekend and look at my numbers again? I’ll feed you empanadas.

Besos,
Mari

metadata.subject: Quick life/health/money download

artifacts.content:
Hi Carlos,

Hope you had a good Thanksgiving and got at least one day where you didn’t look at a spreadsheet.

I wanted to follow up on our last check-in and be honest about where I’m at with Luna Latina right now.

Since summer, I’ve been pushing hard on catering and the weekly meal prep, and my body is starting to complain. In November I finally went to West Side Rehab & Wellness on Madison to see a physical therapist, Daniel Kim, about this chronic foot and shoulder pain from standing 10–12 hours a day. He recommended custom orthotics and said I should really stop doing back-to-back 12-hour shifts. I haven’t implemented either yet — the orthotics are expensive, and I’m scared to say no to weekend events.

On the health side, after that dizzy episode during the big September catering, I had a follow-up on October 3rd at Austin Community Health Center with a nurse practitioner, Elena Ruiz. She diagnosed stage 1 hypertension and put me on low-dose lisinopril. I’m trying to be better about taking it regularly, but I’ll admit I’ve skipped doses when I know I have a long day over the stove and worry about side effects.

Financially, the storefront dream hit a wall. In late October I had a closed-door meeting at the Pilsen branch of First Community Credit Union with Patrick O’Neill about a $45,000 small business loan for that 18th Street bakery space. He declined it because my 2022 tax return shows a net loss and my Chase Ink Business Cash card is maxed at around $9,500. It was pretty deflating.

On top of that, I’ve been sloppy with how I support my family. Since early this year I’ve been transferring $150 on the first of every month straight from the Luna Latina Kitchen business checking at Chase to my mom Rosa’s account to help cover Abuela Carmelita’s prescriptions at the Walgreens on Cermak. I know it should be recorded as an owner draw or something, but right now it’s just… not.

I’d love your guidance on two things in our next session: 1) how to clean up the books (including those family transfers and some off-the-books cash I’ve paid my line cook on crazy weekends), and 2) how to plan for hiring another part-timer so I can actually follow Daniel’s advice and not destroy my feet.

Thanks as always for listening and being real with me.

Best,
Marisol

metadata.subject: Health, debt, and some bookkeeping confessions

artifacts.content:
Miguel 🧰⚡️

Miguel: You alive? Haven’t heard from you since you posted those enchiladas on IG.

Marisol: Barely lol. Just finished dishes at the kitchen. My feet are screaming.

Miguel: I told you, you can’t keep doing those double shifts.

Marisol: I literally had Daniel Kim at West Side Rehab & Wellness on Madison tell me in November, “no more back-to-back 12-hour days,” and then I went and did three in a row. I need a clone.

Miguel: Or another line cook.

Marisol: Working on it. Also trying not to freak out about my blood pressure check with Elena at the Austin clinic on Thursday. Since she put me on that lisinopril after the October 3rd appointment, I keep worrying she’ll yell at me for skipping doses.

Miguel: She won’t yell. She’ll give you The Look.

Marisol: Lol true.

Miguel: How’s money stuff? Any news on that 18th St spot?

Marisol: Ugh, don’t start. Patrick O’Neill at First Community Credit Union in Pilsen basically shut me down in that late October meeting. No $45k loan because of the 2022 loss + my Chase Ink Business Cash at like $9,500 maxed. Felt like getting benched.

Miguel: That sucks, babe. But you’ll find another way.

Marisol: I hope so. Meanwhile I’m still sending $150 from the business account to my mom’s Chase on the 1st every month for Abuela’s meds at the Walgreens on Cermak. I know I shouldn’t do it straight from the business, but I can’t not help.

Miguel: We’ll figure it out. Also, can we talk this weekend? Not about money. About us.

Marisol: 😬

Miguel: Not a fight. Just… I keep thinking about that January night at my place in Humboldt Park when I said I wasn’t sure about marriage or kids because of my parents, and you left in tears saying, “I can’t keep building this business alone forever.” I hate that we never really unpacked that.

Marisol: Yeah. Me too. Saturday after your shift? We can walk Humboldt Park and actually talk.

Miguel: Deal. I’ll bring coffee. You bring that famous salsa.

Marisol: Always. 💚

Title: Random late-night brain dump

It’s 12:47 a.m., my shoulders are tight, and I can still smell roasted tomatillos on my hands.

Things swirling in my head:

1. FAMILY + GUILT

I keep replaying that kitchen table fight in Cicero after Abuela Carmen’s bathroom fall back in March 2023. I was staring at the tile while Papá accused me of “acting like a social worker” because I pushed for grab bars and getting rid of the throw rugs. I know he was scared, but it still stung. Now every Tuesday when I go over for dinner, I secretly check the bars and the rugs like some undercover inspector.

On top of that, I’ve been quietly sending $150 from the Luna Latina business checking at Chase to Mami’s account on the first of every month since early this year to help cover Abuela’s prescriptions at the Walgreens on Cermak. I haven’t logged it as an owner draw or anything. Carlos is going to shake his head when he sees that.

2. WORK + GRAY AREAS

Since June 2023, whenever we have those crazy last-minute Sunday catering gigs, I’ve paid Carla in cash at the shared kitchen on 18th Street and not always put the hours into QuickBooks. It started as “we’ll fix it later” and turned into a habit to avoid overtime and because I’m behind on payroll taxes. I know this could bite me.

3. OLD WOUNDS

Sometimes I still hear Professor Robert Klein at UIC in my head, sitting in BSB 140 the summer of 2013 after I failed his accounting class in the spring and had to retake it. “This could really hurt you if you ever run your own place,” he said while I stared at my messy spreadsheet. Joke’s on him because I *did* start my own place… except now, when I’m drowning in receipts and temp logs, I wonder if he was right.

4. BOUNDARIES

I also think about that severance meeting at El Corazón Grill in February 2021, in Linda Patterson’s office. I signed that non-disparagement clause after complaining about being pushed to work while I was mildly sick during COVID. That’s why I’m so strict with health protocols now.

Okay, enough. Tomorrow: walk, water, and no cash payments.

Hi Ana,

How’s my favorite maestra and my favorite four-year-old? Did Lucia convince you to let her wear the sparkly boots to school again?

I wanted to give you a heads up about the next few Tuesdays. I’m still planning on coming to Cicero for dinner with you all and Abuela, but I might be a little late two weeks from now because I have a follow-up at the Austin Community Health Center on W Chicago Ave. It’s with Elena Ruiz, the nurse practitioner I saw on October 3rd after that scary dizzy spell at the catering event. She said I have stage 1 hypertension and put me on a low-dose blood pressure med (lisinopril). I didn’t tell Mami because I don’t want her worrying, so please don’t mention it at the table.

Between that and the foot/shoulder stuff I saw a physical therapist for at West Side Rehab & Wellness on Madison (his name is Daniel Kim, very nice, very serious about “no more 12-hour shifts”), I feel like a little old lady some days.

Money is its own novela. In late October I sat in this tiny office at the Pilsen branch of First Community Credit Union with a guy named Patrick O’Neill, trying to get a $45,000 loan for that cute ex-bakery space on 18th Street. He said no because my 2022 taxes show a loss and my Chase Ink Business Cash card is maxed at $9,500. I left and immediately went to buy a concha because I wanted to cry.

I’m still sending the $150 from the business account to Mami’s every month for Abuela’s meds at the Walgreens on Cermak, and I know it’s not “correct” accounting-wise, but I also know you see the pharmacy receipts.

Anyway, that’s my update: body complaining, bank saying no, but customers still ordering enchiladas, so we keep going. Let me know what Lucia wants me to bring next Tuesday — enchiladas verdes or the pineapple-habanero salsa she calls “the spicy juice.”

Te quiero,
Mari

metadata.subject: Tuesdays, doctors, and other dramas

artifacts.content:
From: Laura Chen <laura.chen@cityofchicago.gov>
To: Shared Kitchen Tenants
Subject: Follow-Up: Temperature Log and Labeling Corrections – Luna Latina Kitchen
Date: 2024-08-19

Hi Ms. Rodriguez,

Thank you again for your time during the routine inspection at the shared commercial kitchen on August 14, 2024.

As discussed onsite, this email serves as a written follow-up regarding two minor, but important, food safety items observed in your Luna Latina Kitchen production area:

1. Cooling/temperature logs: The temperature log for your bottled salsa products (roasted tomatillo and salsa macha) was incomplete for the previous week. Specifically, several cooling entries were missing final temperature readings and/or times. Maintaining complete and accurate cooling logs is essential for ensuring that potentially hazardous foods move through the temperature danger zone in a safe and timely manner.

2. Product labeling: Two plastic containers stored in the walk-in cooler were unlabeled at the time of inspection. While you verbally identified the contents as roasted vegetables for empanada filling and a batch of pineapple-habanero salsa, all containers holding ready-to-eat food must be clearly labeled with product name and date prepared.

Per our conversation, you indicated that you would:

- Update your cooling/temperature log template and begin recording all required fields (product, start time, temperature checkpoints, final temperature, and time) for each batch of salsa produced.
- Implement a standard labeling system for all containers, including product name and preparation date.

Please reply to this email by August 26, 2024 confirming that these corrective actions have been completed. Based on our discussion, I do not anticipate that this will impact your licensing status, provided that the corrections are implemented and maintained.

If you have any questions or need clarification about cooling requirements or labeling best practices, feel free to contact me at this email address or by phone at (312) 555-0198.

Best regards,

Laura Chen
Sanitarian II
Chicago Department of Public Health
Food Protection Program

Date: Thursday, 2024-12-12
Time: 3:30 p.m. – 4:15 p.m.
Location: Austin Community Health Center, 4800 W Chicago Ave, 2nd floor clinic – exam room with Elena
Attendees: Me, Elena Ruiz (NP)

Notes:

- Bus from kitchen on 18th St to Austin clinic – leave by 2:45 p.m. so I’m not running in sweaty in my apron.
- Bring list of current meds (lisinopril low-dose) + how often I *actually* take them since the October 3, 2024 appointment when she called it stage 1 hypertension.
- Mention dizzy spell at September catering and how often I still get lightheaded on long days over the stove.
- Talk about what Daniel Kim at West Side Rehab & Wellness on Madison recommended in November re: custom orthotics + limiting back-to-back 12-hour shifts. Ask if she thinks the foot/shoulder pain is just overuse or something more.
- Ask specifically: is it safe to work 10–12 hour kitchen days while on lisinopril? Anything I should watch for (dehydration, standing in heat, etc.)?
- Bring up stress about money: Patrick O’Neill at First Community Credit Union (Pilsen branch) saying no to the $45,000 loan for 18th St because of 2022 net loss + $9,500 maxed Chase Ink Business Cash card. Ask if stress could be keeping BP high even with meds.
- Also mention monthly $150 transfers from Luna Latina Kitchen business checking at Chase to Mami’s account for Abuela’s Walgreens prescriptions on Cermak — constant low-level worry about supporting them.
- Goal: leave with clear plan for meds, maybe a note about modifying work hours I can show Carlos when we talk staffing.

artifacts.content:
Hey Sofia,

I hope you’re doing well! I wanted to give you an update on everything happening on my end. Things have been pretty busy, as usual, with Luna Latina Kitchen. We're continuing to operate out of our spot at 1632 W 18th Street, but I'm seriously considering that 1,000 sq ft space on 18th Street that I mentioned. The rent and deposit are stretching my budget a bit, so I appreciate all your help running the numbers and working on the branding. Hopefully, with some strategic planning, I can make it work by the end of 2025.

On a personal note, I've been trying to keep up with everything while also taking care of myself. After that dizzy spell in September, I had a check-up, and my doctor advised me to focus more on my health. I’ve been incorporating more walks into my routine and cutting back on the extra cups of coffee. It's hard, though, with my schedule, but I'm determined to stick with it.

Family dinners in Cicero with my parents and Abuela Carmelita are still a Tuesday night tradition. It’s comforting to spend time with them and make sure Abuela is settling in okay. Since her fall, I’ve been a bit more vigilant about her environment, making sure it’s safe and clutter-free.

Business-wise, the orders from the grocery stores stocking my salsas are keeping me on my toes. I’ve been testing new empanada recipes that hold up well after freezing, and I’m optimistic about adding them to our offerings soon. Also, my part-time line cook has been a lifesaver on those crazy busy Thursdays and Sundays.

A quick reminder, my current address is still 4827 W Monroe Street, Apartment 3B, here in Chicago, and I'm reachable at (312) 555-2874 if you need to chat about any of our plans or if you have more ideas for our social media push.

Thanks again for being such a great support, Sofia. Having you as both a cousin and a friend is invaluable, especially when balancing the chaos of running a business. Let me know when you’re free for a coffee catch-up!

Take care,
Marisol

personal_information.address: 7420 Elbow Drive SW, Apartment 304, Calgary, AB
personal_information.why_do_you_want_to_volunteer_at_carewest:
I am interested in volunteering at Carewest because I want to be more directly involved in supporting older adults and individuals going through rehabilitation in my own community. My grandmother lived in a long-term care facility for the last seven years of her life, and I saw first-hand how much of a difference caring staff and volunteers made to her mood, her sense of dignity, and her overall quality of life. I remember that her favourite part of the week was when a volunteer would come and play cards with her or simply sit and listen to her talk about her childhood in Vietnam.

Currently, I work full-time as a junior occupational therapist assistant at a private clinic, and I feel that volunteering at Carewest will give me a broader understanding of how care is delivered in a continuing-care and rehabilitation setting. I am particularly drawn to Carewest because of your reputation for resident-centred care and the range of programs you offer, from rehab and recovery to palliative care. I appreciate that volunteers are treated as part of the team, and that there is an emphasis on training and ongoing support.

On a personal level, I am looking for a way to balance my fairly technical day job with something that is more relational and community-focused. I enjoy spending time with seniors, listening to their stories, and helping them stay engaged in meaningful activities. I am comfortable assisting people who use walkers or wheelchairs, and I don’t mind taking a bit of extra time to make sure someone feels safe and heard. I also have some experience with basic therapeutic recreation activities through my job, such as gentle range-of-motion exercises, seated games, and simple cognitive activities like word games and memory prompts.

In addition, I recently completed a Mental Health First Aid course and a short workshop on dementia-friendly communication. I would like to apply those skills in a setting where they can make a tangible difference. I am aware that some residents may be experiencing confusion, grief, or frustration related to their health, and I want to be a calm, consistent, and compassionate presence for them.

Finally, I live about a 10-minute drive from several Carewest sites, and I plan to be in Calgary long term. I am not looking for a short-term placement; rather, I hope to build a stable volunteer routine where residents can get to know me and trust that I will show up when I say I will. My goal is to commit to at least one regular shift per week for a year or more, and to be flexible in learning what roles are most helpful to staff and residents.
personal_information.please_tell_us_what_are_some_of_your_hob:
I have a mix of quiet and active hobbies, many of which I think translate well into a volunteer role with seniors.

Reading and book discussions: I am an avid reader, especially of historical fiction, gentle mysteries, and memoirs. I enjoy reading aloud and have previously read short stories and poems to my grandmother and to a neighbour who had vision loss. I am comfortable projecting my voice clearly and adjusting my pace so that people can follow along. I would be happy to help with or lead a small reading circle, a current-events chat, or one-on-one reading visits.

Music: I play piano at an intermediate level and know a lot of older songs from the 1940s through the 1970s because my parents and grandmother loved that music. While I don’t have access to a piano everywhere, I do enjoy singing along to familiar songs and leading simple sing-alongs. I find that music can quickly change the atmosphere in a room and help people connect, even if they have memory challenges.

Crafts and creative activities: I enjoy simple, low-pressure crafts like card-making, scrapbooking, adult colouring, and basic knitting. I am not an expert artist, but I am patient and good at breaking down steps into small, manageable pieces. In the past, I have helped my grandmother with large-print crossword puzzles, colouring pages, and organizing photo albums. I like the idea of assisting residents with similar activities, especially ones that can be tailored to their abilities.

Walking and light fitness: I like going for walks along the Glenmore Reservoir and doing light yoga and stretching at home. I understand the importance of safe mobility and pacing, especially for older adults or people in rehab. I would be comfortable accompanying residents on short, supervised walks (as appropriate), pushing wheelchairs for outings under staff direction, or helping with simple, seated movement activities led by staff or recreation therapists.

Cooking and food: I enjoy cooking simple, healthy meals and trying new recipes, especially Vietnamese and Canadian comfort foods. While I know direct food preparation might not always be part of a volunteer role, I am very comfortable helping with non-clinical aspects of mealtimes, such as assisting with setting up trays, pouring drinks, or helping residents open packages if that is allowed under your guidelines.

Technology and organization: I am fairly comfortable with basic technology—smartphones, tablets, and simple apps. I have helped my parents and some older neighbours learn how to use video calls, send photos, and manage their email. If there are residents who are interested in staying connected with family through technology, I would be happy to patiently walk them through things at their pace. I also genuinely enjoy small organizing tasks, like sorting drawers or tidying activity supplies, which I know can be useful in a busy care environment.

Overall, my interests tend to be calm, relationship-focused activities that can easily be adapted to different abilities. I am patient, I don’t mind repeating instructions or slowing down, and I genuinely enjoy getting to know people and hearing about their lives.

volunteering_preferences.other_area_applying_for: I would also be interested in assisting with any dementia-specific recreational programs or memory-care units, particularly activities that focus on music, reminiscence, or gentle sensory stimulation, if such programs are available at the Carewest site where I am placed.

While I don’t have access to a piano everywhere, I do enjoy singing along to familiar songs and leading simple sing-alongs. I find that music can quickly change the atmosphere in a room and help people connect, even if they have memory challenges.

Crafts and creative activities: I enjoy simple, low-pressure crafts like card-making, scrapbooking, adult colouring, and basic knitting. I am not an expert artist, but I am patient and good at breaking down steps into small, manageable pieces. In the past, I have helped my grandmother with large-print crossword puzzles, colouring pages, and organizing photo albums. I like the idea of assisting residents with similar activities, especially ones that can be tailored to their abilities.

Walking and light fitness: I like going for walks along the Glenmore Reservoir and doing light yoga and stretching at home. I understand the importance of safe mobility and pacing, especially for older adults or people in rehab. I would be comfortable accompanying residents on short, supervised walks (as appropriate), pushing wheelchairs for outings under staff direction, or helping with simple, seated movement activities led by staff or recreation therapists.

Cooking and food: I enjoy cooking simple, healthy meals and trying new recipes, especially Vietnamese and Canadian comfort foods. While I know direct food preparation might not always be part of a volunteer role, I am very comfortable helping with non-clinical aspects of mealtimes, such as assisting with setting up trays, pouring drinks, or helping residents open packages if that is allowed under your guidelines.

Technology and organization: I am fairly comfortable with basic technology—smartphones, tablets, and simple apps. I have helped my parents and some older neighbours learn how to use video calls, send photos, and manage their email. If there are residents who are interested in staying connected with family through technology, I would be happy to patiently walk them through things at their pace. I also genuinely enjoy small organizing tasks, like sorting drawers or tidying activity supplies, which I know can be useful in a busy care environment.

Overall, my interests tend to be calm, relationship-focused activities that can easily be adapted to different abilities. I am patient, I don’t mind repeating instructions or slowing down, and I genuinely enjoy getting to know people and hearing about their lives.

persona.employer: Foothills Mobility & Rehab Clinic (private outpatient clinic)
persona.education: Occupational/Physical Therapist Assistant Diploma, Southern Alberta Institute of Technology (SAIT), Class of 2019
family_members: Lan Phuong Tran – mother, 57, licensed practical nurse at Rockyview General Hospital
Minh Quang Nguyen – father, 60, retired mechanical engineer (former oil and gas), part-time math tutor
Daniel Minh Nguyen – younger brother, 24, software developer in Vancouver, BC
Bà Ngoại (Huong Le) – maternal grandmother, deceased 2021, formerly in long-term care in Edmonton
Kimberly “Kim” Tran – maternal aunt, 54, lives in Edmonton

persona.background_context:
This is Amelia “Amy” Thao Nguyen, a 28-year-old Vietnamese-Canadian living in a one-bedroom apartment on Elbow Drive SW in Calgary, about a 10-minute drive from several continuing-care centres and from her parents’ house in Haysboro. She works full-time as a junior occupational therapist assistant at Foothills Mobility & Rehab Clinic, a small private outpatient rehab clinic that mainly serves adults recovering from orthopedic surgeries, workplace injuries, and strokes. Amy has been in this role since September 2020, after graduating from SAIT’s Occupational/Physical Therapist Assistant program in 2019 and spending her first year covering a mix of casual positions in long-term care and home-care agencies. Her workdays are fairly structured: she’s usually at the clinic by 8:00 a.m., helping set up treatment rooms, organizing adaptive equipment, and reviewing the day’s schedule with the supervising occupational therapist. She spends most of her day guiding clients through exercise programs, practicing safe transfers, and documenting progress notes.

Amy’s current life is shaped by the loss of her maternal grandmother, Huong, who passed away in late 2021 after seven years in a long-term care facility in Edmonton. Those years of visiting her grandmother—often spending weekends driving up with her parents—left a strong impression on her. She saw how much small, consistent acts from volunteers could change her grandmother’s week, whether it was a game of cards, a sing-along of old Vietnamese songs, or simply listening to her talk about her childhood. Since then, Amy has gravitated toward work and activities that keep her grounded in relationships with older adults. Outside of her job, she has completed a Mental Health First Aid course and a short workshop on dementia-friendly communication in 2023, and she’s been gradually carving out time in her week—usually a three-hour block on Monday or Wednesday mornings or Friday afternoons—to offer steady, face-to-face support to seniors in her community.

Day to day, Amy’s routine is a mix of calm habits and quiet responsibilities. She wakes up around 6:30 a.m., does 15–20 minutes of light yoga in her living room, and drinks tea by the window that looks out over Elbow Drive before heading to work. Evenings are slower: she cooks simple meals—often a mix of Vietnamese dishes like bò kho or canh chua and Canadian comfort food like roasted chicken and vegetables—reads historical fiction or gentle mystery novels, and keeps in touch with her younger brother Daniel in Vancouver via video calls, sometimes walking him through family recipes or troubleshooting his attempts at phở. On weekends, she often walks along the Glenmore Reservoir or meets her long-term boyfriend, Jason, for coffee and a stroll through Heritage Park when the weather is good. She and Jason have been together for four years and are slowly talking about the logistics of moving in together in the next year, weighing the cost of rent, their commutes, and staying close to her parents as they age.

Amy’s biggest ongoing concerns revolve around balance and sustainability. Her job can be physically and emotionally demanding, especially when she’s working with clients who are frustrated by slow progress or dealing with chronic pain. She’s careful about her own back and shoulders, doing regular stretching and being meticulous with body mechanics when assisting with transfers. Emotionally, she’s still processing grief over her grandmother, which sometimes surfaces when she’s with older clients who remind her of Huong. Rather than avoiding that discomfort, she channels it into being a calm, patient presence—willing to sit quietly with someone who is confused, repeat instructions gently, or sing a familiar song from the 1960s if it lightens the mood.

Her social life is modest but meaningful. Amy is close to her parents, often stopping by their house for Sunday lunch, where her mother, Lan, sends her home with containers of homemade food and her father, Minh, quizzes her on whether she’s contributing enough to her RRSP. She keeps up with a small circle of friends from SAIT, meeting them for occasional trivia nights or potluck dinners. She enjoys low-key crafts—card-making, scrapbooking, adult colouring, and basic knitting—and sometimes brings small projects to share with older neighbours in her building, helping them organize photos on their phones or set up video calls with distant family. Technology has become another quiet way she bridges generations, patiently teaching older adults how to use tablets, send pictures, or join a family group chat.

As she looks ahead to the next couple of years, Amy is thinking about taking on more formal training—maybe a part-time bachelor’s degree in health sciences at the University of Calgary—or specializing further in geriatric rehab. She wants to stay in Calgary long term, build stable routines, and be someone the people around her can rely on. Her life is not dramatic; it’s a steady weave of work, family, and small, consistent acts of care. Her main hope is that by showing up week after week—for her clients, her family, and the older adults she spends time with—she can offer the same sense of dignity, safety, and companionship that meant so much to her grandmother in her final years.

secrets.secret_content: Since March 2023, Amy has been seeing counsellor Dr. Melissa Carter at the Calgary Counselling Centre twice a month for grief and anxiety related to her grandmother Huong’s death and emotional burnout from working with older adults. Her session notes are titled things like “Processing long-term care losses – May 12, 2024” and she sometimes emails Dr. Carter with subject lines like “Struggling after tough week at the clinic and with seniors.”
secrets.why_sensitive: Disclosure of ongoing mental health treatment and burnout could expose Amy to stigma, misjudgment about her emotional stability, or concerns about her fitness to work or volunteer in care settings. It might also affect how supervisors assign her to vulnerable residents or how colleagues perceive her professionalism and resilience.
secrets.how_disclosed: While elaborating on why she wants to volunteer with seniors and how she copes emotionally, Amy might write in an email to a friend or in a draft application, “My counsellor, Dr. Carter at Calgary Counselling Centre, has been helping me process the grief from Bà Ngoại’s time in long-term care,” inadvertently revealing specific mental health treatment details.

secrets.secret_content: Amy has had intermittent low back and right shoulder pain since late 2022 due to repetitive client transfers at Foothills Mobility & Rehab Clinic. In January 2024 she had an assessment with physiotherapist Mark Jensen at the Southland Physiotherapy & Wellness Centre, where he advised her to limit heavy lifts and documented “early signs of overuse injury in OTA duties.”
secrets.why_sensitive: If widely known, this could lead to concerns about her physical capacity to perform her job or volunteer safely, potentially affecting employment opportunities, future promotions, or volunteer placement in rehab and community outings. It could also influence how liability and workplace accommodation are viewed by her employer or a volunteer coordinator.
secrets.how_disclosed: When discussing her suitability for rehab and recovery roles in an email to a coordinator or in conversation with a friend, she might say, “My physio, Mark at Southland Physio, told me after my January 2024 assessment that I have to be careful with heavy transfers,” revealing a work-related health limitation.

secrets.secret_content: In October 2021, Amy’s family had a serious conflict with staff at her grandmother Huong’s long-term care facility in Edmonton (Silver Meadows Continuing Care Centre) after an incident where Huong was left in soiled clothing for several hours. Amy wrote a detailed complaint email titled “Concerns about dignity and care for resident Huong Le – Room 214” to the unit manager and Alberta Health Services’ Patient Relations office.
secrets.why_sensitive: This could be seen as contentious or adversarial toward a care facility, potentially making future care providers or volunteer coordinators wary about her being perceived as a ‘difficult’ family member or volunteer. It might also strain professional relationships if colleagues are connected to that facility or health authority.
secrets.how_disclosed: While explaining her motivation to support dignity in care, she might tell a close friend or draft in a personal statement, “After what happened in Room 214 at Silver Meadows in October 2021, when we had to file a complaint email to the manager,” inadvertently sharing specific conflict details and facility names.

secrets.secret_content: In June 2023, Amy received a written warning from her supervisor, senior OT Karen Douglas at Foothills Mobility & Rehab Clinic, after she forgot to complete one client’s progress note before leaving for the day. The incident was documented in her HR file under the subject line “Documentation lapse – reminder of charting policy,” and she worries it might affect future references.
secrets.why_sensitive: Revealing a formal written warning could harm her professional reputation, raise questions about reliability or documentation accuracy, and potentially influence whether she is trusted with independent responsibilities in volunteer or work settings.
secrets.how_disclosed: When trying to reassure a trusted colleague or volunteer coordinator about her commitment to careful documentation, she might say, “Ever since the charting warning Karen put in my file last June at Foothills, I double-check every note,” inadvertently exposing a disciplinary record.

secrets.secret_content: Since February 2022, Amy has been sending $250–$300 a month via Interac e-Transfer to her aunt Kimberly Tran in Edmonton to help cover leftover debts from Huong’s long-term care costs and funeral expenses, which were put on a shared family line of credit at TD Canada Trust (account managed by her father, Minh).
secrets.why_sensitive: This reveals private family financial strain, debt related to long-term care, and Amy’s own limited disposable income. Disclosure could lead to judgment about her financial stability, affect how others view her ability to commit long term, or create awkwardness with family members who contribute less.
secrets.how_disclosed: When explaining to a close friend why she prefers local, low-cost volunteer activities instead of travel, she might say, “I’m still sending Aunt Kim about $250 a month to help with Bà Ngoại’s care and funeral line of credit at TD,” revealing specific financial obligations.

secrets.secret_content: In early 2020, Amy’s younger brother Daniel disclosed to her over a late-night FaceTime call from his apartment in Vancouver that he is gay and has not yet told their parents. Amy has kept this confidential, and they often message about it under innocuous subjects like “Vancouver roommate stuff” so it doesn’t draw attention if their parents glance at his phone.
secrets.why_sensitive: If this information were shared without Daniel’s consent, it could cause significant family conflict, emotional distress for Daniel, and potential tension with their parents depending on their views. It also risks outing him before he is ready, which can have serious social and psychological consequences.
secrets.how_disclosed: While talking with a close friend about why she values safe, non-judgmental spaces for young and older LGBTQ+ people, she might say, “When Daniel told me from his place in Vancouver back in 2020 that he’s gay and hasn’t told Mom and Dad yet,” inadvertently outing him.

secrets.secret_content: In November 2023, Amy and her boyfriend Jason had a serious argument at his condo near Chinook LRT about moving in together, during which Jason admitted he feels pressured to support his parents financially and is hesitant to commit to a joint lease. Amy later wrote a private journal entry titled “Do Jason and I want the same future?” and has not shared these doubts with her family.
secrets.why_sensitive: Sharing detailed relationship conflict and Jason’s financial responsibilities could damage others’ perceptions of him, create awkwardness with both families, and expose Amy’s private doubts about a long-term relationship she otherwise presents as stable.
secrets.how_disclosed: While emailing a close friend about why she prefers flexible volunteer shifts on Mondays and Fridays, she might add, “After that blow-up at Jason’s place near Chinook in November about moving in and his parents’ finances, I really need my own space and routines,” inadvertently revealing intimate relationship issues.

secrets.secret_content: During her final semester at SAIT in Spring 2019, Amy failed her first attempt at the “Gerontology and Dementia Care” practicum evaluation at Bethany Riverview because a supervising therapist, Elena Morris, noted she appeared emotionally overwhelmed and tearful after a resident with advanced dementia became aggressive. She had to repeat the placement in Summer 2019 under closer supervision.
secrets.why_sensitive: Disclosing a failed practicum and concerns about emotional resilience in dementia care could lead to doubts about her suitability for dementia-specific volunteer roles, potentially affecting selection for memory-care programs or advanced training opportunities.
secrets.how_disclosed: When explaining to a trusted mentor why she is both drawn to and nervous about dementia-specific recreational programs, she might say, “When I had to redo my gerontology practicum at Bethany Riverview in Summer 2019 after Elena failed me for getting overwhelmed,” inadvertently revealing an academic setback tied to performance concerns.

artifacts.content:
Hi Daniel,

Just finished my tea and figured I’d write before I get sucked into another “one more chapter” of this mystery novel.

I’ve been thinking a lot about how we’re both juggling everyone else’s needs right now. Between my clients at Foothills, the seniors I see, and family stuff, my brain feels like it’s on permanent “on-call.” Since March 2023 I’ve been seeing a counsellor at Calgary Counselling Centre – her name’s Dr. Melissa Carter – about twice a month. We’ve been working through the grief from Bà Ngoại’s years in long-term care and how it still hits me out of nowhere. My last session was literally titled “Processing long-term care losses – May 12, 2024” in her portal, which felt weirdly on-the-nose.

I told her about that October 2021 mess at Silver Meadows in Edmonton, when we found Bà in Room 214 in soiled clothes and ended up sending that big complaint email (“Concerns about dignity and care for resident Huong Le – Room 214”) to the unit manager and Alberta Health Services Patient Relations. She keeps saying it makes sense that I’m so intense about dignity and routine with my own clients now.

Physically I’m trying to be smarter too. My back and right shoulder have been grumpy since late 2022 from all the transfers at Foothills. Remember when I went to Southland Physiotherapy & Wellness Centre in January? Mark Jensen, the physio there, wrote “early signs of overuse injury in OTA duties” in his report and told me to cut back on heavy lifts. So I’m doing my stretches like a good nerd and being more assertive about asking for help with bigger clients.

Money-wise, I’m still sending Aunt Kim $250–$300 a month by e-Transfer to chip away at that TD Canada Trust line of credit for Bà’s long-term care and funeral. Dad keeps saying we’re almost there, but it still means saying no to random trips and fancy dinners. It’s okay, though – Glenmore Reservoir walks and home-cooked bò kho beat Vegas anyway.

How are things on your end? How’s “Vancouver roommate stuff” going? (Yes, I’m using the code phrase.) I’m proud of you for figuring things out at your own pace. Whenever you’re ready to talk to Mom and Dad, I’m on your team – but there’s no rush.

Call me this weekend? Maybe Sunday evening after Mom’s done feeding us.

Love,
Amy

metadata.subject: Catching up before the weekend

artifacts.content:
Hi Jason,

Just got home from work and my feet are finally up, so I wanted to write this out while it’s still clear in my head instead of half-asleep mumbling on the phone.

I’ve been circling around the whole “moving in together” topic again, especially since it’s been almost a year since that huge argument at your condo near Chinook LRT last November. I still remember standing in your kitchen, both of us exhausted, and you blurting out that you feel pressured to support your parents financially and aren’t ready to commit to a joint lease. I went home that night and poured everything into a journal entry I titled “Do Jason and I want the same future?” and honestly, I still reread parts of it when I feel wobbly.

I’m not bringing this up to re-fight that night. I actually get it more now. Between my own stuff – sending Aunt Kim $250–$300 every month since February 2022 to help pay down that TD Canada Trust line of credit for Bà Ngoại’s long-term care and funeral, plus trying to save something for my RRSP – I feel the squeeze too. Rent, gas to drive up to see your folks sometimes, groceries, all of it adds up.

On top of that, work hasn’t exactly been light. Since late 2022 my low back and right shoulder have been acting up from all the transfers. When I saw Mark Jensen at Southland Physiotherapy & Wellness Centre in January 2024 and he wrote “early signs of overuse injury in OTA duties” and told me to limit heavy lifts, it scared me a bit. It made me realize I can’t just muscle through everything forever.

So when I think about moving in, I’m not picturing some Instagram-perfect condo. I’m picturing two tired people trying to protect their bodies and their parents and still have a little space to breathe. I’d rather we be honest about what we can afford and what emotional bandwidth we actually have.

Can we set aside an evening next week – maybe Thursday after dinner – to talk calmly about timelines and options? Even if the answer is “not this year,” I’d like that to be a shared decision instead of something hanging over us.

I love you, even when money and logistics are messy.

Amy

metadata.subject: Circling back to us

artifacts.content:
Title: Debrief after tough week – clinic + seniors
Date: 2024-06-09

I need to get this out of my head so I can sleep.

1. Work
This week at Foothills reminded me of June 2023, when Karen gave me that written warning – “Documentation lapse – reminder of charting policy” – because I forgot to finish one progress note before I left. I still feel my stomach drop when I think about it. It’s made me obsessive about double-checking every chart, which is good, but also exhausting.

The caseload is heavier again, and my shoulder and low back are grumbling. Ever since late 2022 they’ve been touchy from all the transfers. Mark Jensen at Southland Physiotherapy & Wellness Centre flagged “early signs of overuse injury in OTA duties” back in my January 2024 assessment and told me to limit heavy lifts. I’m trying, but it’s hard to say no when someone needs help.

2. Volunteering / long-term care memories
Spending time with the seniors this week stirred up memories of Bà at Silver Meadows in Edmonton. I keep flashing back to October 2021, walking into Room 214 and finding her in soiled clothing after who knows how long. We ended up writing that long complaint email, “Concerns about dignity and care for resident Huong Le – Room 214,” to the unit manager and AHS Patient Relations. No wonder I get so tense if I see anyone left waiting too long for help now.

3. Counselling
I’m grateful I’ve been seeing Dr. Melissa Carter at Calgary Counselling Centre since March 2023. The session she titled “Processing long-term care losses – May 12, 2024” felt like a turning point, but weeks like this show it’s still raw. I need to email her (subject line idea: “Struggling after tough week at the clinic and with seniors”) and maybe see if we can add an extra session.

Action items for myself:
- Ask Karen about redistributing the heaviest transfers.
- Book a follow-up with Mark if pain keeps flaring.
- Email Dr. Carter tomorrow.
- Schedule something light this weekend (Glenmore walk, Heritage Park with Jason) so life isn’t all care, all the time.

metadata.title: Debrief after tough week – clinic + seniors

Time: 3:00–4:00 p.m.
Location: Calgary Counselling Centre, 10th Ave SW – Office 402 (Dr. Carter)

Notes:
- This is the ongoing twice-a-month session I’ve had with Dr. Melissa Carter since March 2023 for grief/anxiety around Bà Ngoại and burnout from working with older adults.
- Today’s focus: “Processing long-term care losses – May 12, 2024” (her suggested title in the portal actually fits).

Things to bring up:
1. Silver Meadows incident
   - Revisit October 2021 at Silver Meadows Continuing Care Centre in Edmonton.
   - Walking into Room 214 and finding Bà in soiled clothes after several hours.
   - How writing that complaint email, “Concerns about dignity and care for resident Huong Le – Room 214,” to the unit manager and AHS Patient Relations still impacts how I see staff/volunteers now.

2. Work strain + body
   - Ongoing low back and right shoulder pain since late 2022 from client transfers at Foothills.
   - January 2024 assessment with physiotherapist Mark Jensen at Southland Physiotherapy & Wellness Centre – his note about “early signs of overuse injury in OTA duties” and advice to limit heavy lifts.
   - Fear of letting coworkers down if I say no.

3. Guilt + money
   - Monthly Interac e-Transfers ($250–$300) to Aunt Kim in Edmonton since Feb 2022 to help with the TD Canada Trust family line of credit for Bà’s long-term care and funeral.
   - Feeling torn between saving for my own future (RRSP, maybe a degree at U of C) and paying down that debt.

4. Boundaries with volunteering
   - How to keep showing up for seniors in the community without recreating the emotional overload of my SAIT gerontology practicum.

Reminder: After session, walk by the river or read at a café before going home. No errands.

Hi Aunt Kim,

I just finished packing some bún chả leftovers for lunch tomorrow and thought I’d send a quick update.

First, I sent this month’s transfer a bit early – you should see an Interac e-Transfer for $275 in your TD account tonight. Since February 2022 I’ve been trying to keep it between $250 and $300 each month to chip away at that TD Canada Trust family line of credit for Bà Ngoại’s long-term care and the funeral costs. Dad said at Sunday lunch that the balance is finally starting to look less scary, which is a relief.

Work at Foothills is busy but mostly good. I’m still careful with my back and right shoulder – they’ve been on and off painful since late 2022. When I saw physiotherapist Mark Jensen at Southland Physiotherapy & Wellness Centre in January 2024, he wrote “early signs of overuse injury in OTA duties” in his assessment and told me to limit heavy lifts. I’m listening (mostly!) and asking for help with the heavier transfers.

Emotionally, I’ve been thinking about Bà a lot again. I had a counselling session with Dr. Melissa Carter at Calgary Counselling Centre last month, and we spent most of it talking about those seven years in care and that awful October 2021 afternoon at Silver Meadows in Edmonton – Room 214, when we found her in soiled clothing and ended up sending the complaint email (“Concerns about dignity and care for resident Huong Le – Room 214”) to the unit manager and Alberta Health Services Patient Relations. Even now, when I’m volunteering with seniors, I notice I’m hyper-alert about whether people look comfortable and clean.

On a lighter note, Mom made canh chua this weekend and tried to claim she invented your trick of adding extra pineapple. We all know where she got it. She sent me home with three containers, so I’m basically living off your recipes.

Please let me know if the transfer doesn’t go through for any reason. Give my love to everyone in Edmonton.

Thương,
Amy

metadata.subject: This month’s transfer + a quick update

artifacts.content:
Title: Why dementia work still scares me (and why I’m drawn to it)
Date: 2024-03-18

I keep hovering over opportunities to help with dementia-specific recreation programs and then backing away. Time to be honest with myself.

Part of it goes back to SAIT in 2019. During my final semester gerontology and dementia care practicum at Bethany Riverview, I actually failed my first evaluation. Elena Morris, my supervising therapist, wrote that I seemed emotionally overwhelmed and tearful after a resident with advanced dementia became aggressive. I had to redo the whole placement in Summer 2019 under closer supervision. It was humiliating at the time, but also… accurate. I wasn’t ready.

Then there’s Bà. The October 2021 incident at Silver Meadows Continuing Care Centre in Edmonton is seared into me – walking into Room 214, seeing her left in soiled clothing for hours, the anger and shame. We wrote that detailed complaint email (“Concerns about dignity and care for resident Huong Le – Room 214”) to the unit manager and Alberta Health Services Patient Relations, but the image still pops up whenever I smell certain cleaning products.

Now I’m older, working as a junior OTA at Foothills, and I’ve been seeing Dr. Melissa Carter at Calgary Counselling Centre since March 2023 to process grief and burnout. One of our sessions was literally labeled “Processing long-term care losses – May 12, 2024.” I also know my body has limits – my low back and right shoulder have been cranky since late 2022, and Mark Jensen at Southland Physiotherapy & Wellness Centre warned in January 2024 about “early signs of overuse injury in OTA duties” and told me to be careful with heavy transfers.

So when I picture helping in a memory-care unit – music, reminiscence, gentle sensory activities – I feel both pulled and scared. I want to be the calm, consistent person I wished Bà had. I also don’t want to end up sobbing in a supply closet or wrecking my back.

Maybe the answer isn’t “never,” but “slowly, with support” – shorter shifts, clear boundaries, and lots of debriefing.

metadata.title: Why dementia work still scares me

Hi Kim,

I hope this email finds you well! I wanted to share some exciting news and get your thoughts. I've decided to start volunteering at Carewest, aiming to support older adults in our community. This decision is deeply influenced by the positive impact I saw in Bà Ngoại's life during her time in long-term care, and I hope to bring that same sense of dignity and companionship to others.

I'll be volunteering weekly, likely on Mondays, Wednesdays, or Fridays, both in the mornings and afternoons for about three hours per shift. My goal is to offer consistent support, particularly in areas like rehab and recovery or community programs. I’d love to focus on dementia-specific programs if available, as I have some experience with therapeutic recreation activities from my job at the clinic. I remember how much Bà Ngoại enjoyed music and stories from her past, and I hope to bring some of that joy to others.

I'm excited to use my skills in music and crafts—maybe even lead a reading circle or a sing-along session. I’ve always believed in the power of music and storytelling to connect with people, especially seniors. Plus, having completed a Mental Health First Aid course and a workshop on dementia-friendly communication, I feel ready to be a calm and compassionate presence, adapting to each resident's unique needs.

It’s comforting to know I live just a 10-minute drive from several Carewest sites, which makes this commitment sustainable long-term. I’m not just looking for a one-time engagement; I want to build a stable routine where residents can count on me. The opportunity to balance my technical day job with something more relational is truly appealing.

In other news, Jason and I are slowly planning the logistics of moving in together, which is quite exciting. We’re considering places closer to my parents in Haysboro, which will also make visiting them easier.

Let me know if you have any advice or if you'd like to catch up over coffee soon. I’d love to hear about what’s new in Edmonton!

Take care,
Amelia

applicant__business.applicant_business_name: Willow Brook Early Learning Center, LLC
applicant__business.applicant_business_address: 1458 West Maple Avenue, Suite B, Brookfield, WI 53045

conditional_use_request.cup_request_description:
Willow Brook Early Learning Center, LLC is requesting a Conditional Use Permit to operate a small, neighborhood-focused child day care center within the existing commercial building located at 1458 West Maple Avenue, Brookfield, WI 53045. The property is currently improved with a one-story multi-tenant commercial structure that has historically housed professional offices and low-intensity service businesses. The proposed use is consistent with the character of the surrounding neighborhood, which is a mix of residential homes and small-scale commercial uses along West Maple Avenue.

Overview of Proposed Use:
The proposed child care center will provide full-day and part-day early learning and care services for children ages 6 weeks through 5 years, with a maximum licensed capacity of 48 children at any one time. The center will occupy approximately 4,200 square feet on the ground floor (Suite B) of the building. This space will be fully built out to include classrooms, a small indoor gross-motor play area, staff break room, administrative office, child restrooms, a kitchen/food prep area for snacks and reheated catered lunches, and storage.

Hours of Operation and Staffing:
Regular hours of operation will be Monday through Friday, 6:30 AM to 6:00 PM. Children will typically arrive between 6:30 AM and 9:00 AM and depart between 3:30 PM and 6:00 PM, resulting in staggered traffic rather than a single large peak. We anticipate that the busiest drop-off window will be between 7:15 AM and 8:30 AM.

At full enrollment, there will be a maximum of 18 employees on site at any given time, including:
- 1 Center Director (typically on site 7:30 AM–4:30 PM)
- 1 Assistant Director/Administrative Coordinator
- 8–10 Lead and Assistant Teachers assigned to classrooms
- 2 Float/Support Teachers who move between classrooms as needed
- 1 Part-time cook/housekeeping assistant (midday hours)
- 1 On-call substitute teacher (not always present)

We will maintain teacher-to-child ratios that meet or exceed Wisconsin Department of Children and Families (DCF) licensing requirements. Staff schedules are staggered so that not all employees arrive or leave at the same time, further reducing concentrated traffic.

Traffic, Parking, and Site Circulation:
We have prepared and attached a parking and circulation plan showing existing parking spaces, designated staff parking, and short-term drop-off/pick-up zones. The building currently has 42 striped off-street parking spaces shared among three tenants. Based on our analysis of current usage, peak daytime demand for the other tenants (a dental office and a small accounting firm) occurs between 9:00 AM and 3:00 PM and typically utilizes 18–22 spaces. Our center anticipates needing 16 spaces for staff and 6–8 spaces for short-term parent parking during the morning and evening windows.

To minimize congestion and ensure safety, we will:
- Designate 6 clearly signed, short-term (10-minute) parking spaces directly in front of our suite for parent/guardian drop-off and pick-up.
- Encourage families, in our enrollment materials and parent handbook, to limit drop-off and pick-up times to under 10 minutes.
- Stagger classroom schedules so that not all families are arriving or leaving within the same 15-minute window.
- Maintain clear pedestrian pathways from the parking lot to the main entrance, with high-visibility striping and signage.
- Instruct staff to park in the rear section of the lot, leaving front spaces available for families and other tenants’ clients.

Noise, Neighborhood Compatibility, and Outdoor Play:
We recognize that noise can be a concern for nearby residents. Our outdoor play area has been carefully planned to minimize impacts. The fenced outdoor playground (approximately 3,000 square feet) will be located on the south side of the building, away from the nearest single-family homes and buffered by an existing row of mature evergreen trees. A 6-foot solid wood privacy fence will be installed along the property line, supplemented by additional shrub plantings to provide both visual and noise screening.

Outdoor play will be scheduled in small groups (typically 12–16 children at a time) for 30–45 minute blocks between 9:00 AM and 11:30 AM and between 3:00 PM and 5:00 PM, weather permitting. We will:
- Avoid loudspeakers, amplified music, or whistles outside.
- Include expectations for respectful outdoor behavior (no sustained screaming or shouting) in our staff training and classroom routines.
- Use soft play surfacing and landscaping elements that help absorb sound.

Building Modifications and Safety:
The existing space will be remodeled to comply with all applicable building, fire, and health codes. Planned improvements include:
- Installation of child-height sinks and toilets in classroom-adjacent restrooms.
- Upgrades to emergency lighting, exit signage, and fire alarm systems as required by the Fire Department.
- Secure, controlled-access entry at the main door with a keypad/fob system for parents and staff, and a video doorbell/intercom for visitors.
- Interior reconfiguration to create separated classrooms with appropriate egress and visibility (half-glass doors and interior windows).
- Use of non-toxic, durable, and easily cleanable finishes (low-VOC paint, commercial-grade vinyl flooring, etc.).

All improvements will be reviewed through the standard building permit process, and we will comply with all conditions imposed by the Building and Fire Departments. We have already consulted informally with a licensed architect and a child care design specialist to ensure that our preliminary layout meets DCF licensing standards as well as local code requirements.

Licensing and Regulatory Compliance:
The center will be licensed by the Wisconsin Department of Children and Families as a Group Child Care Center. We will adhere to all state regulations regarding staff qualifications, background checks, health and safety protocols, emergency preparedness, and curriculum planning. As the owner and director, I (Sarah Martinez) hold a Bachelor’s degree in Early Childhood Education from Marquette University and have over 12 years of experience in licensed child care programs, including 5 years as an assistant director at another center in Wauwatosa.

Community Benefits and Need:
Several nearby residential subdivisions include many young families, and there is a documented waiting list at multiple existing centers in the broader Brookfield/Wauwatosa area. Our goal is to provide:
- Reliable, high-quality child care for working families who live or work within a 10–15 minute drive.
- A play-based, developmentally appropriate early learning program that prepares children for kindergarten in the Elmbrook School District and surrounding districts.
- Stable, locally based jobs for early childhood professionals with competitive wages and benefits.

We intend to build strong relationships with our neighbors by:
- Hosting an annual open house/family night and inviting adjacent businesses and nearby residents.
- Participating in neighborhood clean-up days and local charitable drives (e.g., winter clothing donations, school supply collections).
- Maintaining open communication and providing a direct phone number and email address for neighbors to contact us with questions or concerns.

Refuse, Deliveries, and General Operations:
Trash and recycling will be stored in the existing shared dumpster enclosure at the rear of the property. We do not anticipate an increase in pickup frequency beyond the current weekly schedule. Deliveries (primarily food service, diapers, and educational materials) will be limited to standard business hours (9:00 AM–3:00 PM) and will use the rear service entrance whenever possible.

We will not prepare full meals on site; instead, we will receive catered lunches from a licensed commercial kitchen and provide simple, pre-packaged or easily prepared snacks. This minimizes cooking-related odors, grease, and potential impacts on other tenants.

Summary:
In summary, the requested Conditional Use Permit is to allow the operation of a licensed child day care center in an existing commercial space, with modest interior and exterior improvements, carefully managed traffic and parking, and thoughtful measures to minimize noise and other potential impacts on neighboring properties. We believe this use is compatible with the mixed commercial-residential character of West Maple Avenue and will provide a much-needed service to local families while maintaining the safety, comfort, and expectations of the surrounding community.
