persona.background_context:
This is Daniel Marcus Holloway, a 41‑year‑old recreation program coordinator living on Oakview Ridge Drive in the northern part of High Point, North Carolina. He and his wife, Rachel Marie Holloway, moved into their current home in late 2019 after nearly a decade renting a townhouse off W Greenway Street in Winston‑Salem, where Daniel had lived since his early twenties. The move cut his commute to the Deep River Recreation Center to about fifteen minutes and put them in what they felt was a calmer neighborhood for their two kids, 11‑year‑old Evan and 8‑year‑old Chloe. Daniel still thinks of Winston‑Salem as “home base” — his parents, Leonard and Margaret (“Maggie”), remain there, and he drives over most Wednesday evenings to help his mom with grocery runs and small errands now that her arthritis has been flaring up.

Daniel works full‑time for the City of High Point Parks & Recreation Department, based at Deep River Recreation Center. His official title is Recreation Program Coordinator, but in practice he’s the go‑to person for youth sports leagues and many of the community special events the department puts on: spring soccer, fall flag football, youth basketball, the Deep River 5K & Family Fun Run, and seasonal festivals in the parks. His schedule is flexible but busy—he’s generally available mornings and afternoons Monday, Tuesday, Thursday, Friday, and often Saturday for practices, planning, coach meetings, and park clean‑up days, but he tries hard to protect his evenings for family time and coaching his son’s basketball team. Sundays he usually reserves for church with Rachel and the kids at a small congregation in High Point and for catching up on laundry, meal prep, and rest.

A graduate of R.J. Reynolds High School in Winston‑Salem (Class of 2002), Daniel went on to UNC Greensboro, where he completed a B.S. in Recreation and Parks Management with a concentration in Community Recreation in May 2006. That major fit him: he’d grown up playing rec league basketball and baseball at city parks, and some of his best memories are of long summer evenings at Hanes Park. He’s kept his professional skills up to date; in March 2023 he renewed his American Red Cross Adult & Pediatric First Aid/CPR/AED certification in Greensboro, which runs through March 2025. He’s proud of that card in his wallet—not because he wants to use it, but because, working around kids and older volunteers, he feels better knowing he can respond if something goes wrong.

On a typical weekday, Daniel is up around 6:15 a.m. He makes coffee, packs simple lunches—turkey sandwiches for Evan, peanut butter and jelly for Chloe, leftovers for himself—and checks the weather to decide whether to keep practices outside or move them into the gym. Rachel, who works as a medical billing specialist for a local orthopedic practice, leaves a little earlier than he does, so they tag‑team the kids’ school drop‑offs: he takes Evan to middle school and Chloe to elementary on his way toward Deep River. Once at work, his mornings are a mix of answering emails from parents about registration questions, coordinating with part‑time staff and volunteer coaches, and walking the fields to check for hazards—holes, broken glass, or muddy spots that could cause injuries. Because he has his North Carolina Class C driver’s license in good standing, he’s often the one who drives the city van to pick up cones, portable goals, and event supplies from the central warehouse.

Afternoons are when the energy ramps up. By 2:30 or 3:00 p.m., kids start trickling in for after‑school programs, and Daniel shifts from desk work to being on the floor: setting up drills, greeting parents by name, and mediating the occasional disagreement between 10‑year‑olds who both insist they were fouled. During the spring and fall, his Fridays are spent double‑checking game schedules, printing rosters, and walking the complex with maintenance staff to make sure fields are lined and trash cans emptied before Saturday’s games. He rarely stays late into the evening—he’s learned that if he doesn’t draw a line, weekend events will swallow all his time—so he aims to be home by 6:00 p.m. most nights, except on the occasional Thursday coaches’ meeting.

At home, life is a familiar mix of warmth and low‑level chaos. Evan has just started sixth grade and is navigating the jump to middle school; Daniel can see his son caught between wanting more independence and still needing structure. They’ve had more conversations lately about social media, screen time, and how to handle kids who talk tough at school. Chloe, in third grade, is the family extrovert—constantly asking to sign up for new activities. Right now she’s in a beginner soccer program that Daniel doesn’t run but quietly observes from the sidelines, resisting the urge to offer suggestions to the coaches. Evenings, when they’re not at practice, are spent around the kitchen table with homework spread out, Rachel answering patient portal messages on her laptop, and Daniel chopping vegetables for a simple stir‑fry or putting chicken in the oven.

The last couple of years have brought a few new pressures. Daniel’s mom, Maggie, had a minor fall in late 2023 that scared the family, even though she recovered without surgery. Since then, Daniel’s been more deliberate about checking in, sometimes stopping by her Winston‑Salem apartment on Friday afternoons before Saturday game days. His dad, Leonard, still insists on mowing his own lawn, but Daniel has started gently pushing the idea of hiring a neighborhood kid to help. At the same time, costs have crept up—gas, groceries, and particularly youth sports gear. Daniel and Rachel have had a few late‑night talks about budgeting: how to pay down their mortgage a bit faster, whether to replace their aging sedan in the next year or two, and how to start putting aside more for Evan’s eventual college expenses.

Despite the worries, Daniel genuinely loves what he does. He gets a quiet satisfaction from seeing kids who were shy at the start of the season running out onto the field with confidence by the last game, and from watching families linger after events at the park, talking and laughing as the sun goes down. He’s currently planning the fall festival and a charity 5K at Deep River, coordinating with local vendors and a couple of high school clubs that want to earn service hours by helping with the course and water stations. He’s also been talking with a colleague about starting a low‑cost Saturday morning multi‑sport clinic for kids whose families can’t afford travel teams.

Looking ahead, Daniel occasionally thinks about going back to school for a master’s in public administration or nonprofit management, but for now he’s focused on doing his current job well, being present for his family, and keeping his own health in decent shape by squeezing in early‑morning runs a few days a week. His main concerns are familiar ones: making sure his kids feel supported and safe, keeping his parents cared for as they age, and maintaining the kind of neighborhood and community spaces that meant so much to him growing up. In quiet moments—often driving home along NC‑68 as the late afternoon light hits the trees—he feels grateful that his work, his family life, and his roots in Winston‑Salem and High Point all tie together in a way that still feels honest to who he is.

secrets.secret_content: In February 2024, Daniel quietly started seeing a physical therapist, Dr. Karen Ellis at Cone Health Outpatient Rehabilitation in High Point, for chronic knee pain that flares up after long Saturdays running youth games at Deep River Recreation Center, and he worries it may eventually limit his ability to demonstrate drills for the kids.
secrets.why_sensitive: Sharing this could invite questions about his physical fitness for a very active job, potentially affecting how supervisors assign him field-based duties or consider him for promotions. It could also lead to unwanted sympathy or speculation from parents and colleagues about his long-term health and job security.
secrets.how_disclosed: When emailing a colleague about fall 5K volunteer coverage, he might write, “I can help coordinate youth volunteers at Deep River, but I probably shouldn’t run the course myself this year because of the knee stuff Dr. Ellis at Cone Health is still working on with me.”

secrets.secret_content: Since Maggie’s fall in November 2023, Daniel has been the primary one managing her increasing needs, including quietly meeting with a social worker at Novant Health Forsyth Medical Center in Winston-Salem in March 2024 to discuss the possibility of in-home assistance or eventually moving her to assisted living, which his father Leonard strongly opposes.
secrets.why_sensitive: This reveals sensitive family conflict about his parents’ independence and finances, and might embarrass his mother or anger his father if they learned he was exploring assisted living options behind the scenes. It could also affect how neighbors or church members perceive the family’s situation.
secrets.how_disclosed: When explaining to a trusted coworker why he avoids Wednesday volunteer shifts, he might say in a text, “I keep Wednesdays clear because that’s when I drive over to Winston to help Mom and meet with the social worker at Forsyth about possible in-home care—Dad doesn’t even want most folks knowing we’re talking about assisted living yet.”

secrets.secret_content: In August 2023, Daniel and Rachel fell two months behind on their mortgage with Piedmont Federal Savings Bank after unexpected medical bills from Maggie’s fall, and Daniel briefly considered picking up paid weekend event work at a private sports complex in Greensboro, even though his city employment contract discourages outside recreation work that could compete with Parks & Rec programs.
secrets.why_sensitive: This combines financial strain with a potential conflict-of-interest issue that could put his reputation and job at risk if his employer thought he was moonlighting for a competing facility. It also exposes the family’s precarious finances, which they likely don’t want widely known among coworkers or other parents.
secrets.how_disclosed: When declining a Saturday volunteer request, he might tell a close friend on the phone, “I’d love to help with the 5K, but I may have to take a paid shift over at Triad Elite Sports Complex in Greensboro again—after the mortgage scare last August we can’t afford to slip, even though I’m not supposed to be working for a private league on weekends.”

secrets.secret_content: In May 2024, Daniel received a verbal warning from his supervisor, Melissa Grant, at Deep River Recreation Center after a parent emailed the Parks & Recreation director complaining that Daniel refused to bend the rules to let their son play in an older age bracket, and he now worries that another complaint during a high-profile event like the Deep River 5K could jeopardize his chances for a future promotion.
secrets.why_sensitive: A disciplinary warning is a private HR matter; if widely known, it could damage his professional reputation among colleagues, volunteers, and parents, and might be misinterpreted as him being difficult or unfair with families.
secrets.how_disclosed: When recruiting a trusted volunteer coach by email, he might write, “I need you to help me stick to the age-bracket rules this fall—after that complaint Melissa talked to me about in May, I can’t afford another issue during the 5K or festival.”

secrets.secret_content: Since late 2022, Daniel has occasionally attended a small men’s support group on Monday evenings in a classroom at New Hope Community Church in High Point to talk about stress, burnout, and feeling overwhelmed balancing work, parenting, and caring for his aging parents, something he hasn’t told most of his coworkers or even his dad.
secrets.why_sensitive: Participation in a support group reveals emotional and mental health vulnerabilities that could be stigmatized or misunderstood, especially in a work environment where he’s expected to be upbeat and energetic. It might change how colleagues, supervisors, or parents view his reliability or resilience.
secrets.how_disclosed: When turning down an evening volunteer role, he might quietly tell a close colleague after a coaches’ meeting, “I’m actually at that men’s group at New Hope on Mondays—it’s the only space I have to talk about the stress of juggling Deep River, the kids, and my mom’s situation, so I really need to keep my evenings clear.”

secrets.secret_content: In January 2024, Daniel’s sister, Carla Denise Holloway, confided that her husband, Anthony Carter, had run up a significant credit card balance gambling on sports betting apps, and Daniel quietly helped them draft a repayment plan at Carla’s kitchen table in Winston-Salem one Wednesday night, asking her not to tell their parents or Rachel the full extent of the debt.
secrets.why_sensitive: This exposes another family member’s financial and behavioral problems (gambling), which could damage Anthony’s reputation, strain family relationships, and create tension in Daniel’s marriage if Rachel felt key financial stressors were being hidden from her.
secrets.how_disclosed: When explaining to Rachel why he needs to keep driving to Winston on Wednesdays, he might say, “I’m helping Carla and Anthony sort out some money stuff—after the gambling debt he racked up last January, I promised I’d be there on Wednesdays to go over their repayment plan, so I can’t add volunteer shifts those afternoons.”

secrets.secret_content: In October 2023, Daniel’s son Evan had a panic episode during a crowded middle school basketball game at the Deep River gym, leading Daniel and Rachel to schedule an evaluation with child psychologist Dr. Lauren Patel at Brenner Children’s Hospital in Winston-Salem, where they’re now exploring whether Evan has an anxiety disorder triggered by performance pressure and social media bullying.
secrets.why_sensitive: This involves a minor’s mental health and potential diagnosis, which is highly sensitive and could lead to stigma or teasing if classmates, other parents, or volunteer coaches found out. It might also influence how others treat Evan or question Daniel’s parenting choices.
secrets.how_disclosed: When asking a trusted assistant coach to cover a Saturday clinic, Daniel might text, “Can you take the lead at Deep River this weekend? Evan’s got a follow-up with Dr. Patel at Brenner about the panic episode he had at the game in October, and I need to be there with him.”

secrets.secret_content: In March 2024, during planning for the Deep River 5K, Daniel informally agreed with local vendor “Triad Snack Shack” owner Miguel Alvarez to reserve them a prime spot near the finish line in exchange for a small under-the-table discount on snack packs for Evan’s basketball team, a deal he now regrets because it bypassed the city’s standard vendor selection process.
secrets.why_sensitive: This could be seen as an ethics or procurement violation, potentially putting his job at risk and damaging trust with both the city and other vendors if it came to light as favoritism or misuse of his position.
secrets.how_disclosed: When venting to a close coworker after hours, he might say, “I’ve got to be more careful with vendor stuff at the 5K—after that off-the-books discount Miguel at Triad Snack Shack gave me in March, I’m worried it could look bad if anyone digs into how I picked spots at Deep River.”

artifacts.content:
From: daniel.holloway84@gmail.com
To: rachel.holloway@outlook.com
Subject: Monday/Wednesday juggling act
Date: April 8, 2024

Hey Rach,

Typing this from the Deep River office before the after‑school crew rolls in. I wanted to get on the same page about the next few weeks, especially Mondays and Wednesdays, before our calendars get away from us again.

Mondays: I really want to keep that men’s group at New Hope Community Church on my schedule. You know the one I’ve been going to off and on since late 2022, the support group for guys talking about stress and burnout. The last couple of months with Mom’s arthritis flaring, Evan’s stuff, and work ramping up for the fall 5K, I’ve actually needed that space more. It’s the only time I’m not “Coach Daniel” or “Dad” or the one sorting out everyone’s problems. So as much as I hate being out another evening, I’d like us to treat that 7–8:30 p.m. block like an appointment I can’t skip.

Wednesdays: I’m still planning to drive over to Winston after work. Mom’s grocery runs are part of it, but I’ve also got that ongoing thing with the social worker at Novant Forsyth. Ever since her fall back in November 2023, they’ve been talking with me about in‑home help and even the possibility of assisted living down the line. Dad’s still digging in his heels, and he doesn’t want folks knowing we’re even discussing it, so I feel like I’ve got to keep showing up in person.

On top of that, Carla’s leaning on me more. After Anthony told her about the sports betting mess in January — that credit card bill he ran up on those apps — I promised I’d help them stick to the repayment plan we sketched out at their kitchen table. It’s not something I want Mom and Dad or even the kids hearing the full story about yet.

Can we look at the kids’ practice schedules tonight and make sure I’m not leaving you stranded on homework duty those nights? I can shift some afternoon stuff at Deep River, but evenings are pretty much off the table for me right now.

Love you,
D

metadata.subject: Monday/Wednesday juggling act

artifacts.content:
From: daniel.holloway84@gmail.com
To: melissa.grant@highpointnc.gov
Subject: Deep River 5K volunteer coverage + course duties
Date: May 21, 2024

Hey Melissa,

I’ve been looking over the draft plan for the Deep River 5K & Family Fun Run and wanted to flag a couple of things while there’s still time to adjust.

First, on race day roles: I’m happy to take the lead on coordinating youth and community volunteers at the pavilion and along the family loop, but I probably shouldn’t run the course myself this year. The chronic knee pain that flares up after long Saturdays has gotten worse, and I finally started seeing a physical therapist — Dr. Karen Ellis at Cone Health Outpatient Rehab here in High Point — back in February 2024. She’s got me doing strengthening work and specifically told me not to push distance running for a while. I’ll still be mobile for setup, just not racing.

Second, about vendors and parent interactions. I really want this 5K to be clean and by‑the‑book. I know you talked with me in May about that parent complaint over age brackets — the one that made it up to the director because I wouldn’t bump their son into an older division. I heard you when you said I need to stay consistent but also document things better so there’s no question about fairness. I do not want another issue like that hanging over me, especially tied to a high‑profile event.

Connected to that, I’ve been thinking a lot about how we assign vendor spots. Back in March I made an informal side arrangement with Miguel from Triad Snack Shack to give him a prime finish‑line spot in exchange for a small discount on snack packs for Evan’s basketball team. At the time it felt harmless, but the more I sit with it, the more it bothers me that it didn’t go through the usual city process. For this 5K, I’d prefer we do everything through the standard rotation and written agreements so there’s no perception of favoritism.

If you’re okay with it, I’d like my official role to be: volunteer coordination, kids’ warm‑up area, and post‑race family activities, with someone else listed as course marshal/race lead.

Happy to talk this through when you have a minute.

Thanks,
Daniel

metadata.subject: Deep River 5K volunteer coverage + course duties

artifacts.content:
Thread: Daniel & "Coach Mike" (assistant coach)
Date: February 2, 2024

Mike: You good to run the Saturday clinic this week? I can take check‑in if you want to focus on drills.

Daniel: Actually, could we flip that? I need you to take the lead on the on‑court stuff this Saturday.

Mike: Yeah man, no problem. Everything okay?

Daniel: Mostly. Evan’s got a follow‑up with that child psychologist in Winston — Dr. Lauren Patel at Brenner Children’s Hospital — Saturday morning. It’s about that panic episode he had back in October during the middle school game in the Deep River gym. The one where he froze and couldn’t breathe when the stands filled up.

Daniel: We’ve been doing evaluations with her to see if it’s an anxiety thing tied to performance and some junk on social media. I really want to be there with him and Rachel.

Mike: Oh wow, I didn’t realize it was that serious. Definitely go. I’ll handle the kids.

Daniel: Thanks. Also, my knee’s been barking after long days out here. I finally caved and started PT with a Cone Health therapist in February, so me not sprinting around the court for three straight hours is probably a good idea anyway.

Mike: Getting old, Coach 😂

Daniel: For real. Between that, Mom’s appointments in Winston, and trying to keep up with all the Deep River stuff, I’m feeling it.

Mike: Anything you need me to emphasize with the clinic kids?

Daniel: Just keep it fun, stick to the equal‑playing‑time rule, and if any parent starts pushing about bumping their kid up an age group, shut that down. After that complaint last spring where the dad went over my head because I wouldn’t move his son up, Melissa gave me a pretty clear warning about staying tight on the rules.

Mike: Got it. I’ll be the bad guy if needed.

Daniel: Appreciate you, man.

metadata.title: Text thread with Mike about Saturday clinic

artifacts.content:
Title: Stuff I don’t want to drop (mid‑2024)
Date: June 10, 2024
Location: Kitchen table, after kids’ bedtime

Just a brain dump so I don’t keep spinning this in my head.

1. Mom and Dad
- Mom’s arthritis + that fall in November 2023 shook all of us more than we admit. I’m still driving to Winston most Wednesdays.
- Keep following up with the social worker at Novant Health Forsyth in Winston‑Salem. We talked in March 2024 about in‑home assistance and maybe assisted living if things get worse. Dad hates the idea and doesn’t want anyone at church knowing we’re even considering it.
- Need to find a way to talk to him about safety without making him feel like we’re taking his independence.

2. Carla & Anthony
- January 2024: Carla told me about Anthony’s sports betting mess — big credit card balance from those apps.
- We sat at their kitchen table and built a repayment plan. I promised her I’d keep checking in on Wednesdays. Asked her not to tell Mom/Dad or even Rachel all the details yet so it doesn’t blow up into a bigger family fight.

3. Money here at home
- Remember August 2023 when we were two months behind on the Piedmont Federal mortgage after Mom’s medical bills? I still feel that in my chest.
- I even seriously thought about taking paid weekend shifts at Triad Elite Sports Complex in Greensboro. It would’ve helped, but it’s too close to what I do for the city, and the contract really frowns on outside rec work that competes with Parks & Rec.
- Goal: build up a real emergency fund so I never feel that close to the edge again.

4. Me
- Keep going to the men’s group at New Hope on Monday nights. It’s the only place I say out loud how overwhelmed I feel juggling Deep River, the kids, and aging parents.
- Don’t let guilt push me into giving up that time.

Breathe. One thing at a time.

metadata.title: Stuff I don’t want to drop (mid‑2024)

Subject: Quick check‑in (and Wednesday plan)

Hey C,

Just wanted to check in after last week and make sure you’re holding up okay.

I’ve been thinking a lot about that night at your place earlier this month — sitting at your kitchen table in Winston in January, going through Anthony’s credit card statements and seeing how much of that balance was from those sports betting apps. I know that was a gut punch. I’m glad he owned up to it, but I also know you’re the one staring at the numbers at 2 a.m.

I meant what I said: I’m with you both on this. The repayment plan we sketched out is realistic if you stick to it, and I’m okay driving over on Wednesdays for a while to help you go over things line by line. We don’t have to tell Mom and Dad the whole story yet; they’d focus on the gambling part and not the fact that you two are actually trying to fix it.

Just remember we’re all juggling stuff. Between Mom’s arthritis and that fall she had back in November 2023, I’m already meeting with a social worker at Novant Forsyth about possible in‑home help or even assisted living if it comes to that. Dad would absolutely lose it if he knew how far those conversations have gone, so for now it’s just me, the social worker, and you in the loop.

Add in the money scare Rachel and I had last August when we fell two months behind on the Piedmont Federal mortgage after Mom’s medical bills from the fall, and I get the panic you’re feeling. I even thought about picking up paid weekend shifts at that Triad Elite Sports Complex in Greensboro, even though it brushes right up against my city job’s rules. That’s how close it felt.

Point is: you’re not the only one making hard choices. Text me what time works best this Wednesday and I’ll plan my Deep River schedule around it.

Love you,
D

form_info.title: Volunteer History Have you previously served as a volunteer?  Where and when?  Please list below.

volunteer_history.where_and_when: I began volunteering regularly in college around 2010 at various campus and community organizations in Portland, Oregon, including food drives and neighborhood clean-ups. Since moving to Seattle in 2016, I have consistently volunteered with education- and housing-focused nonprofits, most notably with Rainier Valley Community Learning Center (2017–2020) and Evergreen Family Shelter (2020–2024). I typically commit to at least one ongoing weekly volunteer role per year, plus several one-off events such as fundraising runs, book drives, and community fairs.
volunteer_history.current_or_most_recent_your_title: Evening Homework Club Tutor & Program Assistant (Volunteer)
volunteer_history.current_or_most_recent_duties_line_1: Provided twice-weekly after-school homework support and academic mentoring to 8–12 elementary and middle school students living in transitional housing, focusing on reading comprehension, basic math skills, and building consistent study habits.
volunteer_history.current_or_most_recent_duties_line_2: Assisted the program coordinator with setting up and closing the homework room, tracking student attendance and progress in a shared spreadsheet, communicating with parents and shelter staff about student needs, coordinating small volunteer teams (3–4 volunteers per shift), and developing simple enrichment activities (STEM games, journaling prompts, and read-aloud sessions) tailored to children experiencing housing instability or trauma.
volunteer_history.current_or_most_recent_reason_for_leavin: Relocated to a different part of the city and started a new full-time job with longer evening hours, making it difficult to reliably attend the weekday homework sessions.

I discussed my changing schedule with the volunteer coordinator in early 2024, and we agreed that it would be better for the students to have a tutor who could commit to being there every week. I stayed on through the end of the school year (June 2024) to help transition my responsibilities, document the routines I had established for the reading groups, introduce the new volunteers to the students, and ensure that the children had continuity and did not feel abruptly abandoned. I remain on their mailing list and have offered to come back for occasional weekend events when my schedule allows.
volunteer_history.volunteer_history_duties_2_line_1: Supported an evening English-as-a-Second-Language (ESL) class for adult learners by circulating during small-group and pair activities, helping students practice conversation, pronunciation, and basic writing tasks such as filling out forms, drafting simple work emails, and preparing for job interviews.
volunteer_history.volunteer_history_duties_2_line_2: Assisted the lead instructor with classroom logistics, including preparing handouts, setting up audio equipment, taking attendance, and recording student participation notes; provided individualized support to learners with very limited literacy; occasionally led warm-up exercises and conversation circles focused on real-life topics like visiting the doctor, talking to a child’s teacher, or navigating public transportation; and offered informal tech help using translation apps and email on students’ phones when appropriate.
volunteer_history.volunteer_history_reason_for_leaving_2_l: In-person classes were suspended in March 2020 due to the COVID-19 pandemic, and I was unable to continue regularly once the program shifted to a fully virtual model.

When the center moved classes online, many of the students I worked with did not have stable internet access or were sharing devices with multiple family members. My own work responsibilities also increased significantly when my employer transitioned to remote operations, and I was logging unpredictable hours. I helped the instructor for a few weeks with phone check-ins and basic Zoom orientations, but I could not commit to the full virtual schedule. After talking with the staff, we decided to formally end my weekly volunteer role while leaving the door open for future in-person opportunities, which eventually led me to shift my focus to the homework club at Evergreen Family Shelter once in-person youth programs resumed.
volunteer_history.volunteer_history_duties_3_line_1: Welcomed clients to the pantry, conducted brief intake screenings to verify eligibility based on income and household size, and helped families select appropriate food items while maintaining dignity, privacy, and respect for cultural and dietary needs.
volunteer_history.volunteer_history_duties_3_line_2: Restocked shelves, sorted and checked donations for quality and expiration dates, assembled emergency food boxes for delivery to homebound seniors, assisted with weekly inventory counts, and helped train newer volunteers on pantry procedures, food safety basics, and how to de-escalate stressful situations when clients were anxious or frustrated due to long wait times or limited supplies.
volunteer_history.volunteer_history_reason_for_leaving_3_l: Moved from Portland, Oregon to Seattle, Washington for a new full-time job opportunity and could no longer attend weekly shifts at the pantry.

During my final month, I coordinated with the volunteer manager to gradually hand off my regular Thursday evening shift and update some of the intake instructions I had been helping to refine, so that newer volunteers would have clear written guidance. I left in good standing and have stayed in occasional contact with a former fellow volunteer through social media; I also still donate annually to support the pantry’s holiday food drive even though I no longer live in the area.

work_history.work_history_duties_1_line_1: Analyzed customer behavior and operational data for mid-sized nonprofit and education-sector clients, building dashboards and recurring reports in Tableau and Power BI to help them track program outcomes, donor engagement, and internal performance metrics; regularly cleaned and merged data from multiple systems (CRM, email marketing, and event registration platforms) using SQL and Python.
work_history.work_history_duties_1_line_2: Collaborated with cross-functional teams (account managers, software engineers, and client success staff) to translate non-technical client questions into clear analytical projects, presented findings to both technical and non-technical stakeholders, documented methodologies and data dictionaries, mentored two junior analysts on best practices for data quality and visualization design, and contributed to internal process improvements such as standardizing report templates for nonprofit clients.
work_history.work_history_reason_for_leaving_1_line_1: Accepted a new position with a smaller mission-driven analytics firm that focuses exclusively on supporting education and housing nonprofits, aligning more closely with my long-term career and volunteer interests.
work_history.work_history_reason_for_leaving_1_line_2: After five years at North Sound Data Solutions, I felt that I had grown significantly in my technical skills but wanted to move into a role where the majority of my client work directly supported organizations addressing educational equity and housing stability—the same areas where I have been volunteering. I discussed my goals with my manager during my annual review in early 2024, and when an opportunity arose at a specialized social-impact analytics firm, I applied and was offered the role. I provided over a month of notice, documented my active projects in detail, trained my replacement on key client accounts, and stayed on call for questions during the first two weeks after my departure to ensure a smooth transition for the clients I had worked with for several years.
work_history.work_history_duties_2_line_1: Supported senior researchers on program evaluation projects for local governments and nonprofits by conducting literature reviews, cleaning and coding survey data in SPSS and Excel, helping design questionnaires and interview protocols, and assisting with the preparation of draft reports and slide decks for client presentations.
work_history.work_history_duties_2_line_2: Coordinated logistics for focus groups and stakeholder interviews (scheduling, reminder calls, room setup, and note-taking), contributed to basic descriptive analyses and visualizations, maintained organized project files and version control for research instruments, and communicated regularly with clients to clarify data questions and follow up on missing information while adhering to confidentiality and data protection standards.
work_history.work_history_reason_for_leaving_2_line_1: Relocated from Portland to Seattle and sought a more advanced, analytics-focused role after completing several multi-year evaluation projects.
work_history.work_history_reason_for_leaving_2_line_2: By mid-2019, I had reached a natural transition point on two major projects that were wrapping up, and I had also decided to move to Seattle to be closer to family and pursue broader career opportunities. My supervisors were aware of my relocation plans several months in advance, and we coordinated my departure so that final reports and data handoffs were completed before my last day. They provided strong references that helped me secure my subsequent position at North Sound Data Solutions, and we remain in professional contact for occasional consulting questions and networking.

persona.education: B.A. in Sociology, Portland State University (2011); Graduate Certificate in Data Analytics, University of Washington Professional & Continuing Education (2021)
family_members: Daniel McAllister – younger brother, 33, high school social studies teacher in Tacoma, WA
Janet McAllister – mother, 64, retired elementary school teacher, lives in Olympia, WA
Robert “Bob” McAllister – father, 67, semi-retired electrician, lives in Olympia, WA
Maya Chen – long-term partner, 35, school counselor at Rainier Vista Middle School in Seattle, WA
Oliver Chen-McAllister – nephew (through Maya’s sister), 6, lives in Bellevue, WA
Emily Chen – Maya’s older sister and close friend, 38, software project manager in Bellevue, WA
persona.background_context:
This is Laura, a 36-year-old senior data analyst who has slowly but deliberately steered her career toward the same causes she cares about in her volunteer life. She lives in a modest but light-filled two-bedroom apartment on Maple Terrace in Seattle’s Rainier Valley, where the sound of light rail trains passing in the distance has become part of the backdrop of her evenings. In October 2024 she started a new role at HarborBridge Impact Analytics, a small mission-driven firm that works almost exclusively with education and housing nonprofits. The move came after five years at North Sound Data Solutions, where she honed her technical skills and mentored junior analysts but increasingly felt pulled toward work that more directly aligned with her long-standing volunteer commitments in education and housing stability.

Her days are structured but full. On a typical weekday, she’s up by 6:30 a.m., often before her partner, Maya, who works as a middle school counselor. Laura brews coffee, checks the news on her phone, and squeezes in a short run along nearby residential streets or a quick yoga session in their living room before logging on around 8:30. Her work ranges from cleaning messy program data from youth tutoring initiatives to building dashboards in Tableau that help shelter directors understand which families are staying the longest and why. Because HarborBridge is small, she often jumps on Zoom calls with executive directors and program managers who aren’t especially data-savvy, translating complex metrics into plain language and visual stories they can use with boards and funders. She’s still within her first year there, so she’s balancing the excitement of designing new reporting frameworks with the stress of proving herself to a tight-knit team and learning each client’s quirks.

Evenings have recently shifted for her. From 2020 until the end of the 2023–2024 school year, twice a week she headed straight from work to Evergreen Family Shelter, where she served as an Evening Homework Club Tutor & Program Assistant. There she helped 8–12 elementary and middle school students living in transitional housing with reading, math, and study habits, and quietly coordinated small teams of fellow volunteers, tracked progress, and designed simple enrichment activities that would be grounding for kids dealing with housing instability and trauma. When she accepted the new job at HarborBridge and moved from a shared house in Columbia City to Maple Terrace in early summer 2024, her commute and longer evening hours made it hard to be consistently present. After talking it through with the volunteer coordinator, she stayed until June 30, 2024, seeing the school year through, documenting her routines for reading groups, and helping onboard new volunteers so the kids wouldn’t feel abruptly abandoned. She still misses walking into the homework room and having a few of the kids run up to show her their spelling tests, but she remains on the shelter’s mailing list and occasionally helps at weekend events.

Her path to Evergreen grew out of earlier experiences. Shortly after moving to Seattle in 2016, she began volunteering at Rainier Valley Community Learning Center as an Adult ESL Classroom Assistant, supporting evening English classes from January 2017 until in-person operations shut down in March 2020. She remembers specific students—like a father who practiced introducing himself for parent-teacher conferences—and how she would walk between tables helping with pronunciation, job interview role-plays, and basic writing tasks, then stay late to help the instructor tidy the room and update participation notes. When the pandemic forced everything online, she helped for a few weeks with phone check-ins and teaching Zoom basics, but many of the learners lacked stable internet or devices, and her own job at North Sound suddenly demanded irregular hours as clients scrambled to adjust. After some honest conversations with the staff, they agreed to formally end her weekly role while leaving the door open for future in-person opportunities. That transition, and the sense of unfinished work, helped crystallize her desire to focus even more heavily on youth and housing once programs reopened, which is what ultimately drew her to Evergreen.

Her commitment to community work started earlier in Portland. From 2013 to mid-2016, she spent Thursday evenings at Portland Community Food Share, doing intake and helping to distribute food. She got used to the rhythm of greeting families, verifying eligibility based on income and household size, and quietly watching for signs of embarrassment or stress so she could offer help in a way that preserved dignity. She learned how to restock shelves efficiently, check donation quality, and assemble emergency food boxes for homebound seniors. In her last year there, she started informally training new volunteers, walking them through food safety practices and how to de-escalate when tensions ran high due to long waits or limited supplies. When she moved to Seattle in summer 2016 for a job opportunity, she worked with the volunteer manager during her final month to hand off her regular shift and refine written intake instructions. She still donates each holiday season, a small ritual that keeps her connected to that chapter of her life.

Professionally, Laura’s trajectory has been steady and intentional. After graduating from Portland State University in 2011 with a degree in sociology, she spent several years as a Research Assistant at Bridgeview Research & Consulting in downtown Portland. From September 2015 to July 2019, she supported program evaluations for local governments and nonprofits, cleaning survey data in SPSS, coding open-ended responses, and helping design questionnaires and interview protocols. She coordinated logistics for focus groups and stakeholder interviews, took meticulous notes, and learned how to communicate data questions diplomatically with clients while respecting confidentiality. By 2019, with a few multi-year projects wrapping up and her desire to be closer to her parents in Washington growing, she decided to relocate to Seattle. Bridgeview supervisors knew months in advance and helped her transition out cleanly, even providing references that later helped her land the Data Analyst role at North Sound.

At North Sound Data Solutions, where she worked from August 12, 2019 to September 30, 2024, Laura shifted from evaluation support into full-fledged analytics. She became proficient in SQL and Python for data cleaning and integration, and she built dashboards in Tableau and Power BI for mid-sized nonprofit and education-sector clients. Over time, she became the go-to person for nonprofit accounts, often translating vague client questions into concrete analytical projects. She mentored two junior analysts, teaching them about data quality checks and visualization design, and collaborated with software engineers and account managers on product improvements. By early 2024, she felt a strong pull to specialize in social-impact work, particularly in education and housing—the same areas where she volunteered. After discussing her goals during her annual review, she began quietly exploring options and eventually accepted the opportunity at HarborBridge, providing over a month’s notice so she could document projects, train a replacement, and stay available for questions during the handoff.

Outside of work, Laura’s life is anchored by close family ties and a stable partnership. Her parents, Janet and Bob, live in Olympia; she drives down once a month, often on a Sunday, to have dinner and help her dad with small home repairs or tech issues. Her mom, a retired elementary school teacher, is both proud and amused that Laura’s work and volunteering have led her back into classrooms and homework help in a different way. Her younger brother Daniel, a high school social studies teacher in Tacoma, shares her interest in equity and education, and they trade stories about the challenges their students face—from food insecurity to housing instability. Laura’s partner, Maya, understands these pressures well through her own role as a school counselor. They met in 2018 at a mutual friend’s board game night and have been living together for the past three years, sharing a mix of routines: Sunday grocery runs, midweek vegetarian stir-fries, and occasional spontaneous late-night walks when either of them needs to decompress.

Lately, Laura has been adjusting to the subtle but real changes that came with moving to Maple Terrace and starting the new job. The new role is satisfying but demanding, and she’s still figuring out how to protect her evenings so she doesn’t slide into old habits of logging back on after dinner. She misses the structured, twice-weekly homework club shifts that used to separate her workday from her community life. To fill that gap without overcommitting, she’s been exploring shorter-term or more flexible volunteer opportunities—like weekend book drives or data-cleaning projects for small nonprofits that can be done remotely. At the same time, she and Maya have been talking cautiously about longer-term plans: whether they can afford to stay in the neighborhood if rents rise again when their lease renews next summer, and what starting a family might look like given their workloads and the cost of childcare in Seattle.

Her daily concerns are ordinary but meaningful. She worries about her parents’ aging—her father’s back has been troubling him more, and she’s quietly started researching electricians’ retirement options and resources for caregivers. She keeps an eye on her own health, managing occasional stress-related insomnia by sticking to an evening routine of reading physical books instead of scrolling on her phone. She’s midway through a stack of library holds about housing policy and trauma-informed education, partly out of professional interest and partly because she wants to understand more deeply what the kids at Evergreen and the families in her clients’ datasets are actually experiencing. On Fridays, she and Maya often meet friends at a neighborhood café or a food truck pod, decompressing from the week; on Saturdays, she might spend a few hours tinkering with a personal data visualization project or helping her nephew Oliver troubleshoot a math game over video chat.

Overall, Laura’s life is in a phase of intentional consolidation. She has aligned her job with her values, built a home and partnership she feels grounded in, and maintained ties to the volunteer communities that shaped her. At the same time, she’s navigating the practical constraints of time, energy, and money that come with living in an expensive city while trying to do mission-driven work. Her story at the moment is one of careful balancing: between ambition and sustainability, between showing up for others and not burning herself out, and between honoring the kids and adults she’s worked with in shelters, ESL classrooms, and food pantries, and making space for her own future to take shape.

secrets.secret_content: Since February 2023, Laura has been seeing therapist Dr. Karen Liu at Harborview Behavioral Health Clinic in Seattle for stress-related insomnia and early signs of burnout, with notes in her MyChart account titled “Session re: work/volunteer boundary setting” and “Processing secondary trauma from shelter homework club.”
secrets.why_sensitive: Disclosure of ongoing mental health treatment and burnout could expose Laura to stigma, affect how supervisors perceive her reliability or resilience, and influence future hiring or promotion decisions in a small mission-driven sector where reputations travel quickly.
secrets.how_disclosed: While emailing the Evergreen Family Shelter volunteer coordinator to explain stepping back—subject line “Stepping down after June 30 shift”—she might write that her therapist, Dr. Liu at Harborview, has encouraged her to reduce evening commitments because of stress-related insomnia and burnout from juggling work and the homework club.

secrets.secret_content: In November 2024, Laura’s father, Bob McAllister, had a fall at their Olympia home and was evaluated at Providence St. Peter Hospital for worsening chronic back issues; Laura has quietly taken over organizing his physical therapy schedule and researching caregiver resources, keeping a shared “Dad’s PT & meds” Google Sheet with her mother, Janet.
secrets.why_sensitive: Details about her father’s health and emerging caregiving role are private family matters; sharing them broadly could feel like a violation of her parents’ privacy and could also signal to employers or colleagues that she may have upcoming caregiving constraints affecting her availability.
secrets.how_disclosed: When explaining to her former manager at North Sound why she wanted a role with more predictable hours, she might mention in a follow-up email that after her dad’s November 2024 fall and back evaluation at Providence St. Peter, she needs flexibility to drive to Olympia for his PT appointments and coordinate care with her mom.

secrets.secret_content: Laura and her partner, Maya Chen, have been in couples counseling with licensed marriage and family therapist Rachel Ortiz at Capitol Hill Therapy Collective since August 2024, meeting every other Wednesday evening to talk through long-term plans around starting a family and disagreements about work–life balance.
secrets.why_sensitive: Couples counseling and relationship tensions are highly personal; disclosure could invite gossip among friends or colleagues, affect how family members view their relationship stability, and potentially impact future decisions about parenting or financial planning if others judge or interfere.
secrets.how_disclosed: When declining a request from Evergreen to return for a regular weeknight shift, she might write in a personal note to the volunteer coordinator that Wednesday evenings are now reserved for ongoing couples sessions with therapist Rachel Ortiz on Capitol Hill so she and Maya can work through family-planning decisions and boundaries around evening work.

secrets.secret_content: In July 2024, after moving to the Maple Terrace apartment and seeing their rent increase notice for the July 2025 renewal, Laura had a 45‑minute meeting with financial planner Eric Dawson at BECU’s Rainier Valley branch to discuss whether she and Maya could afford future childcare costs and possibly buying a condo without taking on unsustainable debt.
secrets.why_sensitive: Her detailed financial planning, worries about housing affordability, and potential debt exposure are private; if widely known, it could affect how family perceives her stability, influence landlords’ or lenders’ attitudes, or create awkwardness at work if colleagues learn she feels financially stretched in a mission-driven role.
secrets.how_disclosed: In a late‑night text thread with her brother Daniel about maybe taking on extra consulting gigs, she might mention that she met with BECU advisor Eric Dawson in July 2024 and learned that unless they cut back expenses or increase income, a condo and daycare in Seattle would likely push them beyond a safe debt-to-income ratio.

secrets.secret_content: During her final year at North Sound Data Solutions (January–September 2024), Laura quietly documented several instances where senior account manager “E.” pressured her on Zoom to alter nonprofit client retention metrics in a Tableau dashboard before a board presentation, leading her to keep a private folder on her home laptop titled “NSDS – data integrity concerns” with saved emails and redlined reports.
secrets.why_sensitive: Allegations of pressured data manipulation could create serious professional and legal repercussions for both Laura and her former employer, including reputational damage, strained industry relationships, or even litigation if mishandling is alleged; being known as a whistleblower can also affect her future job prospects in a tight-knit nonprofit analytics community.
secrets.how_disclosed: When venting to a trusted former colleague over Signal about why she really left North Sound, she might reference a March 2024 email from account manager E. (“Can we smooth out the retention dip before the board sees this?”) and mention that she kept a “NSDS – data integrity concerns” folder in case things escalated.

secrets.secret_content: In October 2022, Laura had a consultation at UW Medicine’s Roosevelt Women’s Health Clinic about potential future fertility preservation, including pricing out one cycle of egg freezing, after a tense conversation with Maya where they realized they might not be ready to start trying for a baby until their late thirties.
secrets.why_sensitive: Fertility planning and concerns about reproductive timelines are deeply personal and can expose her to judgment from family, colleagues, or community members; they could also intersect with discrimination or intrusive questions at work if supervisors assume upcoming parental leave or shifting priorities.
secrets.how_disclosed: In a long email to her close friend Emily Chen titled “Trying to figure out timing,” she might explain that part of why she’s hesitating to take on another weekly volunteer role is that after the October 2022 visit to UW Roosevelt about egg freezing costs, she and Maya are trying to keep bandwidth open in case they decide to start fertility treatments in the next year or two.

secrets.secret_content: Laura’s younger brother Daniel confided to her in May 2023, over coffee at Olympia Coffee Roasting’s Tacoma location, that he is considering leaving classroom teaching due to burnout and had a brief leave of absence that spring after a panic attack during a staff meeting about budget cuts and student homelessness data, which Laura had helped analyze in a side project.
secrets.why_sensitive: This exposes Daniel’s mental health struggles and professional instability without his consent; if shared beyond trusted circles, it could damage his reputation at his school, affect future hiring in education, and strain sibling trust if he feels Laura disclosed his crisis.
secrets.how_disclosed: When explaining to Maya why working with housing-unstable students can feel overwhelming, Laura might mention in a late-night conversation that the same kind of homelessness data she analyzed for a district project contributed to Daniel’s May 2023 panic attack and short leave, which they discussed at Olympia Coffee in Tacoma.

secrets.secret_content: In March 2021, Laura reported a neighbor in her former Columbia City shared house, Mark H., to their landlord via an email titled “Concerns about safety and repeated late-night outbursts,” after several incidents where he pounded on her bedroom wall and shouted slurs when he heard her and Maya talking late at night, which she interpreted as homophobic harassment.
secrets.why_sensitive: This involves potential harassment, safety concerns, and sexual orientation discrimination; disclosure could revive conflict with the former neighbor, expose her to retaliation or doxxing if identities became known, and make her feel unsafe if widely circulated in her current community or workplace.
secrets.how_disclosed: When chatting with a trusted coworker at HarborBridge about why she feels strongly about safe housing environments, she might recount the March 2021 email to her Columbia City landlord about neighbor Mark’s late-night slurs and wall-pounding, connecting it to why she’s cautious about where she and Maya live now.

artifacts.content:
Hi Daniel,

Finally catching up on life admin and wanted to actually write instead of just sending you memes.

First, how are you holding up this semester? I keep thinking about that morning at Olympia Coffee in Tacoma back in May 2023 when you told me about the panic attack during the staff meeting – the one where they were going over the homelessness data and budget cuts, and you had to take that brief leave. I’m still angry that the district piled all of that on you with no real support. It’s been on my mind again because a couple of my HarborBridge projects are using similar student housing instability data, and I keep flashing back to that conversation with you at the window table.

On my end, things are…busy, in a familiar way. HarborBridge is good overall – it actually feels like the work matters – but I’m trying not to repeat the North Sound pattern of saying yes to everything. My therapist, Dr. Karen Liu over at Harborview Behavioral Health, has been pretty blunt about that. I’ve been seeing her since February 2023 for the stress-y insomnia and the whole early-burnout cocktail. We literally have MyChart session notes titled “work/volunteer boundary setting” and “processing secondary trauma from shelter homework club,” which is both on-the-nose and weirdly validating.

Between the Evergreen homework club kids’ stories and the datasets I stare at all day, my brain sometimes doesn’t know the difference between my own life and other people’s crises. Dr. Liu keeps reminding me that I’m not actually responsible for fixing the entire housing system.

Speaking of housing, you’ll appreciate this: in July, after we moved to Maple Terrace and got that cheerful notice about how much rent might jump at renewal in July 2025, I booked a 45‑minute meeting with a BECU financial planner, Eric Dawson, at the Rainier Valley branch. We went line by line through “what if we buy a tiny condo and pay for daycare at the same time?” and he basically said, very politely, that it would push us right up against a sketchy debt‑to‑income ratio unless one of us earns more or we seriously trim expenses.

So that’s been fueling my late‑night spreadsheet habit.

Anyway, I mostly wanted to say: I’m proud of you for staying in the classroom as long as it still feels right, but if you decide to walk away, that doesn’t make you a failure. Text me your next free weekend and I’ll drive down to Olympia; we can drag Dad out for coffee and I’ll bring you the housing policy book I keep ranting about.

Love,
Laura

metadata.subject: Checking in (and money/brain stuff)

artifacts.content:
Hi Mom and Dad,

Just got home and wanted to write while everything is still fresh instead of trying to recap over a rushed phone call.

Dad, I’m really glad you finally let us take you to Providence St. Peter after the fall last week. Watching you hobble around the living room pretending it was “just a tweak” was not convincing. Hearing the doctor in Olympia actually say the words “worsening chronic back issues” at least gives us something concrete to work with.

On the drive back to Seattle, I was thinking about how much you both already juggle, so I set up a shared Google Sheet called “Dad’s PT & meds” and invited Mom’s Gmail address. It has tabs for the physical therapy schedule, medications (with doses), and questions for the next appointment. Mom, I know you have your notebook system, but this way I can help from here – rescheduling PT if needed, calling the office, etc.

I also want to be honest that this is part of why I pushed so hard to leave North Sound when I did. Officially, I told them it was about aligning with education and housing nonprofits (which is true), but in a follow‑up email to my old manager I explained that after Dad’s fall and the St. Peter evaluation in November, I need enough flexibility to drive down to Olympia for PT appointments without getting side‑eyed for leaving “early.” HarborBridge is smaller and more mission‑driven, and so far they’ve actually meant it when they say “family first.”

Please don’t read this as me resenting anything. I want to be part of this. I just need us to be organized so it doesn’t all land on Mom by default.

Can we do a quick Zoom this Sunday afternoon to walk through the spreadsheet and the next few weeks of PT? I’ll also bring down my drill next visit so, Dad, you can supervise while I pretend to know what I’m doing with the loose railing.

Love you both,
Laura

metadata.subject: Following up after Dad’s fall

artifacts.content:
Hi Maya,

You’re in the other room grading those advisory reflections, but if I say all of this out loud right now I’ll forget half of it, so: email.

I just got off the phone with Erin from Evergreen Family Shelter. She was lovely, as always, and asked (very gently) if I’d consider coming back for a regular weeknight homework club slot in the winter. I told her no, and I feel both relieved and guilty.

I explained that the new HarborBridge job has me on Zoom with clients until 5:30 or 6 some nights, and that I promised myself (and you) I wouldn’t go back to sprinting from work to a shift twice a week. I also mentioned that Wednesday evenings are now sacred because of our every‑other‑week sessions with Rachel Ortiz at Capitol Hill Therapy Collective. I didn’t go into detail, just said we’re using that time to work through long‑term planning and work–life boundaries. It still felt weird to say “our couples therapist” out loud to someone outside the inner circle, even though we’ve been seeing Rachel since August.

I also found myself telling Erin more than I expected about why I stepped down in June. I said that my therapist, Dr. Karen Liu at Harborview Behavioral Health, has been pretty clear that the stress‑related insomnia and early signs of burnout weren’t going to magically disappear if I kept stacking evening commitments on top of a full‑time analytics job. She even titled one of my MyChart notes “processing secondary trauma from shelter homework club,” which is exactly what it felt like some nights walking home.

Saying all of that, I realized how much the Columbia City house stuff is still in my body too. I didn’t tell Erin this part, but when we were talking about safe, stable housing for the kids, I flashed back to that March 2021 email I sent our old landlord – the one titled “Concerns about safety and repeated late-night outbursts,” after Mark pounded on the bedroom wall and yelled those slurs because he heard us talking. No wonder I care so much about kids having someplace they can exhale.

Anyway, thank you for holding that Wednesday space with me and for not rolling your eyes when I say no to things now. Let’s stick to the plan of stir‑fry and one episode of something mindless tonight.

Love,
L

metadata.subject: Evergreen call + boundaries

artifacts.content:
Working title: “Why I left North Sound (for me only, not for anyone else).”

1. Official story is still true: I wanted work that aligns more directly with education and housing nonprofits. HarborBridge gives me that.

2. Unofficial but equally true: those last nine months at North Sound (Jan–Sep 2024) were a slow drip of ethical heartburn. I keep thinking about that March 2024 email from E. (“Can we smooth out the retention dip before the board sees this?”) and the Zoom where he screen‑shared my Tableau dashboard and tried to nudge me toward changing cohort definitions so the line wouldn’t look so jagged. I said no, but the pressure was there.

3. That’s why there’s a folder on my personal laptop called “NSDS – data integrity concerns.” It has redlined reports, email threads, and my own notes. I’ve never shown it to anyone. It exists because I needed to know I wasn’t imagining the gaslighting.

4. Layer on top of that the stuff outside work: seeing Dr. Karen Liu at Harborview Behavioral Health since February 2023 for stress‑related insomnia and early burnout signs; MyChart notes literally titled “work/volunteer boundary setting.” The nights I couldn’t fall asleep after homework club at Evergreen because I was replaying kids’ stories about bouncing between shelters.

5. Then Dad’s fall in early November 2024 and the trip to Providence St. Peter in Olympia, the doctor talking about “worsening chronic back issues,” me quietly setting up the “Dad’s PT & meds” Google Sheet so Mom wouldn’t have to carry it all. Realizing I need a job where leaving at 3 p.m. to drive south for a PT appointment doesn’t feel like an act of rebellion.

6. And the practical layer: that July 2024 meeting with Eric Dawson at the BECU Rainier Valley branch about whether we can afford both a condo and daycare someday without blowing our debt‑to‑income ratio. His face when he walked me through the numbers was…sobering.

All of these pieces matter. Remember this when you start wondering if you “overreacted” by leaving.

metadata.title: Why I really left North Sound

artifacts.content:
Event: Individual therapy – Dr. Karen Liu
Date: Thursday, 2024-03-14
Time: 5:30–6:20 p.m.
Location: Harborview Behavioral Health Clinic, 3rd floor

Description/Notes to self:

– This is session #6 with Dr. Liu since starting in Feb 2023. Goal today: be honest about how frayed I feel.

– Topics to cover:
  • Work: North Sound crunch, E. pushing on “smoothing” retention metrics again before the April board deck. Bring printout of that March email where he wrote, “Can we smooth out the retention dip before the board sees this?” and my draft response.
  • Volunteering: Evergreen Family Shelter homework club. Talk about why I still can’t shake the stories from the kids even after I get home. Ask if it’s normal that I wake up at 3 a.m. worrying about whether they’re still in the same room at the shelter.
  • Insomnia: track how many nights this week I’ve been up past 1 a.m. scrolling instead of reading. Mention the MyChart note she titled “processing secondary trauma from shelter homework club” and whether there’s anything more concrete I can do besides “better sleep hygiene.”

– Also want to briefly mention the October 2022 consult at UW Medicine Roosevelt Women’s Health Clinic about possible egg freezing. It’s still floating in the back of my mind. Maya and I keep saying we’ll revisit the whole “kids” question when things calm down, but between my schedule, her counseling caseload, and the rent at Maple Terrace, I don’t see a magical calm period appearing.

– Ask for ideas on boundaries so I don’t say yes to another weekly volunteer gig on top of everything just because someone needs help.

Subject: Trying to figure out timing (and bandwidth)

Hey Emily,

I owe you about 17 updates, but I’ll start with the big brain‑space one.

You remember that October 2022 appointment I had at UW Medicine’s Roosevelt Women’s Health Clinic to talk about fertility preservation? The one where the doctor walked me through egg‑freezing timelines and printed out the price ranges for one cycle and the meds, and I came home with a folder I promptly hid in the closet? That’s still very much in play.

Maya and I had another long talk after our Wednesday session with Rachel Ortiz at Capitol Hill Therapy Collective. We’ve been seeing her every other week since August to work through the “are we actually going to try for a baby?” question and all the work–life stuff around it. Rachel keeps gently asking what we’re willing to give up to make space, and I keep staring at my calendar like it’s going to rearrange itself.

Part of why I’m hesitating to jump back into anything like the twice‑weekly Evergreen homework club schedule is that I want some margin if we decide to move forward with fertility treatments in the next year or two. Between potential monitoring appointments at Roosevelt, my HarborBridge workload, and Maya’s school counselor hours, we’re already playing Tetris.

And then there’s the money. After that July 2024 meeting with Eric Dawson at the BECU Rainier Valley branch – where he basically showed me that adding daycare plus a mortgage on even a tiny condo would push us right up against a not‑great debt‑to‑income ratio – I’m very aware that an egg‑freezing cycle isn’t pocket change either.

All of this is layered on top of the usual Laura stew: worrying about my Evergreen kids even after stepping back in June, trying not to bring work trauma home (thank you, Dr. Liu at Harborview, for the “boundary setting” pep talks), and now helping coordinate Dad’s PT schedule after his St. Peter back evaluation.

Anyway, this is long, but you’re one of the few people I can dump the whole messy picture on. If you have bandwidth for a Zoom soon, I’d love to hear how you and Oliver navigated the “when” decision, even though your path is obviously different.

Love,
Laura

metadata.subject: Trying to figure out timing

artifacts.content:
Thread: Laura & ex‑coworker (Signal)

[2024-09-18, 9:07 p.m.]
Jess: so how’s harborbridge? you look less dead in your IG stories lol

Laura: lol low bar but yeah, better

Jess: real talk – did you actually leave NSDS just for “mission alignment” or was there more tea 👀

Laura: short answer: both

Laura: longer answer (since this is encrypted): by the time I left in Sept I had a whole folder on my home laptop called “NSDS – data integrity concerns.”

Jess: oh wow

Laura: remember that March 2024 email from E.? “Can we smooth out the retention dip before the board sees this?” that one. plus the zoom where he tried to get me to redefine cohorts so the Tableau line looked nicer. I kept redlines + my notes bc I honestly started doubting my own memory.

Jess: jesus. glad you got out

Laura: same. but it took Dr. Liu basically spelling out “this is burning you out” before I believed myself.

Jess: therapist?

Laura: yeah, Karen Liu at Harborview Behavioral Health. been seeing her since Feb 2023 for stress‑insomnia + the whole early‑burnout thing. MyChart literally has a note titled “processing secondary trauma from shelter homework club.”

Jess: oof

Laura: add on top of that Dad’s fall last November – we spent half a day at Providence St. Peter in Olympia while they evaluated his back. I’m now the unofficial project manager for his PT + meds via a shared Google Sheet w/ my mom.

Jess: that’s a lot

Laura: and meanwhile Maya + I are in couples sessions w/ Rachel Ortiz at Capitol Hill Therapy Collective every other Wed trying to figure out work‑life + maybe‑kids. so yeah, “mission alignment” is true, but also: I needed out before something snapped.

Jess: proud of you for not setting yourself on fire for dashboards

Laura: lol that should be on a mug

Hi Daniel,

I hope you’re doing well! I wanted to catch up and share some updates, especially since I’ve had a few changes recently. As you know, since moving to Seattle in 2016, I’ve been deeply involved in volunteering with education and housing nonprofits. My most recent stint was with Evergreen Family Shelter from 2020 until this past June. I was supervising about four volunteers there, coordinating evening homework sessions for kids in transitional housing, but had to step back due to the new role at HarborBridge.

Before that, I was an Adult ESL Classroom Assistant at Rainier Valley Community Learning Center from early 2017 to March 2020. It was such a rewarding experience helping adult learners with English through practical exercises like drafting emails and job interview prep. Unfortunately, when classes went virtual due to COVID, many faced connectivity issues. I tried to support with Zoom, but ultimately, my own work had me logging unpredictable hours. We agreed it was best for me to step back, hoping to return in-person someday.

I started volunteering back in college around 2010 in Portland, where I worked with Portland Community Food Share from 2013 to 2016. I was involved in intake, helping families navigate their options with dignity, and even trained new volunteers on food safety. I had to leave when I moved to Seattle for my job at North Sound Data Solutions in August 2019. It's been a journey from Portland to Seattle, aligning my career with my passions.

Speaking of work, my current phone contact has changed to (206) 555-2843, which you can use to reach me outside of emails. Also, I’ve been reminiscing about those days at Bridgeview Research & Consulting in Portland, where I started in 2015. It was there that I honed my skills in data analysis, which set the stage for my current position.

On the personal front, Maya and I are contemplating what the future holds, maybe even starting a family. It’s a lot to consider with our workloads and Seattle’s high cost of living. We’re still figuring it out, but it’s exciting to think about.

Let’s chat soon—I’d love to hear more about your teaching adventures in Tacoma!

Take care,
Laura

event_info.special_needs_or_medical_conditions_we_s:
I don’t have any major medical conditions that would prevent me from fully participating in the conference, but there are a few minor things that may be helpful for the organizers to know in case of an emergency or when planning seating and room arrangements.

1) Mild seasonal allergies: I have mild seasonal allergies (primarily to grass and tree pollen). This usually just means I may sneeze a bit more if outdoor sessions or networking events are held in grassy areas, but it does not require any special medical support. I usually carry over-the-counter antihistamines (cetirizine) with me and take them as needed.

2) Sensitivity to very strong fragrances: Strong artificial fragrances (especially some perfumes and heavily scented cleaning products) can occasionally trigger mild headaches or light nausea for me. It’s not severe, but if possible I prefer to sit a bit away from heavily scented candles or diffusers. If there is a designated fragrance-free or low-scent area in conference rooms, I’d appreciate being able to sit there.

3) Food-related note (not an allergy): I do not have any food allergies, but I try to avoid large amounts of dairy during the daytime sessions because it tends to make me a bit sluggish and can occasionally upset my stomach. I’m fine with small amounts of cheese or milk in coffee, but I generally prefer non-dairy milk options (oat or almond) if they are available. This is more of a preference than a medical requirement, but it does help me stay more alert and engaged during long sessions.

4) General physical needs: I am able to walk and stand without assistance, but I do have a minor lower back issue from an old sports injury (nothing serious). If sessions involve prolonged standing, I may look for a chair or a place to sit occasionally. During longer plenary sessions, I really appreciate chairs with at least minimal back support. No special accommodations are strictly necessary, but I mention this in case there is any flexibility around seating or stretch breaks.

Overall, I’m fully able to participate in all activities, including the tours and regular conference schedule. These notes are just to give context in case there are options for seating areas, fragrance-light zones, or non-dairy beverage alternatives.
event_info.anything_else_we_should_know_about_you:
I’m attending as a representative of Whitaker Analytics Group, a small data consulting firm I founded in 2018 that focuses on helping mid-sized nonprofits and membership associations make better use of their data for decision-making. My background is in economics and statistics, and I spent about ten years working inside a national nonprofit before moving into consulting. Because of that, I tend to approach sponsorships like this as both a networking opportunity and a chance to genuinely support the community I’m working with.

A few personal details that might be useful for planning and context:

1) Professional interests: I’m especially interested in sessions related to data governance, responsible AI use for associations, and practical case studies about improving member engagement through analytics. If there are any breakout groups or roundtables on those topics, I’d be very eager to join or even contribute informally. I’ve done several workshops on using dashboards to tell stories to boards of directors, and I’m always happy to share slide decks or examples if that would be helpful.

2) Personality and networking style: I’m fairly outgoing once I’ve met people, but I tend to be a little quiet during the first hour of an event while I get my bearings. If there are any structured networking activities (e.g., small-group introductions, topic tables, or sponsor meet-and-greet sessions), those really help me connect with people more naturally. I also make a point of being approachable at our sponsor table—if you’re assigning table locations, I don’t mind being in a fairly high-traffic area because I actually enjoy answering questions from attendees.

3) Travel details: I’ll be flying in from Austin on Monday afternoon and staying at the main conference hotel through Thursday morning. That means I’ll be available for Tuesday and Wednesday daytime and evening events, but I’ll likely head out early on Thursday to catch my flight. If there are any sponsor briefings or logistics meetings, it’s easiest for me to attend them on Tuesday morning before sessions begin or during a mid-morning break.

4) Dietary preferences: As mentioned above, I try to go light on dairy during the day and prefer balanced meals with some protein and vegetables so I’m not falling asleep after lunch. I’m not vegetarian or vegan, but I’m very happy with vegetarian options. If there are buffet-style meals, I usually build a plate with lean protein, vegetables, and something light like rice or salad. I drink coffee in the morning (ideally with oat milk if available) and switch to tea or water in the afternoon.

5) Hobbies and conversation starters: Outside of work, I’m a long-distance runner and usually get up early to run a few miles around the city I’m visiting. If there are safe running paths near the hotel, any maps or local tips would be appreciated. I also enjoy local coffee shops and bookstores, so recommendations from staff or other attendees are always welcome. I mention this because I’ve found that casual conversations about running routes or local spots often turn into really meaningful professional connections.

6) Accessibility and comfort: I don’t require formal accessibility accommodations, but I do appreciate clear signage and printed schedules or maps. I tend to take notes by hand as well as on my laptop, so a table surface or at least a chair with an armrest is helpful. If there are any quiet corners or lounge areas where people can step away from the noise for a few minutes, I often use those between sessions to recharge.

7) Sponsorship expectations: My main goals as a sponsor are (a) to learn more about the challenges associations are facing around data and technology, (b) to meet potential partners and clients in a low-pressure setting, and (c) to support this event so it continues in future years. I’m not interested in hard selling; I prefer longer-term relationships where we can be a helpful resource even if someone never becomes a paying client. If there are any guidelines about how sponsors should engage with attendees (e.g., limits on email follow-ups, expectations around prize drawings or swag), I’d appreciate having those clearly laid out so we can align with the culture of the event.

If you need any additional details from me ahead of time—such as a short bio, logo files, or information about any materials we plan to bring for our table—please feel free to reach out by email. I try to respond within 24 hours on weekdays.
