persona.education: B.A. in Psychology, University of California, San Diego (2010); M.D., University of Colorado School of Medicine (2014); Psychiatry Residency, University of Colorado (2014–2018); Addiction Psychiatry Fellowship, University of Colorado (2018–2019); Board-certified in Psychiatry and Addiction Psychiatry.
family_members: Daniel Morales (38, long-term partner, currently doing contract work in the hospitality/events industry)
Michael Nguyen (17, younger brother, high school student, living with Alexandra as his legal guardian)
Lan Pham (62, mother, lives in Colorado Springs, CO, multiple chronic health conditions)
Bao Nguyen (65, father, lives in Colorado Springs, semi-retired mechanic)
Sophie Morales (4, Alexandra’s and Daniel’s daughter)
Linh Tran (34, cousin, lives in Aurora, CO, close friend and informal childcare support)
persona.background_context:
This is Alexandra Nguyen, a 36-year-old addiction psychiatrist living in a modest two-bedroom apartment in Denver’s Athmar Park neighborhood with her partner Daniel, their four-year-old daughter Sophie, and her 17-year-old brother Michael. Weekdays start early: she’s usually up by 5:45 a.m. to squeeze in a short yoga video and coffee before waking Sophie and checking that Michael is actually up for school and has taken his morning meds. By 7:30 a.m., she and Daniel are tag-teaming drop-offs—either preschool for Sophie and high school for Michael, or, on days when Daniel has a contract event, Alexandra does both before driving across town to Riverside Community Behavioral Health Center.

At Riverside, Alexandra’s days are full and often emotionally heavy. She spends most mornings in back-to-back 30-minute visits with patients dealing with opioid, methamphetamine, and alcohol use disorders, many with co-occurring schizophrenia, bipolar disorder, or PTSD. She leads a small interdisciplinary team—two social workers, a nurse, a peer specialist, and a part-time psychologist—focused on expanding access to medications for opioid use disorder and running bilingual relapse-prevention groups. Two afternoons a week she carves out time for group visits, case conferences, and quick hallway consults with primary care clinicians about complex patients. She’s become the informal go-to person for anything involving buprenorphine, extended-release naltrexone, or harm reduction, and she still takes quiet pride when she sees naloxone kits in patients’ bags and hears about another overdose reversed in the community.

Outside of work, the last few years have felt like a constant balancing act. Daniel’s layoff in mid-2021 and his now-unpredictable contract income have made finances tight, and Alexandra keeps a careful spreadsheet of expenses on her laptop—student loan payments, a lingering personal loan from residency, increased grocery and gas costs, and Michael’s specialist appointments and medications. She became Michael’s legal guardian after their mother’s health declined due to complications from diabetes and heart failure; coordinating his care, his therapy, and IEP meetings with his school counselor has become a second job. Evenings are a mix of ordinary family chaos and quiet responsibility: cooking simple dinners, reviewing Michael’s homework, FaceTiming with their parents in Colorado Springs, and reading picture books with Sophie before bed. On good nights, she and Daniel sit on the small balcony with tea, talking about his hopes of finding a more stable job and her ideas for a trauma-informed care project at the clinic.

Alexandra’s main personal worries revolve around sustainability—of her career, her finances, and her ability to be present for her family. She feels deeply committed to staying in a safety-net setting and often brings home ideas from recent conferences or webinars to share with her team, drafting handouts in the evenings or planning brief in-service talks over weekends. She tries to protect at least one weekend afternoon for family time—taking Sophie to the Denver Zoo, walking along the South Platte River, or visiting her parents in Colorado Springs when they’re stable enough for company. She keeps in touch with a small circle of colleagues from residency and fellowship through group texts and occasional coffee meetups downtown, where they swap clinical tips and commiserate about burnout. Despite the strain, Alexandra still feels that her work matters, especially when a long-time patient shows up more stable, a family member thanks her for listening, or Michael jokes with her in the car on the way home, sounding more like a typical teenager than a kid who’s been through too much.

secrets.secret_content: Since late 2020, Alexandra has been seeing Dr. Melissa Hart, a therapist at Denver Health’s Outpatient Behavioral Health Clinic, for monthly sessions focused on burnout and anxiety related to her addiction psychiatry work, with recurring notes in her calendar labeled “telehealth check-in w/ Dr. Hart – compassion fatigue” on Wednesday afternoons.
secrets.why_sensitive: Disclosure of her own mental health treatment could expose her to stigma as a psychiatrist, raise concerns about her fitness for duty among colleagues or credentialing bodies if misinterpreted, and affect future leadership or academic opportunities.
secrets.how_disclosed: While drafting a more personal explanation in the Comments section or in a follow-up email to the MAP committee, she might write something like, “Per my therapist, Dr. Hart at Denver Health, I’ve been trying to set boundaries around work to reduce burnout, but the financial stress has made that difficult,” to illustrate the impact of her financial strain on her well-being.

secrets.secret_content: In August 2019, during her final year of fellowship at the University of Colorado, Alexandra required a six-week reduction in clinical hours after being hospitalized overnight at UCHealth University of Colorado Hospital for a stress-related cardiac workup following an episode of chest pain and near-syncope on the inpatient psychiatry unit.
secrets.why_sensitive: A documented history of stress-related medical events could be seen as a liability by employers or malpractice insurers, and might prompt intrusive questions about her capacity to handle high-intensity clinical roles.
secrets.how_disclosed: When elaborating on the “small personal loan I took out during residency when I briefly had to reduce my hours due to a health issue,” she might specify in an email to the program administrator, “That was after I ended up in the UCHealth ER with chest pain from overwork and had to cut back my shifts for six weeks,” to justify the origin of the debt.

secrets.secret_content: Since September 2021, Alexandra has been Michael Nguyen’s legal guardian under a formal guardianship order issued by Denver Juvenile Court, case number beginning “2021JG…”, after their mother, Lan Pham, was hospitalized twice at Penrose Hospital in Colorado Springs for complications of heart failure and poorly controlled diabetes.
secrets.why_sensitive: Legal guardianship records and the circumstances leading to them involve both her brother’s and her mother’s vulnerabilities; disclosure could embarrass family members, invite judgment about her parents’ functioning, and expose Michael to stigma at school or in social settings.
secrets.how_disclosed: When expanding on “we became legal guardians for my younger brother,” she might write to a colleague or in a more detailed justification, “The Denver Juvenile Court granted me legal guardianship of Michael in September 2021 after Mom’s second ICU stay at Penrose—so all the medical and school decisions now fall on me,” to show the scope of her caregiving responsibilities.

secrets.secret_content: Michael Nguyen has been followed at Children’s Hospital Colorado in Aurora since early 2020 for a chronic autoimmune condition requiring daily medication and quarterly visits with pediatric neurologist Dr. Aaron Feldman, with Alexandra tracking his dosing and refills in a shared Google Sheet titled “Michael meds & labs – DO NOT EDIT.”
secrets.why_sensitive: Details of Michael’s specific diagnosis, specialist, and treatment regimen are protected health information; sharing them could violate his privacy, lead to stigma among peers, or affect future insurance and employment opportunities if widely known.
secrets.how_disclosed: In trying to illustrate the financial and time burden of his care, she might add in the Comments box or in an email to AAAP staff, “Between Michael’s quarterly neurology visits with Dr. Feldman at Children’s in Aurora and his meds, which I track in our ‘Michael meds & labs’ spreadsheet, we’ve taken on several hundred dollars a month in out-of-pocket costs,” inadvertently revealing specific medical details.

secrets.secret_content: As of February 2022, Alexandra is carrying approximately $185,000 in federal medical school loans serviced by FedLoan Servicing and a $9,500 personal loan from Elevations Credit Union taken out in October 2018 to cover living expenses when her hours were reduced, with a color-coded Excel file on her laptop labeled “Nguyen_Financial_Triage_2022.xlsx” tracking minimum payments and projected deferments.
secrets.why_sensitive: Exact debt amounts, lenders, and financial tracking practices could be used for identity theft or targeted scams, and could also expose her to professional stigma or judgment about her financial management, especially within a relatively small specialty community.
secrets.how_disclosed: When clarifying what she means by “ongoing student loan obligations” in a narrative to the MAP committee or in a side email to a mentor, she might write, “I’m still sitting on about $185k with FedLoan plus a $9.5k Elevations Credit Union personal loan I took in 2018—my ‘Nguyen_Financial_Triage_2022’ sheet is basically red every month,” revealing precise, sensitive financial details.

secrets.secret_content: In November 2021, Alexandra filed an internal incident report at Riverside Community Behavioral Health Center after a senior attending, Dr. Robert Klein, dismissed her concerns about unsafe benzodiazepine co-prescribing in a hallway conversation, telling her, “You’re overreacting; this is how we’ve always done it here,” which she documented in an email to her medical director with the subject line “Concern re: benzo co-prescribing and clinic risk exposure.”
secrets.why_sensitive: Reporting a senior colleague can strain professional relationships, harm her reputation if perceived as a troublemaker, and potentially affect future references or promotions, especially in a tight-knit clinical community.
secrets.how_disclosed: In explaining her commitment to evidence-based practice and the pressures of working in a safety-net setting, she might mention in a more candid version of the Comments or in a note to an AAAP mentor, “After Dr. Klein told me I was ‘overreacting’ about benzos last November, I ended up emailing our medical director about clinic risk,” inadvertently revealing internal conflict and names.

secrets.secret_content: During a tense phone call in January 2022 from her parents’ kitchen in Colorado Springs, Alexandra’s father, Bao Nguyen, told her, “You’re choosing your job over this family,” after she declined to take an unpaid leave from Riverside to move in and care full-time for her mother, a conflict she later summarized in a journal entry titled “Dad’s ultimatum – 1/9/22.”
secrets.why_sensitive: This exposes deep family conflict and cultural expectations around caregiving; if shared broadly it could damage relationships, invite judgment from colleagues about her boundaries, or be used against her in any future family legal disputes about care decisions.
secrets.how_disclosed: While trying to convey the emotional strain behind her increased caregiving responsibilities in an email to a trusted AAAP colleague or in a more personal addendum, she might write, “Dad actually said, ‘You’re choosing your job over this family’ when I couldn’t move to Colorado Springs in January 2022,” sharing a private family conflict.

secrets.secret_content: On several evenings in late 2021, Alexandra emailed her close residency friend, Dr. Priya Shah, from her Riverside work account with the subject line “Burnout + $$ panic” describing crying in the parking lot after clinic and briefly wondering if she should “just quit and go part-time telepsychiatry for a corporate chain” to keep up with bills.
secrets.why_sensitive: These messages reveal acute emotional distress and thoughts about leaving her safety-net role; if seen by her employer or others, they could lead to questions about her stability, commitment, or suitability for leadership roles.
secrets.how_disclosed: When emphasizing the severity of her financial stress and burnout to justify MAP assistance, she might reference those emails in a side note to a mentor or committee member: “I’ve even emailed Priya from my Riverside account with subject lines like ‘Burnout + $$ panic’ after crying in the parking lot,” inadvertently exposing highly personal emotional struggles.

artifacts.content:
From: alexandra.nguyen@riversidecbhc.org
To: priya.shah@ucdenver.edu
Subject: Burnout + $$ panic (again, sorry)
Date: 2022-02-10

Hey Priya,

Typing this from the Riverside parking lot because apparently that’s my new crying spot. Don’t worry, I’m okay-ish, just wrung out.

Clinic was a lot today—three new meth inductions, one guy I’ve followed for two years relapsed after his brother OD’d, and then a hallway “debate” with Klein about benzos that brought me right back to last November. Remember when he told me I was “overreacting” about the clonazepam scripts and “this is how we’ve always done it here”? I still have the email I sent our medical director with the subject line “Concern re: benzo co-prescribing and clinic risk exposure” sitting in my Sent folder like a little time bomb. Nothing really changed, except now he avoids eye contact.

The work stuff would be manageable if the money piece wasn’t so tight. Daniel’s contract gigs are all over the place, and I’m staring at my stupid “Nguyen_Financial_Triage_2022.xlsx” every weekend trying to make the FedLoan payment and the Elevations Credit Union personal loan both happen. I’m still sitting on about $185k in med school loans plus that $9.5k I took out in 2018 after the UCHealth scare, when I ended up in the ER at University of Colorado Hospital with chest pain/near-syncope from overwork and had to cut back my fellowship shifts for six weeks. It feels like I’m still paying for that one night.

I actually told Dr. Hart at my Denver Health telehealth session yesterday (the usual Wednesday afternoon “telehealth check-in w/ Dr. Hart – compassion fatigue” block on my calendar) that I’ve fantasized about quitting safety-net work and just doing part-time telepsych for some corporate chain to dig out of this hole. She didn’t freak out, just asked what it would mean for how I see myself. Cue more tears.

Anyway, I know you’re slammed too, but if you’re up for a Zoom or coffee sometime soon, I’d love to see your face and talk about literally anything other than RVUs and loan servicers.

Love,
Alex

metadata.subject: Burnout + $$ panic (again, sorry)

artifacts.content:
Title: Running list – Michael care & guardianship logistics
Date: 2022-02-18

1. Guardianship paperwork
- Remember: formal legal guardianship for Michael since Sept 2021 – Denver Juvenile Court case starting “2021JG…”. I’m the one who signs for everything now.
- Need to scan the latest order and upload to shared family folder so school and doctors stop asking me to re-fax it.
- When I get overwhelmed, remind myself *why* this happened: Mom’s two hospitalizations at Penrose Hospital in Colorado Springs last summer/fall (heart failure + out-of-control diabetes, including that ICU stay). There really wasn’t another option.

2. Medical
- Michael’s chronic autoimmune thing – continue follow-up at Children’s Hospital Colorado in Aurora.
- Next quarterly neurology visit with Dr. Aaron Feldman (peds neuro) – confirm date/time, make sure it doesn’t conflict with my Wednesday afternoon group.
- Keep the “Michael meds & labs – DO NOT EDIT” Google Sheet updated. Double-check daily dosing column on Sundays and refill dates so we don’t end up in pharmacy hell.
- Track out-of-pocket costs; it feels like “several hundred a month” in copays/meds alone. Add to budget tab in Nguyen_Financial_Triage_2022.

3. School
- Email his counselor about IEP check-in; bring guardianship order and med list.
- Remind Michael (again) that I’m the legal decision-maker now, not because Mom and Dad don’t love him, but because they were drowning when Penrose discharged her the second time.

4. Emotional stuff
- He’s still mad about moving from Colorado Springs to Denver. Validate that, but also be honest about Dad’s comment in January (“You’re choosing your job over this family”) and how impossible that ultimatum felt.
- Maybe bring this up with Dr. Hart at next compassion-fatigue session; balance between being his guardian and his sister is blurry.

5. Practical
- Color-code his appointments on the family Google Calendar so Daniel knows when he has to cover Sophie pickup.
- Pack snacks + headphones for the long Aurora clinic days.

metadata.title: Running list – Michael care & guardianship logistics

Event: Telehealth check-in w/ Dr. Hart – compassion fatigue
Date: Wednesday, 2022-01-26
Time: 4:15–5:00 p.m.
Location: Home office (corner of bedroom), Zoom – Denver Health Outpatient Behavioral Health Clinic
Attendees: Alexandra Nguyen, MD; Melissa Hart, PhD

Notes:
- Monthly therapy – started late 2020 after that run of overdoses + feeling numb in clinic.
- Agenda for today:
  • Update on Riverside workload – MOUD expansion, more meth cases, fallout from November 2021 incident w/ Dr. Klein (he told me in the hallway, “You’re overreacting; this is how we’ve always done it here,” when I raised concerns about unsafe benzo co-prescribing). I filed the internal incident report and emailed our medical director (“Concern re: benzo co-prescribing and clinic risk exposure”), but tension still lingers.
  • Talk through ongoing “Burnout + $$ panic” loop – Daniel’s unstable contract income, my loan situation (approx. $185k federal med school loans w/ FedLoan + $9.5k Elevations CU personal loan from Oct 2018 when I reduced hours after UCHealth overnight chest-pain workup). Mention how I obsess over the “Nguyen_Financial_Triage_2022.xlsx” spreadsheet.
  • Check in about guardianship stress since Sept 2021 Denver Juvenile Court order making me Michael’s legal guardian. Juggle between his Children’s Hospital Colorado neurology appointments with Dr. Feldman, meds tracked in “Michael meds & labs – DO NOT EDIT,” and Mom’s health at Penrose.
  • Process January phone fight w/ Dad in their Colorado Springs kitchen (“You’re choosing your job over this family”) when I said I couldn’t move in and quit Riverside to be full-time caregiver.

Reminders:
- Ask Dr. Hart for concrete boundary strategies (email curfew, no opening EMR after 9 p.m.).
- Bring up occasional fantasy about quitting safety-net work for corporate telepsych and whether that’s avoidance vs. realistic option.
- Log in 5 minutes early to test audio so I’m not flustered.

metadata.title: Telehealth check-in w/ Dr. Hart – compassion fatigue
metadata.location: Home / Zoom – Denver Health Outpatient Behavioral Health Clinic

artifacts.content:
From: alexandra.nguyen@riversidecbhc.org
To: linh.tran@gmail.com
Subject: Can we steal you for Saturday?
Date: 2022-03-02

Hi Linh,

Is there any chance you’re free this Saturday afternoon to hang with Sophie and Michael for a few hours? Daniel picked up a last-minute event gig at the convention center, and I’m trying to squeeze in a real grocery run plus maybe 30 minutes of not being “Dr. Nguyen” or “Mom” or “legal guardian” to anyone.

Michael has his next neurology follow-up with Dr. Feldman at Children’s Hospital Colorado in Aurora later this month, and I need to sit down with the “Michael meds & labs – DO NOT EDIT” sheet and make sure we’re not missing any labs before we go. His dosing schedule has gotten more complicated, and between that and everything with Mom after her Penrose ICU stays, my brain is mush.

On top of that, I spent half of last night staring at my stupid loan spreadsheet (the infamous Nguyen_Financial_Triage_2022.xlsx) instead of sleeping. I keep running the numbers on the ~$185k in federal loans and the $9.5k personal loan from Elevations I took out back in 2018 after the UCHealth chest-pain scare forced me to cut my hours. It feels like no matter how many extra notes I finish at home, the balances don’t move.

Work has been its own soap opera. Since that November hallway thing with Dr. Klein (“You’re overreacting; this is how we’ve always done it here”), it’s been awkward. I did the right thing filing the incident report and emailing our medical director about the benzo co-prescribing, but being the squeaky wheel is exhausting.

Anyway, if you’re free 1–4 p.m., I can drop the kids at your place in Aurora on my way to Costco. I’ll bring banh mi from New Saigon as a bribe. Sophie will want to show you her new dinosaur stickers, and Michael mostly just needs Wi-Fi and snacks.

Love,
Alex

metadata.subject: Can we steal you for Saturday?

artifacts.content:
Thread: Alex & Dad
Date: 2022-01-10

Bao: We need to talk about yesterday.

Alexandra: I know, Ba. I’m still at Riverside finishing notes but I can text a little.

Bao: Your mother was crying all night. She heard you say you cannot come to Colorado Springs to stay. She thinks you don’t care.

Alexandra: I do care. That’s why I’ve been driving down after clinic, juggling Michael’s appointments, everything with Penrose after her ICU stays. I just can’t quit my job and move in full-time.

Bao: You are choosing your job over this family. I said that in the kitchen yesterday and I mean it.

Alexandra: That’s not fair. Since September, I’ve been Michael’s legal guardian because of the Denver Juvenile Court order. All the decisions for him are on me now. I have to keep my income and my insurance for his care at Children’s in Aurora and Mom’s meds too.

Bao: When we were your age, we didn’t think like this. Family first.

Alexandra: Family first is why I took guardianship when you both were overwhelmed after Mom’s second hospitalization at Penrose. It’s why I track Michael’s meds in that Google Sheet and take him to see Dr. Feldman every few months. It’s why I sit in therapy with Dr. Hart at Denver Health talking about compassion fatigue so I don’t burn out and disappear on everyone.

Bao: Therapy again? You are strong, you don’t need that.

Alexandra: I need it, Ba. Last time I ignored stress I ended up in the UCHealth ER in 2019 with chest pain and had to cut my hours. I’m trying not to repeat that.

Bao: …We talk more when you come this weekend.

Alexandra: Okay. I’ll bring Sophie. Please tell Mom I love her.

Title: Money + work brain dump (late-night)
Date: 2022-02-22

Couldn’t sleep again, so trying to get this out of my head instead of scrolling.

1. Debt snapshot
- ~185,000 in federal med school loans (FedLoan) + 9,500 personal loan from Elevations Credit Union (took it Oct 2018 when I reduced hours after the UCHealth University of Colorado Hospital overnight chest-pain/near-syncope workup on the inpatient psych unit). Still feels surreal that one episode = years of payments.
- Keep updating Nguyen_Financial_Triage_2022.xlsx every Sunday. Color coding helps but also makes it obvious how tight things are.

2. Work stress
- Since November 2021, still thinking about the incident with Dr. Robert Klein at Riverside. Hallway outside Exam 7: I raised concerns about unsafe benzo co-prescribing, he said, “You’re overreacting; this is how we’ve always done it here.” I went back to my office shaking, then wrote that email to our medical director (“Concern re: benzo co-prescribing and clinic risk exposure”) and filed the incident report. Proud I did it, but also worried I’ve branded myself as difficult.

3. Burnout signs
- Parking lot crying spells after clinic, then emailing Priya from my Riverside account with subject lines like “Burnout + $$ panic.” Not my finest boundary moment, but she gets it.
- Wednesday afternoon therapy with Dr. Melissa Hart at Denver Health’s Outpatient Behavioral Health Clinic (calendar label: “telehealth check-in w/ Dr. Hart – compassion fatigue”) has been a lifeline. Talked last week about the fantasy of quitting safety-net work for a corporate telepsych chain just to make the numbers less terrifying.

4. Family overlay
- Michael’s guardianship (Denver Juvenile Court 2021JG…) and his quarterly Children’s Hospital Colorado neurology visits with Dr. Feldman, plus meds tracked in “Michael meds & labs – DO NOT EDIT,” add time and money stress.
- Dad’s “You’re choosing your job over this family” line from their Colorado Springs kitchen keeps echoing.

Goal: bring this whole tangled mess to Dr. Hart next session instead of pretending I’m fine.

From: alexandra.nguyen@riversidecbhc.org
To: self (personal)
Subject: Things I don’t want to forget to mention to Dr. Hart
Date: 2022-03-09

Alex,

Quick brain dump before you lose track of all this.

1. Work
- Talk about how the clinic still feels different after the November 2021 hallway run-in with Dr. Klein (“You’re overreacting; this is how we’ve always done it here”) and the email/incident report (“Concern re: benzo co-prescribing and clinic risk exposure”). You’re still carrying that tension into every team meeting.

2. Old health scare
- Revisit August 2019 at UCHealth University of Colorado Hospital – chest pain + near-syncope on the inpatient psych unit, overnight cardiac workup, then six-week reduction in fellowship hours. You keep minimizing it, but it’s the reason for the Elevations Credit Union loan and some of the current anxiety about “breaking” again.

3. Money spiral
- Be honest about how much time you spend staring at Nguyen_Financial_Triage_2022.xlsx, re-running the ~185k FedLoan balance + 9.5k Elevations loan numbers. The late-night catastrophizing is getting worse.

4. Guardianship + family
- Since Sept 2021 Denver Juvenile Court guardianship order (2021JG…), you’re juggling Michael’s care: Children’s Hospital Colorado neurology with Dr. Feldman, meds tracked in “Michael meds & labs – DO NOT EDIT,” school IEPs.
- Layer on Mom’s Penrose Hospital admissions and Dad’s January kitchen ultimatum in Colorado Springs (“You’re choosing your job over this family”). That sentence is living rent-free in your head.

5. Parking lot moments
- Those evenings you sit in the Riverside parking lot after clinic, crying, then emailing Priya with subject lines like “Burnout + $$ panic” and wondering if you should “just quit and go part-time telepsychiatry for a corporate chain” to keep up with bills.

Bring this to the next Wednesday “telehealth check-in w/ Dr. Hart – compassion fatigue” so you don’t spend 20 minutes saying, “Everything’s fine, just busy.”

– A

metadata.subject: Things I don’t want to forget to mention to Dr. Hart

artifacts.content:
Hi Linh,

I hope you're doing well! Things have been quite busy on my end, and I wanted to update you on some developments, both personal and professional.

First, I’m planning to attend the 2022 AAAP Annual Meeting soon. It's been a while since I last attended—back in 2019 in San Diego. I also went to a regional meeting in Denver in October 2021. These meetings are such valuable opportunities to learn and grow, especially with the focus on addiction psychiatry, which is my passion. This year, the registration, airfare, lodging, and dues will total up to $1,605.00. It's quite a sum, especially with our current financial situation. As you know, Daniel’s contract work has been unpredictable since his layoff, and supporting Michael has added to our expenses. I’m applying for assistance through the AAAP's Member Assistance Program to help cover these costs. Balancing everything has been a bit of a challenge lately, but I’m hopeful we’ll get through it.

At Riverside, my work continues to be both challenging and rewarding. Our clinic has seen a surge in patients with substance use disorders, particularly opioids and methamphetamines. I lead a team focused on expanding access to medications for opioid use disorder, and we’re piloting bilingual relapse-prevention programs. It’s important work, but it can be heavy, especially when dealing with patients who face homelessness or have legal issues.

On the home front, Sophie is growing up so fast—she’s already four! Michael seems to be doing better, though managing his care is almost a second job. It's been good having you nearby for support—thank you for all the times you've helped with Sophie. Hopefully, we can plan a day to take the kids to the Denver Zoo or even down to visit Mom and Dad in Colorado Springs. It would be nice to have a break and catch up properly.

Lastly, I’m still in touch with some colleagues from residency, and we're trying to arrange a coffee meetup soon. It's always good to connect and share our experiences in this demanding field. I miss those get-togethers and hope to have more time for them in the future.

Looking forward to hearing from you soon.

Take care,

Alexandra

form_info.title: AI Aspire  AIA Illinois   AIA Illinois 2021 Student Leadership Conference
form_info.extracted_text:
AI Aspire  AIA Illinois  
AIA Illinois 2021 Student Leadership Conference

2021 Application | September 10 11, 2021

Make plans to participate via Zoom.

All applicants must submit a RESUME (one page maximum) and a brief and specific statement of why they would like to attend this event. Feel free to elaborate on your experiences in AIAS and other leadership roles.

NAME: ___________________________________________  DATE: _____________________________________________

Year in School and AIAS Position: ___________________________________________________________________________

Expected Date of Graduation: _______________________  Program:   Undergraduate   Graduate

How long have you been involved in AIAS? _______________

What are your future aspirations after graduation? (brief statement)
_____________________________________________________________________________________________________
_____________________________________________________________________________________________________
_____________________________________________________________________________________________________

Do you have a specific interest in participating in this leadership conference, or is there a specific area that you are most looking forward to attending? If so, what?
_____________________________________________________________________________________________________
_____________________________________________________________________________________________________
_____________________________________________________________________________________________________

Would you be comfortable with s small in-person meet-up with fellow attendees over the summer, if one could safely be arranged?   Yes   No

As best as you might know it, please give an indication of what your school schedule might look like for the fall: 
_____________________________________________________________________________________________________

AIA Illinois welcomes current architecture students who wish to participate in two half days (Friday afternoon and Saturday morning) filled with unique learning and networking opportunities.

Please submit to: Tyler Porter at tporte4@artic.edu and Mary Young at myoung@aiail.org

AIA Illinois                                               aiail.org

applicant_information.name: Jordan Elise Ramirez
applicant_information.year_in_school_and_aias_position: 4th-year B.Arch student, AIAS Illinois State University Chapter Vice President
applicant_information.how_long_have_you_been_involved_in_aias: Actively involved for 3 years (since Fall 2018), leadership roles since Fall 2019

interests_and_goals.future_aspirations_after_graduation_brie:
After graduation in May 2023, I plan to begin my AXP hours at a mid-sized firm in Chicago that focuses on community-centered design, adaptive reuse, and affordable housing. I grew up in Aurora, Illinois, in a neighborhood where many families lived in aging buildings that were not energy efficient and often felt disconnected from public spaces and transit. That experience has shaped my long-term goal: to become a licensed architect who uses design as a tool for equity, especially for working-class and immigrant communities.

In the short term (0–5 years after graduation), my focus will be on:
- Completing my AXP hours and passing the ARE exams as efficiently as possible, with the goal of licensure within 5–7 years.
- Gaining broad project experience, especially in early design phases and community engagement processes, so I can understand how decisions are made before a project is “locked in.”
- Building technical depth in sustainable design strategies, including envelope performance, passive design, and integration of low-cost but high-impact energy upgrades. I am especially interested in retrofit strategies for existing multifamily buildings because that feels directly relevant to the neighborhoods where I grew up.

In the medium term (5–10 years), I see myself taking on more leadership responsibilities within a firm or organization. I want to be the person in the room who can translate between community members, engineers, and clients, making sure that resident voices are not just heard but actually reflected in the final design. I also hope to stay involved in AIAS/AIA, potentially serving in mentorship roles for students and emerging professionals. I have benefited enormously from upperclassmen and practitioners who took the time to critique my portfolio, talk honestly about burnout, and explain the realities of office culture, and I want to pay that forward.

In the long term (10+ years), I aspire to either become a partner at a practice that prioritizes social impact and inclusive design processes or possibly start a small practice or non-profit design collaborative that works directly with community organizations, housing advocates, and local governments. My dream is to work on projects like small-scale infill housing, community centers, and adaptive reuse of vacant buildings into vibrant mixed-use spaces that support local entrepreneurs and cultural activities.

Parallel to practice, I would like to teach part-time—perhaps as an adjunct studio instructor—because I’ve discovered through AIAS that I really enjoy mentoring and facilitating discussions about design ethics and professional responsibility. Ultimately, I want a career that balances design excellence, technical rigor, and community accountability, and I see this leadership conference as one step toward developing the skills and network I need to get there.
interests_and_goals.specific_interest_in_participating_in_co:
I am particularly interested in this conference because it sits at the intersection of leadership, professional practice, and advocacy—three areas that I’ve been exploring informally through AIAS but want to approach in a more structured and intentional way.

First, I’m eager to learn concrete strategies for leading from the middle. As an AIAS chapter vice president, I’m often in a position where I’m not the final decision-maker, but I’m responsible for coordinating between our executive board, general membership, and our faculty advisors. I would love to hear from firm leaders and emerging professionals about how they navigated similar roles early in their careers—how they built credibility, communicated clearly, handled conflict, and advocated for student or junior staff needs without burning bridges. Specific panel discussions or workshops on communication styles, giving and receiving feedback, and managing up/down would be especially valuable.

Second, I’m very interested in any sessions that address equity, diversity, and inclusion in architectural leadership. As a first-generation college student and a Latina in architecture, I’ve sometimes felt like I’m “translating” between worlds—explaining the profession to my family while also explaining my community’s experiences to classmates and faculty. I would like to hear candid conversations about how leaders in Illinois firms are working to make their offices more inclusive, how students can participate in those efforts, and how we can move beyond statements into measurable actions (such as mentorship programs, transparent promotion criteria, and equitable project assignments).

Third, I’m looking forward to the networking opportunities, especially the chance to meet students from other schools across Illinois and practitioners who are willing to share honest stories about their career paths. I’ve only ever attended national AIAS events online, and I sometimes feel disconnected from what’s happening locally. I hope to come away from the conference with:
- At least a few new professional contacts I can follow up with for portfolio reviews and informational interviews.
- A better understanding of the range of firm cultures and project types in Illinois, particularly in Chicago and mid-sized cities.
- Ideas for joint events or collaborations that our AIAS chapter could organize with other schools (for example, a shared portfolio workshop, a multi-school firm crawl, or a virtual panel on licensure and AXP).

Finally, I’m very interested in any content that connects leadership to public engagement and advocacy. My studio work and AIAS involvement have made me curious about how architects can more effectively communicate with non-architects, especially around topics like zoning, housing policy, and climate resilience. If the conference includes discussions about architects’ roles in civic processes—such as serving on commissions, participating in public meetings, or partnering with community groups—I would be excited to learn how students can start preparing for that kind of leadership now. Overall, I see this conference as a chance to broaden my perspective beyond the studio and begin to define what kind of leader I want to be in the profession.

availability_and_preferences.indication_of_fall_school_schedule:
For the Fall 2021 semester, I will be enrolled full-time in the 4th-year B.Arch sequence at Illinois State University. My schedule is fairly intense but predictable, and I’ve already mapped out my studio and coursework to make sure I can commit to professional development opportunities like this conference.

Tentative weekly schedule (subject to minor adjustments once the semester begins):
- Monday:
  - 9:00 AM – 11:00 AM: Building Systems II (lecture)
  - 1:00 PM – 5:00 PM: Comprehensive Design Studio (studio)
  - 6:00 PM – 7:30 PM: AIAS executive board meetings (bi-weekly)
- Tuesday:
  - 10:00 AM – 11:15 AM: Architectural Theory and Criticism
  - 1:30 PM – 3:00 PM: Urban Design Seminar
  - Late afternoon/evening generally reserved for studio work and group meetings.
- Wednesday:
  - 9:00 AM – 11:00 AM: Building Systems II (lab/discussion)
  - 1:00 PM – 5:00 PM: Comprehensive Design Studio
- Thursday:
  - 10:00 AM – 11:15 AM: Architectural Theory and Criticism
  - 1:30 PM – 3:00 PM: Urban Design Seminar
  - 4:00 PM – 5:30 PM: AIAS general body meetings (every other week)
- Friday:
  - 9:00 AM – 12:00 PM: Open studio work time (informal but typically used for desk crits and group coordination)
  - Afternoons generally more flexible; I typically use Friday afternoons for model building, site visits, or catching up on readings.

I also work a part-time campus job (approximately 8–10 hours per week) at the College of Fine Arts fabrication lab, usually scheduled in 2–3 hour blocks on Tuesday and Thursday evenings. I’ve already spoken with my supervisor about my desire to attend professional events, and they are supportive and willing to adjust my shift schedule on specific dates when needed.

Because this conference is scheduled for a Friday afternoon and Saturday morning in September, I anticipate being available for the full duration. On Fridays, I can block out my schedule after 12:00 PM, and on Saturdays I am fully open except for occasional family obligations that I can plan around with advance notice. I am also comfortable participating via Zoom from either my apartment (where I have a reliable internet connection and a quiet workspace) or a reserved study room on campus if needed.

If there are any optional small in-person meet-ups associated with the conference in the Bloomington–Normal or Chicago area, I would be comfortable attending provided that current public health guidelines are followed (masking indoors as required, reasonable distancing, and respect for anyone who prefers to remain virtual). I am fully vaccinated and accustomed to following our university’s COVID protocols on campus. Overall, my fall schedule is busy but manageable, and I am confident I can prioritize and fully engage in the conference activities.

persona.background_context:
This is Jordan Elise Ramirez, a 24‑year‑old Latina emerging professional in architecture who grew up in Aurora, Illinois and now splits her time between Normal and Chicago. She’s in the final stretch of her B.Arch at Illinois State University, on track to graduate in May 2023, and is serving as the AIAS Illinois State University Chapter Vice President. Her days are a careful balance of comprehensive design studio, her part‑time job in the College of Fine Arts fabrication lab, and ongoing chapter planning meetings where she coordinates guest speakers, portfolio reviews, and firm tours. She’s been deeply involved in AIAS since Fall 2018 and moved into leadership in Fall 2019, which has given her a tight circle of friends and mentors across different year levels.

Over the past year, Jordan has been commuting to Chicago on select weekends and school breaks to work as a part‑time intern at Lopez + Kwan Collaborative Architecture, a mid‑sized firm that focuses on community‑centered design, adaptive reuse, and affordable housing—exactly the kind of practice she’s always hoped to join. The firm originally brought her on for a summer position in June 2022 after she cold‑emailed a project architect she met through an AIAS networking event; when the summer ended, they kept her on remotely for 10–15 hours a week during the semester. Her work ranges from building energy model diagrams and material research for retrofit projects in Pilsen, to helping prepare boards and diagrams for community meetings in Little Village. She’s already started logging AXP hours with the goal of licensure within 5–7 years, and she keeps a spreadsheet on her laptop color‑coded by experience area and project.

Jordan currently lives in a modest two‑bedroom apartment on W Willow Street in Normal with her cousin Carolina (“Caro”) Alvarez, a graphic design major. The apartment is walking distance from campus and close enough to the fabrication lab that Jordan can run back home for quick meals between classes and late studio nights. Their living room is a mash‑up of model shelves, plants rescued from end‑of‑semester studio cleanouts, and a folding table that doubles as a pin‑up wall when Caro is working on branding projects. Most weekdays during the semester look similar: Building Systems II or theory classes in the morning, long blocks of studio in the afternoon, then hours in the fabrication lab or studio in the evening. Monday nights are usually reserved for AIAS executive board meetings, and Thursday late afternoons are for general body meetings—Jordan spends Wednesday nights finalizing agendas and slides, often texting with other board members about last‑minute room changes or whether to order pizza to boost attendance.

Her parents, Miguel and Ana Lucia, still live in the same Aurora neighborhood where Jordan grew up, in a brick duplex that has chronic draft issues and an aging boiler that kicks on loudly in the winter. Those childhood experiences of cold bedrooms, high utility bills, and the lack of safe, well‑lit public spaces nearby are a constant reference point for her. She calls home every Sunday evening, usually putting them on speakerphone while she tidies her desk. Her mom asks about sleep and food first, always suspicious of how many meals are just coffee and granola bars, while her dad wants to hear about “the buildings” and whether any of them are near his old delivery routes. Her younger sister Sofia is a high school senior now, talking about possibly going into civil engineering, and Jordan has been helping her with college essays over Google Docs. Their younger brother Daniel, a freshman in high school, mostly pops into calls to show off new soccer cleats or complain about geometry homework.

In the background of everything is Jordan’s steady focus on the future: she’s already researching ARE study materials, bookmarking firms in Chicago that do strong community engagement, and saving every bit of extra money from her fabrication lab job and internship for exam fees and a planned move to Chicago after graduation. She keeps a pinned note on her phone outlining short‑, medium‑, and long‑term goals: finish AXP, pass exams efficiently, and eventually either become a partner at a socially driven practice or start a small collaborative that works with housing advocates and community groups. She’s also intrigued by the idea of teaching part‑time as an adjunct studio instructor once she has some solid professional experience, after discovering through AIAS that she enjoys mentoring, leading discussions about design ethics, and helping younger students navigate burnout and imposter syndrome.

Lately, her main concerns are time and energy. This final academic year is demanding: a comprehensive studio project on adaptive reuse of a vacant warehouse, a capstone research paper on low‑cost retrofit strategies for multifamily housing, ongoing AIAS programming, and her remote responsibilities with Lopez + Kwan. She’s learned to block out Friday afternoons for model building and desk crits, and she guards Saturday mornings for rest or long walks around campus with a podcast about housing policy in her earbuds. She’s fully vaccinated and comfortable with in‑person meetups when protocols are followed, so she occasionally attends small gatherings in Bloomington–Normal or Chicago to stay connected with peers and professionals beyond Zoom. Despite the hectic schedule, Jordan feels an underlying sense of purpose: each class, site visit, and community meeting feels like another step toward becoming the kind of architect she wished her neighborhood had when she was growing up—someone who can translate between residents, engineers, and clients, and make sure working‑class and immigrant communities see their needs and stories reflected in the built environment around them.

secrets.secret_content: Since March 2021, Jordan has been seeing campus counselor Dr. Melissa Chang at the Illinois State University Student Counseling Services in DeGarmo Hall every other Thursday for anxiety and burnout related to studio and AIAS leadership, with a recurring calendar note titled “Dr. Chang – talk about sleep + panic before crits.”
secrets.why_sensitive: Disclosure of ongoing mental health treatment and anxiety symptoms could expose Jordan to stigma from peers, faculty, or employers, potentially affecting how seriously her leadership and reliability are perceived. It could also impact future employment opportunities if shared beyond trusted circles.
secrets.how_disclosed: While explaining why certain late‑afternoon time blocks are not available in the fall schedule, she might email an advisor or AIAS board member, “I can’t meet Thursdays after 3:30 because that’s when I see Dr. Chang at Counseling Services—my anxiety got really bad last semester around studio reviews and I’m trying to stay on top of it.”

secrets.secret_content: During Spring 2021, Jordan developed stress‑related gastritis, confirmed at Advocate Bromenn Medical Center in Normal after a March 2021 ER visit for severe stomach pain; she now keeps prescription omeprazole in her studio locker and avoids all‑nighters because, as she told Caro, “I don’t want another 3 a.m. trip to Bromenn over my stupid stomach.”
secrets.why_sensitive: Revealing a stress‑related medical condition and ER visit could lead others to view her as physically fragile or unable to handle demanding workloads, which might influence internship responsibilities or leadership opportunities. It also exposes private medical history she may not want widely known.
secrets.how_disclosed: When describing why she blocks off Friday mornings for rest in her schedule explanation, she might write to a mentor, “After ending up at Bromenn with stress‑induced gastritis last spring, my doctor told me I have to protect my sleep and not stack late studio nights back‑to‑back.”

secrets.secret_content: Since August 2020, Jordan has been sending $150–$200 a month from her fabrication lab paycheck via Zelle to her mom Ana Lucia to help cover overdue Nicor Gas and ComEd bills at their Aurora duplex, after a winter 2019–2020 shutoff notice scared the family; she tracks these transfers in a spreadsheet tab labeled “family utilities support.”
secrets.why_sensitive: This reveals her family’s financial instability and utility shutoff risk, which could be embarrassing and may lead to stigma or assumptions about her socioeconomic status. It could also pressure her to take on more work or justify schedule constraints to faculty or employers.
secrets.how_disclosed: When explaining why she needs to maintain a minimum number of fabrication lab hours despite a heavy course load, she might tell a close professor or AIAS advisor, “I can’t really cut back that campus job because I send money home every month—last year my parents almost had the gas shut off in Aurora.”

secrets.secret_content: In January 2021, Jordan’s dad Miguel quietly picked up occasional late‑night delivery shifts for an unlicensed courier service in Chicago to pay down medical debt from her mom’s 2019 surgery at Rush Copley Medical Center, something Jordan learned from a worried phone call with her mom titled “Miguel’s side job” in her call log.
secrets.why_sensitive: This exposes her family’s medical debt and her father’s potentially unsafe, under‑the‑table work, which could cause embarrassment, invite judgment, or even legal/insurance complications if widely known. It also highlights financial and health vulnerabilities in her immediate family.
secrets.how_disclosed: While elaborating privately to a mentor about why she’s passionate about stable, affordable housing and benefits, she might say, “Part of why I care so much about equitable practice is watching my dad take sketchy overnight delivery runs in Chicago just to keep up with my mom’s hospital bills from Rush Copley.”

secrets.secret_content: During Summer 2022, Jordan’s project lead at Lopez + Kwan, senior associate Eric Nolan, informally asked her in a June 14, 2022 Teams chat titled “Pilsen retrofit hours” to log 4–5 extra AXP‑eligible hours for a Little Village community meeting she didn’t actually attend, so “we can show more engagement,” which made her uncomfortable but she complied once.
secrets.why_sensitive: This suggests potential falsification of professional experience records and misrepresentation of community engagement, which could damage her reputation, jeopardize licensure documentation, and create legal or ethical issues for both her and the firm if disclosed.
secrets.how_disclosed: In a confidential conversation with a trusted faculty mentor about ethical dilemmas in practice, she might say, “I’m excited about AXP but I already had a weird situation where Eric at Lopez + Kwan told me to log hours for a Little Village meeting I didn’t attend—he messaged me in Teams about ‘rounding up engagement hours’ and I still feel uneasy about it.”

secrets.secret_content: In Fall 2020, Jordan received an academic integrity warning from the Illinois State University School of Architecture after a Building Systems I professor, Dr. Harold Benson, flagged her HVAC load calculation assignment for “excessive similarity” to a classmate’s; the issue was resolved with a written warning and mandatory workshop, but it remains noted in her internal department file.
secrets.why_sensitive: An academic integrity warning can seriously damage perceptions of her honesty and professionalism, potentially affecting recommendations, scholarships, or future academic opportunities if disclosed beyond necessary channels.
secrets.how_disclosed: When privately discussing her leadership growth with an AIAS regional director, she might say, “Part of why I’m so careful about crediting people’s work as VP is because I had a scare with an academic integrity warning in Dr. Benson’s Building Systems class in Fall 2020—our assignments were too similar and it’s still in my file.”

secrets.secret_content: Since October 2020, Jordan has been in a closeted relationship with another architecture student, Mariah Lopez, and only a few close friends know; she keeps a hidden photo album on her phone labeled “studio inspo” that actually contains pictures from their weekend trips to Chicago, because she worries her conservative aunt in Aurora will see anything more obvious when scrolling through her photos at family gatherings.
secrets.why_sensitive: Revealing her sexual orientation and hidden relationship could cause family conflict, affect how she’s treated within her extended family or community, and expose her to bias or gossip among classmates or colleagues if shared without her consent.
secrets.how_disclosed: When emailing a close friend about why attending the leadership conference matters, she might write, “I’m hoping there’s some space to talk about being out—or not—at work. Mariah and I have been together since October 2020 and I still haven’t told most of my family.”

secrets.secret_content: In May 2021, Jordan’s younger brother Daniel was briefly suspended from West Aurora High School for a hallway fight where another student made racist comments about their family’s neighborhood; Jordan has an email from the assistant principal titled “Incident involving Daniel Ramirez – May 6” that she sometimes re‑reads when thinking about equity and safety in schools and public spaces.
secrets.why_sensitive: Sharing her brother’s disciplinary record and the racial harassment he experienced exposes private family matters and could stigmatize him or their neighborhood, as well as retraumatize the family if discussed casually.
secrets.how_disclosed: When explaining to a trusted professor why she’s drawn to designing safer, more inclusive public spaces, she might say, “My brother got suspended after a kid at West Aurora called our block ‘ghetto’ and things escalated—every time I think about school design and public space, I think about that May 6 email from his assistant principal.”

secrets.secret_content: To afford travel to a national AIAS conference in Spring 2022, Jordan quietly put $450 on a Discover student credit card in her name, opened at the ISU Credit Union branch in January 2022, and has been carrying a balance at 21.99% APR, making only minimum payments of $35 a month while hoping to pay it off once she increases her internship hours in Chicago.
secrets.why_sensitive: This reveals personal debt, potential financial strain, and less‑than‑ideal credit management, which could affect how others view her financial responsibility or stability and might influence decisions about stipends, grants, or unpaid opportunities.
secrets.how_disclosed: When asking a close mentor whether a travel stipend is available for another conference, she might admit, “I’m still paying off the Discover card I opened at the ISU Credit Union for the Spring 2022 AIAS trip—I put about $450 on it and the interest is killing me, so I have to be careful about new travel costs.”

Event: Dr. Chang – talk about sleep + panic before crits
Date: Thursday, 2021-09-09
Time: 3:30–4:20 PM
Location: Illinois State University Student Counseling Services, DeGarmo Hall – 3rd floor

Notes:
- This is the every-other-Thursday session with Dr. Melissa Chang. Keep this block sacred; no AIAS meetings, no fab lab shifts. Last spring I kept blowing off anything that wasn’t studio and ended up a mess by midterms.
- Main things to bring up:
  • The way my chest tightens the week before reviews, especially night before desk crits. That one crit in Building Systems where I thought I’d pass out is still stuck in my head.
  • Sleep: still doing the 2 a.m. doom scroll after studio instead of actually resting. Need strategies that are realistic during comps semester.
  • The March 2021 ER trip to Advocate Bromenn for the stomach pain. They called it stress-related gastritis and put me on omeprazole. I keep the bottle in my studio locker now so I don’t forget, but I’m still nervous it’ll flare if I pull another all-nighter.
- Ask her how to set boundaries so I don’t stack late nights back-to-back. She’ll probably remind me again that ending up at Bromenn at 3 a.m. over “stupid stomach” pain is not worth one more section drawing.
- Also want to talk about feeling like I’m “translating” between worlds all the time—family, studio, AIAS. Feels like I’m never fully off.

Reminder: walk over from Willow St. by 3:05, bring water, phone on Do Not Disturb. Text Caro I’ll be back by 4:45 so she doesn’t worry if I go straight to studio after.

artifacts.content:
From: jordan.e.ramirez@gmail.com
To: sofia.ramirez17@gmail.com
Date: 2021-10-03
Subject: hey kiddo + essay thoughts

Hey Sof,

Just finished my Sunday desk clean-up and our speakerphone call with Mom and Dad, so I wanted to send you a longer note before the week gets wild again.

First: I read your draft about wanting to do civil engineering. It’s really good. I like the part where you talk about buses never lining up with practice and how sidewalks just stop in your neighborhood. That’s the kind of detail admissions people actually remember.

I keep thinking about that email from your assistant principal back on May 6 — “Incident involving Daniel Ramirez – May 6.” I still have it starred. The way that kid called our block “ghetto” and then Daniel got suspended after the fight… it’s exactly why I care so much about how schools and public spaces feel. If you want, you could mention that moment in the essay, not in a dramatic way, just as something that made you start noticing which neighborhoods get investment and which don’t.

Things here are busy. Studio is all adaptive reuse this term (vacant warehouse into housing + community space), which makes me think a lot about Aurora. Dad said on the phone he’s been driving past more empty buildings on his delivery routes again. I hate that he’s still picking up those sketchy late-night courier shifts in Chicago to chip away at Mom’s Rush Copley bills from the 2019 surgery. Mom sounded tired tonight when she mentioned “Miguel’s side job,” but she also said the debt is finally moving in the right direction.

I’m okay, just tired. I’m trying not to repeat last spring, when I ended up at Advocate Bromenn in Normal with that stress stomach thing. So: more sleep, less caffeine (in theory), and walks around campus with podcasts about housing nerd stuff.

Send me your next draft when you’re ready. Proud of you.

Love,
Jordan

metadata.subject: hey kiddo + essay thoughts

From: jordan.e.ramirez@gmail.com
To: prof.martin@ilstu.edu
Date: 2022-03-21
Subject: quick check-in + something I’ve been wrestling with

Hi Professor Martin,

Hope your break was at least a little restful. I’m back in Normal after a few days in Aurora and wanted to follow up on our hallway conversation about “gray areas” in practice.

I’ve been really excited about starting AXP through my internship at Lopez + Kwan in Chicago. The work on the Pilsen retrofit and the Little Village community meetings lines up almost perfectly with what I want to do long-term. But something happened last summer that’s been sitting wrong with me.

On June 14, 2022, my project lead there, Eric Nolan (a senior associate), messaged me in a Teams chat called “Pilsen retrofit hours.” He asked me to log 4–5 extra AXP-eligible hours for a Little Village community meeting I didn’t actually attend, “so we can show more engagement.” It was framed like rounding up, and I went along with it once, but I’ve felt uneasy ever since, especially because community engagement is the whole point of the work.

Part of why this hits a nerve is that I already had a scare with academic integrity. In Fall 2020, in Dr. Benson’s Building Systems I class, my HVAC load calc assignment got flagged for “excessive similarity” to a classmate’s. It ended with a written warning and a mandatory workshop, and it’s still in my internal department file. Since then I’ve been almost overcorrecting about credit and honesty.

I’m not asking you to fix anything, I just don’t want to shove this under the rug. If you have time for coffee sometime next week, I’d appreciate your perspective on how to navigate this without burning bridges or compromising my values.

Best,
Jordan

metadata.subject: quick check-in + something I’ve been wrestling with

artifacts.content:
Title: Fall 2022 reality check – time/energy/money
Date: 2022-08-10

Okay, honest inventory before semester starts:

TIME
- Class + comps studio schedule is basically M/W 1–5, T/Th theory + urban seminar, Friday mornings for desk crits.
- Fab lab job has to stay at ~8–10 hours/week. I can’t really cut back because I’ve been sending $150–$200 a month via Zelle to Mom since August 2020 to help with Nicor Gas and ComEd at the Aurora duplex. That winter 2019–2020 shutoff notice scared everyone, and I don’t want them that close to losing heat again.
- Thursdays after 3:30 are blocked for Dr. Chang at Counseling Services in DeGarmo Hall. Anxiety got really bad last semester around studio reviews, and those every-other-Thursday sessions (“Dr. Chang – talk about sleep + panic before crits”) are non-negotiable.

HEALTH
- Remember March 2021: ER at Advocate Bromenn in Normal, stress-induced gastritis. Omeprazole lives in the studio locker now. No back-to-back all-nighters. Protect Friday mornings for rest or quiet model building.

MONEY
- Still carrying the Discover student card balance (~$450) from the Spring 2022 AIAS national trip I put on it. 21.99% APR is brutal, so no new travel unless there’s a stipend.
- Internship hours at Lopez + Kwan: aim for 10–12 hours/week during semester, more over breaks to attack the card and keep sending money home.

BOUNDARIES
- Say no to extra AIAS events if they fall during counseling or family call time.
- One full off-screen block each weekend (walk around campus with a housing podcast, no laptop).

Goal: finish this year proud, not wrecked.

From: jordan.e.ramirez@gmail.com
To: caro.alvarez@ilstu.edu
Date: 2022-11-06
Subject: groceries / family stuff / can we breathe this week?

Caro,

Studio just let out and my brain is oatmeal, but I wanted to sync on life logistics before we both disappear into Illustrator and Rhino.

1. Groceries: Can you grab rice, black beans, eggs, and coffee if you go to Aldi tomorrow? I’ll do a Costco run with Mateo next weekend when I’m in Aurora.

2. Money: I finally looked at my “family utilities support” tab again. Since August 2020 it’s basically the same pattern: $150–$200 a month from my fab lab paycheck going to Mom’s Nicor Gas and ComEd via Zelle. She told me last night they got another “reminder” letter, not as scary as the winter 2019–2020 shutoff notice but enough that my stomach did the thing.

3. Parents: Mom says Dad is still taking those occasional late-night courier runs in Chicago, the unlicensed ones, to chip away at her Rush Copley surgery bills. She called it “Miguel’s side job” again. I hate picturing him driving around at 2 a.m. for under-the-table pay, but I also get it.

4. Daniel: She also mentioned Daniel’s finally back on track after that suspension in May 2021, the hallway fight where the kid called their neighborhood “ghetto.” I still think about that assistant principal email when we talk about “equity in public space” in seminar.

5. Us: I have Dr. Chang this Thursday in DeGarmo (aka cry-about-studio o’clock), so I’ll be home after 5. Can we do a quiet dinner? No guests, no studio people, just us and maybe The Great British Bake Off in the background.

Text me if you think of anything else we need for the apartment. I’ll be home after I finish lasering these stupid façade panels.

Besos,
J

artifacts.content:
From: jordan.e.ramirez@gmail.com
To: mariah.lopez@ilstu.edu
Date: 2023-01-24
Subject: leadership feelings dump before crit

Hey you,

Writing this instead of spiraling before tomorrow’s desk crit.

I’ve been thinking a lot about what kind of “leader” I actually want to be after graduation. Part of it is all the AIAS stuff, part of it is…everything else.

I keep going back to three things:

1) My dad picking up those sketchy overnight courier shifts in Chicago after Mom’s surgery at Rush Copley in 2019. Mom told me in that worried “Miguel’s side job” call in January 2021 that he was doing unlicensed late-night runs to pay down her medical debt. That picture of him driving around at 2 a.m. just to keep the house afloat is burned into my brain when people talk about “benefits” and “stability.”

2) Daniel’s suspension email from May 6, 2021 — “Incident involving Daniel Ramirez – May 6.” He gets racially harassed in a hallway, someone calls our block “ghetto,” and he’s the one who ends up suspended. Every time studio talks about “safe public space” I think about that hallway.

3) My own mess-ups. The academic integrity warning from Fall 2020 in Dr. Benson’s Building Systems class still sits in my file. And then last summer, June 14, 2022, when Eric at Lopez + Kwan messaged me in that “Pilsen retrofit hours” Teams chat and asked me to log 4–5 AXP hours for a Little Village community meeting I didn’t attend “so we can show more engagement.” I went along once and have regretted it since.

All of that is why I care so much about equity, and also why I’m low-key terrified of messing up again. Add on top of that the fact that most of my family still doesn’t know we’ve been together since October 2020, and I feel like I’m constantly editing myself depending on the room.

Anyway, thanks for being the one person I don’t have to translate for.

Love,
J

metadata.subject: leadership feelings dump before crit

artifacts.content:
Hey Caro,

I hope you're doing well! I just wanted to catch up on some exciting news and my plans for the upcoming fall semester. As you know, I'm finishing up my final year in the B.Arch program at Illinois State University, and it's going to be a busy one with my role as the AIAS Chapter Vice President. Since I've been with AIAS for about three years now, moving into leadership roles since Fall 2019, I'm really looking forward to all the opportunities ahead.

I'm thrilled to tell you about a leadership conference happening this September that I'm planning to attend. It's right up my alley, focusing on leadership, professional practice, and advocacy. These are areas I've been exploring ever since I started with AIAS, but this conference promises a more structured approach. It's going to be perfect for me, especially since I'm interested in learning strategies for leading from the middle, which is crucial for my current vice president role. Plus, there will be discussions on equity, diversity, and inclusion in architectural leadership.

After graduation in May 2023, my goal is to start my AXP hours at a firm in Chicago that centers on community-focused projects, just like Lopez + Kwan where I'm interning. Growing up in Aurora, I saw firsthand how much of a difference thoughtful design can make, especially in neighborhoods with aging, inefficient buildings. I want to use architecture as a tool for equity, especially for working-class and immigrant communities. 

For the fall semester, my schedule is quite packed. I'm enrolled full-time, and it looks like it'll be intense but manageable. Here's a quick rundown:
- Mondays: Building Systems II and Comprehensive Design Studio, with AIAS meetings in the evening.
- Tuesdays: Architectural Theory and Criticism, and Urban Design Seminar, plus studio work.
- Wednesdays: More Building Systems II and studio time.
- Thursdays: Theory and Urban Design again, followed by AIAS general body meetings.
- Fridays: Open studio work in the morning, and then flexible afternoons for model building.

Luckily, I've managed to arrange my part-time job at the fabrication lab around all this, so I can attend the conference without any issues. And yes, I'm fully vaccinated and comfortable with attending in-person meetups, as long as health protocols are in place.

We'll need to catch up more once the semester starts! Maybe we can brainstorm some new ideas for our living room setup to better accommodate both our projects. Let me know what you think.

Take care,
Jordan

metadata.subject: Excited About the Upcoming Conference and Fall Plans

demographic_profile.how_did_you_hear_about_naiop_other:
My first exposure to NAIOP was through a panel discussion hosted jointly by the NAIOP Chicago chapter and the UIC Real Estate Club in fall 2024. The event focused on the future of industrial and last‑mile logistics development on Chicago’s South and West Sides. I attended because I’m interested in adaptive reuse of older warehouse buildings and wanted to hear directly from practitioners doing deals in neighborhoods I’m familiar with.

After that event, my program advisor, Professor Laura Jenkins, followed up with an email to a small group of students who had expressed interest in commercial real estate development. She specifically recommended that we look into NAIOP’s student membership because of the access to local chapter programming, mentorship opportunities, and project tours. She also mentioned that several recent graduates in UIC’s MUPP program had credited their NAIOP involvement with helping them land internships at development firms downtown.

I then went to the NAIOP website to read more about the national organization and the Chicago chapter. I spent a good amount of time looking through the event calendar, the Developing Leaders section, and some of the research reports on industrial absorption and office conversion trends. That research component was particularly appealing to me because I’m working on a studio project that involves repositioning an older industrial corridor near Pilsen into a mixed‑use employment district, and some of NAIOP’s white papers provided data points I could reference in my market analysis.

Finally, at a UIC Real Estate Club meeting later in the semester, a recent alum named Daniel Park, who is now a development associate at a mid‑sized Chicago firm, talked about how his NAIOP student membership helped him build a professional network beyond campus. He described how attending monthly breakfast meetings and walking up to people after panels—something that initially felt awkward—eventually led to a summer internship. Hearing that very specific, practical example of how student membership can translate into real‑world opportunities is ultimately what convinced me to apply for membership this year, rather than waiting until after graduation.

answers.value:
My first exposure to NAIOP was through a panel discussion hosted jointly by the NAIOP Chicago chapter and the UIC Real Estate Club in fall 2024. The event focused on the future of industrial and last‑mile logistics development on Chicago’s South and West Sides. I attended because I’m interested in adaptive reuse of older warehouse buildings and wanted to hear directly from practitioners doing deals in neighborhoods I’m familiar with.

After that event, my program advisor, Professor Laura Jenkins, followed up with an email to a small group of students who had expressed interest in commercial real estate development. She specifically recommended that we look into NAIOP’s student membership because of the access to local chapter programming, mentorship opportunities, and project tours. She also mentioned that several recent graduates in UIC’s MUPP program had credited their NAIOP involvement with helping them land internships at development firms downtown.

I then went to the NAIOP website to read more about the national organization and the Chicago chapter. I spent a good amount of time looking through the event calendar, the Developing Leaders section, and some of the research reports on industrial absorption and office conversion trends. That research component was particularly appealing to me because I’m working on a studio project that involves repositioning an older industrial corridor near Pilsen into a mixed‑use employment district, and some of NAIOP’s white papers provided data points I could reference in my market analysis.

Finally, at a UIC Real Estate Club meeting later in the semester, a recent alum named Daniel Park, who is now a development associate at a mid‑sized Chicago firm, talked about how his NAIOP student membership helped him build a professional network beyond campus. He described how attending monthly breakfast meetings and walking up to people after panels—something that initially felt awkward—eventually led to a summer internship. Hearing that very specific, practical example of how student membership can translate into real‑world opportunities is ultimately what convinced me to apply for membership this year, rather than waiting until after graduation.
