persona.occupation: Graduate Research Assistant / Urban Planning Intern
persona.employer: University of Illinois at Chicago (College of Urban Planning & Public Affairs) and part-time intern at Lakeshore Urban Partners, a mid-sized Chicago real estate and planning consultancy
persona.education: Current Master of Urban Planning and Policy (MUPP) student at the University of Illinois at Chicago, concentration in Real Estate and Land Use; expected graduation May 2026. Previously completed a B.A. in Geography and Environmental Studies from Illinois State University in 2021.
family_members: Thomas Reynolds – father
Karen Reynolds – mother
Emily Reynolds – younger sister
Grandma Joan Reynolds – paternal grandmother
Aunt Lisa Donnelly – maternal aunt
Prof. Laura Jenkins – faculty advisor and mentor (not family, but central figure in his life)
persona.background_context:
This is Michael (Mike) Reynolds, a 26‑year‑old master’s student living in a modest one‑bedroom, third‑floor walk‑up on West 34th Street, just a short bike ride from Pilsen and a quick bus ride to the UIC campus. He’s midway through his Master of Urban Planning and Policy at the University of Illinois at Chicago, focusing on real estate and land use, with an expected graduation in May 2026. His days are a mix of classes, studio work, and a graduate research assistantship with a UIC professor who studies industrial corridors and last‑mile logistics on Chicago’s South and West Sides. A couple of afternoons a week, he also puts in paid hours as a planning and market analysis intern at Lakeshore Urban Partners, a mid‑sized real estate consultancy that advises on adaptive reuse and mixed‑use projects.

Mike grew up in a middle‑class suburb west of Chicago, the older of two kids. His dad, Thomas, is a project manager for a commercial HVAC company, and his mom, Karen, works as a nurse at a community hospital. His younger sister, Emily, is finishing her undergraduate degree in communications at DePaul and occasionally crashes on his couch when she’s out late at events downtown. Sunday evenings are often reserved for a family video call—part update session, part gentle interrogation about whether he’s sleeping enough and eating something besides takeout. His grandmother, Joan, recently moved into an assisted living facility in Naperville after a mild stroke in early 2025, and that’s been a low‑level worry in the back of Mike’s mind; he tries to visit her once a month, usually taking the Metra out on a Saturday.

Right now, Mike’s daily life is defined by a careful balancing act. On weekdays, he’s usually up by 6:30 a.m., making coffee in a chipped blue mug and scrolling through real estate and planning newsletters before heading to campus. Two mornings a week, he meets with his research team to go over industrial vacancy data and truck traffic counts for a studio project examining an aging warehouse district not far from where he lives. That same corridor has become a kind of obsession for him—he rides his bike past it deliberately, noting which buildings have new signage, which lots sit empty, and how delivery vans queue up during peak hours. In the afternoons, he either attends seminars or logs into Zoom meetings with colleagues at Lakeshore Urban Partners, where he helps pull demographic and rent comps for clients considering office‑to‑industrial conversions.

Socially, Mike’s life revolves around a small circle of classmates from his program and a few friends he’s made through the UIC Real Estate Club and local professional meetups. About once a month, he attends an early‑morning panel downtown—often about industrial development, last‑mile logistics, or neighborhood redevelopment—and then hustles back to campus for class. Networking still feels a bit awkward, but he pushes himself to introduce himself to at least one new person at every event, inspired by stories from recent alumni who turned those encounters into internships and job offers. On quieter nights, he cooks simple meals—pasta, stir‑fries, or frozen dumplings from a grocery store in Chinatown—and works at his small kitchen table, headphones on, refining pro formas or revising maps for studio.

Financially, he’s careful. His graduate assistantship covers part of his tuition and provides a modest stipend, but he still watches his budget closely. He keeps a no‑frills Visa card for groceries and transit, pays it down diligently, and tracks expenses in a simple spreadsheet his dad helped him set up. Rising rents in the neighborhood are a constant concern; his landlord hinted at an increase when his lease comes up for renewal in August 2026, so Mike is already thinking ahead about whether he might need a roommate or a slightly cheaper place a bit farther from campus. The irony of studying affordable housing and equitable development while worrying about his own housing costs isn’t lost on him.

This year, his main goals are to finish his current studio project strong, secure a summer 2026 internship with a development or investment firm that is active on the South and West Sides, and build a more robust professional network. He often stays after class to chat with his advisor, Professor Laura Jenkins, who has become a key mentor and sounding board for him. Outside of school and work, he carves out time on Saturday mornings to run along the lakefront or explore new coffee shops with Emily when their schedules align. He’s generally healthy but keeps an inhaler on hand for mild exercise‑induced asthma, something he’s managed since high school.

Underneath the busy schedule and long to‑do lists, Mike’s main concern is making sure that the path he’s on—urban planning with a real estate focus—leads to work that feels both financially stable and meaningful. He worries occasionally about graduating into a soft office market and shifting capital flows, but he also feels energized by the possibilities in industrial reuse, last‑mile logistics, and neighborhood‑scale mixed‑use projects. Most nights, as he shuts his laptop around midnight and glances at the city lights from his living room window, he’s tired but quietly optimistic, believing that the relationships he’s building and the projects he’s working on now will set the foundation for the career he’s hoping to build in Chicago.

secrets.secret_content: Since September 2023, Mike has been seeing a UIC Counseling Center therapist, Dr. Hannah Patel, every other Thursday afternoon in Room 312 of the Student Services Building to manage anxiety and occasional panic episodes tied to workload and worries about post‑graduation job prospects. He keeps a recurring Google Calendar event titled “H. Patel – counseling” and once emailed his advisor, subject line: “Need to leave seminar early this Thursday for appointment,” to avoid saying it was therapy.
secrets.why_sensitive: Disclosure of ongoing mental health treatment and panic episodes could expose him to stigma among classmates, faculty, and future employers in a relatively small professional community. It might affect how professors or supervisors at Lakeshore Urban Partners view his reliability or resilience, and he may worry about being perceived as unstable or high‑risk for demanding project roles.
secrets.how_disclosed: While elaborating on how NAIOP events helped him professionally, he might write to Prof. Jenkins or a close classmate: “I’ve actually been talking with Dr. Patel at the UIC Counseling Center since last fall about my anxiety around networking and job searches, and that NAIOP Chicago breakfast in October 2024 was the first event I went to after a session with her.” This could also slip into a reflective email about how he’s pushing himself to attend more NAIOP panels despite anxiety.

secrets.secret_content: In March 2025, Mike took two days off from his research assistantship to help his grandmother, Joan Reynolds, move from her condo in Naperville into the Maple Grove Assisted Living facility after a mild stroke treated at Edward Hospital. He coordinated with his father, Thomas Reynolds, via a shared email thread titled “Grandma Joan – move‑in weekend details,” where they discussed her short‑term memory lapses and concerns about whether Medicare would cover enough of the assisted living costs.
secrets.why_sensitive: Details of his grandmother’s health decline, cognitive issues, and financial worries around her care are private family matters. Sharing them broadly could embarrass his grandmother, strain family relationships, or expose the family’s financial vulnerabilities to people in his academic or professional circles.
secrets.how_disclosed: In a longer narrative about why he’s interested in industrial and mixed‑use development near suburbs like Naperville, he might tell a mentor or classmate: “When my grandma Joan had her stroke in early 2025 and we moved her into Maple Grove, I started paying a lot more attention to how older industrial corridors and nearby services affect seniors’ access to care.” That kind of context could easily appear in an email or personal statement draft he shares for feedback.

secrets.secret_content: Since August 2024, Mike has been carrying about $9,500 in federal student loans from his undergrad at Illinois State plus a new Grad PLUS loan for his MUPP program. In October 2024 he emailed his dad from his UIC account (subject: “Can we go over my loan repayment plan this weekend?”) attaching a spreadsheet that shows he’s been putting part of his Lakeshore Urban Partners paycheck toward credit card debt after briefly maxing his no‑frills Visa on rent and groceries during a gap in his assistantship funding over the summer.
secrets.why_sensitive: Specific loan balances, temporary over‑extension on his credit card, and reliance on family advice for repayment planning expose his financial vulnerability. In a tight‑knit professional field like real estate and planning, this could affect how colleagues or potential employers perceive his stability or negotiating leverage, and could be embarrassing socially if classmates learned he’d briefly maxed his card to cover rent.
secrets.how_disclosed: When explaining why he chose the cheaper NAIOP student membership option or asking a trusted mentor about whether dues are “worth it,” he might write: “I’m already juggling about $9.5k from undergrad plus the new Grad PLUS loan, and I had to lean on my Visa last summer when my assistantship funding lapsed, so I’m trying to be really careful about adding any new expenses.” This kind of detail could appear in a private email to his dad or Prof. Jenkins.

secrets.secret_content: In February 2025, Mike received a Slack message from his supervisor at Lakeshore Urban Partners, Elena Ruiz, about a market analysis memo on an industrial‑to‑last‑mile conversion near Pilsen. He had accidentally included preliminary rent comps that weren’t fully vetted, prompting Elena to DM him: “Let’s talk about the Pilsen comps before we send anything to the client.” They later met in Conference Room B at the firm’s Loop office, where she gently warned him that another similar mistake could jeopardize his chances at a full‑time offer after graduation.
secrets.why_sensitive: An internal performance issue and informal warning from his supervisor could damage his reputation if shared outside the firm or among classmates competing for similar roles. It might make him look careless with data and weaken his standing with faculty who recommend him for internships or NAIOP‑connected opportunities.
secrets.how_disclosed: While explaining to a close friend or mentor how NAIOP events are helping him grow professionally, he might say: “After Elena pulled me aside in February about those Pilsen rent comps, I started going to more NAIOP Chicago panels on industrial leasing to make sure I really understood the market.” This kind of self‑reflective explanation could show up in a draft personal statement or networking email that he later reuses in form answers.

secrets.secret_content: During the Spring 2024 semester, Mike received an Incomplete in his core “Quantitative Methods for Urban Planning” course at UIC after falling behind on a major regression analysis project while juggling his new internship at Lakeshore Urban Partners. He had to meet privately with the instructor, Dr. Kevin Morales, in his office on the 4th floor of CUPPA Hall to negotiate a late‑submission plan and narrowly avoided the Incomplete turning into a failing grade in August 2024.
secrets.why_sensitive: An Incomplete and near‑failing situation in a core quantitative class could undermine how faculty, employers, or scholarship committees perceive his academic strength, especially in a data‑heavy real estate concentration. It may also be embarrassing among peers and could affect future reference letters if widely known.
secrets.how_disclosed: In an email asking Prof. Jenkins for advice about balancing NAIOP involvement with coursework, he might write: “I don’t want a repeat of Spring 2024, when I had to ask Dr. Morales for an Incomplete in Quant Methods because I overcommitted between class and the internship.” A similar explanation could slip into a reflective narrative about his path into real estate and land use.

secrets.secret_content: In November 2022, Mike had a nighttime asthma flare while running along the lakefront and ended up in the emergency department at Rush University Medical Center, where he was treated with a nebulizer and given a new prescription for an albuterol inhaler and a steroid taper. The discharge summary in his MyChart portal flags him as having “moderate persistent asthma,” and his primary care physician, Dr. Angela Kim at UIC Family Medicine, advised him in a March 2023 visit to avoid high‑pollution routes when biking near industrial corridors on the South Side.
secrets.why_sensitive: Details about emergency treatment, the classification of his asthma as “moderate persistent,” and medical advice tied to environmental exposures could be used to question his fitness for demanding fieldwork or travel‑heavy roles. He may worry that employers or colleagues in a physically active, site‑visit‑oriented field will see him as less reliable or higher‑risk, and he may not want classmates to know about a frightening ER visit.
secrets.how_disclosed: In a candid conversation with a classmate about why he sometimes avoids certain site tours or early‑morning runs before NAIOP events, he might say: “After that ER trip to Rush in November 2022, Dr. Kim told me to be careful biking through the heavy truck routes on the South Side because of my asthma.” This detail could also appear in a reflective email to a mentor about how his health shapes his interest in cleaner industrial redevelopment.

secrets.secret_content: In October 2024, after attending a NAIOP Chicago breakfast panel at a Loop hotel, Mike had an awkward networking encounter where a senior developer, Mark Ellison from HarborPoint Capital, made a dismissive comment about “planners who just want to talk equity.” Mike later vented in a Signal chat with two classmates, sending a screenshot of Ellison’s LinkedIn profile and writing, “This is exactly why I get anxious about these events.” He has since quietly avoided talking to Ellison at subsequent NAIOP functions.
secrets.why_sensitive: Sharing screenshots and negative comments about a named senior professional in his local industry could harm Mike’s reputation if circulated, potentially closing doors to internships or jobs. It could be seen as unprofessional gossip or disrespect toward an influential figure in a relatively small Chicago real estate community.
secrets.how_disclosed: When emailing a trusted mentor about his mixed feelings on NAIOP networking, he might write: “After that October 2024 NAIOP breakfast where Mark Ellison basically dismissed my question about equity, I honestly didn’t want to go back for a while.” Similar comments could appear in group texts with classmates recapping events.

secrets.secret_content: In January 2025, Mike’s parents, Thomas and Karen Reynolds, confided during a Sunday evening video call that they had taken out a home equity line of credit on their house in Wheaton to help cover Emily’s tuition at DePaul and some of Grandma Joan’s assisted living costs. His mother asked him not to mention it to Emily yet, saying, “We don’t want her to feel guilty about school,” and Mike later noted in his personal budgeting spreadsheet, “Don’t count on parents for grad school help anymore.”
secrets.why_sensitive: The family’s use of home equity to manage education and eldercare costs is a private financial decision that could expose them as being more financially stretched than they appear. Disclosure could cause tension with Emily, embarrassment for his parents, or affect how others perceive their socioeconomic status, especially in professional or academic contexts focused on real estate and finance.
secrets.how_disclosed: While explaining to a close friend why he’s personally covering NAIOP dues and living expenses, he might say: “My parents just took out a HELOC in January 2025 to help with Emily’s DePaul tuition and Grandma’s place in Naperville, so I really don’t want to ask them for help with my grad stuff.” This could also appear in a candid email to a financial aid counselor or advisor when discussing his need for scholarships.

artifacts.content:
Hi Mom and Dad,

Finally coming up for air long enough to write something longer than a text. This week has been a mash‑up of studio, Lakeshore, and family stuff swirling in the background.

I’ve been thinking a lot about Grandma Joan since that move‑in weekend back in March 2025, when we got her settled at Maple Grove Assisted Living after the stroke at Edward. I keep flashing back to us labeling her dresser drawers and you and I, Dad, whispering in the hallway about her short‑term memory lapses and whether Medicare would actually cover enough of Maple Grove. That whole “are we going to have to sell the condo faster than planned?” conversation is still rattling around in my head.

I know January’s news about the HELOC was a big step too. When you told me on our Sunday video call that you’d taken out a home equity line on the Wheaton house to help with Emily’s DePaul tuition and Grandma’s bills, it kind of hit me that my grad stuff really has to be on my own shoulders now. Don’t worry, I wrote a big “don’t count on parents for grad school help anymore” note in my budget spreadsheet so I stop mentally spending money that doesn’t exist.

I’m managing, just being extra careful. I’m still chipping away at that ~$9,500 from undergrad plus the new Grad PLUS loan. After that gap in assistantship funding last summer when I basically maxed my little no‑frills Visa on rent and groceries, I promised myself I’d never coast on “it’ll work out” again. Lakeshore paychecks are going straight to groceries, Ventra, and knocking the card back down.

On the health front, I’m okay, but I’m more cautious than I used to be. I keep thinking about that ER trip to Rush in November 2022 after the asthma flare on the lakefront. When Dr. Kim at UIC Family Medicine upgraded me to “moderate persistent asthma” and told me in March 2023 to avoid the worst truck routes on the South Side when I bike, it weirdly connected to my planning work—like, I literally feel what bad air does.

Anyway, didn’t mean for this to be a downer. Classes are good, studio’s intense but interesting, and Emily crashed on my couch again last weekend after some DePaul thing downtown. I’ll try to come out to Naperville with you next month to see Grandma.

Love you both,
Mike

metadata.subject: Quick life + money + Grandma update

artifacts.content:
Hi Prof. Jenkins,

Hope your week’s going smoothly. I wanted to give you a quick update and ask for some advice about balancing everything this semester.

Since September 2023 I’ve been seeing a therapist at the UIC Counseling Center, Dr. Hannah Patel, every other Thursday afternoon in Room 312 of the Student Services Building. We’ve been working on anxiety and the occasional panic episodes tied to workload and the “what happens after graduation?” loop that won’t leave my brain. It’s been really helpful, but it does mean I sometimes need to duck out of things a bit early. For example, I’ll need to leave seminar early this Thursday for that appointment.

I keep thinking about spring 2024 and really don’t want a repeat of that Quantitative Methods situation. When I ended up with the Incomplete in “Quantitative Methods for Urban Planning” and had to meet with Dr. Morales in his office on the 4th floor of CUPPA Hall to negotiate the late regression project, it was a wake‑up call about over‑committing between classes and the new Lakeshore internship. I barely avoided that turning into an F last August.

On the work side, Lakeshore has been great overall, but I did get a reality check in February this year. I sent a draft market memo on an industrial‑to‑last‑mile conversion near Pilsen and accidentally included preliminary rent comps that weren’t fully vetted. Elena DM’d me on Slack—“Let’s talk about the Pilsen comps before we send anything to the client”—and then pulled me into Conference Room B at the Loop office. She was kind about it, but she did say another slip like that could jeopardize a full‑time offer down the line.

All of that is to say: I really want to stay involved with NAIOP events and the Real Estate Club without letting things slide again. Do you have any suggestions for how to prioritize which breakfasts/panels are actually worth the 7 a.m. train ride, versus which I can skip without missing too much?

Thanks as always for your guidance,
Mike

metadata.subject: Balancing NAIOP, classes, and Lakeshore

artifacts.content:
Personal budget + life check‑in – 2025-10-01

Money:
- Current loans: about $9,500 from ISU undergrad + Grad PLUS for MUPP. Need to pull exact numbers from servicer before interest capitalization.
- Remember October 2024 freak‑out: emailed Dad (“Can we go over my loan repayment plan this weekend?”) with that spreadsheet showing how I’d briefly maxed the no‑frills Visa on rent + groceries when the assistantship funding lapsed over the summer. Don’t repeat that.
- Parents: after the January 2025 Sunday call when Mom and Dad told me they took out the HELOC on the Wheaton house to help with Emily’s DePaul tuition and Grandma Joan’s Maple Grove costs, I decided: no more leaning on them for grad expenses. Stick to what I wrote in the sheet: “Don’t count on parents for grad school help anymore.”

Family/health:
- Grandma: moved from Naperville condo into Maple Grove Assisted Living in March 2025 after mild stroke at Edward Hospital. I took two days off RA work to help move. Shared email thread with Dad titled “Grandma Joan – move‑in weekend details” has notes about her short‑term memory issues and Medicare coverage worries. Visit at least 1x/month via Metra.
- My asthma: ER trip to Rush in Nov 2022 after nighttime lakefront run = serious. Discharge said “moderate persistent asthma.” Dr. Angela Kim (UIC Family Medicine) in March 2023: avoid heavy‑pollution bike routes, especially truck corridors on South Side. Don’t be macho—keep inhaler in backpack.

Mental/work stuff:
- Counseling with Dr. Hannah Patel at UIC Counseling Center, Room 312 SSB, every other Thursday. Helps with anxiety about networking, NAIOP events, and post‑grad job stuff.
- Remember Elena’s February 2025 warning in Conference Room B at Lakeshore about the Pilsen rent comps. Double‑check data before sending anything. Use it as motivation, not shame.

Action items this month:
1) Update loan spreadsheet with new balances.
2) Set calendar reminders for Maple Grove visits.
3) Map out low‑pollution bike routes to campus and Lakeshore.
4) Pick 2 NAIOP/Real Estate Club events max this month.
5) Sleep more than 6 hours on weeknights.

Hey Emily,

Thanks again for letting me crash your couch last Friday after the Real Estate Club happy hour. My place is still closer to campus, but your DePaul crowd is definitely more fun.

I’ve been thinking about that NAIOP Chicago breakfast I dragged you to last October at the Loop hotel—the one where the senior developer, Mark Ellison from HarborPoint Capital, basically brushed off my question about equity. When he made that “planners who just want to talk equity” comment, it kind of confirmed why I get so anxious at those things. I remember sending that ranty Signal message to Sam and Priya with a screenshot of his LinkedIn and writing, “This is exactly why I get anxious about these events.” Not my most professional moment, but it felt good to vent.

Weirdly, that same week I had a session with Dr. Patel at the UIC Counseling Center (Room 312 in SSB, every other Thursday like clockwork). We ended up talking about how those networking situations trigger the same panic-y feeling I get when I start spiraling about loans and jobs. She’s the one who pushed me to keep going to NAIOP breakfasts instead of avoiding them after the Ellison thing.

Also, I know Mom and Dad asked me not to say anything yet, but I feel like you should know at some point that they took out a home equity line on the Wheaton house back in January to help with your DePaul tuition and some of Grandma Joan’s Maple Grove costs. Mom literally said, “We don’t want her to feel guilty about school,” so please don’t bring it up with them yet—I’m just mentioning it so you understand why I’m extra twitchy about money lately.

Anyway, next time you’re staying late downtown, my couch is open. I’ll even make pasta that isn’t just jarred sauce.

Love,
Mike

metadata.subject: NAIOP weirdness + money stuff

artifacts.content:
Event: H. Patel – counseling
Time: Thursday, 2025-11-13, 3:30–4:20 p.m.
Location: UIC Counseling Center, Student Services Building, Room 312
Guests: (private)

Description/Notes (private):
- Biweekly session with Dr. Hannah Patel about anxiety + occasional panic episodes tied to workload and job worries.
- Remember last time we talked about how that October 2024 NAIOP Chicago breakfast with Mark Ellison at the Loop hotel still lives rent‑free in my head—his crack about “planners who just want to talk equity” made me want to disappear. Bring up how I’ve been avoiding him at later events instead of challenging myself to ask better questions.
- Update her on work: February 2025 conversation with Elena in Conference Room B at Lakeshore after the Pilsen rent comps mistake. She said another error like that could hurt chances at a full‑time offer. Talk about how that’s feeding the perfectionism spiral.
- School: remind myself I survived the Spring 2024 Incomplete in Quant Methods with Dr. Morales on CUPPA 4th floor. It sucked, but I finished the regression project and didn’t fail. Use that as evidence I can recover from screw‑ups.
- Family/financial stress check‑in: parents’ HELOC on the Wheaton house (January 2025) for Emily’s DePaul tuition + Grandma Joan’s Maple Grove costs; my own ~$9.5k undergrad loans + Grad PLUS; last summer’s near‑max on the Visa during the assistantship gap.
- Physical health: mention I’ve been avoiding late‑night lakefront runs since the Nov 2022 ER trip to Rush for the asthma flare. Dr. Angela Kim’s advice about avoiding heavy truck routes on the South Side still feels weirdly at odds with my interest in industrial corridors.

Sam: You still going to that NAIOP industrial panel tomorrow morning?

Mike: Yeah, 7:30 a.m. at that Loop hotel again. I’m trying not to bail even though I keep picturing last October’s breakfast with Mark Ellison.

Sam: The “planners who just want to talk equity” guy?

Mike: Yep. After that I literally sent you and Priya his LinkedIn screenshot on Signal and wrote, “This is exactly why I get anxious about these events.” Not my finest hour lol.

Sam: Honestly, totally fair.

Mike: I’ve actually been unpacking that with Dr. Patel at the UIC Counseling Center. Been seeing her every other Thursday in SSB 312 since Sept 2023 for the whole anxiety/panic thing around workload + networking.

Sam: That’s good you’re talking to someone.

Mike: It helps. Especially after this year—Grandma Joan’s move to Maple Grove Assisted Living in Naperville in March after the stroke at Edward, parents taking out the HELOC in January to help with Emily’s DePaul tuition + Grandma’s bills, my loans piling up… it all kind of blends into one big stress blob.

Sam: Yikes, I didn’t realize your folks did a HELOC.

Mike: Yeah, Sunday night video call special. Mom literally said “don’t tell Emily yet, we don’t want her to feel guilty about school.” So I’m trying to carry my own grad stuff—about $9.5k from ISU plus the new Grad PLUS—and not lean on them.

Sam: No wonder you’re grinding so hard at Lakeshore.

Mike: Speaking of, I’m triple‑checking every data pull now after Elena’s February “let’s talk about the Pilsen comps” chat in Conference Room B. One more mistake and there goes any full‑time offer.

Sam: You’re learning from it though. That counts.

Mike: That’s what Dr. Patel keeps saying. Anyway, if I chicken out tomorrow, text me something mean.

Hi Dad,

Can we set aside some time this weekend to look at my updated loan spreadsheet again? I’ve been revising it since we talked back in October 2024 when I emailed you that first version (subject line “Can we go over my loan repayment plan this weekend?”). That was right after I’d basically maxed my no‑frills Visa on rent and groceries during the assistantship funding gap, and I don’t want to drift back into that territory.

Right now I’m tracking roughly $9,500 from undergrad at ISU plus the Grad PLUS loan for the MUPP program. With Lakeshore hours and the RA stipend, I can cover living expenses, but I want to be more intentional about how much extra I’m throwing at the card versus saving a small cushion for emergencies (like, say, another nighttime asthma flare and ER visit like the one at Rush in November 2022).

Dr. Kim at UIC Family Medicine keeps reminding me that the “moderate persistent asthma” label isn’t the end of the world, but between that, biking around industrial corridors for studio, and the general stress of juggling things, I’d like a little buffer so an unexpected copay doesn’t mean I’m living on instant ramen for a month.

I’m also factoring in the family side of things now that I know about the HELOC you and Mom took out in January 2025 to help with Emily’s DePaul tuition and Grandma Joan’s Maple Grove costs. I wrote a big note to myself—“don’t count on parents for grad school help anymore”—but I’d still really value your advice on payoff order and what a realistic monthly target looks like while I’m still in school.

If you’re free Sunday afternoon, maybe after the usual video call with Mom and Emily, we could screen‑share for 30–40 minutes?

Love,
Mike

metadata.subject: Can we revisit my loan plan?

artifacts.content:
Hey Emily,

Hope you're doing well and not drowning too much in finals! I thought I'd take a break from my endless to-do list and catch you up on a few things. I finally decided to join NAIOP (the big real estate organization I mentioned a while back). My faculty advisor, Prof. Laura Jenkins, recommended it, and I think it’ll be a great way to expand my network, especially with my focus on urban planning and real estate development. I first heard about them at a panel discussion co-hosted by NAIOP Chicago and the UIC Real Estate Club last fall. It was all about industrial and last-mile logistics development on the South and West Sides of Chicago—right up my alley!

After the event, Prof. Jenkins sent out an email suggesting a bunch of us join as student members. She said a few past grads used it to land solid internships. I checked out NAIOP’s website and saw they offer a ton of resources, like reports on industrial absorption trends and office conversions, which will definitely help with my studio project on repositioning that old industrial corridor near Pilsen.

By the way, I signed the membership on December 11th, so it feels official now. It’s kind of funny—I’m always learning about affordable housing and equitable development, yet trying to figure out my own housing situation here on West 34th Street. The landlord hinted at a rent increase come August 2026, so I’m already thinking about maybe finding a roommate or moving a bit farther from campus. Send good vibes my way!

I’ve been keeping busy with my research assistantship at UIC and my internship at Lakeshore Urban Partners. Between all the seminars, studio work, and my runs by the lakefront, I barely have time to breathe. But I’m managing—I even bought some frozen dumplings from Chinatown to make life easier. Oh, and don’t worry, I keep my inhaler with me when I go running.

Hope to catch up soon—maybe a coffee run along the lakefront when we're both free? Let me know how your communications classes are going. Also, say hi to Mom, Dad, and Grandma Joan from me during the next family video call!

Take care,

Michael T. Reynolds

i_proposed_use_permit_request.proposed_use_permit_request_state_in_det:
The applicant, Monterey Coast Roasters, LLC (owner/operator: Daniel Everett Collins), is requesting a Use Permit to allow the operation of a small-batch coffee roasting facility with an accessory tasting room and retail sales area within an existing light-industrial/commercial building located in Pacific Grove. The proposed use will occupy approximately 2,000 square feet of an existing 4,500-square-foot structure that has historically been used for light manufacturing and storage. No exterior additions to the building footprint are proposed at this time; only interior tenant improvements and minor exterior upgrades (signage, repainting, and replacement of existing non-compliant lighting with shielded, dark-sky compliant fixtures) are planned.

The primary use will be the roasting of specialty coffee beans in small batches (5–15 kg per roast), packaging of roasted beans, and wholesale distribution to local cafés, restaurants, and grocery stores within Monterey County and adjacent areas. A secondary, accessory use will include a small on-site tasting bar where customers can sample coffee, purchase brewed beverages to go, and buy packaged coffee beans and related merchandise (e.g., mugs, filters, brewing equipment). Seating will be limited to a small indoor bar and a few stools (no full-service restaurant seating), and there will be no amplified music, live entertainment, or alcohol service.

The roasting operation will utilize one primary gas-fired roaster and one smaller sample roaster. Both units will be professionally installed and vented through a high-efficiency afterburner system designed to minimize odors, smoke, and particulate emissions. The applicant has already consulted with a mechanical engineer and the Monterey Bay Air Resources District to ensure compliance with all applicable air quality regulations and will obtain any necessary air quality permits. Roasting will occur during daytime business hours only, with no overnight roasting. Deliveries of green coffee beans will typically occur once or twice per week via standard box trucks or cargo vans, scheduled during off-peak traffic hours whenever feasible.

The applicant is requesting the Use Permit to: (1) establish the coffee roasting production facility as the primary use; (2) allow limited retail sales and on-site tasting as an accessory use in a zoning district where such combined uses require discretionary approval; and (3) formalize existing parking allocations and loading activities associated with the mixed commercial/industrial building. The proposed use is consistent with the City’s General Plan and zoning designation for light industrial/commercial flex space, and is intended to be a low-intensity, neighborhood-compatible operation.

The applicant has operated a similar but smaller roasting operation in a shared commissary kitchen in Seaside since 2021 and is seeking this Use Permit in Pacific Grove to expand capacity and to be closer to the local customer base, many of whom live and work in Pacific Grove. The business emphasizes sustainable sourcing (direct trade and fair trade coffees), environmentally responsible packaging (compostable or recyclable bags and minimal plastic), and community engagement (monthly public cuppings, educational workshops about coffee origins, and fundraising collaborations with local schools and nonprofits).

Noise impacts are expected to be minimal. The roasters generate a low mechanical hum comparable to a residential clothes dryer when the bay doors are closed. All roasting will occur indoors, with doors and windows closed while the roaster is in operation. There will be no outdoor grinding or packaging. Any grinder equipment for the tasting bar will be located away from shared walls and will be operated only during business hours. Odor control is addressed through the afterburner and high-efficiency filtration system; based on consultation with the equipment manufacturer and other operators, any residual coffee aroma will be faint and transient, similar to that experienced near a typical café.

The applicant is not proposing any late-night hours, amplified entertainment, or alcohol service, and does not anticipate the use generating significant additional traffic beyond what is typical for low-intensity commercial operations. Most retail customers are expected to be local residents who walk, bike, or drive short distances, as well as a modest number of tourists exploring Pacific Grove. The wholesale side of the business will largely operate through scheduled deliveries and online orders, which will not require frequent customer trips to the site.

In summary, this Use Permit request is for a small-scale, craft-oriented coffee roasting facility with an accessory tasting and retail area, designed to be compatible with surrounding uses, environmentally responsible, and supportive of the local community and economy.

ii_operational_details.days_hours_of_operation:
Monday–Friday: 7:00 a.m. – 6:00 p.m. (roasting typically 8:00 a.m. – 4:00 p.m.; tasting bar/retail open 8:00 a.m. – 6:00 p.m.)
Saturday: 8:00 a.m. – 4:00 p.m. (no wholesale deliveries; light roasting only in the morning)
Sunday: 9:00 a.m. – 2:00 p.m. (retail/tasting only; no roasting or deliveries except in rare, pre-approved circumstances)

Roasting operations will be limited to Monday–Saturday daytime hours, with no roasting after 5:00 p.m. on weekdays and no roasting after 1:00 p.m. on Saturdays. There will be no roasting at all on Sundays or City-recognized major holidays (New Year’s Day, Memorial Day, Independence Day, Labor Day, Thanksgiving, Christmas). Staff may occasionally be on-site 30–60 minutes before opening and after closing for cleaning, inventory, and equipment checks, but no production or customer-facing activities will occur outside the stated public hours.
ii_operational_details.what_if_any_retail_services_will_be_prov:
The primary retail component will be a small, on-site coffee bar and packaged coffee sales area occupying approximately 400 square feet at the front of the tenant space. Retail services will include:

1. **Brewed Coffee and Espresso-Based Drinks (To-Go Focused):**
   - Drip coffee, cold brew, and a limited menu of espresso drinks (e.g., espresso, macchiato, cappuccino, latte, Americano, and a small selection of seasonal specialty drinks) prepared on a commercial espresso machine and batch brewers.
   - Drinks will be primarily to-go, served in compostable cups and lids. There will be a small standing bar and 4–6 stools for customers who wish to briefly sit while enjoying their beverage, but there will be no table service or full café-style seating.
   - Non-dairy milk options (such as oat and almond milk) will be available, as well as basic add-ins (sugar, syrups, etc.). There will be no deep frying or full kitchen; food offerings will be limited to pre-packaged baked goods supplied by local bakeries that are delivered daily.

2. **Packaged Coffee Beans and Subscriptions:**
   - Freshly roasted whole-bean coffee in 8 oz, 12 oz, and 2 lb bags, featuring single-origin coffees and seasonal blends. Grinding will be available upon customer request using a commercial grinder located behind the counter.
   - Customers will be able to sign up in person for home delivery or mail-order subscriptions, which will then be fulfilled off-site through a third-party shipping service. Subscription management will be done online, but sign-up assistance will be available at the retail counter.

3. **Coffee Brewing Equipment and Branded Merchandise:**
   - Retail sales of small-scale home brewing equipment such as pour-over drippers, filters, French presses, hand grinders, and digital scales, all geared toward home use rather than commercial or industrial-grade equipment.
   - Branded merchandise including ceramic mugs, reusable tumblers, tote bags, T-shirts, and hats featuring the Monterey Coast Roasters logo and references to Pacific Grove landmarks.

4. **Educational and Community-Oriented Retail Services:**
   - Periodic public coffee tastings (cuppings) where customers can purchase a ticket or reserve a spot (often free or donation-based) to sample several coffees side by side and learn about flavor profiles, origins, and brewing methods.
   - Sales and sign-ups for small-group workshops (e.g., “Intro to Home Brewing,” “Understanding Espresso at Home,” “Coffee and Sustainability 101”), which will typically be held on weekday evenings after regular retail hours or on Saturday mornings before peak traffic.

The retail area is intentionally modest in scale and is designed to complement the primary production use rather than operate as a full-service café or restaurant. The goal is to provide a direct connection between the production side of the business and local customers, encourage education about coffee sourcing and preparation, and offer a neighborhood-friendly place to pick up freshly roasted beans and a quick beverage without creating significant parking or noise impacts.
ii_operational_details.describe_any_other_pertinent_details:
Business Name: Monterey Coast Roasters, LLC
Owner/Operator: Daniel Everett Collins

Daniel Collins is a 38-year-old Pacific Grove resident who has lived in the area for over a decade. He holds a degree in Environmental Studies and previously worked in sustainable agriculture and food systems before transitioning into specialty coffee. Since 2021, he has operated a small roasting operation out of a shared commissary kitchen in Seaside, primarily selling to local farmers’ markets and a handful of cafés. The business has grown to the point where a dedicated production space is needed to increase capacity, improve operational efficiency, and better serve local wholesale accounts.

**Site and Neighborhood Context:**
The proposed tenant space is located in an existing mixed-use/light industrial building that already houses a small woodworking shop and a marine equipment repair business. The immediate surroundings include other low-intensity commercial and light industrial uses, with the nearest residential properties located approximately one block away. The proposed use is consistent with existing activity levels in the area and is not expected to significantly increase traffic, noise, or parking demand beyond what the site and surrounding street network can reasonably accommodate.

**Parking and Access:**
The tenant space has access to a shared on-site parking lot with a total of 14 striped spaces, including one ADA-accessible space, and a clearly marked loading zone at the rear of the building. Based on anticipated staffing levels (typically 2–3 employees on site during normal hours, with a maximum of 4 during overlap or training periods) and the modest retail component, peak parking demand is expected to be well within the available on-site capacity. Wholesale deliveries and shipments will primarily utilize the rear loading zone, minimizing conflicts with customer parking at the front.

Most wholesale deliveries of green coffee beans (in 60–70 kg bags or similar) will arrive once or twice per week via box truck, typically scheduled mid-morning on weekdays to avoid school drop-off and commute peaks. Outgoing wholesale deliveries will be consolidated and typically handled by a single van route once per day, often in the early afternoon.

**Waste Management and Environmental Practices:**
The business will generate typical commercial solid waste streams, including cardboard boxes, paper, coffee chaff (a lightweight byproduct of roasting), and limited amounts of food-related waste from the retail operation. The applicant plans to:
- Use clearly labeled bins for trash, recycling, and compostable material within the tenant space.
- Coordinate with the City’s waste hauler to ensure appropriate service levels and proper sorting of recyclables and organics.
- Partner with a local community garden or composting program to divert coffee chaff and spent grounds from landfill, using them as soil amendments where appropriate.

The roasting equipment will be selected for energy efficiency and compliance with local and state air quality regulations. An afterburner will be installed to reduce smoke and odor emissions, and regular maintenance will be performed to keep equipment running efficiently and safely. All lighting upgrades will use LED fixtures and, where feasible, will include motion sensors in back-of-house areas to reduce energy consumption.

**Noise and Odor Mitigation:**
To minimize potential impacts on neighboring tenants and nearby residents, the following measures will be implemented:
- Roasting operations will be confined to daytime hours only, with no roasting on Sundays or late at night.
- The roaster and afterburner will be located away from shared walls, with vibration-dampening mounts where appropriate.
- The roll-up bay door will remain closed during roasting cycles except when briefly opened for loading/unloading, to help contain operational noise and odors.
- Venting will be directed upward and away from neighboring properties, with filtration and afterburner systems designed to minimize visible emissions and strong odors.

**Community Engagement and Benefits:**
Daniel and his small team are committed to being good neighbors and active participants in the Pacific Grove community. Planned community-oriented efforts include:
- Hosting monthly open-house events where residents can see the roasting equipment, learn about the roasting process, and ask questions about sourcing and sustainability.
- Partnering with local schools and nonprofits to create custom coffee blends where a portion of sales supports fundraising efforts (for example, a “Pacific Grove High School Breakwater Blend” with a donation per bag sold going to school programs).
- Offering discounted coffee to local teachers, first responders, and City staff as a gesture of appreciation.
- Participating in local events such as street fairs, holiday markets, and community festivals, with a portable pour-over bar or mobile espresso cart.

**Staffing and Training:**
Initially, the business will employ 6 people:
- 1 full-time owner/roaster (Daniel Collins).
- 1 full-time production assistant responsible for packaging, labeling, and inventory.
- 2 part-time baristas/tasting room attendants.
- 1 part-time delivery driver/wholesale account support.
- 1 part-time administrative/marketing assistant who may work remotely part of the time.

All staff will receive training on:
- Food safety and cleanliness in the retail/tasting area.
- Safe operation of the roaster and related equipment (for production staff).
- Customer service expectations, including being respectful of neighbors and managing noise (e.g., keeping doors closed during peak roasting times, controlling music volume inside the shop).

**Safety and Compliance:**
The applicant will coordinate with the City’s Building and Fire Departments to ensure that all required safety measures are implemented, including:
- Installation and regular testing of smoke detectors and fire extinguishers.
- Clear marking of exits and maintenance of accessible egress routes.
- Proper storage of any cleaning chemicals away from food and beverage areas.
- Development of an emergency procedures guide for staff, including how to respond to equipment malfunctions, gas odors, or other safety concerns.

In addition, the business will maintain appropriate business licenses, seller’s permits, and health department approvals for the retail and beverage service components. The applicant understands that any significant expansion of the retail area, change in use (such as adding a full kitchen or alcohol service), or substantial modification of hours may require additional City review and approvals.

Overall, the intent is to operate a well-managed, small-scale craft roasting facility that fits comfortably within the existing commercial/industrial fabric of Pacific Grove, provides high-quality local products, and maintains open communication with City staff and neighbors to address any concerns that may arise over time.

Owner/Operator: Daniel Everett Collins

Daniel Collins is a 38-year-old Pacific Grove resident who has lived in the area for over a decade. He holds a degree in Environmental Studies and previously worked in sustainable agriculture and food systems before transitioning into specialty coffee. Since 2021, he has operated a small roasting operation out of a shared commissary kitchen in Seaside, primarily selling to local farmers’ markets and a handful of cafés. The business has grown to the point where a dedicated production space is needed to increase capacity, improve operational efficiency, and better serve local wholesale accounts.

**Site and Neighborhood Context:**
The proposed tenant space is located in an existing mixed-use/light industrial building that already houses a small woodworking shop and a marine equipment repair business. The immediate surroundings include other low-intensity commercial and light industrial uses, with the nearest residential properties located approximately one block away. The proposed use is consistent with existing activity levels in the area and is not expected to significantly increase traffic, noise, or parking demand beyond what the site and surrounding street network can reasonably accommodate.

**Parking and Access:**
The tenant space has access to a shared on-site parking lot with a total of 14 striped spaces, including one ADA-accessible space, and a clearly marked loading zone at the rear of the building. Based on anticipated staffing levels (typically 2–3 employees on site during normal hours, with a maximum of 4 during overlap or training periods) and the modest retail component, peak parking demand is expected to be well within the available on-site capacity. Wholesale deliveries and shipments will primarily utilize the rear loading zone, minimizing conflicts with customer parking at the front.

Most wholesale deliveries of green coffee beans (in 60–70 kg bags or similar) will arrive once or twice per week via box truck, typically scheduled mid-morning on weekdays to avoid school drop-off and commute peaks. Outgoing wholesale deliveries will be consolidated and typically handled by a single van route once per day, often in the early afternoon.

**Waste Management and Environmental Practices:**
The business will generate typical commercial solid waste streams, including cardboard boxes, paper, coffee chaff (a lightweight byproduct of roasting), and limited amounts of food-related waste from the retail operation. The applicant plans to:
- Use clearly labeled bins for trash, recycling, and compostable material within the tenant space.
- Coordinate with the City’s waste hauler to ensure appropriate service levels and proper sorting of recyclables and organics.
- Partner with a local community garden or composting program to divert coffee chaff and spent grounds from landfill, using them as soil amendments where appropriate.

The roasting equipment will be selected for energy efficiency and compliance with local and state air quality regulations. An afterburner will be installed to reduce smoke and odor emissions, and regular maintenance will be performed to keep equipment running efficiently and safely. All lighting upgrades will use LED fixtures and, where feasible, will include motion sensors in back-of-house areas to reduce energy consumption.

**Noise and Odor Mitigation:**
To minimize potential impacts on neighboring tenants and nearby residents, the following measures will be implemented:
- Roasting operations will be confined to daytime hours only, with no roasting on Sundays or late at night.
- The roaster and afterburner will be located away from shared walls, with vibration-dampening mounts where appropriate.
- The roll-up bay door will remain closed during roasting cycles except when briefly opened for loading/unloading, to help contain operational noise and odors.
- Venting will be directed upward and away from neighboring properties, with filtration and afterburner systems designed to minimize visible emissions and strong odors.

**Community Engagement and Benefits:**
Daniel and his small team are committed to being good neighbors and active participants in the Pacific Grove community. Planned community-oriented efforts include:
- Hosting monthly open-house events where residents can see the roasting equipment, learn about the roasting process, and ask questions about sourcing and sustainability.
- Partnering with local schools and nonprofits to create custom coffee blends where a portion of sales supports fundraising efforts (for example, a “Pacific Grove High School Breakwater Blend” with a donation per bag sold going to school programs).
- Offering discounted coffee to local teachers, first responders, and City staff as a gesture of appreciation.
- Participating in local events such as street fairs, holiday markets, and community festivals, with a portable pour-over bar or mobile espresso cart.

**Staffing and Training:**
Initially, the business will employ 6 people:
- 1 full-time owner/roaster (Daniel Collins).
- 1 full-time production assistant responsible for packaging, labeling, and inventory.
- 2 part-time baristas/tasting room attendants.
- 1 part-time delivery driver/wholesale account support.
- 1 part-time administrative/marketing assistant who may work remotely part of the time.

All staff will receive training on:
- Food safety and cleanliness in the retail/tasting area.
- Safe operation of the roaster and related equipment (for production staff).
- Customer service expectations, including being respectful of neighbors and managing noise (e.g., keeping doors closed during peak roasting times, controlling music volume inside the shop).

**Safety and Compliance:**
The applicant will coordinate with the City’s Building and Fire Departments to ensure that all required safety measures are implemented, including:
- Installation and regular testing of smoke detectors and fire extinguishers.
- Clear marking of exits and maintenance of accessible egress routes.
- Proper storage of any cleaning chemicals away from food and beverage areas.
- Development of an emergency procedures guide for staff, including how to respond to equipment malfunctions, gas odors, or other safety concerns.

In addition, the business will maintain appropriate business licenses, seller’s permits, and health department approvals for the retail and beverage service components. The applicant understands that any significant expansion of the retail area, change in use (such as adding a full kitchen or alcohol service), or substantial modification of hours may require additional City review and approvals.

Overall, the intent is to operate a well-managed, small-scale craft roasting facility that fits comfortably within the existing commercial/industrial fabric of Pacific Grove, provides high-quality local products, and maintains open communication with City staff and neighbors to address any concerns that may arise over time.

persona.background_context:
This is Daniel Collins, a 38-year-old small business owner and head roaster who has built his life around the Monterey Peninsula over the past decade. He and his wife Emily, a part-time pediatric occupational therapist at a clinic in Monterey, rent a modest two-bedroom apartment in a 1950s fourplex a few blocks up from the coast in Pacific Grove. Their days are shaped as much by the rhythms of the ocean as by school schedules and roasting batches of coffee. Their son Oliver just started first grade at Forest Grove Elementary in August 2024, and their daughter Maya is in a three-mornings-a-week preschool program run by a local church on Pine Avenue. 

Daniel’s workdays follow a steady pattern. On weekdays, he’s usually up around 5:45 a.m., grinding a test batch from the previous day and brewing a pour-over before quietly packing school lunches at the small galley kitchen counter. He and Emily trade off morning duty: on Mondays, Wednesdays, and Fridays he walks Oliver to school—often cutting down to the Recreational Trail for part of the way if the weather is clear—while Emily loads Maya into the car for preschool drop-off. By 7:30 a.m., he’s typically at the roasting space in a light-industrial pocket of Pacific Grove, checking the day’s wholesale orders on a shared spreadsheet, calibrating the main gas-fired roaster, and coordinating with his production assistant about packaging runs. Roasting usually starts by 8:00 a.m. and runs in planned blocks through early afternoon, with Daniel keeping a close eye on air quality compliance, afterburner temperatures, and roast profiles. In between batches he tastes brews at the small front bar, chats with regulars picking up beans, and handles quick check-ins with local café owners about upcoming deliveries.

Home life for the Collins family is busy but close-knit. Evenings are a mix of practical routines and small rituals: simple dinners built around farmers’ market produce, Lego cities spreading across the living room rug, and bedtime stories that alternate between Oliver’s fascination with space and Maya’s love of animal picture books. Emily works three days a week, so on Tuesdays and Thursdays she’s usually the one shuttling kids to playdates, library story time, or the Monarch Sanctuary, while Daniel stays a bit later at the roastery to finish inventory and prep for the next day. Money is a constant, low-level concern—especially after they replaced their aging Subaru’s transmission in March 2024 and faced a rent increase that summer—but they’ve managed by tightening their budget, taking on a few extra wholesale accounts, and having Emily pick up an occasional Saturday shift at the clinic.

Over the past year, Daniel’s focus has been on carefully growing Monterey Coast Roasters without losing its small, community-centered feel. He’s proud of partnering with two local schools to create fundraising blends and of hosting monthly Saturday morning cuppings that often draw a mix of coffee geeks, curious neighbors, and tourists wandering in from Lighthouse Avenue. At the same time, he’s learning to set boundaries: no roasting on Sundays, and at least one weekend afternoon reserved for family hikes at Point Lobos or tide-pooling with the kids at Asilomar. He worries occasionally about long-term sustainability—both in the environmental sense and in the sense of his own energy levels—but his environmental studies background keeps him focused on incremental improvements: diverting coffee chaff to a community garden, dialing in more efficient roasting curves, and swapping out old lighting for LED fixtures.

Recently, a few meaningful events have shaped his outlook. In late summer 2024, his father had a minor heart scare, prompting Daniel to start running a couple of mornings a week along Ocean View Boulevard and to be more intentional about diet and sleep. In October, his mother finally retired from the Santa Cruz school district and began visiting more often, helping with the kids and quietly urging Daniel and Emily to consider buying a small condo in the area if they can make the numbers work in the next few years. Professionally, the addition of a new wholesale account—a small hotel in Carmel that now serves his coffee in their lobby—has boosted both revenue and his confidence, while also adding pressure to keep operations efficient and reliable.

Day to day, Daniel’s concerns are practical and grounded: making sure the roaster’s maintenance stays on schedule, keeping noise and odors low so neighboring businesses remain happy, planning staff training so his baristas can answer questions about sourcing and brewing, and carving out enough time to read to his kids at night without thinking about green bean inventory. He and Emily talk often about the next five years—whether they’ll be able to stay in Pacific Grove as rents rise, how to balance expanding the business with being present parents, and when it might make sense to hire another full-time employee. For now, he measures success in small markers: a full cupping table on a Saturday morning, a handwritten thank-you note from a school fundraiser, and the quiet satisfaction of locking up the roastery at dusk, stepping out into the cool coastal air, and knowing that tomorrow’s batches are planned and ready to roast.

secrets.secret_content: In November 2024, Daniel and his wife Emily had a 90‑day past‑due balance of $7,800 on their Monterey Coast Roasters business line of credit with Pacific Valley Bank on Lighthouse Avenue in Pacific Grove, after cash flow tightened when a wholesale account delayed payment. Daniel emailed their banker, “Subject: Request for temporary interest‑only payments for Monterey Coast Roasters LOC,” asking for leniency so they could cover rent and payroll while waiting on receivables.
secrets.why_sensitive: Disclosure of serious business debt and past‑due status could damage Daniel’s reputation with the City, landlords, suppliers, and potential partners, and might affect future credit terms or lease negotiations. It could also raise questions about the financial viability of the proposed use permit application.
secrets.how_disclosed: When describing why he needs a dedicated production space and more wholesale capacity, Daniel might over‑explain in an email to a trusted advisor or city staffer, mentioning the specific late payments and the interest‑only arrangement to show how important the expansion is for stabilizing cash flow.

secrets.secret_content: After his father’s heart scare in August 2024, Daniel had his own elevated blood pressure and borderline high cholesterol diagnosed at Community Hospital of the Monterey Peninsula (CHOMP) in Monterey during an October 3, 2024 checkup with Dr. Lauren McKay. He was advised to start lisinopril 10 mg daily and to reduce his 60‑hour workweeks at the roastery. His MyChart message thread is titled, “Question about lisinopril side effects while roasting/working long shifts.”
secrets.why_sensitive: Medical conditions and prescriptions are highly private, and disclosure could lead to stigma, concerns about his fitness to operate equipment, higher insurance scrutiny, or unwanted judgments from neighbors or potential investors about his ability to sustain long business hours.
secrets.how_disclosed: When justifying limited Sunday hours or explaining why roasting stops earlier in the day, Daniel might mention to a city planner, landlord, or colleague that his doctor at CHOMP told him to cut back because of blood pressure issues, referencing the October 2024 visit.

secrets.secret_content: In February 2024, Daniel and Emily had a tense Zoom meeting with their landlord, Mark Jensen of Jensen Properties, about a proposed $350/month rent increase for their apartment at 842 Lighthouse Avenue, Apt 3B. During the call—summarized in Daniel’s notes titled “Rent hike call w/ Mark – 2/12/24”—Emily disclosed that they were one missed paycheck away from having to move in with Daniel’s retired mother, Karen Collins, in Santa Cruz, which deeply embarrassed Daniel.
secrets.why_sensitive: Their near‑housing instability and strained relationship with the landlord could affect how the City, lenders, or partners perceive their stability and reliability. It could also create tension if the landlord or neighbors learned how close they were to moving out or disputing rent increases.
secrets.how_disclosed: When emphasizing the importance of the new roasting space for stabilizing income, Daniel might confide in a business mentor or write in a planning email that the rent increase on Lighthouse Avenue almost forced them to move in with his mother in Santa Cruz after the February 2024 call with Mark Jensen.

secrets.secret_content: In July 2023, Daniel received a formal written warning from the shared commissary kitchen manager, Elena Ruiz, at the Seaside Foodworks facility on Del Monte Boulevard after a neighbor complained about early‑morning roasting noise and coffee odors. The email, subject line “Notice of Policy Violation – Early Roasting Activity 7/11/23,” stated that another complaint could terminate his month‑to‑month agreement.
secrets.why_sensitive: This prior complaint and written warning suggest a history of nuisance issues that could make the City more skeptical about his assurances on noise and odor control in the new Pacific Grove location, potentially jeopardizing the use permit or creating distrust with neighboring tenants.
secrets.how_disclosed: Trying to reassure a planner or nearby business owner, Daniel might mention that he learned his lesson after a July 2023 warning email from the Seaside commissary manager about early‑morning roasting complaints, and that this is why he is stressing afterburners and limited hours in the new application.

secrets.secret_content: In March 2024, Daniel informally paid his production assistant, Tyler Nguyen, cash for several weeks at the Seaside commissary because the business payroll account was overdrawn after the Subaru transmission repair. The text thread with Tyler includes a message from Daniel: “Can I pay you $600 cash this Friday and get you back on payroll by April 15? I’m still waiting on the Carmel hotel invoice to clear.”
secrets.why_sensitive: Off‑the‑books cash payments can raise tax, labor law, and compliance issues. If disclosed, this could harm Daniel’s credibility as an employer, expose him to back‑tax or wage claims, and make regulators more wary of approving his operations.
secrets.how_disclosed: While explaining to a friend or advisor how he plans to grow from one assistant to a six‑person team, Daniel might admit he had to pay Tyler in cash for a few weeks in March 2024 at the Seaside space to keep him from quitting while waiting on the Carmel hotel payment.

secrets.secret_content: In May 2022, Daniel settled an informal dispute with neighboring vendor “Marina Artisan Breads” at the Old Monterey Farmers’ Market after their owner, Carla Jimenez, threatened to report him to the City for operating an unpermitted propane‑powered sample cart. The email thread titled “Re: Propane setup at Old Monterey Market – need to resolve” documents that Daniel agreed to stop using the cart and to cover a portion of Carla’s legal consultation fees to avoid formal complaints.
secrets.why_sensitive: This shows a history of skirting permitting rules and being the subject of a threatened complaint, which could undermine trust with city officials reviewing his current use permit and raise questions about his willingness to comply with regulations.
secrets.how_disclosed: When drafting a personal planning note or talking candidly with a mentor about why he is being meticulous with this use permit, Daniel might reference the May 2022 incident at the Old Monterey Farmers’ Market where Carla almost reported his unpermitted propane cart.

secrets.secret_content: In September 2024, Daniel and Emily met with school counselor Mrs. Angela Perez in Room 12 at Forest Grove Elementary after their son Oliver was repeatedly melting down at pickup on days when Daniel worked late. The counselor’s email, “Meeting recap – Oliver’s afternoon anxiety (9/19/24),” noted that Oliver said, “Daddy’s always at the roaster and I don’t know when he’s coming.”
secrets.why_sensitive: This exposes sensitive information about their child’s emotional struggles and the impact of Daniel’s work hours on family life. Public disclosure could embarrass Oliver, invite judgment of Daniel’s parenting, and complicate school relationships.
secrets.how_disclosed: When explaining to a close friend or in a private note why he insists on no Sunday roasting and earlier evening shutdowns, Daniel might mention the September 2024 meeting with Mrs. Perez about Oliver’s anxiety when he stayed late at the roastery.

secrets.secret_content: In January 2025, Daniel had a confidential one‑hour session with business coach and therapist‑adjacent consultant, Dr. Nina Feld, at her office on Cass Street in Monterey to talk about burnout and feeling “trapped” by the roastery’s growth. His intake form, saved as “Nina Feld – business coaching intake 1‑14‑25,” mentions occasional panic attacks while locking up the Seaside facility at night and fears of “letting the whole community down” if the Pacific Grove expansion fails.
secrets.why_sensitive: Seeking quasi‑therapeutic help for burnout and panic attacks is highly personal. Disclosure could lead to stigma, concerns about his mental stability in operating gas‑fired equipment, or gossip in the tight‑knit local business community.
secrets.how_disclosed: When writing a reflective email to a trusted friend or mentor about why he’s structuring the Pacific Grove operation carefully, Daniel might reference his January 2025 session with Dr. Feld on Cass Street and how panic attacks at the Seaside site pushed him to rethink hours and staffing.

secrets.secret_content: In April 2024, Daniel and Emily applied for a $75,000 SBA 7(a) loan through Monterey Bay Small Business Finance to fund build‑out of the Pacific Grove space. Their loan officer, James Li, requested a detailed “Sources and Uses of Funds” spreadsheet showing that $12,000 would come from cashing out part of Emily’s 403(b) retirement account from the pediatric clinic, a step Emily was deeply uncomfortable with but agreed to in an email titled, “Re: If we have to tap the 403(b)…”
secrets.why_sensitive: Revealing that they are raiding retirement savings to finance the build‑out highlights financial vulnerability and could worry employees, family, or potential partners. It could also affect how conservative underwriters, landlords, or city staff view the long‑term stability of the operation.
secrets.how_disclosed: When describing to a close friend or advisor how they are funding tenant improvements and equipment, Daniel might mention the April 2024 SBA 7(a) application and that Emily reluctantly agreed to cash out part of her 403(b) to cover the gap for the Pacific Grove build‑out.

artifacts.content:
Hey Sam,

Finally sitting down with a mug of the new Guatemala and a very cold leftover chicken nugget from Oliver’s plate, so obviously it’s the perfect time to write you.

You asked how things are *really* going, so here’s the un-Instagram version.

The roastery move toward Pacific Grove has felt like threading a needle with oven mitts on. We’ve been pushing hard on the build-out funding. Back in April 2024 we applied for that $75k SBA 7(a) loan through Monterey Bay Small Business Finance, and James Li (our loan officer) had me put together this absurdly detailed “Sources and Uses of Funds” spreadsheet. The worst part was showing that $12k is coming from cashing out part of Emily’s 403(b) from the pediatric clinic. She sent me this email titled “Re: If we have to tap the 403(b)…” and you could feel how much she hated doing it but did it anyway. I keep thinking, “This better work,” every time I look at that line item.

Cash flow has been a roller coaster. In November 2024 we were 90 days past due on the business line of credit at Pacific Valley Bank on Lighthouse, about $7,800 behind because one of the big wholesale accounts sat on an invoice forever. I actually wrote our banker an email, subject line “Request for temporary interest-only payments for Monterey Coast Roasters LOC,” basically begging for a little breathing room so we could cover rent and payroll. Not my proudest moment, but they gave us a short-term interest-only arrangement, which probably kept me from having a full nervous breakdown.

All of that pressure is part of why I ended up in that one-hour session with the business coach/therapist person, Dr. Nina Feld, on Cass Street back in January. I filled out this intake form (“Nina Feld – business coaching intake 1-14-25”) where I admitted I’d had a couple of panic attacks locking up the Seaside space at night and feeling like if the PG expansion flops I’m “letting the whole community down.” Saying that out loud was… intense, but also a relief.

Day-to-day life is still kids’ lunches, Oliver’s first-grade homework battles, and Maya insisting on wearing her butterfly wings to preschool. Emily’s doing three days a week at the clinic plus the occasional Saturday, which helps, but we’re still in ramen-and-farmers’-market-leftovers mode most nights.

Anyway, that’s the download. How are things in Oakland? Still thinking about coming down for a long weekend? We can bribe you with free coffee and beach time.

Love,
Daniel

metadata.subject: Real update from the caffeine trenches

artifacts.content:
Hi Mom,

Thanks again for coming down last weekend and wrangling Maya while I was at the roastery. The kids are still talking about “Nana’s secret pancake recipe,” which appears to be… more chocolate chips.

I’ve been thinking a lot about what you said about maybe buying a small condo here someday. Part of why I’m pushing to tighten up the roastery operations is so that doesn’t feel like a fantasy. You know how close we were to the edge last year.

Remember that Zoom call with Mark Jensen from Jensen Properties back on 2/12/24? I have a note on my phone titled “Rent hike call w/ Mark – 2/12/24” because it rattled me so much. When he laid out that $350/month increase for 842 Lighthouse, Apt 3B, and Emily blurted out that we were basically one missed paycheck away from having to move in with you in Santa Cruz… I could feel my face burning even through the screen. I know you’d take us in without blinking, but it still felt like failing at being a grown-up.

The business side hasn’t exactly been smooth either. Last March, when the Subaru transmission went, the payroll account was so overdrawn that I ended up paying Tyler in cash for a few weeks at the Seaside commissary. I actually texted him, “Can I pay you $600 cash this Friday and get you back on payroll by April 15? I’m still waiting on the Carmel hotel invoice to clear.” Not exactly model-employer behavior. I’ve been trying to clean that up and make sure everyone is properly on the books now.

The good news is we *are* growing. The Carmel hotel account is steady, the school fundraiser blends are doing well, and people keep asking when the PG tasting room will be fully open. I’m trying to structure things so I’m not working 60-hour weeks forever—more help on deliveries, better systems, that kind of thing.

Anyway, thank you for the gentle nudges and for not making me feel like a disaster when we talk about this stuff. Tell Dad I booked another run along Ocean View for tomorrow morning—his heart scare was the wake-up call I needed too.

Love,
Daniel

metadata.subject: Catching up (and thanks)

artifacts.content:
Title: Why the new PG roastery *has* to run differently

I need this written down where I’ll see it when I’m tempted to say yes to everything.

1. Health
- October 3, 2024 checkup at CHOMP with Dr. Lauren McKay: elevated blood pressure + borderline high cholesterol.
- Started lisinopril 10 mg daily. I literally have a MyChart thread titled “Question about lisinopril side effects while roasting/working long shifts.” That’s not normal for a 38-year-old.
- Dr. McKay told me to cut back from 60-hour weeks at the roastery, especially the late nights.

2. Kids
- September 19, 2024 meeting at Forest Grove Elementary with Mrs. Angela Perez in Room 12 about Oliver’s afternoon meltdowns at pickup.
- Her recap email (“Meeting recap – Oliver’s afternoon anxiety (9/19/24)”) still guts me. Oliver said, “Daddy’s always at the roaster and I don’t know when he’s coming.” That sentence is the reason there is no Sunday roasting and no more 7 p.m. batches.

3. Mental bandwidth
- January 14, 2025 session with Dr. Nina Feld on Cass Street (“Nina Feld – business coaching intake 1-14-25”). I admitted to panic attacks while locking up the Seaside facility, feeling trapped by growth and terrified of “letting the whole community down” if PG fails.
- I don’t want to be the guy who burns out and starts resenting coffee.

4. Boundaries for PG operations
- Roasting: done by 4 p.m. on weekdays, light and *optional* on Saturdays, none on Sundays.
- At least two weeknights where I’m home by 5:30 for dinner, plus one full weekend afternoon for family (Point Lobos, Asilomar, or just Legos).
- Staff up so I’m not doing every delivery and every cupping.

5. Reminder
If I start creeping back toward 60-hour weeks, reread:
- Dr. McKay’s note from CHOMP
- Mrs. Perez’s line about Oliver in Room 12
- Nina’s intake form about panic attacks at Seaside.

None of this is worth it if I’m sick, the kids are anxious, and the business owns me.

metadata.title: Why the new PG roastery has to run differently

artifacts.content:
Hi Elena,

I’ve been meaning to write this for a while, partly to say thank you and partly to let you know what changed after last year.

I still think about that “Notice of Policy Violation – Early Roasting Activity 7/11/23” email you sent me at Seaside Foodworks. At the time I was annoyed and embarrassed, but in hindsight the neighbor’s complaint about the early-morning roasting noise and coffee odors was a gift. Knowing that another complaint could’ve ended my month-to-month agreement there was the first real jolt that I couldn’t just wing it on compliance.

That wasn’t even the first time I’d pushed the line. Remember the Old Monterey Farmers’ Market propane cart mess back in May 2022? The whole “Re: Propane setup at Old Monterey Market – need to resolve” thread with Carla from Marina Artisan Breads is burned into my brain. She was ready to report me for running that unpermitted propane-powered sample cart, and I ended up agreeing to stop using it and even covering part of her legal consultation fees just to keep it from turning into a formal complaint.

Between that, and the noise/odor warning from you, I finally accepted that “ask forgiveness later” doesn’t work when you’re dealing with shared spaces, neighbors, and fire codes.

So, in the new Pacific Grove space, I’m doing it differently: roaster professionally installed with an afterburner, roasting only during daytime hours, bay door closed during cycles, no outdoor grinding or roasting ever. I’ve already talked with the Monterey Bay Air Resources District folks and a mechanical engineer so I’m not guessing about venting this time.

Day-to-day, things are good. We’re still in that light-industrial pocket in PG, neighbors are a woodworking shop and a marine repair place, and I actually like being the boring, low-impact tenant for once. I’m still doing Saturday cuppings and school fundraiser blends, just without propane surprises.

Anyway, thanks again for the tough love back in 2023. It changed how I’m approaching all of this.

Best,
Daniel
