Event: Check-in with James Li re: build-out funding + cash flow

Date/Time: Thursday, 2025-02-13, 9:30–10:30 a.m.
Location: Pacific Valley Bank – Lighthouse Ave branch, PG (James’s office)
Attendees: Daniel Collins, James Li (loan officer)

Notes:
- Bring updated P&L and cash flow projection for Monterey Coast Roasters through Q3.
- Review status of the $75,000 SBA 7(a) loan we started in April 2024 with Monterey Bay Small Business Finance. Confirm remaining disbursements and repayment schedule.
- Be honest about November 2024 hiccup: 90-day past-due balance of $7,800 on the business line of credit here at Pacific Valley Bank. Remind him we emailed “Request for temporary interest-only payments for Monterey Coast Roasters LOC” and that the interest-only period they granted is what kept us current on rent and payroll.
- Explain that part of the build-out money came from Emily cashing out ~$12k of her 403(b) from the pediatric clinic (“Re: If we have to tap the 403(b)…”). Emphasize that we do *not* want to tap retirement again and need the business to carry the rest.
- Update on staffing: currently 6 people (me, production assistant, 2 part-time baristas, part-time driver, part-time admin). Mention that last year, during the March 2024 crunch when the Subaru transmission repair hit, I resorted to paying Tyler $600/week in cash for several weeks at the Seaside commissary while waiting for the Carmel hotel invoice to clear. Make it clear everything is on payroll and above-board now.
- Talk through plan to keep hours manageable (no Sunday roasting, roasting done by late afternoon) so my health doesn’t tank again—mention CHOMP visit and lisinopril briefly if it seems relevant to long-term sustainability.
- Ask James what ratios/targets he wants me watching monthly so we don’t end up back in crisis mode.

artifacts.content:
Thread: Daniel ↔ Emily

2025-01-16 4:42 p.m. – Daniel:
Hey, running a few mins late locking up Seaside. Can you grab Oliver from Forest Grove?

4:43 p.m. – Emily:
Already in the car w/ Maya. We’ll make it. But we need to talk about you being late on Thursdays again.

4:45 p.m. – Daniel:
I know. I keep thinking about that meeting w/ Mrs. Perez in Room 12 back on 9/19/24. Her email (“Meeting recap – Oliver’s afternoon anxiety (9/19/24)”) is still in my head. Him saying “Daddy’s always at the roaster and I don’t know when he’s coming” kills me every time I stay late.

4:47 p.m. – Emily:
Same. That’s why I’m pushing for hard stop times. No more 6:30 p.m. roasts.

4:49 p.m. – Daniel:
Agree. Honestly between that, the CHOMP visit w/ Dr. McKay on 10/3 and starting lisinopril 10 mg, and the panic attacks I told Dr. Nina Feld about in that 1/14/25 session on Cass Street, I can’t pretend the 60-hr weeks are fine anymore.

4:51 p.m. – Emily:
Exactly. Your MyChart msg was literally “Question about lisinopril side effects while roasting/working long shifts.” That’s not a good sign 😂

4:52 p.m. – Daniel:
Dark humor, but true.

4:54 p.m. – Emily:
We’ve come too far to blow this up. We already survived Mark’s $350 rent hike threat last Feb (that Zoom where I told him we were one missed paycheck from moving in w/ your mom in Santa Cruz… still cringe) and the whole 403(b) thing.

4:56 p.m. – Daniel:
Yeah, cashing out part of your 403(b) for the build-out still makes my stomach twist.

4:57 p.m. – Emily:
So: boundaries. No roasting Sundays, done by 4 most weekdays, you home for dinner at least 3 nights. Non-negotiable.

4:59 p.m. – Daniel:
Deal. For real this time.

5:01 p.m. – Emily:
Good. Now get home. Kids want ocean-view night walk if it’s not freezing.

5:02 p.m. – Daniel:
On my way. ❤️

Hey Carla,

Random note from the coffee guy you almost had to lawyer up on a couple of years ago.

I was restocking beans at the Old Monterey Farmers’ Market last week (no propane cart in sight, I promise) and walked past your Marina Artisan Breads stall. It reminded me how much I appreciated the way you handled that whole situation back in May 2022.

At the time, I was stubborn and a little clueless, rolling in with that unpermitted propane-powered sample cart like it was no big deal. When you emailed me in that “Re: Propane setup at Old Monterey Market – need to resolve” thread and made it clear you were ready to report me to the City, I finally realized how far over the line I’d stepped. Agreeing to shut the cart down and cover part of your legal consultation fees was painful in the moment, but it was the right call.

That, plus the July 2023 warning from Elena at Seaside Foodworks (“Notice of Policy Violation – Early Roasting Activity 7/11/23” after the neighbor complained about early-morning noise and coffee odors), has been rattling around in my head as I’ve been setting up the new roasting space in Pacific Grove.

This time around I’m trying to do everything by the book: no propane surprises, roaster installed and vented by pros, afterburner for odor control, roasting only during daytime hours with the bay door closed, no outdoor grinding. I’ve even had conversations with neighboring tenants (wood shop, marine repair) about noise and parking so we stay on good terms from day one.

Anyway, I just wanted to say thanks again for calling me on my nonsense back then instead of going straight to formal complaints. If you ever want to collaborate on a bread + coffee pairing for the market, I’d love to make something cool together—with all the right permits this time.

Hope your season’s going well,
Daniel
Monterey Coast Roasters

metadata.subject: Long overdue thank-you (and hello from PG)

artifacts.content:
Hey Emily,

I wanted to give you an update on how things are going with the new space for Monterey Coast Roasters. I’m really excited about the plans and how everything is coming together. We’ve got the Use Permit application submitted (App Number: UP-2025-0417) for the new location in that light-industrial area of Pacific Grove. The space is perfect—about 2,000 square feet within a 4,500-square-foot building. No major changes to the exterior, just some interior improvements and minor upgrades like signage and making sure the lighting is dark-sky compliant.

The roasting facility will be our primary use, with a cozy little tasting bar where customers can sample our coffees, buy drinks to go, and of course, pick up their favorite beans. I’m keeping the focus on our sustainable practices—compostable cups, environmentally friendly packaging, and partnering with local schools for fundraising blends. We’ll have a small retail section too, featuring brewing equipment and some branded merchandise.

The operations plan is pretty straightforward: Monday to Friday from 7:00 a.m. to 6:00 p.m., roasting mainly in the morning and early afternoon, with the tasting bar open until closing. Saturdays will be lighter, and Sundays will be retail only—no roasting. I’ve made sure we’re in line with all air quality regulations, consulting with a mechanical engineer and the Monterey Bay Air Resources District. The afterburner system will handle emissions and odors effectively.

I think you’ll love the community aspects we’re incorporating. Monthly cuppings, workshops, and collaborations with local nonprofits are all part of the plan. Plus, we’ll continue to divert coffee chaff to local gardens. Noise and odor mitigation measures are in place too, so our neighbors should be happy.

There’s a shared parking area with 14 spaces, which should be more than enough for our needs, given we’ll have up to 4 staff on-site at peak times. We’re avoiding peak traffic for deliveries to minimize disruptions.

I’m really hopeful this move will boost our capacity and efficiency without losing our community-focused vibe. Can’t wait for you and the kids to come by once it’s all set up. I’ll keep you posted on any big developments!

Love,
Daniel

ALASKA TRAVEL INDUSTRY ASSOCIATION

Issue Submission Form

All issues will be brought forth to ATIA's Tourism Policy & Planning Committee for consideration.

3. Brief Description:___________________________________________________________

5. Please list known supporters: ________________________________________________

    by the ATIA board of directors, monitor issue, alert membership, etc.)  
     Support    Opposition    No action at this time - informative only

submitter_information.name: Laura M. Jensen
submitter_information.address: 411 Harbor View Drive, Suite 203, Juneau, AK 99801

issue_details.short_title_of_issue_topic_legislation: Proposed Seasonal Passenger Head Tax Increase for Independent Cruise Excursions in Juneau
issue_details.please_list_known_supporters:
• Northern Lights Adventure Tours, LLC (my company)
• Juneau Small Tour Operators Coalition (informal group of ~18 locally owned companies)
• Several individual Juneau residents who have provided written comments to the CBJ Assembly expressing concern about small business impacts (names on file with CBJ; examples include a local B&B owner and a downtown café operator who rely heavily on independent tour traffic)
• A few independent travel advisors and small agencies that specialize in Alaska itineraries and prefer to book guests with local operators rather than only through cruise line shore excursion desks
• At least two other ATIA member businesses in Juneau (a kayak outfitter and a wildlife photography guide service) who have informally indicated they would support ATIA advocating for a more balanced approach rather than a disproportionate tax on small operators
issue_details.please_list_known_opposition:
• Several members of the City and Borough of Juneau Assembly who have publicly supported the draft ordinance in its current form, emphasizing the need for additional revenue from visitor-related activities to fund infrastructure and mitigate community impacts
• A subset of Juneau residents and neighborhood groups who are primarily focused on reducing visitor numbers and traffic, some of whom have argued in public testimony that any additional costs to visitors or tour providers are justified if they help manage perceived over-tourism
• Representatives from at least one large cruise line who, in stakeholder meetings, have expressed a preference for a structure that places more of the incremental cost burden on independent operators rather than on cruise line–contracted excursions, citing existing contractual rate structures and concerns about onboard price sensitivity
• A few larger tour companies that hold exclusive or primary contracts with cruise lines and have indicated that they can better absorb the cost through volume and negotiated rates, and therefore do not see the current proposal as problematic
issue_details.please_describe_any_fiscal_or_legal_impa:
From a fiscal perspective, our internal analysis suggests that the proposed additional $7 per passenger head tax on independent excursions would reduce our operating margin by approximately 6–8 percentage points on our most popular tours if we attempted to absorb the cost entirely. For example, our standard 4-hour whale watching and glacier viewing tour currently retails at $179 per adult. After paying vessel fuel, maintenance, dock fees, insurance, guide wages (we pay above the local average to attract experienced naturalists), and existing passenger fees, we net roughly 12–15% on that product in a typical season, assuming normal weather and no major cancellations.

If we pass the entire $7 tax directly to guests, our price would need to increase to at least $186–$189 per person once we account for credit card processing fees and commission structures with third-party booking platforms. In our experience, even a $5–$10 increase at this price point can materially affect conversion rates, especially when guests are comparing us against high-volume excursions booked onboard that may already be bundled or discounted by the cruise lines. A 5–10% drop in bookings would have a cascading effect: fewer hours for our seasonal guides, less revenue to invest in vessel upgrades and safety equipment, and reduced capacity to support local vendors (e.g., caterers, fuel suppliers, and transportation providers).

At a business-wide level, we estimate that if this tax is implemented as currently drafted, our annual net income could decline by 20–30%, depending on how elastic demand proves to be. In a worst-case scenario where we both lose volume and feel compelled to absorb part of the tax to remain competitive, we would likely need to:
• Reduce our seasonal staff from 14–18 employees to perhaps 10–12, meaning fewer local jobs for students and young people trying to build experience in the tourism sector;
• Cut back on paid training time and professional development for guides (for example, we currently pay for all of our guides to complete interpretive training and basic wilderness first aid refreshers each spring);
• Delay planned capital investments such as upgrading one of our older vessels to a more fuel-efficient model, which has both economic and environmental implications.

From a legal and compliance standpoint, the draft ordinance would add another layer of reporting and remittance requirements for small operators. As it is, we already track and remit state and local sales taxes, harbor fees, and existing passenger fees. The proposed structure as currently written is somewhat ambiguous about how responsibility for collection and remittance will be divided when a guest books through a third-party agent or online platform versus directly with the operator. There is also some uncertainty about how the gross revenue threshold will be calculated (e.g., based on prior-year tax filings, current-year projections, or multi-year averages), which could create administrative burdens and potential disputes.

Additionally, there may be longer-term legal implications if the tax structure is seen as discriminatory or unfairly targeted at a particular class of businesses (i.e., smaller independent operators) rather than being neutral and tied to actual usage or impact metrics. While I am not an attorney, I have heard informal concerns from other operators that a poorly designed fee structure could invite challenges or require subsequent legal clarification, which would be costly for both the municipality and affected businesses. For that reason, it seems prudent to encourage a more transparent, impact-based approach that is easier to administer and less likely to generate legal disputes down the road.

persona.education: B.A. in Environmental Studies, Western Washington University (2005); various certifications in marine safety and wilderness first aid
family_members: Eric Thomas Jensen – ex-husband, 44, aircraft mechanic for a regional airline in Juneau
Sophie Ann Jensen – daughter, 13, 8th grader at Floyd Dryden Middle School
Caleb Eric Jensen – son, 9, 4th grader at Harborview Elementary School
Karen Louise Miller – mother, 69, retired nurse living in Bellingham, WA
Robert Dean Miller – father, 71, semi-retired carpenter living in Bellingham, WA
Ingrid Jensen – sister-in-law (Eric’s sister), 39, school counselor in Anchorage
persona.background_context:
This is Laura, a 42-year-old small business owner in Juneau who splits her time between the water and a modest two-bedroom apartment overlooking Gastineau Channel. She runs Northern Lights Adventure Tours, a locally owned company that focuses on small-group whale watching, guided hikes, and cultural experiences. Her office mailing address is a suite on Harbor View Drive, but most days in summer she’s either down at the harbor checking on vessels and gear, or shuttling between the dock and her apartment to grab a quick meal before the next tour goes out. She co-parents two kids with her ex-husband Eric, who lives about ten minutes away near the airport, and they’ve worked out a steady routine: the kids are with her Monday through Thursday nights during the school year, then alternate weekends.

Laura’s daily life changes dramatically with the seasons. From May through early September, her alarm goes off at 5:30 a.m. She makes coffee, checks the marine forecast, and scrolls through overnight bookings and cancellations on her laptop at the kitchen table while her daughter Sophie and son Caleb get ready for school. On busy cruise days, she might be coordinating three or four departures: confirming which naturalist is on which vessel, checking that snack orders from a local bakery are correct, and texting her part-time drivers about pickup times at the cruise ship docks. She prides herself on paying above-average wages to her guides—many of them UAS students studying marine biology or environmental science—and she still tries to lead at least one tour a week herself because that’s the part of the job that keeps her connected to why she started the business.

Lately, though, Laura has been preoccupied with the proposed increase in the seasonal passenger head tax for independent cruise excursions. She’s been running numbers late into the night after the kids go to bed, trying to figure out how a $7 per-passenger increase aimed primarily at smaller operators would affect her pricing and staffing. On a typical 10-person whale watching trip, that’s $70 per departure in new fees, on top of fuel, maintenance, insurance, dock fees, and what she already remits in sales and passenger taxes. She knows her tours already cost more than the mass-market options sold on board the ships, and she worries that raising prices further could push some guests back to the big operators. Over the winter, she sat down with a loose coalition of about 18 other small Juneau tour companies—kayak outfitters, photography guides, and a couple of family-run cultural tour businesses—to talk through possible scenarios, from trimming staff hours to delaying long-planned vessel upgrades.

Outside of work, Laura’s life revolves around her kids and a small circle of friends she’s built over the decade she’s lived in Juneau. Sophie is a strong student who recently joined the middle school cross-country team and has started talking about wanting her own camera to take wildlife photos on mom’s boats. Caleb is obsessed with orcas and can rattle off facts about individual whales he’s seen, but he’s also shy and sometimes struggles with the constant influx of summer visitors crowding downtown. On quieter winter afternoons, Laura helps with homework at the kitchen table, then bundles the kids up for walks on the Mendenhall Lake trail or quick trips to the public library. Friday nights, when the kids are with Eric, she might meet a friend for a beer at a downtown brewery or attend a community meeting about tourism and local infrastructure.

In the last couple of years, Laura has tried to carve out more time for her own health and sanity. After a minor back strain in 2023 from hauling coolers and gear, she started going to a low-key yoga class at the local recreation center twice a week during the shoulder seasons. She also adjusted her work schedule so she spends at least one morning a week in the off-season focusing on long-term planning—looking at more fuel-efficient vessel options, researching grants for sustainable tourism initiatives, and exploring how to attract a slightly higher-spending, more independent traveler who might be less price-sensitive. She keeps in close touch with her parents in Bellingham via video calls, especially since her mom had a mild cardiac scare in early 2024, which reminded Laura how far away family really is when you live in Southeast Alaska.

Financially, she’s careful. The business has been profitable, but margins are thin, and she remembers all too vividly the pandemic years when ships stopped coming and she survived mostly on savings, a small emergency loan, and local residents taking discounted tours. That experience makes her especially wary of any new costs that could erode her buffer. If the new tax structure goes through as currently proposed for the 2026 season, she’s bracing for some hard choices: perhaps cutting seasonal staff from 18 to 12, scaling back paid training, or shelving plans to replace one of her older vessels with a more fuel-efficient model. At the same time, she feels a strong commitment to Juneau’s community and environment—she understands residents’ frustration with congestion and infrastructure strain, and she’s determined to stay at the table in local discussions to push for solutions that don’t unintentionally squeeze out the very small, sustainability-minded operators that many visitors come to Alaska hoping to support.

secrets.secret_content: After her minor back strain in August 2023, Laura began seeing physical therapist Mark Delaney at Bartlett Regional Hospital’s outpatient rehab clinic in Juneau every other Thursday morning, and in a March 2024 email to her friend Jenna titled “Might have to cut back a tour slot 😕” she wrote that her PT warned her she’s “one bad lift away from a herniated disc” if she keeps hauling coolers and gear herself on peak cruise days.
secrets.why_sensitive: Detailed medical information about chronic or potentially serious back issues could affect how staff, partners, or insurers perceive her physical capacity to operate boats and manage tours. It might impact workers’ comp or liability insurance rates, and could be used by competitors or city officials to question her ability to safely run her business.
secrets.how_disclosed: While explaining in more detail how the tax increase would force her to cut back on hiring deckhands, she might mention that she has to limit her own heavy lifting per her physical therapist’s advice, referencing specific appointments at Bartlett to justify why she can’t simply “do more herself” to save money.

secrets.secret_content: In February 2024, Laura flew to Bellingham to accompany her mother, Karen Miller, to a follow‑up cardiology appointment with Dr. Anita Chow at PeaceHealth St. Joseph Medical Center after Karen’s mild cardiac scare; in a text thread titled “Summer schedule + Mom’s heart stuff” with her sister‑in‑law Ingrid, she admitted she’s been losing sleep worrying about “what happens if Mom has another episode right in the middle of July cruise madness.”
secrets.why_sensitive: Her mother’s specific cardiac condition and follow‑up care are private health details that Karen might not want shared widely in a professional or public policy context. It could also expose family vulnerabilities and caregiving burdens that might be exploited (e.g., by creditors or in any future legal disputes about availability or reliability).
secrets.how_disclosed: When drafting a longer narrative about how thinly stretched small operators are, Laura might over‑explain her personal constraints, mentioning her mom’s cardiology follow‑ups in Bellingham and the emotional strain as part of why sudden new costs and travel demands are especially hard to absorb.

secrets.secret_content: During the 2020 pandemic shutdown, Laura took out a $75,000 SBA Economic Injury Disaster Loan through Northrim Bank’s Juneau branch, and in an October 2023 email to her accountant titled “Re: EIDL balance + vessel upgrade timing,” she acknowledged she is still carrying a $38,000 balance and deferred one quarterly payment to cover emergency engine repairs on her older tour vessel Aurora Spirit.
secrets.why_sensitive: Specific loan amounts, remaining balances, and deferred payments expose the business’s leverage and cash‑flow vulnerabilities. If widely known, this could weaken her negotiating position with vendors, lenders, or potential partners, and might undermine confidence among staff or community stakeholders in the stability of her company.
secrets.how_disclosed: While illustrating how thin her margins are in a comment or supporting memo, she might mention still paying down the SBA EIDL and having to postpone a payment after an engine failure, to show why another $7 per passenger tax feels untenable.

secrets.secret_content: In a private October 2024 budgeting spreadsheet labeled “2025 worst‑case staffing cuts,” Laura noted that if the head tax passes unchanged she may have to reduce her own draw from Northern Lights Adventure Tours to $2,000 per month for the 2026 season and rely on a personal line of credit at Alaska USA Federal Credit Union to cover rent on her Harbor View Drive apartment and her daughter Sophie’s orthodontist payments at Juneau Family Orthodontics.
secrets.why_sensitive: Details of her low personal income draw and reliance on credit to meet basic living expenses and her child’s medical bills reveal financial strain that could affect her perceived credibility as a business owner and landlord/tenant. It could also be used against her in any future credit applications or negotiations, or create embarrassment if staff learned how precarious her finances are.
secrets.how_disclosed: In a longer narrative to ATIA staff or a sympathetic Assembly member, she might add a personal note about possibly cutting her own pay and dipping into a line of credit to keep staff employed, including reference to specific bills like Sophie’s braces, to make the fiscal impact feel concrete.

secrets.secret_content: During a mediated session at the Juneau Community Justice Center in November 2021, Laura and her ex‑husband Eric renegotiated their parenting plan after a conflict over Eric missing two consecutive Thursday pickups due to overtime at Alaska Seaplane Maintenance; in a December 2021 email to her attorney, Megan Ross at Ross & Caldwell, titled “Re: revised custody calendar,” Laura expressed concern that Eric had shown up smelling of alcohol at one Harborview Elementary pickup that fall.
secrets.why_sensitive: Allegations about Eric’s possible alcohol use around custody exchanges and the existence of a mediated revision to their parenting plan are highly sensitive family and legal matters. Disclosure could damage Eric’s reputation and employment, inflame co‑parenting tensions, and potentially surface in any future custody disputes or legal proceedings.
secrets.how_disclosed: When venting in a side email to a trusted ATIA board contact or a friend about why she can’t easily adjust her tour schedule to late evenings (to absorb higher taxes with more volume), she might reference the strict custody calendar they had to formalize after issues with Eric’s reliability at school pickups.

secrets.secret_content: In a May 2024 email to Sophie’s school counselor, Dana Carlson, at Floyd Dryden Middle School titled “Sophie missing practice + tourism meetings,” Laura disclosed that Sophie has been seeing therapist Rachel Kim at Juneau Youth Services twice a month since January 2024 for anxiety tied to loud crowds downtown and worries about “Mom losing the boat business if the city keeps raising fees.”
secrets.why_sensitive: A child’s mental health treatment and specific anxieties linked to family finances and public policy debates are deeply private. Public disclosure could stigmatize Sophie at school, strain her relationship with her parents, and expose the family’s emotional vulnerabilities to community members involved in the tourism debate.
secrets.how_disclosed: While trying to humanize the impact of the proposed tax in a letter of support or testimony, Laura might be tempted to mention how the situation has affected her daughter’s anxiety and counseling sessions, including the counselor’s name and the connection to tourism politics.

secrets.secret_content: In a confidential March 2024 Zoom call with other members of the Juneau Small Tour Operators Coalition, summarized in her personal notes titled “Coalition – contingency ideas,” Laura admitted that if the ordinance passes unchanged she is considering selling a 40% stake in Northern Lights Adventure Tours to Anchorage‑based investor Michael Han to inject capital for a new fuel‑efficient vessel, even though she worries this could eventually pressure her into signing an exclusive contract with a major cruise line she’s publicly criticized.
secrets.why_sensitive: Plans to sell a significant minority stake and the identity of a potential investor could alarm staff, unsettle community partners who value her independence, and weaken her bargaining position with that investor. It could also be politically awkward given her public stance favoring small, independent operations over large cruise‑line‑aligned entities.
secrets.how_disclosed: In a detailed background email to an ATIA policy staffer explaining how serious the situation is, she might mention that she’s already talking with Michael Han about selling part of the company, to underscore the gravity of the proposed tax for small operators.

secrets.secret_content: After a near‑miss docking incident involving a junior captain bumping a piling at the AJ Dock in July 2022, Laura received a warning letter from her marine insurance carrier, Pacific Marine Underwriters, requiring additional paid training and a temporary reduction in passenger capacity on the vessel Taku Spirit; in an internal memo to staff titled “Insurance compliance – PLEASE READ,” she emphasized they “cannot afford another claim on our record” before renewal in 2025.
secrets.why_sensitive: Prior safety incidents and insurance warnings can damage the company’s reputation, affect future insurance premiums, and be used by competitors or regulators to question her safety standards. If associated with her advocacy on tax policy, it might be spun as evidence that she is financially and operationally fragile for reasons beyond the proposed tax.
secrets.how_disclosed: While explaining why additional regulatory and reporting burdens in the ordinance are hard to manage, she might reference the costly extra training and capacity limits imposed by her insurer after the 2022 incident to show how thin her margins already are.

secrets.secret_content: In August 2023, Laura received a notice of informal inquiry from the CBJ Sales Tax Office about a discrepancy in her 2022 seasonal passenger fee remittances related to bookings processed through the online platform ShoreTripPro; in an email to her bookkeeper, Alan Perez, titled “CBJ audit question – ShoreTripPro commissions,” she worried that unclear rules about who is the “seller of record” might lead to back‑owed fees and penalties of “up to $4–5K if they decide we calculated wrong.”
secrets.why_sensitive: Ongoing or recent tax inquiries can raise questions about her compliance and financial management. Public knowledge of a potential underpayment and possible penalties could harm her credibility when arguing about the fairness of new taxes and might be used by opponents to suggest she is resisting oversight or trying to avoid paying her share.
secrets.how_disclosed: In a detailed explanation to ATIA about the ordinance’s ambiguous remittance provisions, she might mention her recent back‑and‑forth with the CBJ Sales Tax Office over ShoreTripPro bookings as a concrete example, including the estimated penalty risk.

secrets.secret_content: On several Friday nights in early 2024, when the kids were with Eric, Laura met a local Assembly member, David McCormick, at Devil’s Club Brewing Company to discuss tourism policy, and in a March 29, 2024 text to her friend Jenna titled “This is getting messy,” she admitted the conversations had “drifted past policy” and that she was worried people might think she was getting preferential treatment on the head‑tax issue because of a brief romantic involvement.
secrets.why_sensitive: A personal relationship with a local policymaker directly involved in the ordinance debate could create perceived conflicts of interest, accusations of undue influence, and reputational damage for both Laura and the Assembly member. It could undermine the legitimacy of her advocacy and strain co‑parenting or friendships if it became public gossip in a small community.
secrets.how_disclosed: In a candid email or late‑night message to a trusted coalition member explaining why she’s hesitant to approach Assembly member McCormick publicly on the issue, she might reveal the romantic aspect of their prior meetings at Devil’s Club Brewing and her fear of it being misinterpreted.

artifacts.content:
Hi Jenna,

Kids are finally in bed and I’m icing my back on the couch, so it seems like a good time to actually answer your last message instead of just heart‑reacting it.

I saw Mark Delaney at Bartlett Regional’s outpatient rehab clinic again this morning (every other Thursday like clockwork). He was pretty blunt this time – said I’m “one bad lift away from a herniated disc” if I keep hauling coolers and gear myself on those peak cruise days. So, yeah, your subject line from March – “Might have to cut back a tour slot 😕” – is starting to feel less hypothetical and more like doctor’s orders.

The timing just feels brutal with this proposed extra $7 per passenger head tax hanging over us for 2026. I already ran the numbers on a 10‑person whale watch – that’s $70 a departure before we even talk fuel, dock, insurance, and the usual passenger fees. I can’t just “do more myself” to make up the difference when Mark’s telling me to stop pretending I’m still 28 and bulletproof.

On top of that, Mom’s heart stuff is still in the back of my mind. I keep flashing back to February, flying down to Bellingham and sitting with her and Dr. Anita Chow at PeaceHealth St. Joseph’s for that follow‑up cardiology appointment after her little cardiac scare. She keeps saying she’s fine, but I’m the one lying awake at 3 a.m. wondering, “What happens if Mom has another episode right in the middle of July cruise madness?” There’s no easy way to disappear from Juneau for a week in peak season.

Anyway, enough doom. Sophie’s cross‑country meet is Saturday at Savikko, and Caleb is insisting we make orca cupcakes afterward (no idea how to pull that off). If you’re free Friday, kids are with Eric and I could definitely use a beer at Devil’s Club or a lazy movie night.

Thanks for always letting me vent about boats and backs and parents.

Love,
Laura

metadata.subject: Back, boats, and everything in between

artifacts.content:
Hi Alan,

Catching up on a quiet, rainy Sunday and wanted to circle back on a few money things before we get swallowed by early bookings.

First, can you send me an updated amortization on the SBA Economic Injury Disaster Loan I took through Northrim back in 2020? Last time we talked (your October 2023 email, “Re: EIDL balance + vessel upgrade timing”), we were sitting at about a $38,000 balance after I deferred that one quarterly payment to cover the emergency engine repairs on Aurora Spirit. I just want to see where we’ll be by end of 2025 if I keep paying at the current rate versus accelerating a bit.

Second, I’m still stewing on the proposed seasonal passenger head tax increase. If that extra $7 per cruise passenger on independent excursions actually lands the way CBJ is talking, my quick math says we’re looking at a 6–8 point hit to margins on our standard $179 whale/glacier tour unless we bump prices to the $186–$189 range. That’s before we factor in ShoreTripPro’s commission and the credit card fees.

Speaking of ShoreTripPro, I’m still a little rattled from that informal inquiry from the CBJ Sales Tax Office last August about our 2022 seasonal passenger fee remittances. The whole “who is the seller of record” question on those platform bookings is still fuzzy to me. In my email to you then (“CBJ audit question – ShoreTripPro commissions”), I think I estimated we could be on the hook for $4–5K in back‑owed fees and penalties if they decided we calculated wrong. Have you heard anything more definitive from them, or should we assume that risk is still hanging out there?

Given all of the above, I’m trying to decide whether it’s insane to keep the 2026 vessel upgrade for Taku Spirit on the table. Between the EIDL balance, potential CBJ penalties, and the new head tax, I don’t want to get upside‑down.

Happy to swing by your office later this month if a face‑to‑face is easier.

Thanks,
Laura

metadata.subject: EIDL balance, CBJ inquiry, and head tax math

artifacts.content:
Title: 2025–2026 worst‑case planning – do not share

1. Personal draw / household
- If the head tax passes in current form for 2026, assume 10–15% drop in volume + margin compression.
- Worst‑case spreadsheet from October 2024 (“2025 worst‑case staffing cuts”) shows me dropping my own draw to $2,000/month for the 2026 season.
- Would need to lean on personal line of credit at Alaska USA Federal Credit Union to cover:
  • Rent on 411 Harbor View Drive, Apt 4B
  • Sophie’s orthodontist payments at Juneau Family Orthodontics (ask them about stretching payment plan if needed).
- Emotional note: this is not sustainable long‑term, but maybe survivable for one brutal season if it keeps staff employed.

2. Staffing / ownership
- Reduce seasonal staff from 18 to ~12 guides/deckhands.
- Cut paid training hours but keep minimum safety/interpretive standards.
- Revisit March 2024 coalition notes (“Coalition – contingency ideas”):
  • Serious conversation with Michael Han (Anchorage) about selling up to a 40% stake in Northern Lights Adventure Tours.
  • Upside: capital for new fuel‑efficient vessel.
  • Downside: pressure to sign exclusive contract with a major cruise line I’ve been publicly criticizing. Could change the culture of the company.

3. Insurance / risk
- Remember July 2022 near‑miss at AJ Dock (junior captain bumped piling with Taku Spirit).
- Pacific Marine Underwriters warning letter required:
  • Extra paid training
  • Temporary passenger capacity reduction on Taku Spirit
- Internal memo to staff at the time: “Insurance compliance – PLEASE READ” – we truly cannot afford another claim before 2025 renewal.
- New taxes + any future incident = no cushion.

4. Action items
- Update worst‑case spreadsheet quarterly.
- Quietly sound out Michael re: terms that don’t force cruise‑line exclusivity.
- Build in extra training days pre‑season to avoid any repeat of 2022.
- Keep this note for my eyes only.

Hi Ingrid,

Thanks again for talking me off the ledge on Sunday. I figured I’d write everything down while it’s still fresh in my head.

I keep thinking back to that November 2021 mediated session at the Juneau Community Justice Center, when we had to drag in the calendar and renegotiate the parenting plan because Eric missed those two Thursday pickups after overtime at Alaska Seaplane Maintenance. Then that Harborview pickup where he showed up smelling like alcohol… I still remember typing that December 2021 email to Megan Ross at Ross & Caldwell (“Re: revised custody calendar”) with my hands shaking. I don’t want to go back to that level of conflict.

The structure we landed on mostly works now – kids with me Monday–Thursday nights during the school year, alternating weekends – but it also means I can’t just add late evening tours to chase revenue if this new head tax goes through. There’s not much flexibility baked in.

Layer on top of that Mom’s health. Flying down to Bellingham in February to sit with her and Dr. Anita Chow at PeaceHealth St. Joseph for that follow‑up cardiology appointment really rattled me more than I let on. I’ve been losing sleep wondering what happens if she has another episode in the middle of July when we’re running three departures a day.

And then there’s Sophie. Since January she’s been seeing Rachel Kim at Juneau Youth Services twice a month for anxiety – mostly about the downtown crowds and, honestly, about “Mom losing the boat business if the city keeps raising fees.” I even emailed her school counselor, Dana Carlson, in May (“Sophie missing practice + tourism meetings”) to explain why she was skipping cross‑country some afternoons when I had to be at tourism meetings.

I feel like the head‑tax debate has seeped into every corner of our lives: custody logistics, my mom’s cardiology follow‑ups, Sophie’s counseling sessions. Meanwhile, Caleb just wants more time with orcas.

Anyway, thank you for listening and for not judging Eric when I vent. The kids adore their Aunt Ingrid, and so do I.

Love,
Laura

metadata.subject: All the moving pieces

artifacts.content:
Event: Mom cardiology follow‑up – Bellingham

Date: 2024-02-22
Time: 10:30 a.m. – 1:30 p.m. (Pacific)
Location: PeaceHealth St. Joseph Medical Center, Cardiology Clinic – with Dr. Anita Chow
Attendees: Me, Mom (Karen Miller)

Notes:
- Fly SEA > BLI night before, stay at Mom and Dad’s.
- Bring list of Mom’s meds + notes from her mild cardiac scare last fall.
- Questions for Dr. Chow:
  • How likely is another episode in next 1–2 years?
  • Any restrictions that would make it hard for Mom to fly to Juneau in summer?
  • Warning signs Dad should watch for.

Tourism/work tie‑ins:
- If prognosis is uncertain, I need to plan for possibility of another emergency trip mid‑season (July/August) when cruise traffic is insane.
- Factor into 2025–2026 staffing plans – can’t be the only one who can cover every departure.

Personal:
- I’ve been losing sleep over this, especially with the head‑tax mess brewing and how thinly stretched I already feel.
- Block off time after appointment to walk the waterfront with Mom if she’s up for it.
- Call Sophie and Caleb after we’re done; reassure them Nana is okay.

Reminder: check in with Ingrid later about how much she can help if I need to duck out of Juneau for a few days mid‑season.

metadata.title: Mom cardiology follow‑up – Bellingham
metadata.location: PeaceHealth St. Joseph Medical Center, Bellingham, WA

artifacts.content:
Thread: “Summer schedule + Mom’s heart stuff”

Ingrid: You got the draft cruise calendar yet? Trying to figure out when to visit you vs. when to hide in Anchorage 😂

Me: Lol. Same. Just got the tentative schedule and it’s wall‑to‑wall ships mid‑July.

Me: Honestly I’m more stressed about Mom than the ships right now.

Ingrid: How did the PeaceHealth appointment go? You never really said.

Me: February trip was rough. Sitting in that exam room at St. Joseph’s with Dr. Chow talking about “mild cardiac events” while I’m mentally counting how many July departures I can cancel if something happens again.

Me: I keep thinking, what if Mom has another episode right in the middle of July cruise madness? I can’t just walk away from the harbor for a week.

Ingrid: You’d walk away. We’d figure it out.

Me: Maybe. But between the proposed head tax, the EIDL I’m still paying down, and that CBJ Sales Tax Office inquiry last August about the ShoreTripPro remittances (remember, I told you it could be like $4–5K if they decide I calculated wrong), the idea of canceling tours makes my stomach hurt.

Ingrid: Ugh. That’s a lot.

Me: And then there’s Sophie. She’s still seeing Rachel Kim at Juneau Youth Services twice a month. Her big fear is literally “Mom losing the boat business if the city keeps raising fees.”

Me: I hate that she even knows what a passenger head tax is.

Ingrid: She also knows her mom is stubborn as hell and takes care of people.

Me: Trying. Right now I just feel like I’m juggling Nana’s heart, city politics, and orca facts for Caleb.

Ingrid: One day at a time, cousin.

Me: Yeah. One ship at a time too.

Hey [Jenna],

I need to say this out loud to someone who isn’t in the tourism bubble.

You know how I’ve been going to those community tourism meetings? On a few Fridays earlier this year, when the kids were with Eric, I started grabbing a beer at Devil’s Club Brewing with David McCormick after the sessions. He’s on the Assembly, and at first it was just debriefing policy stuff – head tax scenarios, harbor infrastructure, all that.

But by late March the conversations had definitely drifted past policy. In that March 29 text I sent you (“This is getting messy”), I wasn’t exaggerating. There was a brief romantic thing, nothing dramatic, but in a town the size of Juneau, it feels like the whole world.

Now every time the head‑tax ordinance comes up at an Assembly meeting, I’m hyper‑aware that people might think I’m getting some kind of preferential treatment because I had a few too many IPAs with him this spring. The irony is he’s actually been more cautious about talking to me since then, which is probably the right call, but it leaves me without someone on the inside I can be totally candid with.

Combine that with the financial stress (still carrying that $38K‑ish SBA EIDL balance, worst‑case spreadsheet saying I might have to cut my own draw to $2,000/month in 2026 and lean on the Alaska USA line of credit just to cover Harbor View rent and Sophie’s braces), and I feel like I’m walking around with a neon sign over my head that says “Conflict of interest + broke.”

I’m trying to keep all of this separate from the public fight over the tax, but it’s getting harder. I don’t want the coalition to feel blindsided if rumors start.

Anyway, thanks for being my non‑tourism, non‑politics friend who still listens to all the tourism and politics.

Love,
L

metadata.subject: Messy overlaps

artifacts.content:
Hi Mom,

I hope you're doing well in Bellingham and taking it easy after your recent health scare. We've been thinking about you a lot, and I try to remind myself to enjoy the little moments with Sophie and Caleb, especially now that summer is winding down.

Things have been busy here in Juneau. I'm still juggling everything with Northern Lights Adventure Tours, and we're facing some challenges. There's a proposed increase in the seasonal passenger head tax, which is a statewide issue, and it's causing us small operators quite a bit of concern. I'm committed to supporting efforts that protect our community, but it's tough when these costs could potentially impact my business so deeply.

I'm grateful for the supportive network here, though. Our coalition of small tour companies is actively engaged, and we're trying to make our voices heard. It's important that we stick together to ensure that sustainable tourism remains viable in Southeast Alaska. Thankfully, I am a current ATIA member, which gives me a platform to advocate for our interests more effectively.

On the home front, Sophie is thriving at Floyd Dryden Middle School and just joined the cross-country team. She's even talking about wanting her own camera to take wildlife photos on our tours. Caleb, meanwhile, is as fascinated as ever with orcas and loves sharing fun facts about them.

Eric and I have kept our co-parenting routine steady, with the kids staying with me Monday through Thursday. We're making it work, and I'm glad to have some structure amidst the chaos. Our apartment at 411 Harbor View Drive, Suite 203, has a beautiful view that never fails to bring a sense of peace, even on the busiest days.

I try to find moments for myself, too, attending yoga classes and focusing on long-term planning for the business. I'm looking into more fuel-efficient vessel options and researching grants for sustainable tourism. It's a balancing act, but I'm hopeful we can navigate these changes positively.

Please give my best to Dad. I look forward to our next video call and catching up more. Give yourselves a big hug from us, and let me know if there's anything you need.

Love,

Laura

Tel: (907) 555-2834
Email: laura.jensen@nlatours.com

form_info.title: Norman Public School Foundation   Scholarship Application

short_answer_questions.what_are_your_interests:
Personally, I’m very family-oriented and curious by nature. I grew up in Norman and have always been the kind of person who wants to know how and why things work, whether that’s a computer, a recipe, or a social issue. I love spending time with my younger brother helping him with homework, and I also really enjoy cooking with my mom on weekends—we try new recipes we find online and then tweak them to make them our own. I’m also a big reader; I like realistic fiction and memoirs, especially stories about people who are the first in their family to go to college, because I see myself in those experiences.

Academically, my strongest interests are in computer science, mathematics, and their connection to real-world problem solving. I took AP Computer Science Principles as a junior and am currently in AP Computer Science A, and I discovered that I really enjoy programming—especially the feeling of breaking a big problem down into smaller parts and then building a solution step by step. I also like statistics because it helps me understand data behind issues I care about, like access to education and financial aid. In addition, I’ve enjoyed my English classes, particularly research-based assignments where I can dig into a topic and present it clearly to others.

Extracurricularly, I’m involved in several activities that reflect those interests. I’m a member of the Norman High Robotics Club, where I work mainly on programming but also help with documentation and outreach. Last year, our team mentored a group of middle school students at Whittier Middle School, and I helped design a simple lesson plan to introduce them to block-based coding. I’m also in our school’s Math Club, where we prepare for competitions and run a free after-school tutoring program for Algebra I students. Through that, I’ve discovered I really like teaching and explaining concepts in different ways until they click for someone.

Outside of STEM, I play clarinet in the school band and have been part of the marching band for three years. Band has taught me discipline, time management, and how to contribute to a group where everyone’s part matters. I also volunteer regularly at the Norman Public Library on Saturday mornings, shelving books and helping with children’s reading events. Being around younger kids and seeing them get excited about books and learning has reinforced my desire to work in a field where I can help make technology and education more accessible.

Overall, my interests center around technology, problem solving, education, and community service. I like roles where I can both work through technical challenges and then turn around and explain those solutions to others in a way they can understand and use.
short_answer_questions.please_explain_your_financial_need_or_a_:
My family’s financial situation is the main reason I am applying for this scholarship, and I want to be as transparent and detailed as possible. I am a first-generation college student; neither of my parents completed a four-year degree. My mom works full-time as a medical assistant at a local clinic in Norman, and my dad is a delivery driver for a regional shipping company. Together, their combined annual income is modest and is stretched thin between housing, utilities, food, transportation, and medical expenses for our family of four (my parents, my younger brother, and me).

We rent a small duplex in Norman, and our rent has increased twice in the last three years. My parents have been very open with me about our finances because they want me to understand the reality of paying for college. They do not have a college savings account for me; any extra money they have usually goes toward unexpected expenses, like car repairs or medical co-pays. For example, last year my dad had to miss work for several weeks due to a minor surgery, and even though he eventually recovered, the lost wages and extra medical bills set us back significantly. Around the same time, our old car needed a major repair, which forced my parents to take on additional credit card debt.

Because of this, I have been working part-time during the school year at a local grocery store, averaging about 12–15 hours per week. I use this income to pay for my own school-related expenses, such as AP exam fees, club dues when required, band expenses, and basic personal items. I have also been setting aside as much as I can for college, but with my limited hours and the need to help with some household expenses (like contributing to the gas bill during the winter), it is difficult to save a meaningful amount.

I know that even with federal aid and any grants I may qualify for, there will still be a significant gap between what college costs and what my family can realistically contribute. My parents have told me that they will support me however they can, but they simply cannot afford to take on large loans or co-sign for significant amounts of debt. I am very determined not to let finances be the reason I cannot pursue a degree in computer science, but I also want to be responsible and minimize the amount of student loan debt I take on.

This scholarship would directly help cover my first-year expenses, including tuition, fees, books, and required technology (such as a reliable laptop that can handle programming and data analysis software). Right now, I share an older family laptop with my brother that frequently crashes and is not dependable enough for college-level coursework. Having this scholarship would reduce the number of hours I need to work during the school year, allowing me to focus more on my studies, participate in research or internship opportunities, and get involved in student organizations related to my major.

In addition, receiving this scholarship would give me a sense of security and encouragement. I have spent a lot of time filling out the FAFSA, researching scholarships, and carefully comparing college costs because I know how much every dollar matters for my family. Being awarded support from the Norman Public School Foundation would show my parents that our community believes in my potential and that their sacrifices are helping me move forward. It would also help me avoid having to choose a college solely based on cost, and instead consider which program best supports my long-term goals of working in technology and educational access.

In summary, my family’s limited income, lack of savings for college, and ongoing financial obligations mean that I will need substantial aid to attend a four-year university. This scholarship would be a critical part of my financial plan, helping me bridge the gap between what we can afford and the true cost of college, and allowing me to focus more on learning and contributing to my campus and community.
short_answer_questions.what_are_your_plans_for_furthering_your_:
After graduating from Norman High School this spring, my plan is to attend a four-year university and major in Computer Science, with a possible minor in either Education, Data Science, or Mathematics. I have already applied to and been accepted at the University of Oklahoma (OU) in Norman, as well as Oklahoma State University, but OU is my top choice because it allows me to stay close to my family while attending a strong program in computer science.

In the short term (the next four years), I plan to focus on building a solid foundation in programming, algorithms, and data structures, while also taking general education courses that broaden my understanding of communication, ethics, and social issues. I am particularly interested in courses that combine technology with real-world applications, such as human-computer interaction, educational technology, and data analysis for social impact. I want to learn multiple programming languages (beyond Java and Python, which I have already started in high school) and gain experience working on team-based projects that resemble real software development environments.

I also plan to take advantage of research and internship opportunities as early as possible. At OU, I hope to apply for any undergraduate research programs in the computer science department, especially those focused on using technology to support learning—for example, building educational apps, tutoring systems, or tools that help teachers track student progress. I am also very interested in internships with local companies or organizations where I can apply what I am learning in class to real problems, such as improving websites, creating simple data dashboards, or automating repetitive tasks.

Beyond the academic coursework, I plan to be involved in student organizations like the Association for Computing Machinery (ACM), Women in Engineering or Women in Computing groups, and possibly organizations that focus on mentoring K–12 students in STEM. I would like to continue the kind of outreach I started in high school, such as helping middle school students learn basic coding and showing them that technology can be for everyone, not just a specific type of person.

Looking further ahead, my long-term goal is to work in a role where I can use computer science to help improve access to education and information, especially for students from low-income or underrepresented backgrounds. This might mean working as a software developer for an educational technology company, a nonprofit organization that builds learning tools, or even a school district’s technology department. Another possibility I am considering is eventually pursuing a master’s degree in either Computer Science or Educational Technology, depending on how my interests develop during my undergraduate years.

I also see myself continuing to give back to my community in Norman or wherever I end up living. Because I am a first-generation college student, I know how confusing and intimidating the college process can be. I want to be the kind of person who comes back to local high schools to talk about college, financial aid, and careers in technology, and to mentor students who might not see themselves in those spaces yet.

In summary, my educational plan is to complete a bachelor’s degree in Computer Science at a four-year university, engage deeply in both coursework and hands-on experiences like research and internships, and then use that education to build a career focused on creating and improving tools that make learning more accessible and effective. This scholarship would help me take the crucial first steps in that journey by making it financially possible for me to enroll and fully commit myself to my studies and campus involvement.

answers.field_label: Please Explain Your Financial Need Or A Scholarship To Further Your Educational Goals
answers.value:
My family’s financial situation is the main reason I am applying for this scholarship, and I want to be as transparent and detailed as possible. I am a first-generation college student; neither of my parents completed a four-year degree. My mom works full-time as a medical assistant at a local clinic in Norman, and my dad is a delivery driver for a regional shipping company. Together, their combined annual income is modest and is stretched thin between housing, utilities, food, transportation, and medical expenses for our family of four (my parents, my younger brother, and me).

We rent a small duplex in Norman, and our rent has increased twice in the last three years. My parents have been very open with me about our finances because they want me to understand the reality of paying for college. They do not have a college savings account for me; any extra money they have usually goes toward unexpected expenses, like car repairs or medical co-pays. For example, last year my dad had to miss work for several weeks due to a minor surgery, and even though he eventually recovered, the lost wages and extra medical bills set us back significantly. Around the same time, our old car needed a major repair, which forced my parents to take on additional credit card debt.

Because of this, I have been working part-time during the school year at a local grocery store, averaging about 12–15 hours per week. I use this income to pay for my own school-related expenses, such as AP exam fees, club dues when required, band expenses, and basic personal items. I have also been setting aside as much as I can for college, but with my limited hours and the need to help with some household expenses (like contributing to the gas bill during the winter), it is difficult to save a meaningful amount.

I know that even with federal aid and any grants I may qualify for, there will still be a significant gap between what college costs and what my family can realistically contribute. My parents have told me that they will support me however they can, but they simply cannot afford to take on large loans or co-sign for significant amounts of debt. I am very determined not to let finances be the reason I cannot pursue a degree in computer science, but I also want to be responsible and minimize the amount of student loan debt I take on.

This scholarship would directly help cover my first-year expenses, including tuition, fees, books, and required technology (such as a reliable laptop that can handle programming and data analysis software). Right now, I share an older family laptop with my brother that frequently crashes and is not dependable enough for college-level coursework. Having this scholarship would reduce the number of hours I need to work during the school year, allowing me to focus more on my studies, participate in research or internship opportunities, and get involved in student organizations related to my major.

In addition, receiving this scholarship would give me a sense of security and encouragement. I have spent a lot of time filling out the FAFSA, researching scholarships, and carefully comparing college costs because I know how much every dollar matters for my family. Being awarded support from the Norman Public School Foundation would show my parents that our community believes in my potential and that their sacrifices are helping me move forward. It would also help me avoid having to choose a college solely based on cost, and instead consider which program best supports my long-term goals of working in technology and educational access.

In summary, my family’s limited income, lack of savings for college, and ongoing financial obligations mean that I will need substantial aid to attend a four-year university. This scholarship would be a critical part of my financial plan, helping me bridge the gap between what we can afford and the true cost of college, and allowing me to focus more on learning and contributing to my campus and community.

answers.field_label: What Are Your Plans For Furthering Your Education
answers.value:
After graduating from Norman High School this spring, my plan is to attend a four-year university and major in Computer Science, with a possible minor in either Education, Data Science, or Mathematics. I have already applied to and been accepted at the University of Oklahoma (OU) in Norman, as well as Oklahoma State University, but OU is my top choice because it allows me to stay close to my family while attending a strong program in computer science.

In the short term (the next four years), I plan to focus on building a solid foundation in programming, algorithms, and data structures, while also taking general education courses that broaden my understanding of communication, ethics, and social issues. I am particularly interested in courses that combine technology with real-world applications, such as human-computer interaction, educational technology, and data analysis for social impact. I want to learn multiple programming languages (beyond Java and Python, which I have already started in high school) and gain experience working on team-based projects that resemble real software development environments.

I also plan to take advantage of research and internship opportunities as early as possible. At OU, I hope to apply for any undergraduate research programs in the computer science department, especially those focused on using technology to support learning—for example, building educational apps, tutoring systems, or tools that help teachers track student progress. I am also very interested in internships with local companies or organizations where I can apply what I am learning in class to real problems, such as improving websites, creating simple data dashboards, or automating repetitive tasks.

Beyond the academic coursework, I plan to be involved in student organizations like the Association for Computing Machinery (ACM), Women in Engineering or Women in Computing groups, and possibly organizations that focus on mentoring K–12 students in STEM. I would like to continue the kind of outreach I started in high school, such as helping middle school students learn basic coding and showing them that technology can be for everyone, not just a specific type of person.

Looking further ahead, my long-term goal is to work in a role where I can use computer science to help improve access to education and information, especially for students from low-income or underrepresented backgrounds. This might mean working as a software developer for an educational technology company, a nonprofit organization that builds learning tools, or even a school district’s technology department. Another possibility I am considering is eventually pursuing a master’s degree in either Computer Science or Educational Technology, depending on how my interests develop during my undergraduate years.

I also see myself continuing to give back to my community in Norman or wherever I end up living. Because I am a first-generation college student, I know how confusing and intimidating the college process can be. I want to be the kind of person who comes back to local high schools to talk about college, financial aid, and careers in technology, and to mentor students who might not see themselves in those spaces yet.

In summary, my educational plan is to complete a bachelor’s degree in Computer Science at a four-year university, engage deeply in both coursework and hands-on experiences like research and internships, and then use that education to build a career focused on creating and improving tools that make learning more accessible and effective. This scholarship would help me take the crucial first steps in that journey by making it financially possible for me to enroll and fully commit myself to my studies and campus involvement.
