project_description.describe_proposal_and_reason_necessary:
I am requesting approval of a Conditional Use Permit and Simple Site Plan review to allow the conversion of my existing detached two-stall garage at 1847 Bluff Street into a small, accessory home office and artist studio, while maintaining one enclosed parking stall and one paved outdoor parking space. The primary use of the property will remain a single-family residence, which I, Daniel Whitaker, occupy as my full-time home.

Existing Conditions:
The property is a 2.5-story wood-frame home constructed circa 1890, located within the Bluff Historic Preservation District on Bluff Street between 18th and 19th Streets. The lot slopes gently from east (alley) down toward Bluff Street. The existing detached garage is located at the rear (east) of the lot, accessed via the public alley. The garage is approximately 22' x 24' (about 528 sq ft) with a low-pitch gable roof, wood lap siding, and an existing concrete slab. There is currently no plumbing in the garage; it has minimal electrical service (one light and one receptacle) and is used primarily for storage and parking one vehicle.

Proposed Project:
The proposal is to partially convert the interior of the garage into a small office and studio space (approximately 320 sq ft) while preserving one standard parking stall inside the structure and one additional parking stall on the existing concrete pad immediately west of the overhead door. The office/studio will be used by me for my work as a remote software engineer and by my spouse, who is an amateur watercolor painter. No employees will work on-site, and no customers or clients will visit the property on a regular basis.

The scope of work includes:
- Framing an insulated wall to separate the remaining garage bay from the new office/studio space.
- Installing code-compliant insulation, drywall, and finishes in the office/studio area to create a comfortable, year-round workspace.
- Adding two operable wood-clad windows on the south elevation of the garage to provide natural light and egress, with trim and proportions to match the existing house and neighboring accessory structures.
- Replacing the current non-historic metal overhead door with a wood carriage-style overhead door that is more compatible with the historic character of the district.
- Upgrading electrical service to provide additional outlets, dedicated circuits for computer equipment, and energy-efficient LED lighting.
- Installing a small, high-efficiency electric mini-split system (heating and cooling) to serve only the office/studio portion of the building.
- Adding a compact half-bathroom (toilet and sink only) within the office/studio, with new plumbing tied into existing sanitary and water service lines on the property in accordance with City and State plumbing codes.
- Maintaining and slightly widening the existing paved parking pad off the alley to ensure one full off-street parking space remains available in addition to the interior garage stall.

Reason the Request is Necessary:
Under the current R-2 Two-Family Residential zoning, a home occupation and accessory structure conversion of this nature is allowed only with a Conditional Use Permit and site plan review to ensure that the use remains clearly incidental to the primary residential use and does not adversely impact surrounding properties. Because I will be conducting my software engineering work from home on a full-time basis, and because the converted area will be finished space with a small restroom, City staff advised that a Conditional Use Permit and Simple Site Plan are required rather than treating this purely as an interior remodel.

The request is necessary for several reasons:
1. Long-Term Remote Work: Since 2020, my employer (a mid-sized health technology company based in Minneapolis) has transitioned to a permanent remote-work model. I am under a long-term employment agreement that specifically allows and encourages remote work from home. Working from the dining room table and spare bedroom has created ongoing challenges with noise, ergonomics, and separation of work and personal life. Having a dedicated, code-compliant office space in the converted garage will allow me to continue my current employment without needing to rent commercial office space.

2. Reduced Commuting and Environmental Impact: Prior to working remotely, I commuted by car approximately 35 miles round-trip daily to an office in Peosta, Iowa. That commute contributed to traffic and emissions. By formalizing a home-based workspace, I can permanently avoid that commute, thereby reducing vehicle trips, fuel consumption, and associated emissions in a way that aligns with the Citys sustainability goals.

3. Compatibility with Neighborhood Character: The proposed use is quiet, low-intensity, and fully contained within an existing accessory structure at the rear of the property. No exterior changes are proposed that would alter the residential character of the home or the streetscape along Bluff Street. The only visible changes from the alley will be the upgraded overhead door, two modestly scaled windows, and minor site improvements such as landscaping and the slight widening of the existing parking pad. No signage is proposed, no outdoor storage will be allowed, and there will be no regular customer or client traffic. Typical daily activity will consist of my walking from the main house to the garage office in the morning, working on a computer for most of the day, and occasionally stepping outside to take phone calls or let our dog out.

4. Preservation and Enhancement of Existing Structures: The detached garage is currently in fair but aging condition. Without this project, we would likely continue to use it primarily as storage, and it would gradually deteriorate. By investing in structural repairs, new windows, siding repairs, and improved roofing, we will prolong the life of the building and improve its overall appearance in a manner consistent with the Bluff Historic Preservation District guidelines. We have already consulted with a local architect familiar with historic design standards to ensure that window proportions, trim details, and materials will be compatible with the surrounding historic context.

5. Personal and Family Considerations: My spouse, Anna, works part-time as a substitute teacher and spends her remaining time painting watercolors and illustrating childrens books on a freelance basis. She currently paints at a small table in our living room, which is not ideal for ventilation or for keeping materials secure from our two young children (ages 5 and 8). The converted studio will provide a safe, organized, and well-ventilated space for her art supplies, along with storage for completed canvases and prints. This will improve safety for our children and reduce clutter in our main living areas.

Operational Details and Commitments:
- Hours of Use: My typical work hours will be 8:00 a.m. to 5:30 p.m., Monday through Friday, with occasional evening or weekend work during software releases. Anna will use the studio intermittently, usually mid-morning or early afternoon, no later than 8:00 p.m.
- Employees: There will be no employees working on-site other than myself. All collaboration with coworkers is virtual, conducted by video conference, chat, and email.
- Customer/Client Traffic: I do not meet with clients in person as part of my job. On rare occasions (anticipated no more than 3	6 times per year), a coworker or project stakeholder may visit for a brief meeting. These visits will be pre-scheduled during normal daytime hours, and visitors will typically park on Bluff Street for a short duration, similar to a normal residential guest.
- Deliveries: I receive occasional small-package deliveries (e.g., computer equipment, office supplies, and art materials) via standard parcel services (USPS, UPS, FedEx). Delivery frequency is approximately 1	3 packages per week, which is comparable to any modern household and will not create additional traffic beyond what is typical for the neighborhood.
- Noise and Odors: The primary office use is computer-based and produces negligible noise beyond normal conversation levels. Anna uses water-based paints and non-toxic materials; no industrial processes, power tools, or heavy equipment will be used in the studio. We will not use oil-based solvents or spray finishes that could generate noticeable odors.
- Parking: The site will continue to provide two off-street parking spaces accessed from the alley (one interior, one exterior), which is consistent with or better than many neighboring properties. Our household owns two vehicles, and we will prioritize parking off-street whenever feasible. The project does not reduce the number of available off-street spaces.

In summary, the requested Conditional Use Permit and Simple Site Plan approval will allow us to make a modest, context-sensitive improvement to an existing accessory structure, support long-term remote employment and a small-scale artistic practice, and preserve and enhance the historic character of our property and the surrounding district. The proposed use is clearly subordinate to the primary residential use, will not generate significant traffic, noise, or visual impacts, and is designed to meet all applicable zoning, building, and historic design standards.

persona.education: B.S. in Computer Science, University of Iowa (2009)
family_members: Anna Whitaker (spouse, 36)
Lily Whitaker (daughter, 8)
Evan Whitaker (son, 5)
Mark Whitaker (father, 67)
Susan Whitaker (mother, 65)
Rachel Carlsen (younger sister, 34)
Emily J. Carver (close friend and planning consultant)
persona.background_context:
This is Daniel Whitaker, a 38-year-old senior software engineer who lives with his family at 1847 Bluff Street in Dubuque’s Bluff Historic Preservation District. He and his wife, Anna, bought the 1890s wood-frame house in late 2019, closing just before Thanksgiving that year after a long search for an older home with character but enough space for a growing family. They moved in with their then-toddler son Evan and preschooler daughter Lily, thinking Daniel would keep commuting to his office in Peosta. When the pandemic hit in early 2020, his employer, NorthStar Health Technologies, shifted to a fully remote model. By mid-2021, his team was officially designated as permanently remote, and Daniel settled into life as a work-from-home engineer supporting a suite of patient portal and telehealth tools for clinics across the Midwest.

Daniel’s weekdays now revolve around balancing focused, heads-down coding work with the rhythms of family life. He typically wakes up around 6:30 a.m., gets coffee going in the kitchen, and helps Lily and Evan get ready for school—Lily is in third grade at Prescott Elementary, and Evan just started kindergarten there in fall 2024. After Anna does the school drop-off most mornings, Daniel walks out back to the converted garage space that serves as his office and an art studio for Anna. The separation from the house has become important to him; before the renovation, he worked at the dining room table and in the spare bedroom, constantly juggling noise from cartoons, laundry, and the dog scratching at the door. Now, he can close the insulated door, log into his 8:00 a.m. standup with his Minneapolis-based team, and focus on debugging code or reviewing pull requests without feeling like he’s in the middle of the household.

The garage conversion, finished in late 2024 after a summer and fall of planning and construction, has quietly reshaped their daily life. Inside, one bay still functions as a normal garage stall, while the other side holds Daniel’s sit-stand desk, dual monitors, and a small couch under newly installed south-facing windows. A compact half-bath and a wall of shelves for Anna’s watercolor supplies make the space feel more like a tiny creative studio than a typical garage. Daniel uses the office primarily during business hours, but on some evenings Anna takes it over, putting on a podcast and painting while he handles bedtime routines with the kids. She works part-time as a substitute teacher in the Dubuque Community School District, picking up two or three days a week, and spends the rest of her time developing a small portfolio of children’s book illustrations. The studio has given her a place to spread out paper, brushes, and prints without worrying that Evan will knock over a rinse jar or Lily will accidentally step on a drying piece.

Family life in the Bluff Street house is busy but mostly settled. On school days, Daniel breaks for lunch around noon, often walking with Anna and their mixed-breed rescue dog, Jasper, down to Washington Park or looping around the neighborhood’s steep streets. In the afternoons he squeezes in code reviews and video calls with designers and product managers before shutting his laptop around 5:30 p.m. If he’s in the middle of a release cycle, he sometimes returns to the office after the kids are in bed, but he tries to keep that to no more than a couple of evenings a month. Anna usually handles dinner—simple meals like sheet-pan chicken or pasta—while Daniel helps Lily with reading logs or a math worksheet and listens to Evan talk endlessly about dinosaurs and construction vehicles.

Extended family is close by but not underfoot. Daniel’s parents, Mark and Susan, live about 20 minutes away on the edge of town. Mark is a semi-retired electrician who occasionally advises Daniel on home projects, including the electrical upgrades for the garage studio, while Susan, a retired nurse, loves picking up the kids for occasional sleepovers. Daniel’s younger sister, Rachel, lives in Madison, Wisconsin, with her partner and visits every couple of months. The kids look forward to her trips because she always brings new board games and art supplies. Over the last year, Daniel has become more aware of his parents’ aging—his dad had a mild heart scare in late 2023—and part of why he values working remotely is the flexibility to drive them to appointments or help with odd jobs when needed.

Daniel’s main ongoing concerns are practical and quietly adult: keeping his job secure in a volatile tech market, staying on top of the old house’s maintenance needs, and making sure the kids feel rooted and supported. NorthStar went through a small restructuring in 2023, and although his team remained intact, he still feels a low-level anxiety about future layoffs. That’s part of why he’s been taking online courses in cloud architecture and data security in the evenings, hoping to keep his skills current and his role indispensable. He also keeps a close eye on the family budget; the garage conversion wasn’t cheap, and although they managed it without overextending, he wants to pay down that chunk of their home equity line of credit within the next five years.

At the neighborhood level, Daniel and Anna are gradually becoming more involved. They know several of their Bluff Street neighbors by name, trade snow-shoveling favors in winter, and attend Washington Neighborhood Association meetings every few months when topics like traffic calming or alley lighting come up. Daniel likes that his quiet, screen-heavy job is tucked into a historic, walkable area where he can step outside and feel the texture of the city’s past in the brick sidewalks and steep hills. On weekends, the family often walks downtown for the farmers’ market in season, or drives out to the Mines of Spain for short hikes when the weather cooperates. They don’t have grand plans to move or dramatically change their lives; their focus right now is on incremental improvements—finishing the kids’ shared bedroom, slowly restoring the home’s original trim, saving for a modest summer road trip to the North Shore of Lake Superior, and keeping the balance between work, art, school, and rest as steady as they can.

secrets.secret_content: In November 2023, Daniel was placed on an informal Performance Improvement Plan (PIP) by his manager, Elena Morales, at NorthStar Health Technologies after a production outage tied to a rushed telehealth deployment. The PIP was never formally documented in HR, but Elena’s email subject line was “Next 60 days – expectations for portal stability,” and Daniel still worries this incident will be used against him if another restructuring happens.
secrets.why_sensitive: Knowledge of a quasi-PIP and a past production incident could mark Daniel as a “risk” employee in future references, internal promotion decisions, or layoffs. If widely known in the small Midwest health-tech community, it could damage his professional reputation and bargaining power, and might be misinterpreted by neighbors or local officials as evidence that his home office use is unstable or problematic.
secrets.how_disclosed: When drafting a more detailed justification for why he needs a quiet, dedicated workspace, Daniel might over-explain in an email to his planning consultant Emily Carver, writing something like, “After that November 2023 outage when Elena put me on that informal PIP, I realized I can’t risk another mistake caused by distractions at home,” which could then be quoted or paraphrased in supporting materials or meeting notes.

secrets.secret_content: To finance the 2024 garage conversion, Daniel and Anna drew $45,000 from a home equity line of credit with Dupaco Community Credit Union in June 2024, after having already carried a remaining $18,000 balance from an earlier roof replacement. Their joint goal, written in a shared Google Sheet titled “Debt Snowball – Studio + Roof,” is to pay this off by December 2029, but they are currently only making interest-plus-minimum payments due to childcare and medical expenses.
secrets.why_sensitive: Detailed debt levels and repayment struggles could affect how lenders, employers, or even neighbors perceive Daniel’s financial stability. If disclosed in a public planning record, it could expose them to predatory lenders, create embarrassment, or weaken their negotiating position in any future refinancing or home sale.
secrets.how_disclosed: In a back-and-forth email with Emily about why they can’t afford to rent outside office space, Daniel might write, “We already have about $63k on the HELOC from the studio and roof—see the ‘Debt Snowball – Studio + Roof’ sheet—so paying for commercial space just isn’t realistic,” and Emily could be tempted to reuse that context when explaining the necessity of the home office in a narrative or at a neighborhood meeting.

secrets.secret_content: Since February 2024, Daniel has been taking sertraline prescribed by Dr. Michael Jensen at MercyOne Dubuque Family Medicine for generalized anxiety, which worsened after NorthStar’s 2023 restructuring. He attends monthly telehealth check-ins from the garage office and keeps a private note on his phone titled “Work stress + meds – questions for Dr. Jensen (Bluff St. office).”
secrets.why_sensitive: Mental health treatment details can be stigmatized and may influence how employers, colleagues, or local officials informally judge his reliability. Public disclosure could cause personal embarrassment, affect future employment opportunities, or change how neighbors interpret his desire for a quiet, separate workspace (e.g., assuming he is unstable).
secrets.how_disclosed: When explaining to Emily or a close neighbor why the separation of workspace is so important, Daniel might say or write, “My anxiety got bad enough that Dr. Jensen at MercyOne put me on meds last February, and doing telehealth check-ins from a quiet office in the garage really helps,” which could accidentally make its way into explanatory emails, staff notes, or meeting comments.

secrets.secret_content: Daniel’s father, Mark Whitaker, had a mild heart attack in October 2023 and now has a stent placed at UnityPoint Health – Finley Hospital in Dubuque. Since then, Daniel has been quietly driving him to follow-up cardiology appointments every few weeks, blocking off his work calendar as “garage office – site inspection” so coworkers don’t know he’s leaving for family medical issues.
secrets.why_sensitive: Details about his father’s cardiac history are private health information that Mark may not want shared beyond close family. Publicly tying Daniel’s flexible remote work and home office justification to his father’s specific diagnosis and treatment could feel like a violation of Mark’s privacy and might cause family conflict or embarrassment if neighbors or church acquaintances learn the details from public records.
secrets.how_disclosed: In an email to Emily explaining why the remote-work arrangement and proximity to his parents matter, Daniel might write, “Since Dad’s heart attack at Finley last October, I’ve been the one taking him to his cardiology follow-ups, so having the garage office lets me duck out mid-day without losing my focus,” and Emily could be tempted to reference “ongoing medical appointments for an elderly parent” with more detail than necessary in support materials.

secrets.secret_content: During the 2023 restructuring at NorthStar, Daniel learned in a private Zoom call with director of engineering, Ravi Patel, that his team was kept only because they agreed to absorb late-night on-call rotations for the telehealth platform. Daniel has quietly been covering some 1:00–3:00 a.m. incident response shifts from the garage since March 2024, despite his official schedule listing only daytime hours.
secrets.why_sensitive: If widely known, this could highlight potential labor issues (uncompensated or under-compensated on-call work) and might create tension with coworkers or management. It could also worry neighbors or local officials if they imagine noisy, after-hours technical work or emergency calls happening in the accessory structure, potentially jeopardizing support for his permit.
secrets.how_disclosed: Trying to show that his use will not disturb neighbors, Daniel might over-explain in an email or at a hearing, saying, “Even when I’m doing those 1–3 a.m. on-call shifts Ravi added after the 2023 restructuring, it’s just me on my laptop in the garage with headphones,” inadvertently revealing internal company arrangements and his irregular work hours.

secrets.secret_content: In August 2024, a neighbor, Tom Gallagher from 1843 Bluff Street, sent Daniel a late-night text complaining that “your wife’s painting lights in the garage are on past 10 again – this is a quiet block,” after Anna worked in the studio until 10:30 p.m. Daniel screenshotted the text and titled it “Tom – studio complaint 8/14/24” in a private folder he shares with Emily to document potential neighborhood concerns.
secrets.why_sensitive: Specific neighbor complaints can inflame tensions on the block and may influence how other residents or the neighborhood association perceive Daniel’s project. If this text and Tom’s name are shared publicly, it could escalate into a personal conflict, harm neighborhood relationships, and make public hearings more adversarial.
secrets.how_disclosed: In preparing for a hearing, Daniel might email Emily, “I attached the screenshot ‘Tom – studio complaint 8/14/24’ so you can see the kind of pushback we’re already getting,” and Emily could be tempted to reference or even quote the message in supporting materials or correspondence with planning staff, unnecessarily exposing Tom’s identity and the private exchange.

secrets.secret_content: Since September 2023, Daniel has been quietly taking evening AWS and security courses through the online program at Northeast Iowa Community College (NICC) to prepare for a possible job search if NorthStar lays off more staff. His Canvas course dashboard includes a pinned note titled “If NSHT cuts remote roles – exit plan 2025,” which he has not shared with his current employer.
secrets.why_sensitive: This reveals that Daniel is actively planning for a potential departure from his current job, which could damage trust with his employer if discovered. It might also fuel speculation among neighbors or planning staff about the long-term stability of his home office use, possibly undermining confidence that the project will remain low-intensity and residential in character.
secrets.how_disclosed: In a detailed justification to Emily about why a flexible, modern workspace matters, Daniel might write, “I’m taking NICC cloud/security classes at night – see my Canvas note ‘If NSHT cuts remote roles – exit plan 2025’ – so the garage office doubles as my study space,” and that phrasing could accidentally appear in drafts or emails that end up in the public record.

secrets.secret_content: Daniel’s sister, Rachel Carlsen, confided during a November 2024 visit to Dubuque that she and her partner are considering a separation, and she has asked Daniel not to mention it to their parents. Daniel has a Signal chat thread with her labeled “Rach – Madison stuff (don’t tell Mom & Dad)” where they discuss whether she might move in temporarily to the finished garage studio if things get worse.
secrets.why_sensitive: Rachel’s relationship difficulties and potential separation are highly personal and not yet public even within the family. If Daniel references the possibility of his sister staying in the studio, it could reveal her private situation, cause family conflict, and make neighbors or city staff think the accessory structure is being used as an unpermitted dwelling unit rather than a home office/studio.
secrets.how_disclosed: Trying to emphasize the studio’s flexibility in an email to Emily or in casual conversation with a neighbor, Daniel might say, “My sister in Madison may need a place to crash in the studio for a while if her relationship falls apart, but it wouldn’t be a full-time dwelling,” inadvertently exposing her personal issues and suggesting quasi-residential use of the space.

artifacts.content:
From: dan.whitaker1847@gmail.com
To: emily.carver@carverplanning.com
Subject: Context on the garage studio + a few worries
Date: February 16, 2025

Hey Emily,

Catching up on a quiet Sunday while the kids are building a cardboard “dinosaur museum” in the dining room. I wanted to give you a little more color on why I’m so attached to this garage office/studio setup, in case it helps you understand where I’m coming from.

The big one is work. Ever since that November 2023 outage, when Elena sent the “Next 60 days – expectations for portal stability” email and basically put me on that informal PIP, I’ve been hyper-aware of how easily distractions can snowball into real mistakes. That whole thing never went through HR, but it scared me. I don’t want to be the guy who takes down telehealth for half the Midwest because Evan needed Paw Patrol and I lost my train of thought.

On top of that, my anxiety got bad enough last winter that Dr. Michael Jensen at MercyOne Dubuque Family Medicine put me on sertraline. I’ve been doing monthly telehealth check-ins from the garage since February 2024. Having a private, quiet room where I can talk openly without the kids barging in or Anna overhearing every detail has honestly made the meds and therapy stuff way easier.

Money is another reason we’re committed to making this space work. We pulled $45k from the Dupaco Community Credit Union HELOC in June 2024 for the conversion, on top of the ~$18k left from the roof. Anna and I have that “Debt Snowball – Studio + Roof” Google Sheet with the dream of being clear by December 2029, but right now we’re basically just paying interest plus a bit because of childcare and random medical bills. Renting outside office space just isn’t realistic.

Anyway, that’s probably more oversharing than you needed, but it’s the honest picture. Day to day it’s mostly just me on Zoom, Anna painting under those south windows, and the kids running laps in the backyard.

Talk soon,
Dan

metadata.subject: Context on the garage studio + a few worries

artifacts.content:
Title: Work stress + meds – questions for Dr. Jensen (Bluff St. office)
Date: March 4, 2025
Location: Garage office – south window desk

Things to bring up with Dr. Michael Jensen (MercyOne Dubuque Family Medicine) on Friday’s telehealth check-in (3:30 p.m.):

1. Sertraline dosage
- Been on current dose since Feb 2024.
- Anxiety better overall, but spikes around release cycles and those 1:00–3:00 a.m. on-call rotations I’ve been covering since March 2024.
- Ask if it’s normal to feel wired after the late-night incident response shifts in the garage and then useless the next afternoon.

2. Sleep
- On-call schedule from Ravi after the 2023 restructuring: unofficial, but I’m doing some of the telehealth platform pager duty from here in the garage a few nights a month.
- Hard to fall back asleep after a 2 a.m. Zoom from this desk.
- Wondering if he has non-habit-forming ideas (no benzos) that won’t mess with the sertraline.

3. Work stress history
- Mention the November 2023 production outage and Elena’s “Next 60 days – expectations for portal stability” email. I still replay that week in my head.
- Ask if my level of catastrophizing about another outage is typical anxiety or veering into something else.

4. Family / caregiving load
- Since Dad’s mild heart attack at UnityPoint Health – Finley Hospital in October 2023 and the stent, I’ve been driving him to cardiology follow-ups every few weeks.
- I keep blocking these on my calendar as “garage office – site inspection” so work doesn’t freak out.
- Ask if it makes sense to look at caregiver support resources, or if that’s more of a therapist thing.

5. Practical
- Confirm it’s okay to keep doing these telehealth visits from the garage office at 1847 Bluff Street (privacy, HIPAA, etc.).
- Ask if he sees any red flags with me taking those evening AWS/security classes through NICC on top of everything.

Remember: don’t minimize stuff. Just say it how it actually feels.

metadata.title: Work stress + meds – questions for Dr. Jensen (Bluff St. office)

artifacts.content:
From: dan.whitaker1847@gmail.com
To: rachel.carlsen@example.com
Subject: Checking in + crazy Bluff Street life
Date: November 20, 2024

Hey Rach,

I’ve been thinking about you since your visit earlier this month and wanted to follow up now that the dust has settled a bit here.

First, I promise I haven’t said a word to Mom or Dad about you and Alex maybe separating. That Signal thread you titled “Rach – Madison stuff (don’t tell Mom & Dad)” is staying between us. If it ever gets to the point where you need a breather, the studio side of the garage here at 1847 Bluff is absolutely yours to crash in for a while. We’ll have to be careful not to make it look like a full-time apartment (no hot plate empire in the corner), but a temporary landing pad is totally fine.

Honestly, we kind of built the space to be flexible. Right now it’s my remote office during the day and Anna’s watercolor cave at night. She’s been out there with her podcasts and painting lights on more lately, which led to our first neighborhood drama: Tom Gallagher from 1843 Bluff Street sent me that late-night text in August — “your wife’s painting lights in the garage are on past 10 again – this is a quiet block.” I was so annoyed I screenshotted it and saved it in a folder as “Tom – studio complaint 8/14/24.”

I’m trying not to escalate anything with him, because we like the rest of the block and the Washington Neighborhood Association folks have been decent. But it’s a reminder that people are watching what we do back here.

Anyway, you’re not a burden, and if you need to come down from Madison for a week or a month, we’ll make it work. The kids would be thrilled — Lily still talks about the board game marathon you did with them last time.

Love you. Text me on Signal if you want to vent more; Gmail feels too grown-up for that.

Dan

metadata.subject: Checking in + crazy Bluff Street life

artifacts.content:
Title: Dad cardiology follow-up – Finley
Date: April 9, 2025
Time: 2:30–4:30 p.m.
Location: UnityPoint Health – Finley Hospital, Cardiology
Attendees: Me, Dad (Mark)

Notes:
- Pick Dad up at his place by 1:55 p.m. so we have time for parking.
- This is another post–heart attack follow-up after the mild MI he had in October 2023 and the stent placement at Finley.
- Bring list of his meds (he keeps losing the paper one). Ask the cardiologist about:
  * How often they want to see him going forward.
  * Whether the occasional chest tightness he mentioned last Sunday is concerning.
  * Any restrictions on ladder work if he insists on “helping” with house projects.

Work cover:
- Block calendar as “garage office – site inspection” from 2:00–4:30 p.m. so no one at NorthStar wonders why I’m offline.
- Finish code review for telehealth portal tickets before lunch.
- If there’s an incident while I’m gone, Ravi or Jenna can grab the pager; I’ll keep my phone on vibrate in the waiting room but would rather not be doing a 1:00–3:00 a.m.-style incident response from the cardiology lobby.

Personal reminders:
- Ask Dad if he wants to stop by Washington Park afterward for a short walk, weather permitting.
- Maybe bring up the idea of him letting me drive him to more of these instead of Susan doing all the shuttling.
- Don’t mention any of this to Mom beforehand; she’ll just worry more.

From: dan.whitaker1847@gmail.com
To: anna.whitaker@example.com
Subject: Money / studio / summer plans
Date: January 22, 2025

Hey love,

I was looking at the “Debt Snowball – Studio + Roof” sheet over lunch and wanted to jot some thoughts down before I forget them.

We’re sitting at around $63k on the Dupaco HELOC now — the original ~$18k from the roof plus the $45k we pulled in June 2024 for the garage conversion. If we keep doing just interest + a little extra, we’re never going to hit that December 2029 goal we wrote in the notes. I get why we’re stuck there (childcare, Lily’s dentist bill, Evan’s ear infection saga, etc.), but it’s weighing on me.

The flip side is that the studio really is pulling its weight. My days in the garage office feel 100x saner than when I was at the dining room table. Even with those random 1:00–3:00 a.m. on-call nights Ravi added after the 2023 restructuring, at least I’m not waking you and the kids up — it’s just me, the laptop, and Jasper snoring on the couch.

It also doubles as my classroom. Those NICC AWS and security courses I’ve been taking in the evenings feel like an insurance policy if NorthStar decides to cut more remote roles. That Canvas note I pinned — “If NSHT cuts remote roles – exit plan 2025” — sounds dramatic, but it helps to have a plan.

Maybe when your subbing schedule calms down we can sit down with the budget and see if there’s anywhere to squeeze out an extra $150–200/month toward principal. Even that would make the spreadsheet graph look less terrifying.

Unrelated: can we try to keep the painting sessions wrapped by 9:30 p.m. on weeknights? I’m still a little paranoid about another “your wife’s painting lights in the garage are on past 10 again – this is a quiet block” text from Tom.

See you in a bit for kid-wrangling.

Dan

metadata.subject: Money / studio / summer plans

artifacts.content:
Thread: Tom – studio complaint 8/14/24 (saved screenshot)
Date: August 14–15, 2024
Participants: Dan (me), Anna

[8/14/24, 10:41 p.m. – Screenshot description]

Text from Tom Gallagher (1843 Bluff):
Tom: “your wife’s painting lights in the garage are on past 10 again – this is a quiet block”

My reply to Tom:
Me: “Hey Tom, thanks for letting us know. Anna’s just wrapping up now. We’ll be more mindful about lights after 10.”

[Forwarded to Anna]

Me: “FYI, this just came in from Tom next door. I took a screenshot and labeled it ‘Tom – studio complaint 8/14/24’ so I don’t forget the wording.”

Anna: “Wow. It’s 10:40, not midnight. I was literally rinsing brushes.”

Me: “I know. But given how twitchy people can be about the studio, I’m trying to keep a paper trail in case anyone starts saying we’re running some wild operation back there.”

Anna: “Ugh. Do you think this is about your late-night on-call stuff too? You’re out there for those 1–3 a.m. telehealth incidents sometimes.”

Me: “Doubt he even notices that. When I’m doing those shifts from the garage it’s just me and a laptop with headphones. No louder than you listening to podcasts while you paint.”

Anna: “Still feels like he’s watching us. Maybe I should set an alarm for 9:55 so I’m definitely done by 10 on school nights.”

Me: “That would help. I’ll also make sure the blinds are down when I’m on my sertraline check-ins with Dr. Jensen so we’re not giving anyone a show.”

Anna: “Cool. I just don’t want this turning into neighborhood drama. I like most of these people.”

Me: “Same. We’ll keep it boring and neighborly. Now come inside, it’s bedtime.”

Title: Career / remote work brain dump – 1847 Bluff studio
Date: May 12, 2025
Location: Garage office couch

Just need to get this out of my head.

Work feels both stable and fragile. On paper, I’m a Senior Software Engineer at NorthStar Health Technologies, fully remote, living this ideal “walk across the yard to the garage office” life. In reality, I still hear Elena’s voice from that November 2023 week after the production outage, when she sent the “Next 60 days – expectations for portal stability” message and pulled me aside for the quasi-PIP talk. No HR record, but it lives rent-free in my brain.

Since the 2023 restructuring, Ravi has us absorbing late-night on-call for the telehealth platform. I’ve been quietly doing some of those 1:00–3:00 a.m. incident response shifts from this couch, even though my official schedule says 8–5. It’s not noisy — just me, logs, and alerts — but it adds to the feeling that everything could fall apart if I miss one alert.

That’s part of why the NICC AWS and security classes matter so much. Logging into Canvas from the south-window desk at 9 p.m., pulling up that note titled “If NSHT cuts remote roles – exit plan 2025,” makes me feel like I have some agency. The garage is my office, but it’s also my backup plan incubator.

Meanwhile, Dad’s heart stuff and the Finley Hospital cardiology runs keep reminding me that time is finite. I block those appointments as “garage office – site inspection” on my work calendar so nobody asks questions, then sit in the waiting room refreshing Slack like a weirdo.

I don’t know if the answer is to double down at NorthStar, polish the résumé, or just keep quietly skilling up out here in the Bluff Street studio while the kids sleep. For now, this space is the one piece that feels like it’s actually under my control.

metadata.title: Career / remote work brain dump – 1847 Bluff studio

artifacts.content:
Hey Emily,

I hope this email finds you well. I wanted to catch up on a few things regarding our home on Bluff Street and some plans we're considering for the future.

First off, I’ve signed off on the property owners' certification today (2025-02-10) as we discussed. I appreciate all the help you’ve provided so far. I noticed the legal description is quite detailed: Lot 12 and the North 10 feet of Lot 13, Block 7, City of Dubuque, Iowa, according to the Original Plat thereof. The parcel ID is 10-03-31-234-012, and the total property lot area is 7,850 sq ft (0.18 acres), which gives us a nice space to work with.

Speaking of which, you mentioned Carver Planning & Design is just around the corner at 920 Main Street, Suite 305. It’s great to have you so close by here in Dubuque, IA 52001. If I need to reach you, I’ll use your office phone at (563) 555-7402 or your cell at (563) 555-7410.

Anna and I have been thinking a lot about how to best utilize our space without getting caught up in any major zoning changes or historic designations. Thankfully, our existing zoning is R-2 Two-Family Residential, which keeps things straightforward. We’re not looking for any variances, subdivisions, or text amendments at this time. Also, we’re steering clear of any historic housing grants or landmark statuses for now. The last thing we want is to get tangled up in demolition or rezoning issues, so we're keeping things simple.

As you know, our neighborhood doesn't have any special exceptions or temporary use permits in play, which is a relief. We can focus on maintaining the home’s character and making those incremental improvements we talked about, like finishing the kids’ shared bedroom and restoring some of the original trim work.

If anything changes on your end or if you have new ideas that might fit our goals, please let me know. Thanks again for your continued support and guidance!

Best,
Daniel R. Whitaker

P.S. If you need to reach me, my phone number is still (563) 417-8294, but you already knew that!

church__community_involvement.how_long_lived_in_hope_west_campus_prope: My family and I have lived in Hope West Campus housing for just under two years. We moved into the community in August 2023 when my dad changed jobs and we were looking for a more affordable and supportive neighborhood close to church and school. Since then, Hope West Campus has really become home for us. I have gotten to know several of our neighbors, especially the families with younger kids, and I often help watch their children when they need to run errands or attend evening Bible study. Living on the property has also made it easier for me to get involved in mid-week church activities, youth group, and volunteer projects because I can walk over to the church campus in less than ten minutes. Overall, our time here has been stable, positive, and has allowed my family to be much more plugged into the life of The Hope Church community.
church__community_involvement.describe_your_community_service_activiti:
Over the past several years, I have been intentional about serving consistently in both my church and the broader Columbus community, mainly focusing on youth, food insecurity, and neighborhood clean-up.

At The Hope Church, I volunteer twice a month with the children's ministry, specifically with the 3rd–5th grade Sunday class. I help set up the classroom, check in the kids, assist with small group discussions, and sometimes lead short activities or object lessons tied to the Bible story for that week. For example, one Sunday we were teaching about the story of the Good Samaritan, and I helped design a role-play activity where the kids practiced what it might look like to help someone who is left out at school or on the playground. Watching them act out these situations and then talk honestly about times when they had either helped or ignored someone in need was really powerful for me.

I also serve regularly with our church's monthly food pantry outreach. On the second Saturday of each month, I help unload the food truck, organize canned goods and produce into family-sized boxes, and then carry groceries out to people's cars. I try to greet each person with a smile, ask how they are doing, and take a moment to really listen if they want to talk. Over time, I have gotten to know a few of the regular families by name, and we will exchange updates about school, jobs, or health. This has helped me see that service is not just about handing out food, but about building relationships and showing people that they are seen and valued.

At school, I am a member of the Central City High School Community Service Club. Through this club, I have participated in several neighborhood clean-up days where we pick up trash along main streets, paint over graffiti (with permission from the city), and help plant flowers in public spaces like the library and community center. One project that meant a lot to me was helping to repaint and clean up the small playground at a nearby apartment complex where a lot of low-income families live. Some of the kids came out to watch us work, and by the end of the day they were playing on the swings and slides that we had just scrubbed down.

In addition, I volunteer as a peer tutor in our school's after-school tutoring program two days a week. I primarily help 9th and 10th grade students with algebra and biology. One of the students I worked with this year was really struggling with math and felt embarrassed to ask questions in class. We met regularly on Tuesdays, and I broke down problems step by step, using real-life examples like splitting pizza or tracking sports statistics. Over the semester, her quiz grades improved from the 60s into the low 80s, and she started raising her hand more in class. Seeing her confidence grow reminded me how impactful it can be just to give someone your time, patience, and encouragement.

Finally, during the summers, I have volunteered at a one-week Vacation Bible School at The Hope Church, helping lead game stations and snack time. This requires a lot of energy and patience, but it is also one of the most joyful weeks of my year. I get to work alongside adult volunteers and other teens, and we make a point to learn each child's name and something unique about them, so they feel personally known and welcomed.

Across all these activities, I have learned that community service is not just a requirement for a scholarship or a line on a college application. For me, it has become a way to live out my faith in everyday, practical ways—by showing up consistently, being reliable, and doing the small tasks that often go unnoticed but make a real difference in people's lives.

education_plans.planned_field_of_study: Associate of Applied Science in Information Technology – Cybersecurity Focus

required_attachments.awards_and_honors:
• National Honor Society – Inducted April 2024, Central City High School. Recognized for academic excellence (GPA above 3.5), leadership, and community service involvement (over 75 documented service hours).

• Honor Roll – Central City High School, 2021–2025. Achieved Honor Roll status every semester from 9th through 12th grade by maintaining a GPA above 3.5.

• STEM Excellence Award – May 2024, Central City High School Science Department. Received for outstanding performance in Honors Biology and Chemistry, consistent high lab report scores, and leadership in group projects.

• Church Youth Leadership Award – December 2023, The Hope Church. Recognized for consistent volunteer service in children's ministry, participation in youth leadership team, and reliability in supporting church outreach events.

• Community Service Recognition Certificate – May 2023, Central City High School Community Service Club. Awarded for completing over 120 verified community service hours during the 2022–2023 school year, including food pantry work, neighborhood clean-up, and peer tutoring.

• Local Tech Pathways Scholarship (one-time book scholarship) – Awarded March 2025 by Columbus Tech Forward Initiative, Amount: $500. Recognized for interest in pursuing a career in Information Technology and for completing an introductory coding workshop series.
required_attachments.personal_essay:
From the time I was young, I have been the person in my family who is called over whenever a phone freezes, a laptop will not connect to the internet, or the printer starts flashing mysterious error codes. What started as simple curiosity and a willingness to click around and figure out what was wrong slowly grew into a genuine passion for technology. Over the past few years, that interest has become more focused as I learned about cybersecurity and the ways that technology can be used both to protect and to harm people. I want to attend college so that I can gain the knowledge, training, and credentials I need to build a career in cybersecurity, helping protect individuals, small organizations, and churches like mine from online threats.

Academically, I have always enjoyed problem-solving and thinking logically, which is why math and science are my strongest subjects. In high school, I took every technology-related elective that was available to me, including an introductory coding class where we learned the basics of Python and JavaScript. I discovered that I love the feeling of working through a bug in my code and finally seeing the program run correctly. More recently, I participated in a weekend workshop sponsored by a local nonprofit where we were introduced to concepts like network security, phishing attacks, and password management. During that workshop, we simulated a phishing email scenario and analyzed the small details that can give away a scam—things like subtle spelling errors, suspicious links, and mismatched sender addresses. It opened my eyes to how vulnerable many people are online, especially those who are not very comfortable with technology.

My long-term career goal is to become a cybersecurity analyst or security engineer, focusing on helping small organizations and community-based institutions that may not have large IT departments. I think about places like my church, local nonprofits, and small businesses that store sensitive data but often cannot afford dedicated security staff. I want to be someone who can come alongside those organizations, assess their systems, identify vulnerabilities, and put protections in place so that they can continue to serve people without being derailed by data breaches or ransomware attacks. I am also interested in the educational side of cybersecurity—helping everyday people learn practical habits like creating strong passwords, recognizing scams, and safely using social media.

Attending Columbus State Community College is the first step in that journey for me. Their Information Technology program with a cybersecurity focus will allow me to build a solid foundation in networking, operating systems, and security principles. As a first-generation college student, I know I will face a learning curve in understanding how to navigate college life, from registering for classes to balancing school, work, and family responsibilities. However, I am motivated to work hard, seek out tutoring and advising when I need it, and take advantage of internship or co-op opportunities that will give me real-world experience.

This scholarship would make a meaningful difference for my family and me. My parents both work full-time—my mom as a medical assistant at a local clinic and my dad as a warehouse supervisor—but we have always lived on a careful budget. Over the past few years, unexpected medical bills and rising living costs have made it difficult for them to set aside money for college. While I plan to work part-time while attending school and to apply for federal financial aid, there is still a gap between what we can afford and the total cost of tuition, books, transportation, and basic living expenses.

Receiving this scholarship would help close that gap and reduce the amount of student loan debt I need to take on, allowing me to focus more on my studies and less on worrying about how every textbook will be paid for. It would also be a tangible sign to my family that our church community believes in me and is invested in my future. My younger siblings often look up to me, and being able to show them that college is possible, especially with the support of our church, is important to me. I want them to see that our circumstances do not have to limit our dreams when we are willing to work hard and ask for help.

My faith is at the center of why I am pursuing higher education and a career in cybersecurity. Growing up in The Hope Church, I was taught that our gifts and talents are not just for ourselves, but are meant to be used in service to others and to bring glory to God. For me, technology has always felt like one of those gifts. I believe that God can use me in this field to help protect people from harm, whether that harm is financial, emotional, or even spiritual. In a world where so much of our lives now happens online, I see cybersecurity as a form of stewardship—caring for the digital resources and personal information that have been entrusted to us.

There have been moments when I have questioned whether I am smart enough or prepared enough for a career in such a technical field. In those times, my faith has reminded me that my identity is not based solely on my grades or how quickly I pick up a new programming language. Instead, I am rooted in the belief that I am created with purpose, and that God can work through my efforts, even when I feel inadequate. Verses like James 1:5, which talks about asking God for wisdom, have encouraged me to pray not just for good test scores, but for clarity, perseverance, and integrity in how I approach my studies.

I also see education as a way to break cycles. My parents have always worked hard, but neither of them had the opportunity to complete a college degree. They have sacrificed a great deal to give me and my siblings a more stable life. By earning a degree and building a career in a growing field, I hope to contribute to my family's financial stability and to be in a position one day to give back—to my parents, to my church, and to other students who come after me. My dream is not just to succeed personally, but to reach a place where I can mentor younger students, especially those from similar backgrounds, and help them navigate their own paths into technology and higher education.

In summary, I want to attend college because I believe it is the next faithful step in developing the skills and knowledge I need to serve others through technology. My career goals in cybersecurity are driven by a desire to protect vulnerable people and organizations and to promote safety and integrity in the digital world. This scholarship would ease the financial burden on my family, allowing me to focus more fully on my education and to make the most of the opportunities ahead of me. Most importantly, my faith shapes how I view this entire journey—not as a path to personal status, but as a calling to use my abilities in a way that honors God and benefits my community.

answers.value: My family and I have lived in Hope West Campus housing for just under two years. We moved into the community in August 2023 when my dad changed jobs and we were looking for a more affordable and supportive neighborhood close to church and school. Since then, Hope West Campus has really become home for us. I have gotten to know several of our neighbors, especially the families with younger kids, and I often help watch their children when they need to run errands or attend evening Bible study. Living on the property has also made it easier for me to get involved in mid-week church activities, youth group, and volunteer projects because I can walk over to the church campus in less than ten minutes. Overall, our time here has been stable, positive, and has allowed my family to be much more plugged into the life of The Hope Church community.

answers.value:
Over the past several years, I have been intentional about serving consistently in both my church and the broader Columbus community, mainly focusing on youth, food insecurity, and neighborhood clean-up.

At The Hope Church, I volunteer twice a month with the children's ministry, specifically with the 3rd–5th grade Sunday class. I help set up the classroom, check in the kids, assist with small group discussions, and sometimes lead short activities or object lessons tied to the Bible story for that week. For example, one Sunday we were teaching about the story of the Good Samaritan, and I helped design a role-play activity where the kids practiced what it might look like to help someone who is left out at school or on the playground. Watching them act out these situations and then talk honestly about times when they had either helped or ignored someone in need was really powerful for me.

I also serve regularly with our church's monthly food pantry outreach. On the second Saturday of each month, I help unload the food truck, organize canned goods and produce into family-sized boxes, and then carry groceries out to people's cars. I try to greet each person with a smile, ask how they are doing, and take a moment to really listen if they want to talk. Over time, I have gotten to know a few of the regular families by name, and we will exchange updates about school, jobs, or health. This has helped me see that service is not just about handing out food, but about building relationships and showing people that they are seen and valued.

At school, I am a member of the Central City High School Community Service Club. Through this club, I have participated in several neighborhood clean-up days where we pick up trash along main streets, paint over graffiti (with permission from the city), and help plant flowers in public spaces like the library and community center. One project that meant a lot to me was helping to repaint and clean up the small playground at a nearby apartment complex where a lot of low-income families live. Some of the kids came out to watch us work, and by the end of the day they were playing on the swings and slides that we had just scrubbed down.

In addition, I volunteer as a peer tutor in our school's after-school tutoring program two days a week. I primarily help 9th and 10th grade students with algebra and biology. One of the students I worked with this year was really struggling with math and felt embarrassed to ask questions in class. We met regularly on Tuesdays, and I broke down problems step by step, using real-life examples like splitting pizza or tracking sports statistics. Over the semester, her quiz grades improved from the 60s into the low 80s, and she started raising her hand more in class. Seeing her confidence grow reminded me how impactful it can be just to give someone your time, patience, and encouragement.

Finally, during the summers, I have volunteered at a one-week Vacation Bible School at The Hope Church, helping lead game stations and snack time. This requires a lot of energy and patience, but it is also one of the most joyful weeks of my year. I get to work alongside adult volunteers and other teens, and we make a point to learn each child's name and something unique about them, so they feel personally known and welcomed.

Across all these activities, I have learned that community service is not just a requirement for a scholarship or a line on a college application. For me, it has become a way to live out my faith in everyday, practical ways—by showing up consistently, being reliable, and doing the small tasks that often go unnoticed but make a real difference in people's lives.
