persona.occupation: Part-time help desk assistant and student
persona.employer: Columbus State Community College IT Support Center (student worker)
persona.education: Recent graduate of Central City High School (Class of 2025), GPA 3.78; first-year student at Columbus State Community College pursuing an Associate of Applied Science in Information Technology – Cybersecurity Focus
family_members: Angela Thompson – mother
Marcus Thompson – father
Kayla Thompson – younger sister (age 14)
Malik Thompson – younger brother (age 9)
persona.background_context:
This is Jordan Michael Thompson, an 18-year-old first-year student at Columbus State Community College who lives with his family at 4829 West Maple Grove Drive in the Hope West Campus housing community on the west side of Columbus, Ohio. He graduated from Central City High School in spring 2025 with a 3.78 GPA and a strong record in math, science, and community service. His parents, Angela and Marcus, have worked hard to keep the family stable—Angela is a medical assistant at a local clinic off West Broad Street, and Marcus is a warehouse supervisor at a regional distribution center that recently switched to earlier shift times. Jordan’s younger sister, Kayla, just started at Central City High as a freshman, and his little brother Malik is in fourth grade at a nearby elementary school.

Jordan is now commuting to Columbus State by bus three or four days a week, taking introductory courses in networking, operating systems, and a first-year seminar that helps him adjust to college expectations. He works about 12–15 hours a week as a student worker at the college’s IT Support Center, answering basic help desk tickets, resetting passwords, and helping other students connect to campus Wi-Fi and online learning platforms. The job fits his natural problem-solving instincts and gives him a front-row seat to how real-world IT support and security policies work. At home, he still plays the role of unofficial tech support—fixing the family’s aging laptop, setting up parental controls on Malik’s tablet, and showing his parents how to spot suspicious emails after a close call when Angela almost clicked on a fake insurance message.

Life at Hope West Campus has settled into a rhythm after nearly two years there. The move in August 2023 was initially stressful because it meant changing neighborhoods and routines, but the proximity to The Hope Church and Jordan’s high school turned out to be a blessing. Jordan can walk to church in under ten minutes, which makes it easy for him to keep serving regularly in the children’s ministry with the 3rd–5th grade class and to help with the monthly food pantry outreach on second Saturdays. Some evenings, especially on Wednesdays, he heads over for youth and young adult gatherings or to lend a hand setting up for events. Around the housing community, a few neighbors know him well enough to trust him with watching their younger kids for an hour or two, and he often earns a little extra cash doing that or helping someone troubleshoot their internet or streaming setup.

Most weekdays, Jordan’s routine is tightly packed. On days he has morning classes, he’s up by 6:30 a.m. to help get Malik ready for school while his parents are finishing their shifts or preparing to leave for work. He usually grabs a quick breakfast—often cereal or toast—and then catches the COTA bus toward downtown, using the ride to review notes or watch cybersecurity videos. After classes, he might have a short shift at the IT Support Center, then heads home in the late afternoon. Evenings are a mix of homework, helping Kayla with algebra, and pitching in with chores like dishes and trash, especially when his parents are exhausted from long days. He tries to carve out at least one or two nights a week to unwind by playing co-op video games with friends online or tinkering with free cybersecurity labs and capture-the-flag challenges.

Recently, money has been a steady background concern in the household. Rising rent and groceries have squeezed the family budget, and a series of medical bills from Angela’s minor but lingering back issues earlier in the year forced them to dip into what little savings they had. Jordan is careful about how he spends; he tracks his bus pass, textbooks, and basic expenses in a simple spreadsheet, always aware that his part-time income and financial aid have to stretch. He’s determined to limit how much he borrows in student loans, so he’s actively looking for additional scholarships and a possible paid internship for the summer after his first year at Columbus State.

Despite the stress, Jordan remains hopeful and focused. He’s deeply motivated by the idea of becoming a cybersecurity analyst or security engineer, especially serving smaller organizations like churches, local nonprofits, and small businesses that lack full-time IT security staff. He’s already imagining how he might one day help The Hope Church audit their systems, lock down their Wi-Fi, and train staff and volunteers to recognize phishing attempts. His faith shapes how he interprets both his studies and his future career—he sees technology as a gift he’s responsible to steward well and views cybersecurity as a way to protect people’s dignity and resources.

Family dynamics are generally warm but busy. Marcus sometimes works overtime, leaving him tired and quiet at the end of the week, but he takes pride in seeing Jordan step into adulthood and often asks about his classes and what he’s learning. Angela, though occasionally anxious about finances and the complexity of college systems, is fiercely proud that her oldest is the first in the family to attend college, and she keeps a copy of his National Honor Society certificate in a folder with other important papers. Kayla looks up to Jordan but also teases him about being a “tech nerd,” while secretly appreciating that he takes time to help her study. Malik adores his big brother and loves when Jordan teaches him simple coding games or lets him sit in on youth events at church when appropriate.

In the coming months, Jordan is focused on keeping his grades strong in his first college semester, adjusting to the increased workload, and continuing to serve at church without burning out. He’s trying to learn how to say no occasionally so he doesn’t overcommit, especially when exams pile up. At the same time, he’s excited about attending a local cybersecurity meetup he found online, hoping to start building a network beyond school and church. Underneath the daily grind, he carries a quiet but steady determination: to finish his associate degree, transfer or step into a solid IT security role, help support his family, and eventually be in a position to mentor younger students from neighborhoods like his who are just discovering that technology might be their way forward too.

secrets.secret_content: In February 2025, Jordan went with his mother Angela Thompson to a billing meeting at Riverside Community Health Clinic on West Broad Street about her lingering lower back issues, where the clinic’s financial counselor, Ms. Ramirez, explained that they had sent a past-due notice titled “URGENT: Outstanding Balance for Physical Therapy Services” for over $1,800 that the family is now on a payment plan for.
secrets.why_sensitive: This exposes detailed health information about his mother (specific condition, treatment, and clinic) and the exact amount of medical debt. Sharing it broadly could embarrass his mother, affect how others at church view the family’s stability, and potentially impact their ability to negotiate future bills or aid. It also reveals financial vulnerability that could be exploited or judged.
secrets.how_disclosed: While expanding on the financial hardships section of his personal essay or in a follow-up email to the scholarship committee, Jordan might write something like, “Back in February 2025 I sat with my mom, Angela, in Ms. Ramirez’s office at Riverside Community Health Clinic on West Broad as she explained the ‘URGENT: Outstanding Balance for Physical Therapy Services’ notice for $1,800 from my mom’s back treatments,” to illustrate why scholarship support matters.

secrets.secret_content: During Spring 2025, Jordan quietly borrowed $300 from his youth leader, Deacon Robert Hayes, after a Wednesday night service at The Hope Church to help his parents cover part of the rent at Hope West Campus when they were short and afraid of getting a “Notice of Late Payment” from the property manager’s office in Building C.
secrets.why_sensitive: This reveals that the family has been close to missing rent and needed informal financial help from a church leader. It could embarrass his parents, strain the relationship with the deacon if widely known, and affect how church staff or neighbors perceive the family’s reliability as tenants. It also exposes a power dynamic and potential boundary issue in church relationships.
secrets.how_disclosed: When trying to show how much the scholarship would relieve pressure, Jordan might write or say, “One night after youth group in Spring 2025, I had to ask my youth leader, Deacon Hayes, to loan us $300 so we wouldn’t get another late notice from the Hope West Campus office,” in an essay draft or in a thank-you email to a mentor.

secrets.secret_content: In October 2025, Jordan’s supervisor at the Columbus State IT Support Center, Ms. Lauren Pierce, informally warned him in a one-on-one meeting in Room 214 of the IT building about a minor security incident where he forgot to lock his workstation during a lunch break, documented in an internal email titled “Reminder: Workstation Lock Policy – Incident on 10/12/25 (Jordan Thompson).”
secrets.why_sensitive: This involves a documented security lapse in a cybersecurity-related job, which could damage his professional reputation, impact future references, or be misinterpreted as a serious breach. If shared widely, it might cause his employer to question his discretion or lead scholarship committees or future employers to doubt his reliability in security roles.
secrets.how_disclosed: While reflecting on lessons learned in cybersecurity in a personal essay or email to a mentor, Jordan might say, “After Ms. Pierce emailed me the ‘Workstation Lock Policy’ reminder about my October 12, 2025 slip-up at the IT Support Center, I realized how small habits matter,” inadvertently revealing an internal HR/IT incident.

secrets.secret_content: During the Fall 2025 semester at Columbus State, Jordan received an official “Academic Progress Warning – CSCC” email from his First-Year Seminar instructor, Dr. Emily Chen, after missing two discussion posts and scoring 68% on his first Networking 101 exam because he was covering extra shifts at the IT Support Center and helping with the church food pantry.
secrets.why_sensitive: This reveals specific academic struggles and an official warning, which could influence how scholarship committees, church leaders, or future schools view his academic reliability. It might also worry his parents or lead to pressure from family and church if they see him as falling behind, and could affect future financial aid if misinterpreted.
secrets.how_disclosed: In a revised personal essay or an email update to a church mentor about how he is balancing responsibilities, Jordan might write, “I even got an ‘Academic Progress Warning – CSCC’ email from Dr. Chen in my first-year seminar after I bombed my first Networking 101 exam with a 68% because I was juggling extra shifts and the food pantry,” revealing more detail than needed.

secrets.secret_content: In November 2024, Jordan’s father Marcus Thompson had a tense phone call in their living room with his warehouse manager, Mr. Daniels, at the Franklin Logistics Distribution Center about being written up for missing an unscheduled Saturday overtime shift, which led to a formal “Attendance Warning – November 9, 2024” in his personnel file and several weeks of anxiety about possibly losing his job.
secrets.why_sensitive: This exposes Marcus’s employment record and disciplinary action, which could embarrass him, affect how church members or neighbors view his reliability, or even cause issues if it reached his employer indirectly. It also reveals the family’s financial precarity and internal stress that they may not want widely known.
secrets.how_disclosed: While explaining in a community-service essay or a note to a church leader why he started picking up extra babysitting and tech-help jobs around Hope West Campus, Jordan might say, “After my dad got that ‘Attendance Warning – November 9, 2024’ from his manager, Mr. Daniels, at Franklin Logistics, I started taking on more side work just in case,” thereby revealing his father’s disciplinary record.

secrets.secret_content: In March 2025, Jordan had a private conversation in the youth room at The Hope Church with Pastor Alicia Morgan, where he admitted feeling overwhelmed and close to burnout from juggling college, the IT Support Center job, and volunteering in children’s ministry, and she suggested he consider meeting with the church’s volunteer counselor, Ms. Denise Carter, for stress and anxiety support.
secrets.why_sensitive: This reveals mental-health-related concerns (stress, possible anxiety) and the fact that he was referred to counseling through his church. Sharing it broadly could subject him to stigma, misunderstandings about his emotional stability, or unwanted attention from church members or scholarship reviewers who might question his ability to cope with added responsibilities.
secrets.how_disclosed: When describing his commitment to service in an essay or email to a scholarship committee, Jordan might write, “In March 2025 I even sat down with Pastor Alicia in the youth room because I felt so burned out that she suggested I talk to Ms. Carter, our volunteer counselor,” as a way to show growth, unintentionally revealing private emotional and counseling information.

secrets.secret_content: In August 2025, Jordan received a “Past Due – COTA Student Pass Payment Plan” notice at his Columbus State email from the campus billing office because he had split the cost of his semester-long bus pass into installments and missed the second payment while prioritizing a textbook purchase for his Operating Systems class.
secrets.why_sensitive: This exposes specific financial strain, missed payments, and his dependence on subsidized transportation, which could lead others to see him as financially unstable or irresponsible. It might influence how scholarship committees, employers, or even peers perceive his reliability, and could be used to pressure him into disclosing more about his finances.
secrets.how_disclosed: When explaining transportation challenges in a note to an advisor or in a scholarship update, Jordan might say, “I actually got a ‘Past Due – COTA Student Pass Payment Plan’ email in August 2025 because I chose to buy my Operating Systems textbook first,” revealing his specific billing issue.

secrets.secret_content: In January 2025, Jordan informally fixed a neighbor’s hacked streaming account in Apartment 3B at Hope West Campus after Mr. Lewis, a single dad from church, showed him an email titled “Your Netflix Password Has Been Changed” that turned out to be part of a phishing scam; Jordan reset the account and quietly advised him not to tell many people what had happened.
secrets.why_sensitive: This incident shows that someone in his immediate community fell for a phishing scam and needed help recovering their account. Sharing it with identifying details could embarrass Mr. Lewis, damage trust with neighbors, and potentially expose account-recovery details or patterns that attackers could exploit. It also blurs boundaries between Jordan’s informal ‘tech support’ and more formal cybersecurity responsibilities.
secrets.how_disclosed: When giving examples of how he uses his tech skills to serve his community in an essay or email, Jordan might write, “In January 2025, I went over to Mr. Lewis’s place in 3B after he got a ‘Your Netflix Password Has Been Changed’ email and realized he’d been phished, so I helped him lock everything down,” unintentionally exposing a neighbor’s digital mistake.

artifacts.content:
Hi Mom,

Just got back to the library between classes, so I wanted to write this out while it’s fresh instead of trying to explain everything tonight when everyone’s tired.

I keep thinking about that day back in February 2025 when we sat in Ms. Ramirez’s little office at Riverside Community Health Clinic on West Broad. She pulled up that “URGENT: Outstanding Balance for Physical Therapy Services” notice and said the total for your lower back physical therapy was a little over $1,800. I remember how quiet you got when she started talking about the payment plan. I know you keep saying, “We’ll figure it out,” but I also see how much it weighs on you.

Things have been tight on my side too. You know in Spring 2025, after that Wednesday night youth service at The Hope Church, I ended up asking Deacon Robert Hayes to spot us $300 so you and Dad could cover rent at Hope West Campus and not get another “Notice of Late Payment” from the office in Building C. I still feel weird about it, even though he was kind and said he was glad to help. I just don’t ever want you to feel like I’m telling people our business behind your back.

On top of that, in August 2025 I got that “Past Due – COTA Student Pass Payment Plan” email from the Columbus State billing office because I split my semester bus pass into installments and missed the second payment after buying my Operating Systems textbook. I handled it, but it was a reminder how close everything is to the edge.

I’m not telling you this to stress you out more. I just want you to know I really am being careful with money and that I’m serious about finding more hours or maybe a summer internship so I can help more. I’m also still looking at extra scholarships so we’re not always playing catch-up.

Love you. I’ll be home after my shift at the IT Support Center tonight.

– Jordan

metadata.subject: Money stuff I didn’t say out loud

artifacts.content:
Title: Stuff I need to remember from this semester

1. School / grades
- That “Academic Progress Warning – CSCC” email from Dr. Emily Chen in First-Year Seminar was a wake-up call. Missing those two discussion posts and getting a 68% on the first Networking 101 exam because I picked up extra shifts at the IT Support Center and helped with the food pantry can’t happen again. 
- New rule: no extra shifts the week before an exam, and block off time right after class to do discussion posts.

2. Work habits
- October 12, 2025 – meeting with Ms. Lauren Pierce in Room 214 of the IT building about forgetting to lock my workstation during lunch. She was nice about it, but that internal email with the subject “Reminder: Workstation Lock Policy – Incident on 10/12/25 (Jordan Thompson)” is now a permanent reminder. I keep telling people to take security seriously, so I’ve got to model it.
- Action: auto-lock set to 3 minutes, and I’m doing the mental “Ctrl+Alt+Delete” check every time I stand up.

3. Church / burnout
- March 2025 talk with Pastor Alicia in the youth room at The Hope Church. Told her I felt close to burning out trying to juggle Columbus State, the IT Support Center job, and children’s ministry. She suggested I meet with Ms. Denise Carter, the volunteer counselor, for help with stress and anxiety. I still haven’t followed up, but I probably should.
- Action: say “no” at least once a month when new things come up, and schedule at least one real rest day.

4. Family / motivation
- November 9, 2024 “Attendance Warning – November 9, 2024” that Dad got from Mr. Daniels at Franklin Logistics after missing that unscheduled Saturday overtime. I remember how stressed he was, pacing in the living room on the phone. That’s part of why I started taking more babysitting and tech-help gigs around Hope West Campus.

All of this is why I’m doing the cybersecurity track at Columbus State and trying to stay focused. I need to make this count.

metadata.title: Semester checkpoint – don’t forget this
metadata.location: Columbus State library, 3rd floor quiet area

artifacts.content:
Hey Pastor Alicia,

I’ve been meaning to follow up since our conversation back in March 2025 in the youth room, when I told you I was feeling overwhelmed and pretty close to burnout from juggling Columbus State, my shifts at the IT Support Center, and serving in children’s ministry. You mentioned maybe talking with Ms. Denise Carter, the volunteer counselor, about stress and anxiety. I’ve been thinking about that more seriously lately.

This fall has been heavier than I expected. Earlier in the semester I got that “Academic Progress Warning – CSCC” email from Dr. Chen in my First-Year Seminar after I missed two discussion posts and scored a 68% on my first Networking 101 exam because I was covering extra hours and still trying to help with the food pantry. It was embarrassing, but also kind of the push I needed.

At the same time, things at home are still tight. I keep replaying that February 2025 meeting at Riverside Community Health Clinic on West Broad, sitting next to my mom in Ms. Ramirez’s office while she explained the “URGENT: Outstanding Balance for Physical Therapy Services” for over $1,800 from Mom’s lower back physical therapy and how they were putting us on a payment plan. Then there was that night in Spring 2025 after youth group when I pulled Deacon Hayes aside and asked if he could loan us $300 so my parents wouldn’t get another “Notice of Late Payment” from the Hope West Campus office in Building C. I’m grateful, but I also carry all of that around in my head.

I still want to serve, especially with the 3rd–5th graders and the food pantry, but I’m realizing I need better boundaries so I don’t crash. Would it be okay if we met sometime after a Wednesday service to talk about what a healthier schedule might look like and whether connecting with Ms. Carter now makes sense?

Thank you for listening and for not making me feel weak for admitting I’m tired.

– Jordan

Hey Mr. Lewis,

Just checking in about your streaming stuff and to make sure everything is still working the way it should.

Back in January 2025 when I came over to Apartment 3B at Hope West Campus and you showed me that email with the subject line “Your Netflix Password Has Been Changed,” it turned out to be part of that phishing scam that was going around. I’m glad we were able to reset the account that night and lock things down before anything worse happened. Like I said then, it happens to a lot of people, so no shame in it at all.

Since then, I’ve seen a bunch of similar scams at my job in the IT Support Center at Columbus State. People click on links that look legit, and next thing you know they’re calling us from the library in a panic. Honestly, it’s part of what keeps me motivated about this cybersecurity track. Even smart folks get fooled when they’re tired or distracted.

If you ever get another weird email like that—anything about passwords changing, “urgent” account problems, or messages asking you to log in from a link—feel free to text or knock before you click. It only takes me a few minutes to look at the sender address, the URL, and a couple other details.

Also, thanks again for trusting me with this kind of stuff. Between helping you, watching the kids down the hall sometimes, and doing the food pantry at The Hope Church, I’m realizing how much tech touches people’s everyday lives. It’s not just computers in a lab; it’s whether somebody can watch a movie with their kid after a long shift without worrying their info got stolen.

Hope things are going smoothly with work and with your son’s homework. If you want, I can show him some of the beginner coding games I’ve been using with my little brother Malik.

– Jordan (3D)

artifacts.content:
Title: Midweek reset – what’s actually on my plate

Writing this out because my brain is trying to hold too many tabs open.

School:
- Columbus State, first year IT – Cybersecurity Focus. Still committed to this.
- Need to keep the momentum after that “Academic Progress Warning – CSCC” from Dr. Chen in First-Year Seminar (missed 2 posts + 68% on first Networking 101 exam). New plan of studying on the COTA bus rides is helping.

Work:
- IT Support Center student worker. Remember the October 12, 2025 thing: Ms. Pierce pulled me into Room 214 about leaving my workstation unlocked at lunch. Internal email titled “Reminder: Workstation Lock Policy – Incident on 10/12/25 (Jordan Thompson)” is a permanent lesson. No repeating that.

Money / family:
- Mom’s back: February 2025 meeting with Ms. Ramirez at Riverside Community Health Clinic on West Broad about the “URGENT: Outstanding Balance for Physical Therapy Services” for her lower back physical therapy (~$1,800). They’re still on the payment plan.
- Spring 2025: asking Deacon Robert Hayes after Wednesday night youth at The Hope Church to loan us $300 so Mom and Dad wouldn’t get a “Notice of Late Payment” from the Hope West Campus office in Building C. Grateful, but I don’t want to make that a habit.
- August 2025: “Past Due – COTA Student Pass Payment Plan” email from Columbus State billing because I prioritized my Operating Systems textbook over the second bus-pass installment. Caught up now, but I need a better buffer.

Church / health:
- March 2025 talk with Pastor Alicia in the youth room about feeling close to burnout. She suggested meeting with Ms. Denise Carter, the volunteer counselor, for stress/anxiety. Still haven’t scheduled it.

Action items for next 2 weeks:
1. Email Ms. Carter and set up a time.
2. Pick up one extra babysitting/tech-help gig, but ONLY if it doesn’t land near exams.
3. Block off one full evening with no school, work, or church—just rest.

Reminder: I’m doing all this to build something more stable for Mom, Dad, Kayla, and Malik. Don’t lose that why.

metadata.title: Midweek reset – school, work, money, church

artifacts.content:
Hey Mom,

I just wanted to give you a quick update on how things have been going here. College life is busier than I expected, but I'm really enjoying my courses at Columbus State. I'm particularly interested in the cybersecurity focus, which is part of my Associate of Applied Science program. I still remember how I used to be the family tech support, fixing our laptop or setting up Malik’s tablet. Now, I’m learning even more about protecting systems, which is super exciting!

I’ve been juggling my part-time job at the IT Support Center with my classes. Working there helps me apply what I learn in real-world situations, like resetting passwords and resolving network issues. I really appreciate the flexibility it offers, so I can still help around the house, especially since Malik is in the fourth grade now, and Kayla just started at Central City High.

Speaking of school, my high school GPA was 3.78, and I’m aiming to keep it up here in college. I’m also on the lookout for scholarships and summer internships to help with finances. I know how hard you and Dad work, especially after those medical bills earlier this year. I hope to ease some of the financial burdens by contributing what I can from my part-time job and hopefully some scholarships.

My faith remains a big part of my life. Even though I’m not a member of The Hope Church officially, I’ve been involved for over five years, serving in the children’s ministry and helping with the food pantry. It’s important to me to give back, especially because our community has supported us so much.

I’m also trying to ensure I stay connected with everyone back home, so please let me know if there’s anything you need help with, like tech issues. Remember to watch out for those phishing emails—we don’t want another insurance scam scare!

Feel free to give me a call at (614) 555-2394 if you want to chat or need anything. I know my number is saved as (614) 555-7812 in some of the older contacts, but the first one is the best way to reach me.

Love,
Jordan

P.S. I attached my latest report card and a letter of recommendation from my coding class teacher, just in case you need them for anything. Also, I’ve been thinking a lot about my long-term goals. I wrote an essay about it, and I might share it with you sometime. It’s about how I see cybersecurity as a way to protect and serve others, inspired by our church's teachings.

Jordan M. Thompson

nominee_information.jurisdiction: City of Peachtree Ridge, Georgia – Community Development Department, Building Safety Division

nomination_details.contact_person_with_phone_or_email: Laura J. McMillan, CBO – Senior Plans Examiner, City of Peachtree Ridge, GA | (404) 555-7823 | lmcmillan@peachtreeridgega.gov

professional_offices_held.nominees_chapter: Metro Atlanta BOAG Chapter – Treasurer (2008–2011); Vice President (2011–2014); President (2014–2017); Board Member Emeritus (2018–present)
professional_offices_held.state_chapter: Building Officials Association of Georgia (BOAG) – Board Director (2010–2013); Vice President (2013–2016); President (2016–2018); Education Committee Chair (2019–2023)
professional_offices_held.region_8: ICC Region VIII – Georgia Representative (2015–2019); Region VIII Vice Chair (2019–2021)
professional_offices_held.icc: International Code Council (ICC) – Code Development Committee Member, International Building Code – Fire Safety (2012–2015); ICC Instructor (2015–present); ICC Governmental Member Voting Representative for City of Peachtree Ridge (2006–present)

achievements_and_justification.civic_involvement_if_known:
Michael has consistently demonstrated that his commitment to public safety extends well beyond his official duties as Chief Building Official.

1. Local civic and volunteer involvement:
- Peachtree Ridge Community Emergency Response Team (CERT): Michael has been an active volunteer instructor with the city’s CERT program since 2009. He regularly leads sessions on building safety, post-disaster building assessment, and safe use of generators and temporary structures after storms. He has personally trained more than 300 residents in basic building safety concepts.
- Habitat for Humanity – Gwinnett County: Since 2010, Michael has volunteered several weekends each year as a construction safety advisor for local Habitat builds. He reviews framing, electrical rough-ins, and stair/guard installations to ensure donated labor still results in safe, code-compliant homes, and he mentors young volunteers who are interested in construction trades.
- Peachtree Ridge High School Career and Technical Education (CTE) Advisory Board: Michael has served on the advisory board since 2015, helping shape the curriculum for the construction pathway and speaking twice per year to classes about careers in building safety, inspections, and code enforcement.

2. Public education and outreach:
- Annual “Building Safety Month” events: For over a decade, Michael has organized and led the city’s participation in ICC’s Building Safety Month. He sets up interactive displays in the lobby of City Hall, prepares simple handouts explaining smoke alarm placement, deck safety, and basement egress requirements, and hosts evening Q&A sessions where homeowners can bring photos or sketches of planned projects and ask questions before they apply for permits.
- Senior citizen outreach: Recognizing that many older residents live in aging homes, Michael worked with the local senior center to offer an annual “Home Safety Checkup” seminar. He developed a checklist for seniors to evaluate trip hazards, handrail needs, and fire safety issues, and he coordinates with local non-profits to help low-income seniors correct the most critical hazards.

3. Professional mentoring and development of others:
- Mentor to new inspectors: Within his jurisdiction and across neighboring cities, Michael is informally known as the person to call when a new inspector or building official is struggling. He has mentored at least a dozen inspectors who have gone on to become senior inspectors or building officials in other Georgia jurisdictions. He often spends his own time after hours helping them study for ICC certification exams.
- Guest speaker at civic clubs: Michael regularly speaks at Rotary Club, Chamber of Commerce, and neighborhood association meetings. His talks focus on how the code is not just a set of rules but a tool to protect families, first responders, and property owners. He has a particular talent for translating technical code language into plain English for business owners and homeowners.

4. Regional and statewide initiatives:
- Post-disaster building safety evaluations: Michael is part of the Georgia post-disaster building safety volunteer cadre. He has deployed after several major storm events, including tornado damage in northwest Georgia and severe flooding in 2018, conducting rapid safety assessments of damaged structures and helping coordinate mutual aid between jurisdictions.

In all of these civic activities, Michael’s approach is patient, educational, and service-oriented. He is known for returning calls from citizens on evenings and weekends and for treating every question—no matter how basic—as an opportunity to build trust and understanding about building safety.
achievements_and_justification.other_notable_achievements:
Over a 35-year career in code enforcement, Michael has amassed a record of accomplishments that have had lasting impact within his jurisdiction, throughout Georgia, and within ICC Region VIII.

1. Leadership in modernizing building safety operations:
- Digital permitting and inspection platform: Michael led the City of Peachtree Ridge’s transition from a fully paper-based permitting process to a modern, online permitting and inspection system implemented between 2012 and 2014. He chaired the internal working group that mapped all existing workflows, selected the software vendor, and oversaw staff training. As a result, average permit turnaround time for single-family residential projects was reduced from 10 business days to 4, and inspection scheduling accuracy and transparency improved substantially.
- Standardized inspection checklists: Recognizing inconsistencies among inspectors, Michael developed detailed, code-referenced inspection checklists for residential and commercial inspections. These checklists have been shared with multiple surrounding jurisdictions and are now widely used as a training tool for new inspectors across Metro Atlanta.

2. Contributions to code development and adoption in Georgia:
- State code advisory role: As an active member of BOAG’s Code Advisory and Education Committees, Michael has provided written comments and in-person testimony on several Georgia state amendments to the International Building Code and International Residential Code, particularly in the areas of fire-resistance-rated construction and deck safety.
- Deck safety advocacy: After responding to several deck collapses early in his career, Michael became a vocal advocate for improved deck design and construction standards. He helped draft local deck construction guidelines, which were later adapted by other Georgia jurisdictions, and he has taught numerous seminars on proper ledger attachment, guard design, and flashing installation.

3. Training and education achievements:
- ICC-certified instructor: Michael became an ICC-certified instructor in 2015 and has since taught dozens of courses across Georgia on topics such as Means of Egress, Fire-Resistance-Rated Construction, Existing Building Code, and Residential Inspections. His classes are consistently well-reviewed, and he is frequently requested by name for BOAG and regional training events.
- Development of in-house training program: Within his own department, Michael created a structured “Inspector Development Program” that outlines the competencies and training milestones for entry-level inspectors to progress to senior positions. This program includes ride-alongs, code study sessions, and cross-training with zoning, fire, and public works staff.

4. Recognition and awards:
- BOAG President’s Award (2017): Michael received the BOAG President’s Award in 2017 in recognition of his leadership in statewide education initiatives and his role in strengthening collaboration between building officials, fire marshals, and emergency management.
- City of Peachtree Ridge Employee of the Year (2011): Michael was selected by his peers and city leadership for his exemplary service during a period of rapid development and for his fair, consistent approach to enforcement.

5. Notable projects and public safety impacts:
- Mixed-use town center redevelopment: Michael oversaw the building safety aspects of the city’s flagship mixed-use town center project, which included multi-story residential over retail, structured parking, and a central civic plaza. He worked closely with developers, design professionals, and the fire department to ensure safe egress, fire separation, and accessibility, while still supporting the city’s economic development goals.
- School safety enhancements: Michael collaborated with the local school district on a multi-year program to review existing school buildings for egress, fire alarm, and accessibility issues. His team identified and helped correct numerous deficiencies, including blocked exits, outdated alarms, and missing or inadequate signage.

6. Professional certifications and continuous learning:
- Michael maintains multiple ICC certifications, including Certified Building Official (CBO), Commercial Building Inspector, Residential Building Inspector, Plans Examiner, and Accessibility Inspector/Plans Examiner. He is known for staying current with each new code cycle and for sharing that knowledge generously with colleagues.

Collectively, these achievements demonstrate not only technical competence but also a sustained commitment to making the built environment safer, more accessible, and more resilient.
achievements_and_justification.additional_comments_on_why_the_nominee_s:
Michael D. Harrington exemplifies the spirit of the Hal Cosper Memorial Lifetime Achievement Award through his sustained, decades-long commitment to building safety, his leadership within BOAG and ICC Region VIII, and his deep personal investment in mentoring the next generation of code officials.

1. Length and breadth of service:
Michael began his career as an entry-level building inspector in 1989 and has served continuously in code enforcement roles for more than 35 years. Over that time, he has progressed from field inspector to plans examiner, deputy building official, and ultimately Chief Building Official for the City of Peachtree Ridge. At each step, he has not only fulfilled his job responsibilities but has also sought out opportunities to raise the standards of practice for everyone around him.

2. Reputation for fairness, integrity, and professionalism:
Within the design and construction community, Michael has earned a reputation as a firm but fair code official. Architects and contractors often remark that, even when they disagree with his interpretation, they know he is consistent, well-prepared, and focused on safety rather than bureaucracy. He is known for taking the time to walk job sites with contractors, explain the underlying life-safety rationale for requirements, and work collaboratively to find compliant solutions instead of simply issuing red tags and walking away.

3. Dedication to mentoring and succession in the profession:
One of the distinguishing aspects of Michael’s career is his focus on developing others. He has personally helped numerous inspectors prepare for and pass their first ICC certification exams, often meeting with them early in the morning before work or staying late to review practice questions. Several current building officials in Georgia credit Michael with encouraging them to pursue leadership roles they might not have otherwise considered. He frequently emphasizes that the future of building safety in Georgia depends on cultivating competent, ethical, and service-minded professionals, and he models that philosophy every day.

4. Impact on BOAG and the broader Georgia building safety community:
Through his roles as BOAG Board Director, Vice President, President, and Education Committee Chair, Michael has helped shape BOAG into a more inclusive, education-focused organization. Under his leadership on the Education Committee, BOAG expanded its training calendar, increased the variety of topics offered, and introduced support for smaller jurisdictions that lack travel budgets by rotating training locations and offering virtual options. Michael has also been instrumental in fostering collaboration between building officials, fire marshals, and emergency management personnel, recognizing that true community resilience requires these disciplines to work together.

5. Alignment with the values of the Hal Cosper Memorial Lifetime Achievement Award:
The essence of this award is to recognize individuals whose careers reflect exceptional dedication, leadership, and service to the building safety profession in Georgia. Michael’s career is a clear match to that description. Like Hal Cosper, Michael is known for:
- Putting public safety and ethical practice above personal convenience or political pressure;
- Treating colleagues, permit applicants, and citizens with respect and patience, even in difficult situations;
- Giving generously of his time to teach, mentor, and support others without seeking personal recognition;
- Working behind the scenes to improve processes, codes, and training resources for the benefit of the entire state.

6. Personal character and everyday actions:
Beyond titles and committees, what truly sets Michael apart are the small, consistent acts of service that rarely appear on a résumé:
- He is often the first to arrive and the last to leave at BOAG conferences, helping set up projectors, registration tables, and handouts.
- He is the person who quietly checks in on colleagues who are going through personal or family challenges and offers to cover inspections or classes so they can take care of themselves.
- When smaller jurisdictions call with questions about complex code issues, Michael never treats those calls as an interruption; instead, he patiently walks them through the analysis and shares sample documents, policies, or checklists that his own city uses.

7. Lasting legacy:
Because of Michael’s work, the City of Peachtree Ridge has a stronger, more modern, and more responsive building safety program. More importantly, hundreds of inspectors, building officials, design professionals, contractors, and students have been influenced by his teaching and example. The policies, checklists, and training materials he has developed will continue to be used long after he retires. His emphasis on integrity, education, and collaboration has helped elevate the profession in Georgia.

For these reasons—his longevity of service, his statewide impact, his unwavering commitment to public safety, and his quiet but powerful leadership—Michael D. Harrington is exceptionally deserving of the Hal Cosper Memorial Lifetime Achievement Award.

answers.value:
Michael has consistently demonstrated that his commitment to public safety extends well beyond his official duties as Chief Building Official.

1. Local civic and volunteer involvement:
- Peachtree Ridge Community Emergency Response Team (CERT): Michael has been an active volunteer instructor with the city’s CERT program since 2009. He regularly leads sessions on building safety, post-disaster building assessment, and safe use of generators and temporary structures after storms. He has personally trained more than 300 residents in basic building safety concepts.
- Habitat for Humanity – Gwinnett County: Since 2010, Michael has volunteered several weekends each year as a construction safety advisor for local Habitat builds. He reviews framing, electrical rough-ins, and stair/guard installations to ensure donated labor still results in safe, code-compliant homes, and he mentors young volunteers who are interested in construction trades.
- Peachtree Ridge High School Career and Technical Education (CTE) Advisory Board: Michael has served on the advisory board since 2015, helping shape the curriculum for the construction pathway and speaking twice per year to classes about careers in building safety, inspections, and code enforcement.

2. Public education and outreach:
- Annual “Building Safety Month” events: For over a decade, Michael has organized and led the city’s participation in ICC’s Building Safety Month. He sets up interactive displays in the lobby of City Hall, prepares simple handouts explaining smoke alarm placement, deck safety, and basement egress requirements, and hosts evening Q&A sessions where homeowners can bring photos or sketches of planned projects and ask questions before they apply for permits.
- Senior citizen outreach: Recognizing that many older residents live in aging homes, Michael worked with the local senior center to offer an annual “Home Safety Checkup” seminar. He developed a checklist for seniors to evaluate trip hazards, handrail needs, and fire safety issues, and he coordinates with local non-profits to help low-income seniors correct the most critical hazards.

3. Professional mentoring and development of others:
- Mentor to new inspectors: Within his jurisdiction and across neighboring cities, Michael is informally known as the person to call when a new inspector or building official is struggling. He has mentored at least a dozen inspectors who have gone on to become senior inspectors or building officials in other Georgia jurisdictions. He often spends his own time after hours helping them study for ICC certification exams.
- Guest speaker at civic clubs: Michael regularly speaks at Rotary Club, Chamber of Commerce, and neighborhood association meetings. His talks focus on how the code is not just a set of rules but a tool to protect families, first responders, and property owners. He has a particular talent for translating technical code language into plain English for business owners and homeowners.

4. Regional and statewide initiatives:
- Post-disaster building safety evaluations: Michael is part of the Georgia post-disaster building safety volunteer cadre. He has deployed after several major storm events, including tornado damage in northwest Georgia and severe flooding in 2018, conducting rapid safety assessments of damaged structures and helping coordinate mutual aid between jurisdictions.

In all of these civic activities, Michael’s approach is patient, educational, and service-oriented. He is known for returning calls from citizens on evenings and weekends and for treating every question—no matter how basic—as an opportunity to build trust and understanding about building safety.

answers.value:
Over a 35-year career in code enforcement, Michael has amassed a record of accomplishments that have had lasting impact within his jurisdiction, throughout Georgia, and within ICC Region VIII.

1. Leadership in modernizing building safety operations:
- Digital permitting and inspection platform: Michael led the City of Peachtree Ridge’s transition from a fully paper-based permitting process to a modern, online permitting and inspection system implemented between 2012 and 2014. He chaired the internal working group that mapped all existing workflows, selected the software vendor, and oversaw staff training. As a result, average permit turnaround time for single-family residential projects was reduced from 10 business days to 4, and inspection scheduling accuracy and transparency improved substantially.
- Standardized inspection checklists: Recognizing inconsistencies among inspectors, Michael developed detailed, code-referenced inspection checklists for residential and commercial inspections. These checklists have been shared with multiple surrounding jurisdictions and are now widely used as a training tool for new inspectors across Metro Atlanta.

2. Contributions to code development and adoption in Georgia:
- State code advisory role: As an active member of BOAG’s Code Advisory and Education Committees, Michael has provided written comments and in-person testimony on several Georgia state amendments to the International Building Code and International Residential Code, particularly in the areas of fire-resistance-rated construction and deck safety.
- Deck safety advocacy: After responding to several deck collapses early in his career, Michael became a vocal advocate for improved deck design and construction standards. He helped draft local deck construction guidelines, which were later adapted by other Georgia jurisdictions, and he has taught numerous seminars on proper ledger attachment, guard design, and flashing installation.

3. Training and education achievements:
- ICC-certified instructor: Michael became an ICC-certified instructor in 2015 and has since taught dozens of courses across Georgia on topics such as Means of Egress, Fire-Resistance-Rated Construction, Existing Building Code, and Residential Inspections. His classes are consistently well-reviewed, and he is frequently requested by name for BOAG and regional training events.
- Development of in-house training program: Within his own department, Michael created a structured “Inspector Development Program” that outlines the competencies and training milestones for entry-level inspectors to progress to senior positions. This program includes ride-alongs, code study sessions, and cross-training with zoning, fire, and public works staff.

4. Recognition and awards:
- BOAG President’s Award (2017): Michael received the BOAG President’s Award in 2017 in recognition of his leadership in statewide education initiatives and his role in strengthening collaboration between building officials, fire marshals, and emergency management.
- City of Peachtree Ridge Employee of the Year (2011): Michael was selected by his peers and city leadership for his exemplary service during a period of rapid development and for his fair, consistent approach to enforcement.

5. Notable projects and public safety impacts:
- Mixed-use town center redevelopment: Michael oversaw the building safety aspects of the city’s flagship mixed-use town center project, which included multi-story residential over retail, structured parking, and a central civic plaza. He worked closely with developers, design professionals, and the fire department to ensure safe egress, fire separation, and accessibility, while still supporting the city’s economic development goals.
- School safety enhancements: Michael collaborated with the local school district on a multi-year program to review existing school buildings for egress, fire alarm, and accessibility issues. His team identified and helped correct numerous deficiencies, including blocked exits, outdated alarms, and missing or inadequate signage.

6. Professional certifications and continuous learning:
- Michael maintains multiple ICC certifications, including Certified Building Official (CBO), Commercial Building Inspector, Residential Building Inspector, Plans Examiner, and Accessibility Inspector/Plans Examiner. He is known for staying current with each new code cycle and for sharing that knowledge generously with colleagues.

Collectively, these achievements demonstrate not only technical competence but also a sustained commitment to making the built environment safer, more accessible, and more resilient.

answers.value:
Michael D. Harrington exemplifies the spirit of the Hal Cosper Memorial Lifetime Achievement Award through his sustained, decades-long commitment to building safety, his leadership within BOAG and ICC Region VIII, and his deep personal investment in mentoring the next generation of code officials.

1. Length and breadth of service:
Michael began his career as an entry-level building inspector in 1989 and has served continuously in code enforcement roles for more than 35 years. Over that time, he has progressed from field inspector to plans examiner, deputy building official, and ultimately Chief Building Official for the City of Peachtree Ridge. At each step, he has not only fulfilled his job responsibilities but has also sought out opportunities to raise the standards of practice for everyone around him.

2. Reputation for fairness, integrity, and professionalism:
Within the design and construction community, Michael has earned a reputation as a firm but fair code official. Architects and contractors often remark that, even when they disagree with his interpretation, they know he is consistent, well-prepared, and focused on safety rather than bureaucracy. He is known for taking the time to walk job sites with contractors, explain the underlying life-safety rationale for requirements, and work collaboratively to find compliant solutions instead of simply issuing red tags and walking away.

3. Dedication to mentoring and succession in the profession:
One of the distinguishing aspects of Michael’s career is his focus on developing others. He has personally helped numerous inspectors prepare for and pass their first ICC certification exams, often meeting with them early in the morning before work or staying late to review practice questions. Several current building officials in Georgia credit Michael with encouraging them to pursue leadership roles they might not have otherwise considered. He frequently emphasizes that the future of building safety in Georgia depends on cultivating competent, ethical, and service-minded professionals, and he models that philosophy every day.

4. Impact on BOAG and the broader Georgia building safety community:
Through his roles as BOAG Board Director, Vice President, President, and Education Committee Chair, Michael has helped shape BOAG into a more inclusive, education-focused organization. Under his leadership on the Education Committee, BOAG expanded its training calendar, increased the variety of topics offered, and introduced support for smaller jurisdictions that lack travel budgets by rotating training locations and offering virtual options. Michael has also been instrumental in fostering collaboration between building officials, fire marshals, and emergency management personnel, recognizing that true community resilience requires these disciplines to work together.

5. Alignment with the values of the Hal Cosper Memorial Lifetime Achievement Award:
The essence of this award is to recognize individuals whose careers reflect exceptional dedication, leadership, and service to the building safety profession in Georgia. Michael’s career is a clear match to that description. Like Hal Cosper, Michael is known for:
- Putting public safety and ethical practice above personal convenience or political pressure;
- Treating colleagues, permit applicants, and citizens with respect and patience, even in difficult situations;
- Giving generously of his time to teach, mentor, and support others without seeking personal recognition;
- Working behind the scenes to improve processes, codes, and training resources for the benefit of the entire state.

6. Personal character and everyday actions:
Beyond titles and committees, what truly sets Michael apart are the small, consistent acts of service that rarely appear on a résumé:
- He is often the first to arrive and the last to leave at BOAG conferences, helping set up projectors, registration tables, and handouts.
- He is the person who quietly checks in on colleagues who are going through personal or family challenges and offers to cover inspections or classes so they can take care of themselves.
- When smaller jurisdictions call with questions about complex code issues, Michael never treats those calls as an interruption; instead, he patiently walks them through the analysis and shares sample documents, policies, or checklists that his own city uses.

7. Lasting legacy:
Because of Michael’s work, the City of Peachtree Ridge has a stronger, more modern, and more responsive building safety program. More importantly, hundreds of inspectors, building officials, design professionals, contractors, and students have been influenced by his teaching and example. The policies, checklists, and training materials he has developed will continue to be used long after he retires. His emphasis on integrity, education, and collaboration has helped elevate the profession in Georgia.

For these reasons—his longevity of service, his statewide impact, his unwavering commitment to public safety, and his quiet but powerful leadership—Michael D. Harrington is exceptionally deserving of the Hal Cosper Memorial Lifetime Achievement Award.
