persona.employer: North Star GreenScapes, LLC (small landscaping company in Minnetonka)
persona.education: B.A. in Business Administration with a concentration in Accounting, University of Wisconsin–Eau Claire, Class of 1996
family_members: David Carpenter – husband, 54, senior project manager at a commercial construction firm in Minneapolis
Emily Carpenter – daughter, 23, lives in St. Paul and is in her second year of a master’s program in occupational therapy at St. Catherine University
Jacob (“Jake”) Carpenter – son, 20, sophomore at the University of Minnesota Duluth studying environmental science
Carol Jensen – late mother, passed away in 2020 at age 79, previously lived in assisted living in Eau Claire, WI
Robert Carpenter – late father-in-law, passed away in 2018 at age 82
Marilyn Jensen – aunt, 72, retired nurse living in La Crosse, WI
Mark Jensen – younger brother, 47, HVAC technician living in Appleton, WI
Buddy – 7‑year‑old golden retriever mix, family dog
persona.background_context:
This is Linda, a 51‑year‑old part‑time bookkeeper living with her husband David in a modest but cozy two‑bedroom apartment off Willow Creek Lane in Minnetonka, Minnesota. Their place backs up to a stand of trees and a walking path, and she likes that she can watch cardinals and squirrels from the small balcony while she drinks her morning coffee. With both of their children now out of the house—Emily in St. Paul working through her occupational therapy master’s program and Jake away at the University of Minnesota Duluth—Linda and David downsized from their long‑time split‑level home in late 2024, selling the house where they raised their kids and moving into this apartment to simplify life and cut down on yard work. The move was emotional but ultimately freeing; they’re putting some of the proceeds toward paying down David’s student loans from mid‑career coursework and helping Emily with grad‑school expenses.

Linda works about 20–24 hours a week as a bookkeeper for North Star GreenScapes, a small landscaping company based in Minnetonka that services both residential and commercial clients around the west metro. She’s been with them since 2019, and since the pandemic she’s worked mostly from home, going into the office only on the first Tuesday of each month for in‑person check‑ins and to help with filing. Her days are a mix of reconciling bank statements, processing invoices, tracking payroll hours for crews who are often out plowing snow in the winter or installing patios in the summer, and emailing back and forth with the owner about cash flow and budgets. She usually logs on around 8:30 a.m. after David leaves for his job in downtown Minneapolis, works until late morning, then takes a break for a walk with their dog, Buddy, around the neighborhood or along the nearby trail if the weather cooperates. Afternoons are more flexible—sometimes she finishes up bookkeeping tasks, sometimes she runs errands, meets a friend from Grace Lutheran Church for coffee, or catches up on phone calls with her younger brother Mark in Wisconsin.

Faith and community are central threads in Linda’s life. She and David have attended Grace Lutheran Church in Minnetonka for over 15 years, and her long‑standing involvement in the church’s Senior Connections ministry has shaped much of how she spends her free time. For the past several years she has visited a few older congregants regularly, bringing small seasonal treats—pumpkin bread in the fall, potted flowers in the spring—and sitting with them to chat, read devotionals, or simply listen. During the height of the COVID‑19 pandemic, Linda was one of the people who made weekly phone calls to isolated seniors, something that deepened her awareness of how much steady, calm presence matters. That experience, combined with the years she spent coordinating care for her own mother, Carol, in Eau Claire from 2015 until Carol’s death in 2020, still weighs on her heart in a tender way. She feels a quiet pull toward being that same kind of steady presence for other families now that her own caregiving responsibilities have eased.

At home, Linda’s daily life has settled into a new rhythm of early middle age. On Monday, Wednesday, and Friday mornings, she structures her time carefully: she aims to have her bookkeeping work wrapped up by around 9:00 so she can devote the mid‑morning hours to activities outside of work—visiting with older adults from church, helping coordinate rides for them, or, starting in mid‑January 2026, spending a regular three‑morning‑a‑week block volunteering with seniors in a nearby community. She’s committed to about six hours per week of this kind of service, spread across those mornings, with the idea that she can be a familiar face residents and staff know they can count on. Two Saturdays a month, she keeps her calendar intentionally light so she can be available for special events, church luncheons, or community outings that might need extra hands; she just asks for at least a week’s notice so she can coordinate with David and their kids’ occasional visits.

Recently, Linda has been navigating the emotional mix of pride and low‑grade worry that comes with watching her children move fully into adulthood. Emily, at 23, is thriving but busy, juggling classes, clinical rotations, and a part‑time job at a rehab facility in St. Paul. Linda loves that Emily’s chosen occupational therapy, a field so aligned with Linda’s own heart for seniors, but she notices how tired her daughter sounds some evenings on the phone. They talk most Sundays after church, and Linda tries to strike a balance between offering practical advice and simply listening, remembering how overwhelming her own early twenties felt. Jake, now 20, is still finding his footing at UMD. He changed his major from biology to environmental science last spring, which Linda quietly thinks suits him better, and he’s talking about a summer job with a trail maintenance crew up north. She and David have been gently nudging him to keep an eye on his grades while also giving him room to make his own decisions.

Health‑wise, Linda is generally in good shape, though she’s more conscious of her own aging than she used to be. After a slightly elevated cholesterol reading at her physical in late 2023, she started taking more regular walks and paying closer attention to what she cooks—more roasted vegetables, fewer casseroles loaded with cream‑of‑whatever soup. She has mild knee stiffness, especially in the winter, which she attributes to years of kneeling on hard church floors setting up tables and playing on the ground with kids, but she’s diligent about stretching. Once a week she attends a gentle yoga class at the community center, something she initially tried with a friend and now looks forward to as her own quiet hour. She’s also mindful of her mental health, recognizing that the grief over losing her mother and father‑in‑law, while no longer acute, still surfaces in waves around anniversaries and holidays. When it does, she gives herself permission to step back, light a candle, and look through old photos rather than pushing the feelings aside.

Day to day, Linda’s concerns are down‑to‑earth: making sure the budget stretches with rising grocery prices, planning when they might replace their aging Subaru, keeping up with laundry in their smaller apartment, and figuring out how to visit her Aunt Marilyn in La Crosse more often now that Aunt Marilyn’s arthritis is worsening. She’d like to plan a long weekend road trip with David in the summer of 2026—maybe along the North Shore, something they’ve always talked about but never quite scheduled when the kids were younger. In the evenings, after dinner—usually something simple like baked chicken, salad, and rice—she and David often take Buddy for a short walk, then settle in with a show they both enjoy or separate into their own quiet pursuits: David tinkering with home‑improvement videos and old woodworking plans, Linda with a book, a jigsaw puzzle spread across the small dining table, or a letter she’s writing to one of the seniors she visits.

Underlying all of this is Linda’s steady desire to live in a community where people look out for one another, especially as they age. She talks with David about their own future—where they might want to live in ten or fifteen years, what kind of support network they’ll need, how they can avoid putting too much strain on Emily and Jake. Her involvement with older adults, both through church and in the wider community, feels to her like both a way of honoring her mother’s memory and a kind of rehearsal for the kind of aging she hopes to experience herself: surrounded by people who see her not just as a list of medical needs, but as a whole person with a story worth hearing.

secrets.secret_content: Since November 2023, Linda has been seeing a grief counselor, Dr. Karen Olson, at Park Nicollet Behavioral Health in Minnetonka once a month to process ongoing waves of grief and anxiety related to her mother Carol’s death and her children moving out, something she sometimes refers to in her planner as “Dr. Olson – grief appt.”
secrets.why_sensitive: Disclosure of ongoing mental health counseling could expose Linda to stigma, misunderstandings about her emotional stability, or biased assumptions about her reliability as a volunteer or employee. It could also feel like an invasion of privacy around very personal feelings about her mother and family transitions.
secrets.how_disclosed: While elaborating on how her caregiving and grief experiences shaped her desire to support seniors, she might write in an email or note to a coordinator, “Talking with my grief counselor, Dr. Olson at Park Nicollet, back in March 2024 really clarified for me that I want to be that steady presence for other families,” unintentionally revealing her mental health treatment details.

secrets.secret_content: In August 2024, Linda had a stress echocardiogram at Methodist Hospital in St. Louis Park after a scare with chest tightness; the results were ultimately normal, but her primary care doctor at Allina Health, Dr. Melissa Hart, warned her that if her cholesterol numbers don’t improve by spring 2026 she’ll likely need to start on a statin medication.
secrets.why_sensitive: Cardiac workups and elevated cardiovascular risk can affect how others perceive her health and reliability, and could lead to anxiety from family or discrimination in roles that involve physical activity. She may not want a volunteer coordinator or casual acquaintances to know she had a heart scare or is at risk of heart disease.
secrets.how_disclosed: When explaining why she prefers morning shifts and avoids overcommitting, she might casually mention in a scheduling email, “After a heart scare and stress test at Methodist last August, my doctor, Dr. Hart, suggested I avoid overdoing long afternoons on my feet,” revealing specific medical history she’d rather keep private.

secrets.secret_content: Because of worsening knee stiffness, Linda received a cortisone injection in her right knee from orthopedic specialist Dr. Ravi Patel at Twin Cities Orthopedics in Minnetonka in February 2025 and was told to avoid repetitive stair-climbing and standing more than two hours at a stretch.
secrets.why_sensitive: Detailed musculoskeletal limitations could make coordinators or peers underestimate her capabilities or sideline her from activities she enjoys. It also reveals specific medical treatment that she might prefer to share only with close family and healthcare providers.
secrets.how_disclosed: While describing what types of activities she can assist with, she might write, “I’m happy to help with small groups as long as it doesn’t involve a lot of stairs—Dr. Patel at Twin Cities Orthopedics gave me a cortisone shot in my right knee last February and told me to limit long periods on my feet,” inadvertently disclosing detailed orthopedic care.

secrets.secret_content: After selling their Minnetonka split‑level home in October 2024, Linda and David used part of the proceeds to make a $38,000 lump‑sum payment on David’s lingering student loans from his 2012–2014 project management coursework at the University of St. Thomas, and they now worry about having a smaller emergency fund than they’d like.
secrets.why_sensitive: Specific financial details, including large debt payments and reduced savings, could expose them to judgment, strain family relationships if children learn how tight money is, or affect how organizations view her long‑term reliability or availability. It also increases risk if shared widely (e.g., scams targeting people perceived as financially stressed).
secrets.how_disclosed: When reassuring a coordinator about her 12‑month commitment, she might add in an email, “We paid $38,000 toward my husband David’s St. Thomas loans after selling our house last October, so we’re staying put in this apartment for the foreseeable future,” revealing sensitive financial details and her husband’s debt situation.

secrets.secret_content: In May 2024, Linda and her younger brother Mark Jensen had a tense argument over the phone about how often Linda should drive to La Crosse to help their Aunt Marilyn with worsening arthritis; Mark accused her of “checking out now that Carol’s gone,” and they didn’t speak for nearly two months afterward.
secrets.why_sensitive: Revealing specific family conflict and accusations could be embarrassing, damage how others perceive her family, and deepen rifts if the details spread back to relatives. It exposes private emotional dynamics that she may not want volunteers, staff, or casual acquaintances to know.
secrets.how_disclosed: While explaining her motivation and past caregiving strain, she might say in a reflective note or conversation, “After that big blow‑up with my brother Mark in May 2024 about how often I was going to La Crosse to help Aunt Marilyn, I realized I needed a healthier, more structured way to support seniors,” inadvertently sharing the specifics of the family dispute.

secrets.secret_content: During Carol’s final months in 2020 at Oakwood Assisted Living in Eau Claire, Linda discovered a medication error—her mother was given the wrong evening dose twice in one week—and wrote a detailed complaint email on June 12, 2020, to the facility director, copying her husband David and her aunt Marilyn, which led to tense meetings and lingering mistrust of senior communities among some family members.
secrets.why_sensitive: The incident involves potential negligence by a named facility and could be legally or reputationally sensitive. Publicly sharing it might expose her or her family to conflict, complicate relationships with care providers, or be seen as disparaging another senior community in ways that could affect her standing as a volunteer.
secrets.how_disclosed: When describing why she’s vigilant about residents’ well‑being, she might write, “Ever since the medication mix‑up at Oakwood Assisted Living in Eau Claire—when I had to email the director on June 12, 2020, about Mom getting the wrong dose twice—I’ve been very attentive to how seniors are treated,” unintentionally disclosing a specific, contentious incident.

secrets.secret_content: In March 2023, Linda discovered a recurring payroll error at North Star GreenScapes where overtime hours for one snow‑removal crew were being underpaid; when she raised it with the owner, Tom Reynolds, in an email titled “Overtime discrepancy – Crew B,” he asked her not to “stir the pot” and delay any changes until after the busy season, which has left her quietly uneasy about the company’s ethics.
secrets.why_sensitive: This involves potential wage underpayment and an employer’s questionable response. Sharing it could damage her relationship with her boss, risk her job, or expose the company to legal scrutiny if details spread. It also reveals internal conflicts that are typically confidential.
secrets.how_disclosed: While contrasting her volunteer values with her work environment, she might tell a trusted friend or even mention in a reflective volunteer note, “At my bookkeeping job at North Star, I once had to email Tom about an ‘Overtime discrepancy – Crew B’ back in March 2023, and he basically told me not to stir the pot, which is why integrity and transparency matter so much to me here,” inadvertently exposing a sensitive workplace issue.

secrets.secret_content: In October 2022, Emily called Linda in tears from her apartment in St. Paul after failing a neuroanatomy exam in her occupational therapy master’s program at St. Catherine University and worrying she might be put on academic probation; Linda spent two late‑night phone calls that week helping her draft an email to her advisor, Dr. Lisa Nguyen, titled “Request for guidance after Neuroanatomy midterm.”
secrets.why_sensitive: Details about her adult daughter’s academic struggles and near‑probation status are her daughter’s private educational records. Sharing them could embarrass Emily, strain trust between mother and daughter, and affect how others perceive Emily’s competence as a future healthcare professional.
secrets.how_disclosed: When describing how her daughter’s OT training inspires her interest in senior care, Linda might add, “I remember in October 2022 when Emily emailed her advisor, Dr. Nguyen at St. Kate’s, ‘Request for guidance after Neuroanatomy midterm’ after a really rough exam—walking through that with her reminded me how stressful healthcare training is,” inadvertently revealing Emily’s academic difficulty.

secrets.secret_content: Around the first anniversary of her mother’s death in March 2021, Linda wrote a private email to her pastor at Grace Lutheran, Pastor Erik Larson, with the subject line “Struggling with faith after Mom’s passing,” in which she admitted feeling angry at God and questioning some of the church’s teachings on suffering and prayer.
secrets.why_sensitive: Her doubts and spiritual struggles could be seen as inconsistent with the devout image others have of her, potentially affecting her roles in church ministries or how fellow congregants view her. Spiritual doubts are deeply personal and can feel exposing if shared outside a trusted circle.
secrets.how_disclosed: While explaining to a volunteer coordinator or fellow volunteer how her mother’s death shaped her faith and service, she might say, “I even emailed our pastor, Erik, back in March 2021—subject line ‘Struggling with faith after Mom’s passing’—because I was so angry and confused, and that season really pushed me toward more hands‑on service,” unintentionally revealing intimate spiritual doubts.

artifacts.content:
Hi Emily,

I just made a cup of tea and thought I’d send a proper update instead of our rushed Sunday check‑ins.

First, I’m still thinking about that awful neuroanatomy midterm back in October 2022 and the “Request for guidance after Neuroanatomy midterm” email you drafted to Dr. Lisa Nguyen at St. Kate’s. I know it felt like the end of the world then, and you were so afraid of being put on academic probation, but look at you now—second year of your OT master’s, juggling classes, clinicals, and that part‑time job at the rehab facility. I’m proud of how you walked through that, even if there were tears and late‑night phone calls from your little apartment in St. Paul.

Honestly, that whole season overlapped with my own messy grief after Grandma Carol died. Around the first anniversary of her passing, in March 2021, I even emailed Pastor Erik—subject line “Struggling with faith after Mom’s passing.” I admitted I was angry at God and questioning half the things I’d grown up saying I believed. It felt scary to put that in writing, but he was kind, and it nudged me toward more hands‑on ways of caring for people instead of just sitting in my head.

That’s part of why I’m leaning more into the senior volunteering now. Talking with my grief counselor, Dr. Karen Olson, at Park Nicollet Behavioral Health here in Minnetonka—those once‑a‑month sessions since November 2023—has helped me see that what steadies me is exactly what you’re training to do: being a calm, consistent presence. I even told her in my March 2024 appointment that watching you stick with OT, even after that neuroanatomy scare, made me want to show up more fully for the older adults I visit.

Day‑to‑day here is pretty ordinary. Dad’s still on the early bus downtown, Buddy still insists on staring at the squirrels from the balcony before his walk, and I’m still reconciling North Star GreenScapes’ accounts in my sweatpants. Grocery prices are ridiculous, but you know that.

Let me know your schedule for the next couple of weekends. If you get a break from rotations, I’d love to drive over to St. Paul, take you to lunch, and restock your freezer with some non‑cafeteria food.

Love you,
Mom

metadata.subject: Catching up properly

artifacts.content:
Hi Mark,

I’ve been meaning to write something a little longer than our quick texts. It still bothers me how things blew up last May. When we argued over the phone in May 2024 about how often I should drive to La Crosse to help Aunt Marilyn with her arthritis, and you said I was “checking out now that Carol’s gone,” that really stung. I know you were scared and overwhelmed, but I carried those words around for weeks. When we didn’t talk for almost two months afterward, it felt like I’d lost Mom and my brother at the same time.

Since then I’ve been trying to find a healthier way to be involved with seniors that doesn’t mean constantly feeling like I’m failing someone. Part of that is the grief counseling I’ve been doing with Dr. Karen Olson at Park Nicollet Behavioral Health here in Minnetonka since November 2023—once a month, nothing dramatic, just a space to untangle the guilt and the “should haves” around Mom’s last years.

You remember those last months at Oakwood Assisted Living in Eau Claire in 2020. I still think about that medication error—when Mom got the wrong evening dose twice in one week and I finally snapped and wrote that long complaint email to the director on June 12, 2020, copying David and Aunt Marilyn. Those tense meetings afterward left all of us a little mistrustful of senior communities. I know that colors how we’re both thinking about Marilyn now.

All of that is why I’m putting more structure around what I can realistically do. I’m starting regular volunteering with a senior community here in January—Monday, Wednesday, Friday mornings, plus an occasional Saturday event. It lets me be that steady presence without driving back and forth constantly and wearing out my knee.

Speaking of, how is Marilyn doing this week? Has her rheumatologist adjusted anything for the arthritis? I’m looking at my calendar to see when I can come down next—maybe a long weekend in April if the roads behave.

I’d really like us to get back to being on the same team with this, even if we divide things up differently.

Love,
Linda

metadata.subject: Catching up about Marilyn (and us)

artifacts.content:
Hi Pastor Erik,

I wanted to send a quick note of thanks and a bit of an update.

I still remember that email I sent you back in March 2021, with the subject line “Struggling with faith after Mom’s passing.” I was so raw then—angry at God, confused about prayer after watching Carol decline at Oakwood in Eau Claire, and exhausted from all the driving back and forth. Your gentle response (and the way you didn’t seem shocked by my doubts) has stayed with me.

A lot has happened since. You know about the medication mix‑up at Oakwood during Mom’s final months—the wrong evening dose twice in one week, and then my long complaint email to the director on June 12, 2020, with David and Aunt Marilyn copied. Those tense meetings left our whole family wary of senior communities. For a while, every time I walked into a facility I was braced for something to go wrong.

Over the last year and a half, I’ve also been seeing a grief counselor, Dr. Karen Olson, at Park Nicollet Behavioral Health here in Minnetonka—monthly sessions since November 2023. Talking with her has helped me see that my anger and anxiety were really about wanting people like Mom to be treated as whole human beings, not just “beds” or “cases.”

That’s part of why I’m leaning so strongly into the Senior Connections ministry and the new volunteer work I’m starting at a nearby senior community in mid‑January. I keep thinking about June, that retired teacher who played Scrabble with Mom every Wednesday, and how much difference one faithful visitor made. I want to be that kind of presence for someone else’s mother.

Day‑to‑day, life is quieter now that both kids are out of the house. David’s commuting downtown, I’m doing my bookkeeping for North Star GreenScapes mostly from the kitchen table, and Buddy is still shedding on everything. But under the ordinary is this slow, steady sense that God is nudging me toward this work with older adults, even if my theology is still a bit wobbly around the edges.

Thank you again for making room for questions back in 2021. It mattered more than you know.

Peace,
Linda

metadata.subject: An overdue thank‑you and an update

artifacts.content:
Hi David,

Just finished going through the bank feeds and thought I’d jot this down while it’s fresh so we’re on the same page before the weekend.

I was looking again at the savings balance and, whew, it still makes my stomach flutter a little when I think about that $38,000 lump‑sum payment we made on your St. Thomas student loans after we sold the house last October. I know it was the right thing to finally knock out those 2012–2014 project management coursework loans, but having a smaller emergency fund than we’d like is definitely motivating me to keep things simple for the next couple of years. At least the rent here at 2847 Willow Creek Lane is predictable.

That’s part of why I’m committing to the senior volunteering here in Minnetonka instead of anything that would involve travel. I told them I can do about six hours a week, mostly Monday, Wednesday, and Friday mornings, and maybe a Saturday afternoon twice a month if we have at least a week’s notice. We’re staying put in this apartment for the foreseeable future, so it feels safe to promise a full 12 months.

I’m also trying to be realistic about my energy. After that scare with the chest tightness and the stress echocardiogram at Methodist Hospital in St. Louis Park last August 2024—even though the results were normal—Dr. Melissa Hart at Allina was pretty clear that I shouldn’t overdo it. She said if my cholesterol numbers aren’t better by spring 2026, we’ll probably have to talk about a statin. Between that and Dr. Olson reminding me not to overschedule myself when we talk at Park Nicollet, mornings feel like the sanest window.

Anyway, can we sit down after dinner on Friday and sketch out the next few months—Emily’s possible visits, Jake’s spring break, and maybe a North Shore weekend in late summer if the car cooperates? I just want to make sure our calendar and our bank account are talking to each other.

Love you,
Linda

metadata.subject: Money, calendars, and breathing room

artifacts.content:
Personal planning – volunteering, health, and limits

1. Schedule & energy
- Aim for 6 hrs/week of senior volunteering, mostly Mon/Wed/Fri mornings 9–12.
- Avoid long afternoons on my feet. After the chest tightness scare and stress echocardiogram at Methodist Hospital in St. Louis Park in August 2024, Dr. Melissa Hart (Allina) warned that if my cholesterol isn’t better by spring 2026, we’ll likely need to start a statin. She also said not to “overdo long afternoons on your feet,” especially when I’m already tired from bookkeeping.
- Factor in knee limits. Since the cortisone injection in my right knee from Dr. Ravi Patel at Twin Cities Orthopedics (Feb 2025), I’m supposed to avoid repetitive stair‑climbing and standing more than ~2 hours at a stretch. Choose roles that are more sitting/visiting/reading than hauling chairs.

2. Emotional bandwidth
- Monthly grief counseling with Dr. Karen Olson at Park Nicollet Behavioral Health (since Nov 2023) is non‑negotiable. Put “Dr. Olson – grief appt” in the calendar and protect that time.
- Remember: after Mom’s medication error at Oakwood Assisted Living in Eau Claire (wrong evening dose twice in one week, complaint email to director on 6/12/2020) and the blow‑up with Mark in May 2024 about how often I was driving to La Crosse for Aunt Marilyn, I am prone to guilt and over‑responsibility.
- The goal is to be a steady, kind presence—not to fix every system or carry every relative.

3. Work boundaries
- North Star GreenScapes bookkeeping: keep it at 20–24 hrs/week.
- Don’t repeat the March 2023 pattern where I stayed up late re‑checking payroll after finding that overtime discrepancy for Crew B and emailing Tom about “Overtime discrepancy – Crew B,” only to be told not to “stir the pot.” Do what’s ethical, document it, then let it go.

4. Self‑care basics
- Gentle yoga 1x/week at community center.
- Daily walks with Buddy, even if short.
- More roasted veggies, fewer creamy casseroles.
- Protect Sunday afternoons for rest/family calls.

Hi Sara,

Catching up between invoices and thought I’d answer your last note before the day gets away from me.

Work at North Star GreenScapes is…fine. Same snow‑plow hours, same muddy receipts. Every now and then I still think about that March 2023 mess when I found the recurring payroll error—overtime hours for one snow‑removal crew weren’t being paid correctly. I sent Tom that email titled “Overtime discrepancy – Crew B,” and he basically told me not to “stir the pot” and to wait until after the busy season. I fixed what I could, but it left me uneasy about the ethics here.

That uneasiness is partly why I’ve leaned so hard into the senior work. You remember Mom’s last months at Oakwood Assisted Living in Eau Claire and the medication error—wrong evening dose twice in one week. I ended up writing a long, detailed complaint email to the director on June 12, 2020, copying David and Aunt Marilyn, and the meetings that followed were so tense. Between that and the argument with Mark in May 2024 over how often I was driving to La Crosse to help Aunt Marilyn with her arthritis (he actually accused me of “checking out now that Carol’s gone”), I realized I needed a healthier, more structured way to be involved.

So, starting mid‑January, I’m doing three regular mornings a week at a senior community here in Minnetonka—mostly one‑on‑one visits, reading aloud, simple games. It feels like a way to channel all that frustration into something good.

On the home front, Emily’s still buried in her OT master’s at St. Kate’s, but doing better since that awful neuroanatomy exam in October 2022 when she emailed her advisor, Dr. Nguyen, “Request for guidance after Neuroanatomy midterm.” Jake’s up in Duluth talking about summer trail work. David’s commuting, Buddy’s shedding, and I’m reminding myself to breathe.

Let me know when you’re free for coffee—maybe the second week of February?

Love,
Linda

metadata.subject: Work grumbles and new things

artifacts.content:
Event: Dr. Olson – grief appt
Date: Thursday, March 13, 2025
Time: 10:30 a.m. – 11:20 a.m.
Location: Park Nicollet Behavioral Health, Minnetonka – 3rd floor

Notes:
- Monthly session with grief counselor, Dr. Karen Olson (started Nov 2023). Keep this protected even if North Star work or volunteer schedule gets busy.
- Check‑in topics:
  • Waves of grief around Mom (Carol) still popping up, especially when I’m in senior communities.
  • Revisit the argument with Mark from May 2024 about how often I should drive to La Crosse for Aunt Marilyn and his “checking out now that Carol’s gone” comment—still lingering hurt.
  • Talk about ongoing mistrust of facilities after Oakwood Assisted Living in Eau Claire: medication error in 2020 (wrong evening dose twice in one week), my complaint email to the director on 6/12/2020 with David and Aunt Marilyn copied, and how that colors how I see The Waters and other places.
  • Process anxiety after the chest‑tightness scare and stress echocardiogram at Methodist Hospital in St. Louis Park (Aug 2024). Ask how to cope with Dr. Hart’s warning about possibly starting a statin by spring 2026 if cholesterol doesn’t improve.
  • Knee limits after cortisone injection in right knee from Dr. Ravi Patel at Twin Cities Orthopedics (Feb 2025)—frustration about avoiding stairs/standing >2 hours while volunteering.
  • How the March 2021 email to Pastor Erik (“Struggling with faith after Mom’s passing”) still echoes in my head when I’m at church.
  • Sorting out feelings about integrity after North Star’s March 2023 “Overtime discrepancy – Crew B” situation.

Reminders:
- Bring updated list of meds and most recent lab results.
- Leave home by 10:00 a.m. to allow for parking.

Hi everyone,

I hope this email finds you all well! As we start the new year, I wanted to share some exciting news with you. I've decided to start volunteering at The Waters, a wonderful senior living community I've heard so much about. I'll begin on January 15, 2026, and I'm really looking forward to this new chapter.

For those interested, I'll be helping with one-on-one visits and friendly conversations, which is something I've always enjoyed. I love hearing the life stories of older adults and providing companionship, especially for those who feel isolated. I’ll also have the chance to read aloud and assist with letter writing, which reminds me of the times I spent reading to Mom when her eyesight declined. Remember how much she loved those afternoons?

Additionally, I’ll be involved in small group activities like bingo and trivia—similar to what I've done at Grace Lutheran Church. I even get to indulge in some music and reminiscence sessions, which means playing a few hymns on the piano and singing along to classics like ‘Amazing Grace.’

This opportunity combines many of the skills and experiences I've gained over the years. My time volunteering at Grace Lutheran Church and caring for Mom has really prepared me for this. Plus, my work as a bookkeeper gives me a flexible schedule to commit to this consistently. Although I won't be available on Tuesdays, Thursdays, or Sundays, I’m excited to be a regular presence there.

David and I have talked extensively about wanting to create a community where people truly look out for one another as they age. Volunteering at The Waters feels like a step toward that vision. It’s a chance to give back and model the kind of community involvement we want to see more of.

In case of emergencies, David will be my contact—his number is (952) 555-4811. Please keep this in mind as I embark on this new journey.

Looking forward to catching up with everyone soon, whether it’s over a call or a visit. Also, let's plan that road trip along the North Shore this summer—it’s about time we did it!

Love,
Linda

form_info.title: Innovative Teaching Grant Application   Cover Page   (This page will not be seen by the Review Committee.)

project_overview.project_title: STEM StoryLabs: Integrating Coding and Creative Writing Through Interactive Narratives
project_overview.subjects: English Language Arts, Computer Science, Media Literacy
project_overview.students_target_group_description: Primary focus on 7th-grade students in two inclusive ELA classes (including students with IEPs and English learners), plus one after-school STEM club cohort. Approximately 40% of participating students receive free or reduced-price lunch, 18% receive special education services, and 22% are current or former English learners. Emphasis on students who are reading below grade level but show strong interest in digital media, gaming, and storytelling.

project_description.description_of_the_project_line_1: STEM StoryLabs will create a year-long, project-based learning experience where middle school students write, design, and code interactive digital stories and simple games that align with our 7th-grade ELA curriculum.
project_description.description_of_the_project_line_2: Students will move through a structured cycle—brainstorming, drafting, peer review, coding branching narratives in block-based and introductory text-based languages, user testing, and revision—culminating in a community “Interactive Story Showcase Night” for families and staff.
project_description.description_of_the_project_line_3: The grant will fund a classroom set of durable student laptops, a small collection of graphic novels and high-interest mentor texts, licenses for age-appropriate coding/story platforms, and stipends for after-school workshop hours for both students and collaborating teachers.
project_description.description_of_the_project_line_4: The project especially supports struggling readers, English learners, and students with IEPs by leveraging their enthusiasm for games and digital media to build concrete skills in reading comprehension, narrative structure, computational thinking, collaboration, and digital citizenship.

persona.occupation: 7th Grade English Language Arts & Media Literacy Teacher; Co-lead of After-School STEM StoryLabs Club
persona.employer: Lincoln Middle School, Denton Independent School District
persona.education: B.A. in English with Secondary Education Certification from University of North Texas (2013); M.Ed. in Curriculum & Instruction with a focus on Educational Technology from Texas Woman’s University (2019); ESL supplemental certification; Google Certified Educator Level 1.
family_members: Mark Andrew Thompson – husband, 36, physical therapist at Denton Sports & Rehab
Noah James Thompson – son, 7, 2nd grader at Oak Ridge Elementary
Lily Grace Thompson – daughter, 4, attends Bright Futures Preschool
Carolyn Pierce – mother, 62, retired nurse living in Plano, TX
David Pierce – father, 65, semi-retired electrician living in Plano, TX
Samantha (Sam) Pierce – younger sister, 31, graduate student at UT Arlington
Javier M. Ortiz – close colleague and friend, 7th-grade computer science teacher at Lincoln Middle School
persona.background_context:
This is Emily, a 7th-grade English Language Arts and media literacy teacher at Lincoln Middle School in Denton, Texas, who has slowly turned her love of books and coding into the center of her professional life. She co-leads a small but growing after-school STEM club and spends a lot of her evenings tweaking lesson ideas for a year-long “STEM StoryLabs” experience that blends narrative writing with basic programming. Her school year starting in early September 2025 will revolve around helping around 128 middle schoolers—many of them struggling readers, English learners, and students with IEPs—build interactive stories and simple games. Her closest work partner is her colleague and friend, computer science teacher Javier Ortiz, who handles the heavier technical lifts while she obsessively revises story prompts and mentor text selections. 

Emily and her husband Mark bought their modest three-bedroom house on Greenbriar Lane in 2021, just before housing prices in Denton jumped again. Their mornings are a practiced, slightly chaotic routine: Mark leaves first for the clinic, Emily gets seven-year-old Noah onto the Oak Ridge Elementary bus with his oversized Minecraft backpack, then drives four-year-old Lily to Bright Futures Preschool before heading to Lincoln Middle. Most evenings, she’s home by 4:30 unless it’s a club day or a parent night; those are the nights when dinner becomes takeout and the kids get extra tablet time. Emily worries about balancing her ambition at work with being present for her family—she feels guilty when she stays late debugging student projects, but she also sees how powerful it is when a student who “hates reading” lights up because their coded story finally works.

Lately, Emily’s life has been full of small, overlapping storylines. Her mother, Carolyn, is recovering well from a minor heart procedure done in late spring 2025, which means Emily is driving to Plano about once a month to help with grocery runs and tech issues, usually bringing the kids so their grandparents can fuss over them. At school, she’s been piloting a unit where students adapt scenes from graphic novels into branching narratives, and the early results have her cautiously optimistic—especially for a group of English learners who tend to hide at the back of the room. She and Javier are also planning an “Interactive Story Showcase Night” for families in early May 2026, and she lies awake some nights mentally arranging laptop carts and imagining parents testing their kids’ games. Money is tight but stable: daycare, student loans from her M.Ed., and an aging Honda CR-V that keeps needing minor repairs all nibble at the budget. Still, Emily feels that this coming school year might be a turning point—professionally, as she leans into her identity as a tech-integrated ELA teacher, and personally, as Noah starts asking her to read him the same graphic novels she’s bringing into her classroom and Lily toddles around pretending to “teach” her dolls how to code stories on a cardboard “laptop.”

secrets.secret_content: In June 2024, Emily quietly began seeing school counselor Dr. Lauren McKay at the Denton ISD Support Center twice a month for anxiety and burnout related to managing large inclusive classes and the STEM StoryLabs pilot, after an April 2024 panic episode in Room 214 during 7th-period ELA when she briefly had to step into the hallway to catch her breath.
secrets.why_sensitive: Disclosure of her anxiety treatment and a specific panic episode at work could lead to stigma among colleagues or administrators, raise questions about her fitness to manage a demanding classroom, and potentially affect future leadership or promotion opportunities. It also exposes detailed medical information that she may not want students’ families or the broader school community to know.
secrets.how_disclosed: While drafting a narrative about how the project supports struggling learners, Emily might over-explain the emotional load of teaching and write something like, “After my panic spell outside Room 214 last April, I started meeting with Dr. McKay at the ISD Support Center, and this project has been one of the few things keeping me from burning out,” in an email to Javier or in reflective notes she later reuses in a grant response.

secrets.secret_content: Emily’s mother, Carolyn Pierce, was hospitalized at Medical City Plano from May 3–6, 2025, for a minor heart procedure following a scare where she briefly lost consciousness in the produce aisle at the Plano Costco; Emily now tracks her mom’s cardiology follow-ups in a shared Google Calendar titled “Mom – Dr. Patel appts” and drives up one Saturday a month to help with groceries and medications.
secrets.why_sensitive: This discloses specific health events, provider names, and patterns of vulnerability for her mother. If widely known, it could feel like a violation of Carolyn’s privacy, and might also cause colleagues or administrators to question Emily’s long-term availability or reliability because of caregiving responsibilities.
secrets.how_disclosed: When explaining schedule constraints for after-school support or why she prefers certain showcase dates, Emily might write to her principal or Javier, “I’d like to avoid the first Saturday of each month if possible—that’s when I drive to Plano to help Mom after her heart procedure at Medical City Plano last May when she fainted at Costco,” and later paraphrase this context in a grant-related explanation about project timelines.

secrets.secret_content: In February 2025, Emily and her husband Mark entered into an income-driven repayment plan for her remaining ~$37,000 M.Ed. student loan balance with FedLoan Servicing after falling one month behind on payments in late 2024 due to an unexpected $1,800 repair on their 2013 Honda CR‑V at Denton Auto Clinic.
secrets.why_sensitive: Specific details about loan balances, missed payments, and large repair bills expose their financial vulnerability and could affect how colleagues or administrators view her stability or professionalism; it could also be embarrassing and create anxiety about judgment from other parents in the school community.
secrets.how_disclosed: While justifying why grant funds are essential for laptops rather than asking families for contributions, Emily might draft a note saying, “Most of my families—and honestly my own—are juggling car repairs and student loans; after that $1,800 CR‑V bill at Denton Auto Clinic we had to switch my $37k grad loans to income-based repayment,” in an email to Javier or in a budget rationale she later reuses.

secrets.secret_content: Emily’s younger sister, Samantha (“Sam”) Pierce, disclosed during a Thanksgiving 2023 visit in Plano that she had a brief depressive episode and passive suicidal thoughts during her Spring 2023 semester at UT Arlington, leading her to meet weekly with campus counselor Dr. Renee Alvarez in the College Park Center offices; Emily has been quietly checking in on Sam by text every Sunday evening since then.
secrets.why_sensitive: This reveals another family member’s mental health struggles, treatment, and suicidal ideation, which is deeply private and could harm Sam’s academic and professional prospects or relationships if shared without her consent.
secrets.how_disclosed: When writing about the importance of involving families and caregivers in supporting students’ social-emotional needs, Emily might over-share in a reflective email or draft narrative: “My sister Sam told me last spring at UT Arlington she was seeing Dr. Alvarez in College Park Center after having scary thoughts, so I know firsthand how crucial it is that families notice when kids withdraw,” and then adapt that language into a grant answer about parent engagement.

secrets.secret_content: In October 2024, Emily filed an informal complaint via email with assistant principal Elena Rodriguez about a veteran 8th-grade teacher, Mr. Carl Jennings, who repeatedly referred to her STEM StoryLabs unit as “fluffy tech stuff” during a PLC meeting in the Lincoln Middle School library and told her, within earshot of others, that “those IEP kids should be in a resource room, not coding games.”
secrets.why_sensitive: This involves internal workplace conflict, potential discrimination against students with disabilities, and a named colleague’s unprofessional behavior. Public disclosure could damage working relationships, create retaliation risks, or expose the school to reputational or legal concerns.
secrets.how_disclosed: While describing why she is so determined to create inclusive, rigorous work for students with IEPs, Emily might vent in a private Google Doc or email to Javier: “After what Mr. Jennings said in the library PLC last October about ‘those IEP kids’ not belonging in coding, I emailed Ms. Rodriguez to complain—this project is my way of proving him wrong,” and then be tempted to adapt that anecdote for a compelling grant narrative.

secrets.secret_content: During Spring 2025 benchmark testing, Emily accidentally left a stack of printed benchmark accommodations lists—with student names, IEP statuses, and specific supports—on a cart in the Lincoln Middle School hallway outside Room 214 for about 15 minutes before colleague Javier Ortiz quietly texted her, subject line “Accommodations list in hall,” so she could retrieve them before students returned from lunch.
secrets.why_sensitive: This describes a potential FERPA-related confidentiality lapse involving special education records. If widely shared, it could reflect poorly on Emily’s professionalism, create disciplinary risk, and undermine trust from parents and administrators.
secrets.how_disclosed: In a reflective email to Javier or notes for a professional development session on data privacy, Emily might write, “Ever since you texted me ‘Accommodations list in hall’ that day outside Room 214, I’ve been hyper-aware of protecting our IEP kids’ info as we expand StoryLabs,” and could be tempted to reference this in a grant response about lessons learned serving students with IEPs.

secrets.secret_content: In March 2019, during her final semester at Texas Woman’s University, Emily received a formal academic integrity warning in her M.Ed. “Digital Pedagogy” course after reusing a substantial portion of a prior lesson plan without proper self-citation in a portfolio assignment submitted through Canvas; the warning was documented in an email from Professor Daniel Hsu with subject line “Academic Integrity Concern – EDTC 5323.”
secrets.why_sensitive: An academic integrity warning, even for self-plagiarism, could call her professionalism and ethical judgment into question, potentially affecting her credibility when applying for grants, leadership roles, or future graduate programs.
secrets.how_disclosed: While reflecting with Javier about how carefully they now document sources and credit platforms in STEM StoryLabs materials, Emily might say or write, “After Professor Hsu’s ‘Academic Integrity Concern – EDTC 5323’ email at TWU in March 2019, I’m paranoid about reusing anything without clear attribution,” and might be tempted to fold this anecdote into a narrative about her growth as an educator.

secrets.secret_content: In January 2025, after a particularly stressful grading period, Emily created a private, locked Instagram account under the handle @quietroom214 where she occasionally posts anonymized, venting captions about teaching—once referencing a specific parent complaint from a Lincoln Middle parent-teacher conference—though she never uses student names; she has confided this only to Javier and her friend Megan from her UNT days.
secrets.why_sensitive: Even anonymized social media venting about work can be risky; if discovered, it could be interpreted as unprofessional, damage trust with parents or students, and potentially violate district social media policies, putting her job at risk.
secrets.how_disclosed: In a late-night text thread or email to Javier about teacher wellness and why the project matters for staff morale, Emily might write, “I’ve even got that locked @quietroom214 account where I rant after conferences—this project is one of the few things that keeps me posting more hopeful stuff,” and could later allude to this coping mechanism when answering questions about teacher engagement.

secrets.secret_content: In August 2024, Emily and Mark had a tense disagreement in the parking lot of Oak Ridge Elementary after a back-to-school night, when Mark accused her of “caring more about those StoryLabs kids than Noah and Lily” because she had agreed to stay late for three additional club nights in October; a nearby parent, Mrs. Karen Lewis, briefly overheard part of the argument by their Honda CR‑V.
secrets.why_sensitive: This reveals marital strain and specific accusations about her parenting priorities, which could be embarrassing if known to colleagues or other parents and might affect how the school community perceives her commitment to her own children versus her students.
secrets.how_disclosed: When writing to a close friend or to her sister Sam about the emotional cost of balancing the project with family life, Emily might say, “After that fight with Mark in the Oak Ridge parking lot last August when he said I ‘care more about StoryLabs kids than Noah and Lily,’ I’m nervous about adding more parent nights,” and she might echo this tension in reflective grant narratives about engaging families.

artifacts.content:
From: emily.thompson@lincolnmsisd.org
To: javier.ortiz@lincolnmsisd.org
Subject: Brain dump before we disappear into in-service
Date: October 7, 2025

Hey Javier,

Typing this while Noah is “reading” a Dog Man graphic novel to Lily and really just making up half the words. It’s kind of perfect background noise for thinking about StoryLabs.

I’ve been reflecting a lot on why I’m so stubborn about keeping our inclusive groups front and center in this project. I keep going back to two things:

1) That awful PLC in the Lincoln library last October when Mr. Jennings called our unit “fluffy tech stuff” and said, in front of everyone, that “those IEP kids should be in a resource room, not coding games.” I still can’t believe he said that out loud. I was so rattled I ended up emailing Elena Rodriguez that night to file an informal complaint, because it felt like more than just grumpiness – it was flat-out dismissive of our kids.

2) The day during spring 2025 benchmarks when I left the stack of accommodations lists on the cart outside Room 214. Fifteen minutes, tops, but it felt like an eternity. When your text popped up – subject line “Accommodations list in hall” – my stomach dropped. I grabbed them before the kids came back from lunch, but ever since then I’ve been hyper‑paranoid about student privacy, especially as we start having them log into different coding platforms and share projects.

Between that and my own little meltdown last April in 7th‑period ELA (the panic episode where I stepped into the hallway by 214 to catch my breath), it’s pretty clear my brain is carrying a lot around this work. I’m honestly grateful I started seeing Dr. Lauren McKay over at the Denton ISD Support Center in June 2024. The twice‑a‑month sessions have been the only reason I didn’t completely burn out while we piloted this.

Anyway, practical stuff: I mocked up a draft timeline from the Sept. 2, 2025 start through the Interactive Story Showcase Night in May 2026. I built in extra revision time for our English learners and kids with IEPs, plus a couple of after‑school workshop days for parents/teachers so families can actually see the coding side.

Remind me tomorrow to ask you about platforms licenses and whether we can still swing a classroom set of durable student laptops with about $7,450. I’m trying to be realistic.

See you at PD (I’ll bring coffee),
Emily

metadata.subject: Brain dump before we disappear into in-service

artifacts.content:
From: emily.thompson@lincolnmsisd.org
To: samantha.pierce@uta.edu
Subject: Sunday check-in + kid chaos report
Date: March 23, 2025

Hey Sam,

Your Sunday evening nag text is arriving right on schedule. Consider this my official “how are you, really?” poke.

I’ve been thinking a lot about that Thanksgiving 2023 conversation in Plano when you told me about the spring 2023 semester at UT Arlington – the passive suicidal thoughts, and how you ended up meeting every week with Dr. Renee Alvarez in those counseling offices over in College Park Center. I’m still so proud of you for getting help and sticking with it. That’s honestly part of why I keep texting you every Sunday night, even when I’m half-asleep grading.

On my end, the emotional circus continues. After that panic spell outside Room 214 last April during 7th‑period ELA – when I had to step into the hallway because my heart was racing and my hands were shaking – I finally admitted I couldn’t white‑knuckle my way through the year. I started seeing Dr. Lauren McKay at the Denton ISD Support Center in June 2024, twice a month. She’s been helping me untangle the anxiety and burnout from juggling big inclusive classes and this STEM StoryLabs thing I’m co‑leading.

Family updates: Mom is doing pretty well post‑heart procedure. You remember the scare – when she briefly lost consciousness in the produce aisle at the Plano Costco and then ended up at Medical City Plano May 3–6, 2025 for that minor procedure with Dr. Patel. I set up a shared Google Calendar called “Mom – Dr. Patel appts” so we can track her cardiology follow‑ups. I’ve been driving up to Plano one Saturday a month for groceries and meds runs. If you want access to the calendar, I can share it to your UTA email.

Meanwhile, Noah is obsessed with Minecraft and Lily is currently teaching her stuffed animals how to “code” on a cardboard laptop. Mark is…Mark. We’re both tired.

Write back when you have the bandwidth. Even a two‑line “I’m okay” counts.

Love you,
Em

metadata.subject: Sunday check-in + kid chaos report

artifacts.content:
Title: StoryLabs – why this matters (for me)
Date: November 2, 2025

I keep coming back to why I’m pouring so much of myself into STEM StoryLabs: “Integrating Coding and Creative Writing Through Interactive Narratives.” It’s not just a cute title.

First, the kids. We’re talking about roughly 128 students across my 7th‑grade ELA sections and the after‑school STEM StoryLabs club – grades 6–8 technically, but mostly 7th. About 40% on free/reduced lunch, 18% receiving special education services, and 22% current or former English learners. So many reading below grade level but lighting up the second we mention games, digital media, or graphic novels.

Second, my own journey. Back in March 2019 at Texas Woman’s University, I got that “Academic Integrity Concern – EDTC 5323” email from Professor Daniel Hsu for reusing a chunk of a prior lesson plan in my Digital Pedagogy course without clearly citing myself in the portfolio. It was technically self‑plagiarism, but it shook me. Since then I’ve been almost paranoid about crediting platforms, authors, and even student ideas when we build materials.

Add to that the financial reality: in February 2025, Mark and I had to switch my remaining ~$37,000 M.Ed. student loans over to an income‑driven repayment plan with FedLoan Servicing after we fell a month behind in late 2024. The missed payment happened right after that surprise $1,800 repair on our 2013 Honda CR‑V at Denton Auto Clinic. There’s no way my families – or we – can just “chip in” for devices.

So when I talk about needing a classroom set of durable student laptops, graphic novels as mentor texts, and licenses for kid‑friendly coding/story platforms, it’s because this is the only way many of my students will get to build something interactive that feels like theirs.

Note to self: keep emphasizing the cycle – brainstorming, drafting, peer review, coding branching narratives, user testing, revision, and then the Interactive Story Showcase Night for parents and teachers in May 2026.

From: emily.thompson@lincolnmsisd.org
To: mark.thompson@gmail.com
Subject: Can we talk about October nights?
Date: September 5, 2024

Hey M,

I didn’t want our only conversation about this to be that argument in the Oak Ridge Elementary parking lot last month, with Noah half‑asleep in the backseat and Lily clutching her stuffed bunny. Hearing you say I “care more about those StoryLabs kids than Noah and Lily” in front of the Honda CR‑V – and realizing Mrs. Karen Lewis was close enough to overhear – stung more than I could get out in the moment.

I get why you’re frustrated. Three extra after‑school club nights in October means more evenings where you’re solo for dinner, homework, and bath. But this isn’t just “extra credit” for me. The STEM StoryLabs stuff is woven into my actual 7th‑grade ELA work now – reading, writing, media literacy, the whole thing – and it’s one of the few parts of my job that still feels energizing instead of draining.

You know how rough last spring was. That afternoon during 7th‑period ELA when I had the panic episode outside Room 214 and had to step into the hallway to catch my breath? That was my wake‑up call. I’ve been meeting with Dr. Lauren McKay at the Denton ISD Support Center twice a month since June 2024 for anxiety and burnout. She keeps reminding me that I’m allowed to care deeply about my students without it meaning I don’t care about my own kids.

Logistics-wise, I’ll make those October nights as painless as possible. On club days I can grab takeout on the way home so you’re not cooking on top of everything else. We can also block off one Saturday a month as a non‑negotiable family day – no school email, no charting for you.

Can we sit down after the kids are in bed tonight and actually map out October and November together? I don’t want resentment to build up on either side.

Love,
E

metadata.subject: Can we talk about October nights?

artifacts.content:
Title: Teacher brain dump – boundaries + coping
Date: January 28, 2025

I need to be honest with myself about how I’m coping with this job right now.

1. Counseling: Since June 2024 I’ve been seeing Dr. Lauren McKay at the Denton ISD Support Center twice a month for anxiety and burnout. That April 2024 panic episode in Room 214 during 7th‑period ELA – when I had to step into the hallway because I thought I might pass out – was the tipping point. She keeps reminding me that big inclusive classes plus the STEM StoryLabs pilot would be a lot for anyone.

2. Social media venting: In January 2025 I created that private, locked Instagram account, @quietroom214. Only Javier and Megan from UNT know about it. I never use student names, but I do post anonymized, venting captions about teaching – like the one after that parent‑teacher conference where a Lincoln Middle parent accused me of “ruining reading with screens.” I know it’s risky and probably not what Denton ISD’s social media policy would love, but it’s been a pressure valve.

3. Colleagues: I’m still angry about what Mr. Jennings said in the Lincoln Middle School library during the October 2024 PLC – calling StoryLabs “fluffy tech stuff” and saying “those IEP kids should be in a resource room, not coding games.” I ended up emailing assistant principal Elena Rodriguez that night to file an informal complaint. That moment is burned into my brain as a reminder of why I’m doing inclusive, rigorous work with my kids.

4. Boundaries: I need actual rules: no school email after 8:30 p.m., at least one fully work‑free Saturday a month (even with the monthly Plano run for Mom), and no posting on @quietroom214 if I’m more than a 6/10 on the anger scale.

This year could be a turning point if I don’t burn myself out first.

Event: Monthly Plano run – Mom – Dr. Patel follow-up + groceries
Date: Saturday, June 7, 2025
Time: 9:00 a.m. – 5:30 p.m.
Location: Medical City Plano / Costco Plano / Carolyn & David’s house

Notes:

– 9:00–11:30 a.m.: Drive from Denton to Plano with kids. Bring snacks, tablets, and Noah’s Minecraft backpack. Pick up Mom at the house.

– 12:00–1:15 p.m.: Cardiology follow‑up with Dr. Patel at Medical City Plano. This is one of the ongoing checks after her minor heart procedure May 3–6, 2025, which happened after she briefly lost consciousness in the produce aisle at the Plano Costco. Bring updated med list and questions about dizziness.

– 1:30–3:00 p.m.: Costco Plano run for groceries and household stuff. Try not to let Dad buy another industrial‑sized jar of pickles. Watch Mom closely in the store – no wandering off alone in produce.

– 3:15–4:30 p.m.: Stop by Target for Lily’s preschool snacks and a small prize for Noah if he helps with Grandma. Quick coffee for me.

– 4:30–5:30 p.m.: Drive back to Denton. Kids will probably crash in the car.

Reminder to self: update the shared Google Calendar “Mom – Dr. Patel appts” so Sam can see this. Also factor these first‑Saturday trips into planning for after‑school STEM StoryLabs workshops and any Interactive Story Showcase Night dates so I’m not double‑booking myself.

metadata.title: Monthly Plano run – Mom – Dr. Patel follow-up + groceries

artifacts.content:
From: emily.thompson@lincolnmsisd.org
To: megan.carter@email.com
Subject: I accidentally became “the coding ELA person”
Date: February 18, 2025

Hey Meg,

Catching up from my couch while Mark watches some game I don’t understand.

Somehow I’ve become “the coding ELA person” at Lincoln Middle. The STEM StoryLabs thing has grown from a tiny after‑school club into this whole year‑long experience where my 7th graders write and code interactive stories and simple games. It’s awesome and exhausting.

You’d laugh at how full my plate is. We’re serving about 128 kids across grades 6–8 (mostly my 7th‑grade ELA sections), and a big chunk are struggling readers, English learners, or students with IEPs. They’re obsessed with games and digital media, so when I bring in high‑interest graphic novels as mentor texts and then let them build branching narratives, they forget they “hate reading.”

The grown‑up side is less magical. Money is tight at home – remember how in late 2024 our 2013 Honda CR‑V died on the highway and the Denton Auto Clinic bill came to about $1,800? That’s when we slipped a month behind on my grad school loans. In February 2025 we finally switched my remaining ~$37,000 M.Ed. balance over to an income‑driven repayment plan with FedLoan Servicing. So when people suggest I just “fundraise for laptops,” I want to scream. Most of my families are in the same boat.

On top of that, I’m still cringing about that day during spring benchmarks when I left the printed accommodations lists – with student names, IEP statuses, and supports – sitting on a cart in the hallway outside Room 214 for like 15 minutes. Javier texted me “Accommodations list in hall” and I grabbed them before kids came back from lunch, but wow, FERPA nightmares.

Coping mechanisms: lots of coffee, the occasional vent on my locked @quietroom214 Insta (no names, I promise), and reading trashy romance on my phone after the kids are asleep.

Tell me grad school at UNT is less chaotic than my middle schoolers.

Love,
Em

metadata.subject: I accidentally became “the coding ELA person”

artifacts.content:
Hi Mom,

I hope you're feeling well and recovering smoothly since the procedure. I wanted to update you on a few exciting developments both at work and home.

First off, wonderful news! I recently received a grant of $7,450 to support our STEM StoryLabs Club at Lincoln Middle School. This will allow Javier and me to purchase new tech tools and resources for the students, which should make a big difference as we prepare for the Interactive Story Showcase Night next May. I can’t wait to see the kids’ faces when they realize what they can create with the new equipment.

At home, things are buzzing with the usual activity. Mark is busy with his work at Denton Sports & Rehab, but he’s been stepping up to help more with the kids' morning routine. Noah is excited to start second grade at Oak Ridge Elementary. He's been obsessed with his Minecraft backpack, and I think he might even be more excited about showing it off than starting school! Lily, meanwhile, is ready for Bright Futures Preschool. She’s been 'teaching' her dolls how to code stories, which is both adorable and a bit of foreshadowing, I think!

We’ve settled quite nicely into our home on Greenbriar Lane since buying it in 2021. It’s hard to believe how much housing prices have jumped since then. It’s a relief we managed to buy when we did. Still, money is a bit tight, with daycare and student loans from my M.Ed. nibbling away at our budget, not to mention the occasional hiccups with the old Honda CR-V. But we’re managing.

I’ll be coming to Plano on the 28th for our usual grocery run and tech help session. I’ll bring Noah and Lily along so they can spend some time with you and Dad. Please let me know if there’s anything specific you need help with or if you’d like me to bring anything special.

Looking forward to seeing you soon!

Love,
Emily

metadata.subject: Exciting Updates and Family Plans!

form_info.title: How did you hear about this housing opportunity?  ________________________  ____________________________________________________________________
form_info.extracted_text:
How did you hear about this housing opportunity?  ________________________

____________________________________________________________________

____________________________________________________________________

Briefly describe your reason for applying. _______________________________

____________________________________________________________________

____________________________________________________________________

Parent/Guardian/Alternate Contact’s Full Name: _________________________

Home Phone: _______________________________________________________

Cell Phone: _________________________________________________________

Address: ___________________________________________________________

City/State/Zip Code: _________________________________________________

E-mail Address: _____________________________________________________

Describe your current living situation.
    ☐ I rent      ☐ I own      ☐ Other

In your own words, describe your level of independence. ________________

____________________________________________________________________

Would you rather:
    ☐ Live in a one-bedroom apartment (by yourself)
    ☐ Live in a two-bedroom apartment (with a roommate)

Ridgewood Supportive Housing includes both one- and two-bedroom apartments. 
We will take your preferences into consideration; however, apartment selection is 
based upon multiple factors including careful review of one’s application and a 
person-to-person interview. Roommate pairings are made according to common 
likes and dislikes as well as schedule.

Revised 4/21                                                                                           4

application_background.how_did_you_hear_about_this_housing_oppo: My case manager at Queens Community Mental Health Center, Angela Martinez, told me about Ridgewood Supportive Housing. She had received an email from the housing coordinator at the clinic with information about openings in supportive apartments in the area. After that, I also looked it up online and saw a short description on the agency’s website and a flyer posted on the bulletin board in the lobby of the clinic. Angela and I discussed it during one of our weekly appointments, and she encouraged me to apply because she felt the level of support and the location in Ridgewood would be a good fit for me.
application_background.briefly_describe_your_reason_for_applyin:
I am applying for Ridgewood Supportive Housing because I am trying to move from a very unstable and overcrowded living situation into a safe, stable apartment where I can continue to work on my recovery and build more independence. Right now I am staying in my older sister’s two-bedroom apartment with her, her husband, and their two young children. I sleep on a pull-out couch in the living room, and there is very little privacy or quiet space, which makes it hard to manage my anxiety and depression. I have a history of major depressive disorder and generalized anxiety, and I attend weekly therapy and medication management. My providers and I agree that having my own consistent, supportive place to live would really help me continue to stabilize, keep my part-time job, and stick with my treatment plan.

I am looking for a supportive housing setting specifically because I still benefit from check-ins and some structure. I can handle most daily tasks on my own—like cooking simple meals, keeping my space clean, and managing my personal hygiene—but I sometimes need reminders and help with organization, especially around appointments and paperwork. I also do better when I know there are staff I can reach out to if I start to feel overwhelmed or if my symptoms worsen. My goal is to eventually be fully independent, but right now I need an environment that balances independence with some built-in support. Living in Ridgewood would also keep me close to my current mental health clinic, my job in Brooklyn, and my family, so I could maintain my existing support network while also having my own space and responsibility.

living_situation_and_preferences.in_your_own_words_describe_your_level_of:
At this point in my life I would describe my level of independence as “mostly independent with some support and structure needed.” I am 32 years old and can take care of all of my basic daily living needs on my own, such as bathing, dressing, cooking simple meals, doing laundry, and keeping my living area reasonably clean. For example, I usually wake up around 7:30 a.m. without anyone needing to wake me, take a shower, prepare breakfast (usually oatmeal or eggs), and get myself ready to go to work or to appointments. I am able to travel independently on public transportation—typically the M and L trains or the Q58 bus—and I use Google Maps on my phone if I am going somewhere new.

Where I need the most support is with organization, time management, and handling stress. I sometimes get overwhelmed by paperwork and forms (like recertifications, insurance letters, or job-related documents), and in the past I have missed deadlines because I put things off when I feel anxious. My case manager currently helps me break tasks into smaller steps, and that has been very helpful. I also benefit from reminders about upcoming appointments, especially medical or psychiatric appointments; I now use a calendar app on my phone with alerts, but I still sometimes double-book myself or forget to confirm things.

Emotionally, I have periods when my depression and anxiety flare up, and during those times my motivation drops and it becomes harder for me to keep up my routines. For example, last winter I went through a rough patch where I stayed in bed most of the day for about a week, and my sister had to check in on me more often and encourage me to shower and eat. Having more of my own space, along with supportive staff available in the building, would help me recognize earlier when I am starting to slip and ask for help before things get too bad.

Financially, I can manage a basic budget with some guidance. I receive SSI and work part-time at a bookstore in Brooklyn. I pay my share of my sister’s household expenses in cash right now (we calculated a fixed amount per month for groceries and utilities). I keep a simple budget written in a notebook and I check my bank balance on my phone. I have not had my own lease before, but I understand the importance of paying rent on time, and I am motivated to be responsible about that.

In summary, I am capable of living on my own in terms of day-to-day tasks, but I do best in an environment where there is some structure, regular check-ins, and people I can reach out to for guidance with more complicated situations, stress, and long-term planning. I am hoping supportive housing will give me the next step toward full independence while still having a safety net in place.

answers.field_label: In Your Own Words Describe Your Level Of Independence
answers.value:
At this point in my life I would describe my level of independence as “mostly independent with some support and structure needed.” I am 32 years old and can take care of all of my basic daily living needs on my own, such as bathing, dressing, cooking simple meals, doing laundry, and keeping my living area reasonably clean. For example, I usually wake up around 7:30 a.m. without anyone needing to wake me, take a shower, prepare breakfast (usually oatmeal or eggs), and get myself ready to go to work or to appointments. I am able to travel independently on public transportation—typically the M and L trains or the Q58 bus—and I use Google Maps on my phone if I am going somewhere new.

Where I need the most support is with organization, time management, and handling stress. I sometimes get overwhelmed by paperwork and forms (like recertifications, insurance letters, or job-related documents), and in the past I have missed deadlines because I put things off when I feel anxious. My case manager currently helps me break tasks into smaller steps, and that has been very helpful. I also benefit from reminders about upcoming appointments, especially medical or psychiatric appointments; I now use a calendar app on my phone with alerts, but I still sometimes double-book myself or forget to confirm things.

Emotionally, I have periods when my depression and anxiety flare up, and during those times my motivation drops and it becomes harder for me to keep up my routines. For example, last winter I went through a rough patch where I stayed in bed most of the day for about a week, and my sister had to check in on me more often and encourage me to shower and eat. Having more of my own space, along with supportive staff available in the building, would help me recognize earlier when I am starting to slip and ask for help before things get too bad.

Financially, I can manage a basic budget with some guidance. I receive SSI and work part-time at a bookstore in Brooklyn. I pay my share of my sister’s household expenses in cash right now (we calculated a fixed amount per month for groceries and utilities). I keep a simple budget written in a notebook and I check my bank balance on my phone. I have not had my own lease before, but I understand the importance of paying rent on time, and I am motivated to be responsible about that.

In summary, I am capable of living on my own in terms of day-to-day tasks, but I do best in an environment where there is some structure, regular check-ins, and people I can reach out to for guidance with more complicated situations, stress, and long-term planning. I am hoping supportive housing will give me the next step toward full independence while still having a safety net in place.

Marco Alvarez – brother-in-law (Elena’s husband), 40
