persona.education: Master of Urban and Regional Planning (MURP), University of California, Irvine (2005); B.A. in Geography, California State University, Fullerton (2000)
family_members: Elena Marie Thompson (wife, 45)
Sofia Isabel Thompson (daughter, 15)
Lucas Daniel Thompson (son, 11)
Miguel Alvarez (father-in-law, 72)
Karen Louise Thompson (sister, 44)
persona.background_context:
This is Daniel Thompson, a 47-year-old Planning Manager with the City of Riverside Planning Department. He supervises a small team of planners handling current development review and environmental compliance, and he’s known in the office for being meticulous about state environmental regulations and local code. In late 2025, a lot of his workday revolves around coordinating with other city departments—especially Parks, Recreation and Community Services—on modest public facility upgrades like the Arlington Branch Library interior renovation and ADA accessibility improvements. Because he lives less than ten minutes from that library, he feels a personal stake in making sure the project genuinely improves access for seniors and students in his own neighborhood.

Daniel and his wife, Elena, bought their three-bedroom home near Arlington High School in 2013, just as their daughter Sofia was starting kindergarten. Now Sofia is a sophomore at Arlington High, juggling honors classes, JV soccer, and an emerging interest in environmental science that Daniel quietly encourages. Their younger son, Lucas, is in sixth grade at a nearby elementary school and spends much of his free time on youth soccer and building elaborate Lego cities on the living room floor. Elena, a bilingual medical assistant at a family clinic off Magnolia Avenue, often works slightly staggered hours, so the two of them coordinate school pickups and practices with a shared Google calendar and a running text thread full of reminders and grocery lists.

Most weekdays, Daniel is out the door by 7:30 a.m., dropping Lucas at school before heading downtown to the Planning Department offices on Main Street. His days are a mix of staff check-ins, reviewing project plans, and fielding calls from residents who are worried about traffic, parking, or neighborhood character. He tries to get out of the office at least once a week to visit active project sites; recently that has meant walking through the Arlington Branch Library with staff from Parks and the library system, talking through practical details like phasing construction so kids still have a quiet place to study during the school year. He takes pride in projects that are small but tangible, especially those that make it easier for people with disabilities to move around their own community.

At home, Daniel’s evenings are a balancing act. On Mondays and Wednesdays he takes Lucas to soccer practice at nearby fields, using the time on the sidelines to answer a few emails or chat with other parents about school issues and the rising cost of living in Riverside. Tuesdays are often taken up with City Council or Planning Commission meetings that run late into the night, so he and Elena have an unspoken rule that Thursday evenings are family nights: simple dinners, no meetings, and at least one board game or movie everyone can agree on. Sundays they usually attend the late-morning Mass at a local Catholic parish, followed by lunch at his father-in-law Miguel’s small house a few blocks away, where family conversations easily slide between English and Spanish.

In the past year, Daniel’s been more conscious of his own health. After a minor scare in early 2025—chest tightness that turned out to be stress and acid reflux—his doctor nudged him to get more exercise and cut back on late-night fast food after long meetings. Now he tries to walk the dog, a mellow mutt named Jasper, around the neighborhood each evening and has started using a standing desk at work. He worries a bit about Sofia’s looming college costs and Miguel’s slowly worsening arthritis, but he and Elena have started putting a little extra into savings and looking into state university options close to home. Despite the usual pressures of work, school schedules, and aging parents, Daniel feels rooted in Riverside; when he drives past projects he’s helped shepherd—like the upcoming improvements to the Arlington Branch Library—he’s reminded why he chose public service over more lucrative private-sector planning work.

secrets.secret_content: In March 2025, after the chest-tightness scare, Daniel’s primary care physician, Dr. Priya Nair at Riverside Medical Clinic on Magnolia Avenue, formally diagnosed him with generalized anxiety disorder and prescribed a low-dose SSRI along with a referral to stress-management counseling. Daniel has a MyChart message from March 18, 2025 titled “Follow‑up on chest pain / anxiety meds” where he asks, “Are you sure it’s okay for me to keep doing late-night Council meetings on this medication?”
secrets.why_sensitive: A documented anxiety diagnosis and prescription history could expose Daniel to stigma at work, raise concerns about his reliability under pressure, and affect future promotions or leadership appointments in a public agency role. If widely known, colleagues or decision-makers might unfairly question his judgment on high-stakes projects or during contentious public hearings.
secrets.how_disclosed: While drafting or revising the exemption justification under deadline, Daniel might email his supervisor or a close colleague saying something like, “I’m waiting on a call from Dr. Nair at Riverside Medical Clinic about adjusting my anxiety meds after the chest episode in March, so I may need to step away from my desk this afternoon before I finalize the CEQA exemption language.”

secrets.secret_content: In September 2025, Daniel’s daughter Sofia had a panic attack during a crowded evening study session at the Arlington Branch Library and was briefly evaluated by a school psychologist, Ms. Angela Ruiz, at Arlington High School the next day. Daniel saved a school email dated September 23, 2025 with the subject line “Confidential: Sofia T. – anxiety concerns after library incident.”
secrets.why_sensitive: Revealing Sofia’s mental health struggles could lead to stigma from teachers, peers, and other parents, potentially affecting her academic opportunities, social relationships, and future college applications. It could also embarrass Sofia and damage her trust in Daniel if she learned he shared this information beyond the immediate family and school support staff.
secrets.how_disclosed: When explaining why he personally cares about quiet study spaces and better wayfinding in the library, Daniel might confide to a trusted Parks staffer or Council aide in an email like, “After Sofia’s panic episode at the Arlington library last month and the follow-up with Ms. Ruiz at Arlington High, I’m especially focused on making these interior changes less overwhelming for teens.”

secrets.secret_content: In August 2025, Daniel and Elena met with financial advisor Mark Chen at the Riverside branch of Golden State Credit Union to discuss a home equity line of credit to help cover Sofia’s anticipated college costs and Miguel’s increasing medical expenses for arthritis treatments at Riverside Community Hospital. Daniel has a meeting note on his phone titled “8/12/25 – HELOC options w/ Mark C.” that includes the line, “We’re already $9,000 on the credit card from Miguel’s injections + Sofia’s AP exam fees/travel.”
secrets.why_sensitive: Disclosure of their growing debt and reliance on a HELOC could make Daniel appear financially unstable, which might be concerning to a public employer that expects fiscal responsibility and could also affect future credit decisions. It also exposes Miguel’s health situation and the family’s limited financial cushion, which they would not want broadcast to colleagues or casual acquaintances.
secrets.how_disclosed: When talking with a close coworker about why he’s pushing for cost-effective, energy-efficient lighting and durable materials in the project, Daniel might say in a side conversation or text, “With us looking at a HELOC at Golden State CU because of Miguel’s hospital bills and Sofia’s college, I’m really aware of how tight family budgets are for the folks who rely on this library.”

secrets.secret_content: In June 2025, Daniel received a confidential email from the City Attorney’s Office (Deputy City Attorney Laura Kim) with the subject line “Attorney–Client Privileged: Potential CEQA challenge – Arlington Library ADA upgrades,” warning him that a local neighborhood association leader, Robert “Bob” Hensley, had informally threatened to sue the City if the project proceeded under a categorical exemption instead of a full Initial Study. The email specifically advised Daniel to keep internal deliberations off personal devices and to avoid discussing legal strategy at public meetings.
secrets.why_sensitive: Revealing the existence of a potential CEQA challenge and privileged legal advice could compromise the City’s legal position, expose Daniel and the department to accusations of mishandling the exemption, and damage public trust. It could also make Daniel personally a target for community criticism or deposition if litigation proceeds.
secrets.how_disclosed: While venting to a trusted colleague or his wife about why he’s being so meticulous in the exemption justification, Daniel might say, “Laura Kim in the City Attorney’s Office emailed me in June about a possible CEQA lawsuit from Bob Hensley if we go categorical on the Arlington upgrades, so I have to be extra careful how we phrase the ‘no unusual circumstances’ section.”

secrets.secret_content: During a closed-session performance check-in in October 2025, Daniel’s direct supervisor, Planning Director Michael Alvarez, informally warned him that a complaint had been lodged by a developer’s representative, Karen Doyle of Magnolia Partners LLC, alleging that Daniel’s public comments about prioritizing accessibility upgrades at the Arlington Branch Library showed “bias against private development” along Magnolia Avenue. Daniel kept a private note on his work computer titled “10/09/25 – Michael mtg – complaint from K. Doyle (Magnolia Partners).”
secrets.why_sensitive: If this complaint and informal warning became widely known, it could undercut Daniel’s perceived neutrality as a public planner, harm his reputation with other applicants, and jeopardize future promotions. It might also be used against him in any future dispute over project approvals, suggesting he has a bias toward public projects over private investment.
secrets.how_disclosed: While emailing a trusted colleague about why he’s carefully framing the library project description, Daniel might write, “After the complaint from Karen Doyle at Magnolia Partners about me ‘favoring’ the Arlington library upgrades, Michael told me to be extra balanced in how I describe beneficiaries in the project narrative.”

secrets.secret_content: In May 2025, Daniel’s father-in-law Miguel Alvarez had a severe arthritis flare and fall in his kitchen on Cypress Avenue, leading to an overnight stay at Riverside Community Hospital and a follow-up rheumatology appointment with Dr. Samuel Ortega at the hospital’s Arthritis & Joint Center. Daniel has a text thread with Elena from May 6, 2025 including the message, “Dr. Ortega says your dad’s mobility will keep getting worse – we may need to push hard for truly ADA-compliant doors and restrooms at Arlington.”
secrets.why_sensitive: Miguel’s declining mobility and prognosis are deeply personal health details. Public disclosure could embarrass him, affect how neighbors and church members treat him, and potentially influence how social services or insurers view his level of independence. It also exposes family caregiving burdens that Daniel and Elena may not want widely known at work.
secrets.how_disclosed: When advocating internally for stronger ADA features, Daniel might mention in a private email to Parks staff, “After Miguel’s fall and consult with Dr. Ortega at Riverside Community, I’ve seen firsthand how hard those heavy doors and non-compliant restrooms are for seniors in our neighborhood.”

secrets.secret_content: In October 2025, Sofia confided to Daniel after a late-night study session at the kitchen table that she was considering not applying to out-of-state environmental science programs because she felt pressured to stay close to home to help with Miguel’s care and Lucas’s after-school schedule. Daniel jotted a note in his personal journal on October 21, 2025: “Sofia crying – feels guilty about leaving Riverside if she goes to UC Davis – says, ‘I can’t just abandon Abuelo and Lucas.’”
secrets.why_sensitive: This reveals intimate family dynamics and emotional pressure on Sofia that could be misinterpreted by teachers, counselors, or extended family as neglect or coercion. It might also influence how others view Daniel and Elena’s parenting choices and could create conflict within the family if relatives learned Sofia felt obligated to stay.
secrets.how_disclosed: When explaining to a close coworker why he’s so invested in making the library a strong local resource for college-bound students, Daniel might say over coffee, “Sofia told me last month she might pass on UC Davis to stay near Arlington High and help at home with Miguel and Lucas, which is part of why I care so much about improving this neighborhood library for kids like her.”

secrets.secret_content: In January 2025, after a particularly contentious Planning Commission meeting about another Magnolia Avenue project, Daniel had a brief emotional conversation with parish priest Father Carlos Mendoza in a side room at St. Catherine’s Catholic Church, admitting he sometimes feels “burned out and resentful” that his public service work—like the Arlington Branch Library improvements—“will never pay enough to really secure the kids’ future.” Daniel later received a short email from Father Carlos on January 29, 2025 with the subject line “Re: Finding balance in your vocation.”
secrets.why_sensitive: This reveals Daniel’s private spiritual counseling and deep doubts about his career and financial adequacy. If colleagues or supervisors knew he felt burned out and resentful, they might question his long-term commitment to the department or his emotional resilience under pressure. It could also be personally embarrassing and strain boundaries between his professional and faith communities.
secrets.how_disclosed: When texting Elena about staying late to polish the CEQA exemption, Daniel might write, “I talked to Father Carlos back in January about feeling burned out on these projects and money worries, but I still want to get this Arlington exemption right even if no one ever notices.”

secrets.secret_content: In July 2025, Daniel received a certified letter at his home on Arlington Station Drive from attorney Thomas Blake of Blake & Howe LLP, representing a nearby property owner who claimed that construction noise and parking disruptions from the Arlington Branch Library renovation would violate a private access easement recorded in 1993. The letter, titled “Notice of Potential Legal Action – Easement Interference, 9556 Magnolia Ave.,” threatened to seek an injunction if the City did not modify its phasing plan.
secrets.why_sensitive: This potential private legal dispute, tied directly to a project Daniel manages, could create a conflict-of-interest perception and expose him to scrutiny about whether he properly disclosed and handled the issue internally. Public knowledge of the letter could damage trust with both the City and neighboring property owners, and might be used to challenge the adequacy of project planning or the CEQA exemption.
secrets.how_disclosed: While emailing the City’s Parks project manager about coordinating construction hours, Daniel might write, “I got a letter at home in July from attorney Thomas Blake threatening action over the rear easement if noise/parking get too bad during the Arlington renovation, so we really need to emphasize limited construction impacts in both our phasing plan and the exemption justification.”

artifacts.content:
From: daniel.thompson@riversideca.gov
To: marisol.vega@riversideca.gov
Cc: michael.alvarez@riversideca.gov
Date: November 14, 2025
Subject: Arlington Library – CEQA exemption narrative (draft)

Hi Marisol,

I finally carved out a quiet block this afternoon to clean up the exemption write‑up for the Arlington Branch Library Interior Renovation and Accessibility Upgrade at 9556 Magnolia Avenue (APN 146-231-014). The Class 1 (Existing Facilities) angle under CEQA Guidelines Section 15301 still looks solid to me – it’s all interior renovation and minor accessibility work, no expansion of the building footprint or capacity, and no change in use.

I tightened the sections on “no expansion of use” and “urban, fully disturbed setting” and added a bit more about standard construction controls (dust, noise, waste disposal) and the fact that we’re not touching any natural areas or historic resources. I also tried to be explicit that there’s no reasonably foreseeable increase in intensity of use – same hours, same staffing, just better lighting, ADA‑compliant restrooms, automatic door openers, etc.

I am being extra fussy about the “no unusual circumstances” paragraph, partly because of Laura Kim’s June email from the City Attorney’s Office about a potential CEQA challenge from Bob Hensley if we proceed under a categorical exemption. I don’t want to hand him anything to latch onto. On a related note, I got that certified letter at home in July from attorney Thomas Blake (Blake & Howe LLP) about possible legal action over the rear access easement and construction noise/parking during the renovation, so I emphasized the short‑term, small‑scale nature of the work and the phasing plan to keep disruption minimal.

I’m aiming to sign as “Daniel R. Thompson, Planning Manager, City of Riverside Planning Department” and date it next Tuesday, November 18, 2025, so we can get the Notice of Exemption on file and in front of City Council. If you have any edits, especially on the cumulative impact and hazardous sites bullets, please send track changes by Monday.

Side note: I may need to step away for a bit tomorrow – I’m waiting on a call from Dr. Priya Nair at Riverside Medical Clinic about tweaking my anxiety meds after that chest episode in March. But I’ll keep an eye on email and can jump back in to finalize language before end of day.

Thanks,
Daniel

Daniel R. Thompson
Planning Manager
City of Riverside Planning Department
3900 Main Street, 3rd Floor, Riverside, CA 92522

metadata.subject: Arlington Library – CEQA exemption narrative (draft)

artifacts.content:
From: daniel.thompson@riversideca.gov
To: parks.pm@riversideca.gov
Date: August 5, 2025
Subject: Arlington Library – construction phasing + neighbor concerns

Hi Jenna,

Circling back on the phasing plan for the Arlington Branch Library interior renovation and accessibility upgrade. I walked the site again yesterday at 9556 Magnolia Ave and I’m still convinced we can keep at least a portion of the building open for students and seniors if we’re smart about sequencing the restroom and entry work.

One thing I need you to be aware of: in July I received a certified letter at my house on Arlington Station Drive from attorney Thomas Blake (Blake & Howe LLP), representing a nearby property owner. The letter was titled “Notice of Potential Legal Action – Easement Interference, 9556 Magnolia Ave.” He’s threatening to seek an injunction if construction noise and parking for contractors interfere with the private access easement recorded in 1993 behind the site. I’ve forwarded it to Laura Kim in the City Attorney’s Office, but it makes our messaging about “limited construction impacts” and tight work hours even more critical.

Given that, can we lock in weekday construction between 8:00 a.m. and 5:00 p.m. only, no weekend work, and require staging in the southeast corner of the parking lot away from the easement? I’d also like to highlight in our materials that most work is interior – reconfiguring shelving, new low‑VOC flooring, LED lighting, ADA restroom upgrades, automatic door openers, etc. – and that there’s no grading, no added parking, no expansion of the footprint.

On a personal note, this project is hitting close to home. After Miguel’s fall in his kitchen on Cypress Avenue back in May and the follow‑up rheumatology appointment with Dr. Samuel Ortega at Riverside Community Hospital’s Arthritis & Joint Center, I’ve seen how much of a difference truly ADA‑compliant doors and restrooms make for seniors. And with Sofia’s panic episode during that crowded evening study session at Arlington Library in September and the follow‑up with school psychologist Angela Ruiz at Arlington High, I’m really conscious of keeping at least one quiet, predictable study zone open during construction.

Let’s chat later this week to tighten the phasing narrative.

Thanks,
Daniel

metadata.subject: Arlington Library – construction phasing + neighbor concerns

artifacts.content:
From: daniel.thompson@riversideca.gov
To: elena.thompson@gmail.com
Date: October 24, 2025
Subject: Long week / Sofia / money stuff

Hey love,

I’m sitting in the car outside Lucas’s practice and finally have a minute to breathe. This week has felt like three rolled into one.

I can’t stop thinking about Sofia at the kitchen table Tuesday night, crying about UC Davis and saying, “I can’t just abandon Abuelo and Lucas.” She’s 15 and already worrying about being responsible for everyone. Part of me is proud she cares so much, and part of me feels awful that our situation is making her feel tied down to Riverside and Arlington High.

It ties right into the money spiral in my head. I keep going back to my notes from that August 12 meeting at Golden State Credit Union with Mark Chen – “8/12/25 – HELOC options w/ Mark C.” and that line I scribbled, “We’re already $9,000 on the credit card from Miguel’s injections + Sofia’s AP exam fees/travel.” Seeing it in writing made my stomach drop. I know the HELOC is probably the only way we can juggle Sofia’s college costs and your dad’s arthritis treatments at Riverside Community Hospital, but it still scares me.

All of that is probably why my chest has been tight again. I messaged Dr. Nair back in March in MyChart (“Follow‑up on chest pain / anxiety meds”) asking if it was okay to keep doing late‑night Council meetings on the SSRI, and she said yes, but weeks like this make me wonder how sustainable this pace is. Between the Arlington Library CEQA exemption, the Magnolia Avenue projects, and the kids’ schedules, I feel like I’m always behind.

I keep thinking about that conversation with Father Carlos in January after the ugly Planning Commission meeting, when I told him I felt burned out and resentful that public service work like the Arlington Branch Library improvements will never really secure the kids’ future. His email later (“Re: Finding balance in your vocation”) helped in the moment, but the doubts creep back when I look at our bank account.

Anyway, this is a lot for a Friday email. Just needed to get it out of my head. Let’s protect Thursday night next week for a real family dinner, no meetings, no library talk.

Love you,
D

metadata.subject: Long week / Sofia / money stuff

artifacts.content:
Title: 8/12/25 – HELOC options w/ Mark C.

Date: 2025-08-12
Location: Golden State Credit Union – Riverside branch (Magnolia Ave)

Met with Mark Chen this afternoon about a home equity line of credit. Elena and I are trying to figure out how to handle both Sofia’s college costs (AP exam fees, potential campus visits, maybe UC Davis or another UC) and Miguel’s growing medical bills from Riverside Community Hospital.

Key points from Mark:

– Current home value vs. remaining mortgage means we qualify for more than we’re comfortable borrowing.
– We’re already $9,000 on the credit card from Miguel’s arthritis injections + Sofia’s AP exam fees/travel. That number hit me hard when I said it out loud.
– HELOC could give us flexibility, but interest rates may creep up. Need to be disciplined.

Things on my mind:

1. Miguel’s health: After his severe arthritis flare and fall in the kitchen on Cypress Avenue in May, and the overnight stay at Riverside Community Hospital plus the follow‑up with Dr. Samuel Ortega at the Arthritis & Joint Center, it’s clear his mobility is only going to get worse. That makes me even more determined to push for truly ADA‑compliant doors and restrooms at the Arlington Branch Library. I keep picturing him trying to wrestle those heavy doors.

2. My own stress: Chest tightness episode in March, then the generalized anxiety diagnosis from Dr. Priya Nair at Riverside Medical Clinic and the low‑dose SSRI. I asked her in that March 18 MyChart message (“Follow‑up on chest pain / anxiety meds”) if it was okay to keep doing late‑night Council meetings. She said yes, but weeks like this make me question how long I can keep this up.

3. Sofia: She’s talking more about staying closer to home for college, maybe because of Abuelo and Lucas. Need to make sure we’re not making her feel trapped just because we’re anxious about money and caregiving.

Action items:
– Talk with Elena about HELOC amount we’d actually use.
– Revisit household budget this weekend.
– Schedule a walk with Father Carlos – still wrestling with resentment about money vs. public service.
– Keep advocating for strong ADA features in Arlington plans at work.

From: daniel.thompson@riversideca.gov
To: karen.l.thompson@yahoo.com
Date: September 26, 2025
Subject: Quick family update before Mom calls you first :)

Hey sis,

Figured I’d send you the unfiltered version of life in Riverside before Mom gets you on the phone and makes it sound like we’re all falling apart.

Biggest thing this month was Sofia’s panic attack at the Arlington Branch Library. She was at one of those crowded evening study sessions two weeks ago, and apparently the noise and people moving around just overwhelmed her. The librarian called Elena, and the next day the school psychologist at Arlington High, Angela Ruiz, did a quick evaluation. We got an email on September 23 titled “Confidential: Sofia T. – anxiety concerns after library incident.” Hearing the word “anxiety” attached to her hit me like a truck, given my own stuff.

You remember my chest‑tightness scare back in March. That led to the visit with Dr. Priya Nair at Riverside Medical Clinic on Magnolia and the generalized anxiety disorder diagnosis. She put me on a low‑dose SSRI and referred me to stress‑management counseling. I even sent her that MyChart message on March 18 (“Follow‑up on chest pain / anxiety meds”) asking if it was okay to keep doing late‑night Council meetings. So yeah, seeing Sofia go through something similar at 15 is… a lot.

Layer on top of that Miguel’s arthritis. In May he had that bad flare and fall in the kitchen on Cypress Avenue, ended up overnight at Riverside Community Hospital, then saw Dr. Samuel Ortega at the Arthritis & Joint Center. Dr. Ortega told Elena his mobility will keep getting worse. That conversation is burned into my brain.

All of this is feeding into how I’m handling the Arlington Branch Library renovation at work. I keep pushing for better ADA doors and restrooms and calmer study spaces, because now it’s not abstract – it’s Abuelo and Sofia. Of course, that’s also what triggered a complaint from a developer rep, Karen Doyle at Magnolia Partners LLC, accusing me of “bias against private development” along Magnolia because I talked publicly about prioritizing accessibility upgrades. Michael pulled me into his office on October 9 for a closed‑door chat about it.

Anyway, kids are otherwise okay, Lucas is still building Lego cities in the living room, and Jasper is his usual lazy self. How are you holding up?

Love,
Dan

metadata.subject: Quick family update before Mom calls you first :)

artifacts.content:
Title: Check‑in with Michael re: Arlington / workload

Date: 2025-10-09
Time: 3:30–4:15 p.m.
Location: Planning Director’s Office – 3900 Main St, 3rd Floor
Attendees: Daniel Thompson, Michael Alvarez

Notes / things to remember:

– This is Michael’s informal performance check‑in he scheduled after last month’s Council marathon.
– Need to talk honestly about workload: Arlington Branch Library Interior Renovation and Accessibility Upgrade (CEQA exemption, public outreach), Magnolia Avenue private projects, plus all the routine development review.
– He’s going to bring up the complaint from Karen Doyle (Magnolia Partners LLC). She told him my public comments about prioritizing accessibility upgrades at the Arlington Branch Library showed “bias against private development” along Magnolia. I have a note on my computer titled “10/09/25 – Michael mtg – complaint from K. Doyle (Magnolia Partners)” – remember to update it after.
– Emphasize that my focus on ADA improvements is driven by real experiences: Miguel’s fall and arthritis flare in May (overnight at Riverside Community Hospital, follow‑up with Dr. Samuel Ortega at the Arthritis & Joint Center) and watching him struggle with heavy doors and non‑compliant restrooms; Sofia’s panic attack during the crowded evening study session at the Arlington library in September and the follow‑up with school psychologist Angela Ruiz at Arlington High.
– Also, be upfront about my own health: chest‑tightness scare in March, generalized anxiety diagnosis from Dr. Priya Nair at Riverside Medical Clinic, low‑dose SSRI, and the March 18 MyChart message (“Follow‑up on chest pain / anxiety meds”) where I asked if it was okay to keep doing late‑night Council meetings. Important that he understands why I’m asking for more predictable evening schedules.
– Ask whether there’s any flexibility on sending Marisol or another senior planner to some of the late Council/Commission meetings so I’m not out past 11 p.m. twice a week.
– Long‑term: I need to say out loud that I’m worried about burnout and the financial pressure we’re under (HELOC discussions with Mark Chen at Golden State Credit Union, Miguel’s hospital bills, Sofia’s looming college costs). I don’t want another year like this one.

Reminder: stay calm, keep it factual, and listen to Michael’s concerns too.

metadata.title: Check‑in with Michael re: Arlington / workload
metadata.location: Planning Director’s Office – 3900 Main St, 3rd Floor

artifacts.content:
Thread: Daniel & Elena – random Thursday

Date: 2025-01-29

Elena: You still at City Hall?

Daniel: Yeah, just wrapped up some Arlington library emails. Heading out in 10.

Elena: Don’t forget we promised Lucas board game night. He’s already set up Catan on the dining table.

Daniel: I won’t bail, promise. Just sitting in the parking garage for a minute.

Elena: Everything okay?

Daniel: Sort of. I just read an email from Father Carlos – subject line “Re: Finding balance in your vocation.” He was following up on our talk at St. Catherine’s after that awful Magnolia Ave Planning Commission meeting earlier this month.

Elena: The night you came home looking like a ghost?

Daniel: Yep. I told him I’ve been feeling burned out and resentful that this public service work – like the Arlington Branch Library improvements I care so much about – will never pay enough to really secure the kids’ future. Between Sofia’s college, Miguel’s arthritis stuff, and our mortgage, it feels like we’re always one step behind.

Elena: I’m glad you talked to him. What did he say in the email?

Daniel: Basically, that it’s okay to name the resentment and still choose the work. He reminded me that projects like making Arlington more accessible for seniors and students are “a quiet kind of justice.” I needed to hear that.

Elena: You’ve been carrying a lot since that chest scare. Did you tell him about the March appointment with Dr. Nair yet?

Daniel: Not yet – that was later. But I might. The anxiety, the late‑night Council meetings, money worries… it’s all tangled together.

Elena: We’ll untangle it together. Come home, eat, play Catan. We can worry about college and HELOCs and library politics another day.

Daniel: On my way. Save me the blue pieces.

Elena: Always. ❤️

Daniel: (no emoji, but heart received.)

Hi Karen,

I hope this email finds you well. I wanted to update you on some exciting developments at work that I think you’ll appreciate, especially since you’ve always been interested in how we integrate community needs into our projects.

The Arlington Branch Library, just around the corner from our place, is set for a modest interior renovation and ADA accessibility upgrade. The City of Riverside, Parks, Recreation and Community Services Department is spearheading the project. The library, built in 1994 and spanning about 12,500 square feet, will remain the same size, but we're making some critical updates without expanding its footprint.

Here's a quick rundown of the upgrades we’re implementing: we're reconfiguring the interior shelving and study areas to improve circulation and sight lines, replacing the old carpet and vinyl flooring with low-VOC, durable materials, and upgrading both interior and exterior LED lighting for better energy efficiency. We’re also installing automatic door openers at the main and staff entrances, modifying the circulation desk to include an accessible service counter, and updating the restroom fixtures to meet current ADA standards. Additionally, we’re adding signage and tactile wayfinding markers, along with making some minor electrical and data improvements to support new public computer stations and Wi-Fi infrastructure.

These improvements aim to extend the library's useful life, ensure compliance with current accessibility codes, and better serve our neighborhood residents, including seniors, individuals with mobility impairments, school-age kids like Sofia and Lucas, and low-income families who rely on the library for free access to digital resources and community programs.

Construction will be phased primarily during off-hours to minimize disruption, ensuring the library remains a vital resource for students from Arlington High School and nearby elementary schools. No new staff positions or extended hours are planned, but maintaining current operations is a priority.

Should you want to discuss this further or have any questions, feel free to reach out. You can also contact the City of Riverside, Parks, Recreation and Community Services Department directly at (951) 826-5371 ext. 214.

Looking forward to catching up soon, maybe during one of our Thursday family nights. Give my love to everyone.

Best,
Daniel

form_info.extracted_text:
Begin the EPIDIOLEX conversation

1. These are the seizure medicines that are currently being taken:

______________________________________________________________

2. These are the types of seizures that are occurring while on these medicines:

______________________________________________________________

3. While on these seizure medicines, seizures still happen:
    ☐ Rarely, if ever
    ☐ At least once a month
    ☐ Once or twice a week
    ☐ About once a day
    ☐ More than once a day

4. This is how satisfied I am with my current seizure medicines:
    ☐ Fairly satisfied
    ☐ Okay, needs improvement
    ☐ It's time to try something different

5. My goals for seizure treatment are:

______________________________________________________________

6. My hopes for EPIDIOLEX are:

______________________________________________________________

7. Additional notes and questions I have:

______________________________________________________________


Please see full Important Safety Information on pages 17-18 of this brochure and click here for full Prescribing Information.

Epidiolex® (cannabidiol)

current_seizure_treatment.these_are_the_seizure_medicines_that_are: Levetiracetam (Keppra) 750 mg by mouth twice daily; Clobazam (Onfi) 10 mg by mouth in the morning and 20 mg at night; Lamotrigine (Lamictal) 100 mg by mouth twice daily; Rescue medication: Diazepam nasal spray (Valtoco) 10 mg as needed for seizures lasting longer than 5 minutes or for seizure clusters (may repeat once after 4 hours per neurologist’s instructions).
current_seizure_treatment.these_are_the_types_of_seizures_that_are:
Despite the current combination of Keppra, Onfi, and Lamictal, I (my son, Jacob) continue to have primarily focal impaired-awareness seizures and occasional generalized tonic-clonic seizures.

1) Focal impaired-awareness seizures:
- These usually start with Jacob suddenly pausing whatever he is doing, staring off, and becoming unresponsive. Sometimes his right hand makes picking or rubbing movements, and his mouth may make chewing motions. He does not respond when we call his name or touch his shoulder. These episodes last about 30–60 seconds.
- Afterward, he is confused, tired, and irritable for about 10–20 minutes. He often doesn’t remember what happened. These tend to occur more often in the late afternoon or early evening, especially on days when he is tired or has missed part of a dose.

2) Generalized tonic-clonic seizures:
- These happen less often than before medications but are still occurring. Typically, Jacob will suddenly stiffen, lose consciousness, and then have full-body rhythmic jerking. During these events, his breathing can sound labored, and he sometimes bites his tongue. We time the seizures and position him on his side for safety. Most of these last around 1–2 minutes, followed by a longer postictal phase where he is very sleepy and may sleep for 1–2 hours.
- We have had to use the Diazepam nasal spray twice in the last 6 months for clusters where he had two generalized tonic-clonic seizures within about 30 minutes.

3) Possible brief absence-like spells:
- We are not completely sure, but there are also brief moments (5–10 seconds) when he seems to ‘zone out’ and not respond briefly, then quickly comes back. These are harder to capture and we are not certain if they are true seizures, but his neurologist is aware of them.

Overall, the medicines have reduced the intensity and number of seizures compared to before treatment, but he is still having a significant number of focal impaired-awareness seizures and occasional generalized tonic-clonic seizures each month.

treatment_goals_and_notes.my_goals_for_seizure_treatment_are:
My main goals for seizure treatment for my son, Jacob, are to significantly reduce both the frequency and severity of his seizures and to improve his overall quality of life—emotionally, socially, and academically.

1) Seizure reduction and safety:
- Right now, Jacob has on average 4–6 focal impaired-awareness seizures per week and usually 1–2 generalized tonic-clonic seizures per month, sometimes in clusters. My first goal is to get to a point where he has no generalized tonic-clonic seizures at all, or at least fewer than one every few months, and where the focal impaired-awareness seizures are reduced to once a week or less.
- Another major goal is to reduce the risk of seizure-related injuries. In the past year he has fallen and hit his head on the coffee table during a seizure, and we had to go to the ER to rule out a concussion. I worry constantly about him in the shower, on the stairs, and when he is walking to and from the school bus. If his seizures were better controlled, we could give him more independence without feeling like we have to watch him every single minute.

2) Cognitive and behavioral stability:
- Jacob is 12 years old and in 7th grade, and this is such a critical time for school and social development. Right now, the combination of seizures and medication side effects makes it hard for him to concentrate. His teachers have noticed he is often very tired in the mornings and sometimes in the middle of class after a seizure. We want a treatment plan that allows him to stay more alert and engaged during the school day.
- Behaviorally, he sometimes gets very irritable and emotional, especially in the evenings. It’s hard to tell how much of this is just being a pre-teen versus medication side effects or the aftermath of seizures. One of my goals is to find a seizure treatment that causes less mood instability and that allows him to feel more like himself.

3) Sleep and daily functioning:
- Another goal is to help him have more predictable and restful sleep. When he has seizures at night or right before bedtime, he often wakes up groggy and has trouble getting ready for school. If we can reduce nighttime seizures, I believe his sleep quality will improve, which will help his mood, energy level, and performance in class.
- I would also like him to be able to participate more fully in normal activities—like riding his bike in the neighborhood (with a helmet and supervision), going to friends’ houses, and participating in after-school clubs—without us being as fearful of a seizure happening at any moment.

4) Long-term outlook and independence:
- Looking longer term, I want to give Jacob the best chance at living as independently as possible as he gets older. That means minimizing ongoing brain injury from repeated seizures, supporting his learning and memory, and helping him build confidence that he can manage his condition.
- I understand that complete seizure freedom may or may not be realistic, but my goal is to get as close as we can to that while still allowing him to have a good quality of life and minimal side effects from medications.

Overall, my goals are: fewer seizures, especially generalized tonic-clonic ones; fewer side effects from medications; better focus and mood; safer daily living; and a more hopeful long-term future for him.
treatment_goals_and_notes.my_hopes_for_epidiolex_are:
I have read and heard that EPIDIOLEX can help reduce seizures in some children with difficult-to-control epilepsy, especially when other medications have not worked well enough. My hopes for EPIDIOLEX for Jacob are both practical and emotional.

1) Better seizure control than we have now:
- I am hoping that adding EPIDIOLEX will significantly reduce the number of focal impaired-awareness seizures he has each week and ideally prevent the generalized tonic-clonic seizures that are still happening about once or twice a month.
- If we could cut his overall seizure frequency by at least half, that would already be a big improvement for him. My ideal hope, even if it might be ambitious, is that he could go several months at a time without any generalized tonic-clonic seizures at all.

2) Potential to simplify or lower doses of other medications:
- Right now, Jacob is on three daily seizure medications, and each one seems to come with its own side effects. Keppra appears to contribute to irritability and mood swings, Clobazam makes him drowsy (especially in the mornings), and Lamotrigine seems to help but we are not sure how much.
- I am hoping that if EPIDIOLEX works well for him, over time his neurologist might be able to lower the doses of some of his other medications or even possibly remove one of them, so that he isn’t so sedated and emotionally up and down all the time.

3) Improved alertness, mood, and school performance:
- I have heard from other parents (in an online support group) that some children on EPIDIOLEX seem more alert and engaged once their seizures are better controlled. I would love to see Jacob be able to focus more in class, participate more in group discussions, and retain what he learns.
- I also hope that if his seizures are less frequent and if we can reduce some of the other meds, his mood might stabilize. Right now, he can be very sweet and funny, but then quickly become angry or tearful, especially after seizures or when he’s tired. I hope EPIDIOLEX will help reduce that emotional roller coaster by cutting down on seizure burden and possibly allowing for fewer sedating meds.

4) More freedom and confidence for Jacob:
- Jacob is very aware that he is different from other kids in his class. He worries about having a seizure in front of his friends and being embarrassed. I hope that if EPIDIOLEX helps reduce his seizures, he will feel more confident going on school field trips, participating in sports (within reason and with safety precautions), and spending time at friends’ houses without feeling so anxious.
- I also hope that, as parents, we can feel a little less on edge all the time. Right now, we plan our entire day around the possibility of a seizure. If EPIDIOLEX works, maybe we can worry a little less and let him be a kid a bit more.

5) Realistic expectations and open communication:
- I understand that EPIDIOLEX is not a miracle cure and that it may not work for everyone. I am hoping for a measurable improvement in seizure control without intolerable side effects, and I want to work closely with his neurologist to monitor any changes in labs (like liver function tests), appetite, sleep, and behavior.
- I would like EPIDIOLEX to be a tool that gives us more options, not something that adds a lot of new problems. I’m prepared to be patient as we adjust the dose and see how Jacob responds, but my hope is that over several months we will see clear benefits that justify staying on it.

In summary, my hopes for EPIDIOLEX are: fewer and shorter seizures, less reliance on some of his other medications, better daily functioning and mood, and a chance for Jacob to feel more like any other 12-year-old boy, instead of feeling defined by his epilepsy.
treatment_goals_and_notes.additional_notes_and_questions_i_have:
Below are some additional notes and questions I would like to discuss with Jacob’s neurologist when considering EPIDIOLEX:

1) Side effects and monitoring:
- I understand that EPIDIOLEX can affect liver function. What is the exact schedule you recommend for baseline and follow-up liver function tests (LFTs)? How often will Jacob need bloodwork in the first year if we start this medication?
- Jacob is already somewhat picky with food and tends to have a low appetite in the mornings. I have read that EPIDIOLEX can sometimes affect appetite, weight, and gastrointestinal comfort (like diarrhea). How common are these issues in your experience, and what should we watch for at home?
- Are there any specific warning signs (for example, extreme fatigue, yellowing of the skin or eyes, unusual bruising, or changes in behavior) that would mean we need to call the office right away or go to the ER while on EPIDIOLEX?

2) Drug interactions with his current medications:
- Since Jacob is currently on Clobazam (Onfi), Keppra, and Lamotrigine, I’d like to understand if EPIDIOLEX tends to increase or decrease the levels of these medicines, especially Clobazam, which I’ve heard can interact and cause extra sedation.
- If EPIDIOLEX is started, will we proactively lower the dose of Clobazam or just monitor and adjust based on how sleepy he becomes?
- Are there any over-the-counter medications or supplements we should avoid while he is on EPIDIOLEX? He occasionally takes melatonin for sleep and a children’s multivitamin.

3) Practical issues: dosing, school, and cost:
- How is EPIDIOLEX usually given (for example, once or twice daily), and does it need to be taken with food? We are already juggling multiple morning and evening medications, so I want to understand how to best fit this into our routine.
- Will the school nurse need any special training or documentation regarding EPIDIOLEX, or is it handled like his other medications? Right now, the nurse mostly manages his rescue medication and keeps a seizure action plan on file.
- I am also concerned about cost and insurance coverage. Do you know if most insurance plans require prior authorization for EPIDIOLEX, and is there a patient assistance program we could apply for if our co-pay is very high? We are currently insured through my employer’s plan (Blue Cross Blue Shield of Illinois).

4) Long-term outlook and goals of therapy:
- If EPIDIOLEX helps reduce his seizures, how long would you typically keep a child on it before considering any changes to their other medications? Are we thinking in terms of years, or could this be a shorter-term option?
- Are there any concerns about long-term use of EPIDIOLEX in a child Jacob’s age (12 years old) in terms of development, hormones, or cognitive function?

5) Seizure documentation and follow-up:
- We currently keep a paper seizure log and sometimes use a phone app to track episodes, triggers, and medications. Is there a particular way you prefer us to track his seizures once EPIDIOLEX is started, so we can clearly see whether it is helping?
- How soon after starting EPIDIOLEX should we expect to see any changes in seizure frequency, if it is going to work for him? Are we talking about weeks, or is it more realistic to evaluate after a few months at a stable dose?

6) Emotional and family impact:
- Lastly, I want to share that this has been very emotionally draining for our whole family. Jacob’s younger sister is anxious about his seizures, and my husband and I are often exhausted and worried. I am hopeful but also nervous about starting something new. If possible, I would appreciate any resources (support groups, educational materials, or counseling referrals) that you think might help us cope with the ongoing stress of managing his epilepsy.

Thank you for taking the time to go through all of this with us. We truly want to be partners in Jacob’s care and to understand the potential benefits and risks of EPIDIOLEX as clearly as possible before we move forward.

- Jacob is very aware that he is different from other kids in his class. He worries about having a seizure in front of his friends and being embarrassed. I hope that if EPIDIOLEX helps reduce his seizures, he will feel more confident going on school field trips, participating in sports (within reason and with safety precautions), and spending time at friends’ houses without feeling so anxious.
- I also hope that, as parents, we can feel a little less on edge all the time. Right now, we plan our entire day around the possibility of a seizure. If EPIDIOLEX works, maybe we can worry a little less and let him be a kid a bit more.

5) Realistic expectations and open communication:
- I understand that EPIDIOLEX is not a miracle cure and that it may not work for everyone. I am hoping for a measurable improvement in seizure control without intolerable side effects, and I want to work closely with his neurologist to monitor any changes in labs (like liver function tests), appetite, sleep, and behavior.
- I would like EPIDIOLEX to be a tool that gives us more options, not something that adds a lot of new problems. I’m prepared to be patient as we adjust the dose and see how Jacob responds, but my hope is that over several months we will see clear benefits that justify staying on it.

In summary, my hopes for EPIDIOLEX are: fewer and shorter seizures, less reliance on some of his other medications, better daily functioning and mood, and a chance for Jacob to feel more like any other 12-year-old boy, instead of feeling defined by his epilepsy.
answers.reasoning: Pre-filled value from ground truth form

answers.field_label: Additional Notes And Questions I Have
answers.value:
Below are some additional notes and questions I would like to discuss with Jacob’s neurologist when considering EPIDIOLEX:

1) Side effects and monitoring:
- I understand that EPIDIOLEX can affect liver function. What is the exact schedule you recommend for baseline and follow-up liver function tests (LFTs)? How often will Jacob need bloodwork in the first year if we start this medication?
- Jacob is already somewhat picky with food and tends to have a low appetite in the mornings. I have read that EPIDIOLEX can sometimes affect appetite, weight, and gastrointestinal comfort (like diarrhea). How common are these issues in your experience, and what should we watch for at home?
- Are there any specific warning signs (for example, extreme fatigue, yellowing of the skin or eyes, unusual bruising, or changes in behavior) that would mean we need to call the office right away or go to the ER while on EPIDIOLEX?

2) Drug interactions with his current medications:
- Since Jacob is currently on Clobazam (Onfi), Keppra, and Lamotrigine, I’d like to understand if EPIDIOLEX tends to increase or decrease the levels of these medicines, especially Clobazam, which I’ve heard can interact and cause extra sedation.
- If EPIDIOLEX is started, will we proactively lower the dose of Clobazam or just monitor and adjust based on how sleepy he becomes?
- Are there any over-the-counter medications or supplements we should avoid while he is on EPIDIOLEX? He occasionally takes melatonin for sleep and a children’s multivitamin.

3) Practical issues: dosing, school, and cost:
- How is EPIDIOLEX usually given (for example, once or twice daily), and does it need to be taken with food? We are already juggling multiple morning and evening medications, so I want to understand how to best fit this into our routine.
- Will the school nurse need any special training or documentation regarding EPIDIOLEX, or is it handled like his other medications? Right now, the nurse mostly manages his rescue medication and keeps a seizure action plan on file.
- I am also concerned about cost and insurance coverage. Do you know if most insurance plans require prior authorization for EPIDIOLEX, and is there a patient assistance program we could apply for if our co-pay is very high? We are currently insured through my employer’s plan (Blue Cross Blue Shield of Illinois).

4) Long-term outlook and goals of therapy:
- If EPIDIOLEX helps reduce his seizures, how long would you typically keep a child on it before considering any changes to their other medications? Are we thinking in terms of years, or could this be a shorter-term option?
- Are there any concerns about long-term use of EPIDIOLEX in a child Jacob’s age (12 years old) in terms of development, hormones, or cognitive function?

5) Seizure documentation and follow-up:
- We currently keep a paper seizure log and sometimes use a phone app to track episodes, triggers, and medications. Is there a particular way you prefer us to track his seizures once EPIDIOLEX is started, so we can clearly see whether it is helping?
- How soon after starting EPIDIOLEX should we expect to see any changes in seizure frequency, if it is going to work for him? Are we talking about weeks, or is it more realistic to evaluate after a few months at a stable dose?

6) Emotional and family impact:
- Lastly, I want to share that this has been very emotionally draining for our whole family. Jacob’s younger sister is anxious about his seizures, and my husband and I are often exhausted and worried. I am hopeful but also nervous about starting something new. If possible, I would appreciate any resources (support groups, educational materials, or counseling referrals) that you think might help us cope with the ongoing stress of managing his epilepsy.

Thank you for taking the time to go through all of this with us. We truly want to be partners in Jacob’s care and to understand the potential benefits and risks of EPIDIOLEX as clearly as possible before we move forward.
