persona.occupation: Independent horse trainer and riding instructor specializing in young and green horses, including BLM mustangs
persona.education: B.S. in Equine Science, Colorado State University (CSU), Fort Collins, CO, Class of 2016
family_members: Mark Allen Cartwright – father, retired high school biology teacher, Fort Collins, CO
Susan Elaine Cartwright (née Miller) – mother, part-time veterinary clinic receptionist, Fort Collins, CO
Jacob “Jake” Allen Cartwright – older brother, 35, mechanical engineer, lives in Greeley, CO
Laura Christine Mendoza – close friend and professional colleague in the horse industry (not related by blood)
“Copper’s Comet” – 13-year-old off-track Quarter Horse gelding, Emily’s personal riding horse
“Juniper” – 6-year-old Australian Shepherd mix, Emily’s dog and barn companion
persona.background_context:
This is Emily, a 31-year-old horse trainer building a quiet but respected career along Colorado’s Front Range. She lives in a modest two-bedroom rental duplex on the east side of Longmont, sharing the space with her Australian Shepherd mix, Juniper, and a rotating pile of saddle pads drying over the backs of kitchen chairs. Most mornings start before sunrise with coffee in a travel mug and a 20-minute drive out to the small property she leases northeast of town, where she keeps her own gelding, Copper’s Comet, and a handful of client horses in training. Her days revolve around groundwork sessions, first rides on young horses, and trailer-loading or desensitization work, with short breaks to answer client texts, post training updates to her Facebook and Instagram pages, and coordinate farrier or vet visits.

Emily’s current training facility is the result of a deliberate move she made in late 2023, when she shifted her primary operation from a boarding barn near Loveland to the leased acreage closer to Longmont. The property has a solid 6-foot pipe-and-rail perimeter, a dedicated 50-foot round pen with safe footing, a smaller 20x20 catch pen attached to a sheltered area for new or especially reactive mustangs, and a simple alley made from panels that she configures as a chute when needed. She spends a lot of time walking those pens, checking for loose hardware or sharp edges, and making sure hay is stacked behind gates and grain bins are latched. She’s cautious by nature—sometimes slower to progress a horse than impatient owners might like—but her reputation around northern Colorado has grown precisely because she prioritizes safety and the horse’s mental state. Word-of-mouth from local barns, including the facility outside Loveland that Laura Mendoza and her sister manage, keeps a steady trickle of green horses, BLM mustangs, and the occasional “problem” horse coming her way.

Life for Emily is a balancing act between passion and practicality. She’s excited to be involved in the 2025 Colorado Front Range TIP Youth & Adult Challenge in Longmont, both as a trainer and as a quiet mentor to a couple of younger riders who are considering taking on their first mustang. She keeps her schedule packed: Mondays and Wednesdays are heavy training days at her leased property, Tuesdays and Fridays she hauls out to clients’ barns for tune-up rides and owner lessons, and most Saturdays she teaches a handful of regular students at the Loveland facility where she first met Laura back in early 2018. Sundays are her nominal “day off,” but in reality they’re often filled with catching up on laundry, cleaning tack in her small backyard, or driving up to Fort Collins to have dinner with her parents, Mark and Susan. Recently, she’s been helping her mom navigate some mild arthritis flare-ups, occasionally taking time midday to drive her to doctor’s appointments when her brother Jake’s engineering job keeps him tied up.

Over the past year, Emily’s main concerns have been financial stability and long-term sustainability. Winter 2024–2025 was harder than usual: a cold snap in January meant extra hours chipping ice from water tanks and higher-than-expected utility costs for keeping the barn’s small tack room from freezing. She’s been slowly building a small emergency fund, stashing away a portion of every training check after learning the hard way in 2020 how quickly an unexpected vet bill can wipe out savings. She’s also paying close attention to her own body—her lower back has started complaining after long days in the saddle, so she’s added yoga classes at a local studio in downtown Longmont on Thursday evenings and short stretching routines in the barn aisle between rides. Despite the long hours, she still makes time for continuing education, occasionally hauling to Fort Collins or Windsor for clinics on biomechanics or mustang gentling, notebook in hand, quietly integrating new ideas into her already methodical approach.

Socially, Emily’s circle is small but solid. Laura is both a close friend and a professional ally; they trade haul-ins, refer clients to each other, and occasionally split the cost of bringing in outside clinicians. Emily isn’t married and doesn’t have children, something she’s generally at peace with, though she sometimes wonders how she might fit a family into a lifestyle that often runs from 6 a.m. to 8 p.m. Her personal life tends to weave into her professional world: dinners with friends are just as likely to involve conversations about hoof angles and saddle fit as anything else, and most of her vacations have been short camping trips in the mountains with Copper and Juniper in tow. Right now, her short-term goals are clear and concrete—keep every horse in her care healthy, present a calm, well-prepared mustang at the 2025 TIP Challenge in Longmont, and continue building a reputation as the person to call when someone has a young or difficult horse that needs patient, thoughtful handling.

secrets.secret_content: Since February 2024, Emily has been seeing sports medicine physician Dr. Hannah Leclerc at UCHealth Longs Peak Hospital in Longmont for chronic lower back pain and intermittent numbness in her right leg, and in an April 10, 2024 visit they discussed the possibility that she may eventually need a lumbar microdiscectomy if conservative treatment (PT and injections) stops working.
secrets.why_sensitive: Detailed medical history about a potential back surgery could affect how clients, event organizers, or insurers view her reliability and physical capability as a trainer, and could lead to lost business or discrimination. It is also deeply personal health information that she may not want widely shared in the tight-knit local horse community.
secrets.how_disclosed: When Laura is writing the “personal comments” section, she might over-explain Emily’s work ethic by mentioning that Emily still rode three horses the day after a spinal injection at UCHealth and quoting an email Emily sent her with the subject line, “Quick heads-up: back doc at Longs Peak says no broncs for a week.”

secrets.secret_content: In November 2023, Emily quietly started attending a small, women-only anxiety support group run by therapist Megan Ortiz, LCSW, at the Longmont Community Counseling Center after experiencing several nighttime panic attacks related to trailer-loading accidents she witnessed in 2020 and 2022.
secrets.why_sensitive: Mental health treatment and panic attacks can carry stigma, especially in a performance- and toughness-oriented horse culture; disclosure could change how clients trust her around high-pressure situations or lead event organizers to question her suitability for handling wild horses.
secrets.how_disclosed: In a private text to Laura about whether she should take on another difficult mustang for the 2025 TIP Challenge, Emily might write, “Megan (my therapist at the community counseling center) thinks I’m managing the panic stuff better now, even after that trailer-loading scare last fall,” and Laura might be tempted to reference Emily’s “work on managing anxiety after a couple of scary trailer incidents” when praising her calm demeanor.

secrets.secret_content: Emily’s mother, Susan Cartwright, was diagnosed with rheumatoid arthritis by Dr. Priya Natarajan at the UCHealth Rheumatology Clinic in Fort Collins in August 2023, and Emily has been periodically leaving midday lessons to drive her to methotrexate monitoring appointments at that clinic.
secrets.why_sensitive: This is Susan’s protected health information and also reveals that Emily has intermittent caregiving responsibilities, which could lead clients or event organizers to see her as less available or reliable, and could strain family privacy if shared beyond close circles.
secrets.how_disclosed: While explaining slight gaps in Emily’s training schedule, Laura might write something like, “This past winter there were a few Wednesdays she left early to drive her mom to RA infusions at the UCHealth clinic in Fort Collins,” inadvertently revealing Susan’s diagnosis and treatment details.

secrets.secret_content: After a colic surgery on her gelding Copper’s Comet at Colorado State University’s Veterinary Teaching Hospital in October 2020, Emily took out a $7,500 CareCredit line and is still paying off the remaining balance in monthly installments, occasionally putting off her own health appointments when the minimum payment and winter hay bill hit at the same time.
secrets.why_sensitive: This exposes specific debt, financial strain, and a pattern of delaying her own care, which could make clients or landlords view her as financially unstable and could affect her credit or negotiating position for future leases or sponsorships.
secrets.how_disclosed: In describing Emily’s commitment to horse welfare, Laura might mention in an email, “She literally went into debt with a CareCredit account after Copper colicked at the CSU vet hospital in October 2020 rather than cut corners,” which reveals Emily’s ongoing debt situation.

secrets.secret_content: In March 2022, Emily had an informal but tense meeting in the office of barn owner Karen Douglas at High Plains Stables near Loveland, where Karen accused her of “poaching” two training clients and suggested she move her operation out by the end of the summer; Emily quietly left that barn by July 2022 after negotiating a handwritten month-to-month exit agreement to avoid a formal dispute.
secrets.why_sensitive: This past conflict with a previous facility owner could damage Emily’s professional reputation, suggest she is difficult to work with, or reignite local barn gossip if widely known, potentially costing her referrals and arena access.
secrets.how_disclosed: When Laura explains the transition from their boarding facility to Emily’s leased property, she might be tempted to include context like, “After that uncomfortable meeting with Karen in the High Plains office in March 2022 about ‘stealing’ clients, Emily decided to lease her own place,” which exposes a sensitive professional conflict.

secrets.secret_content: Since January 2024, Emily has been accepting a small number of cash-only “emergency tune-up” sessions at a private barn outside Berthoud owned by Tom and Erin Wallace, without invoicing through Cartwright Horsemanship, LLC or reporting that income, after they asked her to help with a dangerous gelding on short notice.
secrets.why_sensitive: Unreported income creates potential tax and legal exposure for Emily and could jeopardize her LLC’s standing or insurance coverage if an incident occurred during one of these off-the-books sessions.
secrets.how_disclosed: In praising Emily’s willingness to help in crises, Laura might write or say, “I know she’s even driven down to Tom and Erin Wallace’s place near Berthoud late at night for cash-only ‘emergency’ sessions with a problem gelding,” unintentionally revealing that Emily is doing side work outside her official business records.

secrets.secret_content: During a tense Sunday dinner at her parents’ house in Fort Collins in May 2023, Emily’s brother Jake bluntly told her, “You can’t keep living like a broke 20-year-old wrangling mustangs forever,” which led to a week-long silence between them and an ongoing, unspoken rift about her choice not to pursue a more conventional engineering- or office-type career.
secrets.why_sensitive: This exposes private family conflict and criticism of her career path, which could be emotionally painful and alter how others perceive her support system or stability; sharing the exact quote and context could embarrass both Emily and Jake.
secrets.how_disclosed: While trying to highlight Emily’s determination, Laura might reference the family tension in a phone call or note like, “Even when her brother said last May over dinner in Fort Collins, ‘You can’t keep living like a broke 20-year-old wrangling mustangs forever,’ she doubled down on improving her program,” inadvertently exposing a private family argument.

secrets.secret_content: In September 2022, after a late-night clinic at The Ranch in Loveland, Emily ended a brief romantic relationship with a farrier named Daniel “Dan” Ruiz in the parking lot when she discovered he had been drinking before shoeing a client’s horse earlier that week, telling him, “I can’t date someone who shows up drunk under a horse,” and quietly stopped recommending him to clients.
secrets.why_sensitive: This mixes her personal romantic life with a serious professional concern about another local service provider’s conduct; sharing it could damage Dan’s reputation, expose Emily to retaliation or defamation claims, and pull her into local industry drama she has tried to avoid.
secrets.how_disclosed: While emphasizing Emily’s strong ethics around safety, Laura might allude in writing to Emily breaking up with “a farrier who showed up drunk to a shoeing at The Ranch in Loveland in September 2022,” which could be traced back to Dan Ruiz and become a contentious accusation in the local horse community.

secrets.secret_content: In January 2025, Emily quietly emailed event coordinator Sarah Jensen at the 2025 Colorado Front Range TIP Youth & Adult Challenge from her phone in the parking lot of the Longmont Humane Society, asking, “Is it possible to withdraw late if my mustang comes in hotter than expected? I’m still a little shaken from a trailer wreck I helped with in Weld County last winter,” referring to a multi-horse trailer rollover on County Road 49 in December 2023 where she assisted first responders.
secrets.why_sensitive: This reveals her lingering trauma from a specific local accident and her uncertainty about being able to follow through with the competition, which could lead organizers to question her reliability or mental readiness and could reopen painful memories tied to a public incident.
secrets.how_disclosed: Laura, wanting to show how seriously Emily takes safety and self-assessment, might mention in an email or conversation, “She even emailed Sarah Jensen from the Longmont Humane Society lot in January asking if she could back out of the TIP Challenge if the mustang felt unsafe after that Weld County trailer rollover she helped with,” thereby disclosing both the accident involvement and Emily’s private doubts.

artifacts.content:
Hi Jake,

Catching a quiet half hour between rides and figured I’d actually write instead of just sending you random photos of Juniper covered in mud.

Things here are…busy, as usual. Winter hit the hay bill hard, and I’m still chipping away at that CareCredit line from when Copper colicked at the CSU Veterinary Teaching Hospital back in October 2020. I think I’ve finally got the balance under $3k, which feels like progress. There are definitely months where the minimum payment plus winter hay means I push my own stuff off a bit, but I’m trying to be smarter about that.

Speaking of my own stuff, my back has been a bigger topic lately. I’ve been seeing Dr. Hannah Leclerc, the sports med doc at UCHealth Longs Peak Hospital here in Longmont, since February for the lower back pain and that weird numbness down my right leg. At my April 10, 2024 appointment she brought up the possibility of a lumbar microdiscectomy “down the road” if PT and the injections stop doing their job. Not exactly what I wanted to hear, but also not an emergency. I’m doing the PT religiously and trying to avoid getting launched by anything that thinks it’s in the NFR.

I still think about that comment you made at Mom and Dad’s in Fort Collins in May 2023 — “You can’t keep living like a broke 20-year-old wrangling mustangs forever.” I know it came from a place of worry, but it stuck more than I let on. I am trying to build something sustainable here, even if it doesn’t look like your engineering career path.

On the plus side, the new leased place northeast of Longmont is working out well. The 50-foot round pen drains better than the old arena at High Plains, and having that smaller 20x20 catch pen attached to the shelter has been a lifesaver for the hotter mustangs.

Anyway, enough heavy stuff. How’s work? Still buried in CAD models and broken prototypes? Let me know when you’re free for dinner at Mom and Dad’s — I’ll bring dessert if you promise not to critique my life choices at the table this time.

Love you,
Em

metadata.subject: Quick update between rides

artifacts.content:
Hi Laura,

Finally sitting down after evening feed to answer your last message properly.

First, thank you again for that monster of a reference letter and for generally talking me off ledges when I spiral. I keep reminding myself that you’ve watched me ride 4–6 days a week since 2018 and would probably tell me if I’d suddenly turned incompetent.

The anxiety piece has been…better, mostly. Since November 2023 I’ve been going to that small women-only anxiety support group that Megan Ortiz, LCSW, runs at the Longmont Community Counseling Center. It’s weirdly comforting to hear other people talk about panic attacks that show up out of nowhere. Megan keeps circling us back to those trailer-loading wrecks in 2020 and 2022 that I can’t seem to forget.

You know that multi-horse trailer rollover on County Road 49 in Weld County last December (2023) that I helped with? I thought I was fine afterward, but in January I ended up sitting in the parking lot at the Longmont Humane Society, shaking, and emailing Sarah Jensen from my phone. I literally typed, “Is it possible to withdraw late if my mustang comes in hotter than expected? I’m still a little shaken from that trailer wreck I helped with in Weld County last winter.” She wrote back kindly, but I still felt like such a flake.

On the flip side, Megan says I’m managing things better now — like after that trailer-loading scare last fall, I actually used the breathing stuff instead of just white-knuckling through it. It helps to remember that my “calm” around horses is something I practice, not magic.

Anyway, enough brain talk. I can haul in to your place next Tuesday or Thursday to use the arena — whichever works better around your lesson blocks. I’ve got a sensitive bay gelding who needs to see a busier environment but does not need High Plains drama.

Text me when you have a minute.

Em

metadata.subject: Re: next week + my overthinking brain

artifacts.content:
Hi Mom,

Just wanted to check in after your appointment yesterday and make sure you’re not overdoing it.

I put “Fort Collins – UCHealth Rheumatology Clinic with Dr. Natarajan” into my calendar for next Thursday again so I remember to block out midday lessons. It still blows my mind that it’s already been since August 2023 that she officially called it rheumatoid arthritis. You sounded tired on the phone after the methotrexate labs, but not as wiped as after those first visits.

Please don’t feel bad about me leaving a lesson or two early now and then to drive you up there. Most of my clients are understanding when I explain I’m taking you to UCHealth for RA monitoring, and the ones who aren’t…well, they can find someone else. Horses are important, but you’re more important.

My back stuff is still in the mix too. I saw Dr. Hannah Leclerc at UCHealth Longs Peak Hospital here in Longmont again last week. She reminded me that if the PT and injections stop helping, we may eventually have to talk about a lumbar microdiscectomy. I’m hoping it doesn’t come to that, but I also don’t want to ignore it the way I did in 2022.

On a lighter note, I’m planning to come up Sunday for dinner if that still works. I can bring salad and maybe that lemon cake you like. Tell Dad I’ll help him with the backyard fence after we eat as long as he promises not to lecture me about wearing a back brace.

Love you lots,
Emily

P.S. If you need help opening jars or carrying laundry baskets, CALL ME. Don’t be a hero with those wrists.

metadata.subject: Next Thursday + Sunday dinner

artifacts.content:
Title: Random work/ethics brain dump

I’ve been thinking a lot about where my lines are.

March 2022 keeps popping up in my head — that tense meeting in Karen Douglas’s office at High Plains Stables near Loveland. Sitting in that tiny office with the dusty show ribbons on the wall while she accused me of “poaching” two training clients. I remember her saying maybe it was “time to think about moving your operation out by the end of the summer.” I walked out shaking and ended up negotiating that handwritten month-to-month exit agreement just to avoid lawyers and more gossip. I’m still glad I left by July 2022, even if it was messy.

Then there’s the farrier situation from September 2022 at The Ranch in Loveland. Late-night clinic, everyone exhausted, and finding out Daniel Ruiz had been drinking before shoeing a client’s horse earlier that week. Breaking up with him in the parking lot and saying, “I can’t date someone who shows up drunk under a horse,” felt brutal but necessary. Quietly stopped recommending him after that. Still feel weird when his name comes up at barns.

And now, since January 2024, I’ve been driving down to Tom and Erin Wallace’s private place outside Berthoud for those cash-only “emergency tune-up” sessions with their dangerous gelding. No invoices through Cartwright Horsemanship, LLC, no paper trail, just gas money and some extra cash when the schedule is tight. It started as a one-off favor and turned into a semi-regular thing.

I know unreported income is sketchy, and if anything ever happened out there my insurance might not cover it. But also, those sessions have probably prevented someone from getting seriously hurt.

Need to decide:
- Talk to my accountant before tax time about how to clean this up.
- Set a hard boundary with Tom and Erin: either on the books or I’m out.
- Remember that past drama with Karen and Dan is proof that ignoring red flags never ends well.

Event: UCHealth Longs Peak – spine injection follow-up

Date: 2024-04-11
Time: 10:15 a.m. – 11:30 a.m.
Location: UCHealth Longs Peak Hospital, Longmont – Sports Medicine clinic (Dr. Hannah Leclerc)

Notes:
- Follow-up after yesterday’s lumbar injection for chronic lower back pain + right leg numbness.
- Ask Dr. Leclerc again about what signs would mean we’re really heading toward a lumbar microdiscectomy vs. just managing with PT and the occasional shot.
- Remind her that I still rode three horses the day after the last spinal injection (probably not my best life choice) and that I’m trying to be more realistic about my workload.
- No broncs, no first rides on anything questionable this week — “back doc at Longs Peak says no broncs for a week,” as I joked in that email to Laura.
- Block out the afternoon for light groundwork only + admin (invoices, social posts, scheduling).

Other stuff to keep in mind today:
- Call Mom to see how her hands are doing after her last RA check at the UCHealth Rheumatology Clinic in Fort Collins.
- Text Jake about maybe carpooling to Sunday dinner so I’m not driving back from Fort Collins late alone.
- Double-check hay delivery for Friday so I’m not moving bales right after an appointment.

metadata.title: Spine follow-up – Dr. Leclerc
metadata.location: UCHealth Longs Peak Hospital, Longmont CO

artifacts.content:
Hi Sarah,

Thank you again for all the work you’re putting into the 2025 Colorado Front Range TIP Youth & Adult Challenge here in Longmont. I know coordinating youth and adult competitors, plus all the mustangs, is a huge job.

I wanted to follow up on the email I sent you back in January from the Longmont Humane Society parking lot — the one where I asked, “Is it possible to withdraw late if my mustang comes in hotter than expected? I’m still a little shaken from a trailer wreck I helped with in Weld County last winter.” I really appreciated how kind your response was.

Since then I’ve been doing a lot of work on my end. I’m still attending that small women-only anxiety support group with therapist Megan Ortiz, LCSW, at the Longmont Community Counseling Center. We’ve talked through the multi-horse trailer rollover on County Road 49 in December 2023 and the earlier trailer-loading accidents I witnessed in 2020 and 2022. Between that and some breathing/grounding tools, I feel more prepared to handle a “hotter” mustang if that’s what I draw.

I also wanted to reassure you that my facility setup is still very much focused on safety: 6-foot pipe-and-rail perimeter, the 50-foot round pen with good footing, and the 20x20 catch pen attached to a sheltered area for the first few days. I’m keeping my schedule realistic so I’m not tempted to rush.

All that to say: I’m planning to stay in unless something truly out of the ordinary happens with the horse’s health or mine. I just thought it was fair to update you, since I had reached out in a moment of doubt.

Please let me know if there’s anything else you need from me logistically as we get closer.

Best,
Emily Cartwright
Cartwright Horsemanship, LLC
Longmont, CO
(970) 555-2843

metadata.subject: TIP Challenge – quick update from my end

artifacts.content:
Laura: You alive? Haven’t seen you in my arena in a week.

Emily: Barely, lol. Long week.

Laura: Horses or humans?

Emily: Both. Mom had another RA monitoring appointment at the UCHealth Rheumatology Clinic in Fort Collins, so I bailed on Wednesday afternoon lessons to drive her. Dr. Natarajan tweaked her methotrexate dose again. She was wiped.

Laura: Ugh. How’s your back with all the extra driving?

Emily: Meh. Saw Dr. Leclerc at UCHealth Longs Peak last month. Same talk: if the PT + injections stop working, we might be looking at a lumbar microdiscectomy eventually. Trying not to think that far ahead.

Laura: You, not overthinking? I’ll believe it when I see it.

Emily: Ha. 

Emily: Money stress isn’t helping either. Still paying off that CareCredit from Copper’s colic surgery at CSU’s vet teaching hospital in Oct 2020. Some months it feels like I’m choosing between my own appointments and hay.

Laura: I hate that for you.

Emily: And I keep hearing Jake in my head from that Sunday dinner in Fort Collins last May: “You can’t keep living like a broke 20-year-old wrangling mustangs forever.” 🙄

Laura: Brothers are the worst.

Emily: On the plus side, Tom and Erin’s demon gelding outside Berthoud is finally leading like a citizen. Those late-night cash-only “emergency tune-ups” might actually keep someone from getting killed.

Laura: You know I’m going to nag you about the off-the-books part, right?

Emily: I know. Accountant first, lecture second. Promise.

Laura: Deal. Come haul in Saturday? I’ll trade arena time for coffee.

Emily: Sold.

Hi Laura,

I hope this email finds you well! I've been meaning to catch up and share some updates, especially with the Colorado Front Range TIP Youth & Adult Challenge coming up. It's hard to believe we've known each other for just over seven years now since I first brought Copper's Comet to your facility. Time sure flies!

Things have been busy here in Longmont. The transition to the new property northeast of town has been smoother than expected, and it's been a blessing to have a more dedicated space for the mustangs and client horses. The facilities are simple but effective, and the horses seem to appreciate the quiet environment.

I've been thinking about that young mustang gelding you watched me work with last winter—the one that was nervous about vehicles. Your advice was invaluable, and breaking the process down into small steps really helped him. It's a method I've refined over the years, and now, as I prepare for the TIP Challenge, those skills will be crucial.

I've also been keeping an eye on my own well-being—my back has been acting up a bit after those long days in the saddle. The yoga classes downtown on Thursdays are helping, and I've added some stretches to my routine at the barn. Speaking of health, I was out chipping ice from the water tanks during that cold snap in January, and it reminded me of the importance of maintaining clean, full water sources for the horses.

Your mentorship and friendship have been invaluable, and I often recommend you to clients seeking advice or a second opinion. I always mention your phone numbers, (303) 555-9174 and (303) 555-6028, as well as your email. I hope you don't mind!

Looking forward to seeing you soon, perhaps at the next clinic or when I haul in for a lesson. Thanks again for all your support and guidance over the years. Your insights always help me refine my approach to training.

Take care,
Emily

form_info.title: SALES CALL REVIEW WORKSHEET  TRY ✅ smartsheet for FREE ➲
form_info.extracted_text:
SALES CALL REVIEW WORKSHEET

TRY ✅ smartsheet for FREE ➲

OVERALL CALL GOAL
YES   NO

1. Did the sales rep have a specific goal for the call before the call began?  [   ]    [   ]
2. Was this goal achieved?    [   ]    [   ]

FEEDBACK ON WHY GOAL NOT ACHIEVED OR OTHER GUIDANCE / ACTION POINTS TO WORK ON
_________________________________________________________________________
_________________________________________________________________________
_________________________________________________________________________

SALES PITCH
YES   NO

3. Did the rep follow your sales pitch?   [   ]    [   ]
4. How well did the rep deliver the pitch?   [   ]    [   ]
5. Did the rep demonstrate strong product knowledge?  [   ]    [   ]
6. Was the rep able to answer questions?    [   ]    [   ]

FEEDBACK / ACTION POINTS
_________________________________________________________________________
_________________________________________________________________________

INFORMATION GATHERING AND BUYER RAPPORT
YES   NO

7. Did the rep ask questions that effectively elicited actionable information from the buyer? [   ]    [   ]
8. Did the rep demonstrate understanding of the buyer's needs and empathy?   [   ]    [   ]
9. Did the rep practice active listening?    [   ]    [   ]
10. Did the rep listen more than he or she talked?   [   ]    [   ]
11. Did the rep interrupt the client?   [   ]    [   ]

FEEDBACK / ACTION POINTS
_________________________________________________________________________
_________________________________________________________________________

overall_call_goal.feedback_on_why_goal_not_achieved_or_oth:
Rep (Jordan Alvarez) entered the call with a clear objective: secure verbal commitment from the prospect (operations director at a mid-sized logistics firm) to run a 60-day pilot of our workflow automation platform with two priority teams (dispatch and customer support). This goal was explicitly stated in his pre-call notes and in the first 3 minutes of the conversation when he framed the agenda.

The goal was achieved: by the end of the call, the buyer confirmed interest in a 60-day pilot, agreed on a tentative start date (2025-01-15), and aligned on the internal steps needed (IT security review and budget confirmation from finance). Jordan obtained verbal buy-in and a clear next step: a follow-up call scheduled for 2024-12-19 to finalize the pilot scope and get formal approval.

Guidance / action points to work on:
1) Tighten time management around next steps: Jordan spent a bit too long reiterating features after the buyer had already agreed to the pilot. He could have used that time to more precisely define success criteria (e.g., target reduction in average ticket handling time, number of automated workflows to activate during pilot). Recommend he standardize a quick 3–5 question checklist for defining pilot success metrics before ending the call.

2) Be more explicit about commercial terms earlier: Pricing and pilot terms came up only in the last 7–8 minutes. While the buyer ultimately accepted the high-level range, bringing rough pricing expectations earlier might have helped the buyer mentally align budget with value sooner and ask more meaningful ROI questions.

3) Document mutual action plan in writing: Jordan verbally walked through the steps required on both sides (security review, pilot scope, training session, go-live), but he did not summarize this as a mutual action plan during the call. Recommend he close future calls by confirming a 3–5 step written plan and emailing it to the buyer within 1 hour of the call.

4) Clarify decision-making roles: Jordan asked who else would need to be involved, and the buyer mentioned IT and finance, but he did not probe on who the ultimate economic buyer is vs. who is the technical approver. In future calls, he should explicitly ask: “Who signs off on the budget?” and “Who would say no even if everyone else said yes?” to avoid surprises later in the cycle.

sales_pitch.feedback_action_points_sales_pitch:
Jordan followed the recommended discovery-led pitch structure closely: brief rapport, recap of previous email exchange, 10–12 minutes of needs discovery, then a tailored 15-minute demo focused specifically on the buyer’s top three pain points (manual dispatching, slow customer email response times, and lack of visibility into SLA breaches). He avoided a generic feature dump and instead anchored each feature in a specific problem the buyer had described.

Strengths observed:
1) Clear problem–solution mapping: When the buyer mentioned that their dispatch team manually updates spreadsheets every hour, Jordan immediately pivoted to show the automated status-sync workflows and used specific language from the buyer (“we lose track of trucks when drivers don’t update the sheet”) to describe the benefit. This made the pitch feel highly relevant and personalized.

2) Strong product knowledge: Jordan demonstrated confidence in explaining both high-level value and detailed functionality, including conditional triggers, API integrations, and role-based permissions. When the buyer asked about integration with their existing TMS (TransFleet Pro), Jordan explained the available REST API connectors and typical implementation timelines, and he correctly outlined which parts would require their IT team’s involvement.

3) Effective use of stories and social proof: He referenced two similar logistics clients (one regional, one national) and gave concrete before/after metrics (e.g., “They reduced average ticket response time from 14 hours to 5 hours within the first 45 days of using our automation flows”). These examples helped the buyer visualize tangible outcomes rather than just features.

4) Calm handling of objections: When the buyer raised concerns about their already stretched IT team, Jordan acknowledged the concern, explained how our onboarding team typically takes the lead on initial configuration, and described a low-IT-involvement pilot setup. He did not become defensive and instead reframed the pilot as a way to prove value with minimal internal disruption.

Action points / areas for improvement:
1) Sharpen the opening value statement: Jordan’s initial explanation of the product was a bit wordy and used too many internal terms (“playbooks,” “recipes,” etc.) without defining them. Suggest he develop a single, crisp 1–2 sentence value statement in plain language before going deeper: e.g., “We help logistics teams automate repetitive communication and tracking tasks so dispatchers and support reps can handle more loads with fewer errors.”

2) Use more quantifiable ROI framing: While he cited a few case study metrics, he could have gone further by calculating a rough ROI using the buyer’s own numbers. For example, the buyer shared their average ticket volume and average handle time; Jordan could have quickly estimated potential hours saved per week and connected that to cost or headcount impact.

3) Slow down the demo pacing during key moments: During the workflow builder demo, he clicked through steps a bit quickly, and the buyer asked him to “go back one screen” twice. Recommend he periodically pause and ask, “Does this pace work for you?” or “Should I slow down here?” especially when showing more technical configuration screens.

4) Close with a tighter summary: The closing recap was comprehensive but slightly scattered. He restated benefits, next steps, and a bit of technical detail all together. A clearer structure would help: (1) summarize top 3 pains, (2) summarize how the product addresses each, (3) restate agreed pilot objective and timeline, and (4) confirm next meeting details. This would leave the buyer with a sharper mental picture of why they should move forward.

Overall, Jordan delivered a strong, buyer-centric pitch that aligned well with our playbook. With minor refinements in clarity, pacing, and ROI framing, he can elevate from solid to top-tier performance.

information_gathering_and_buyer_rapport.feedback_action_points_information_gathe:
Jordan did a thorough job of using open-ended questions to draw out the buyer’s current process, pain points, and internal constraints. He began with some light rapport-building around the buyer’s location (Dallas–Fort Worth area) and recent weather issues impacting their trucking routes, which helped the conversation feel less transactional and more like a peer discussion.

Information gathering strengths:
1) Strong discovery questions: He asked layered questions such as, “Walk me through what happens from the moment a customer calls about a delayed shipment until the issue is fully resolved,” which led the buyer to describe multiple handoffs between dispatch, customer support, and billing. This surfaced three separate bottlenecks (manual spreadsheet updates, delayed email responses, and lack of visibility for the billing team) that Jordan later tied directly to specific product capabilities.

2) Quantifying impact: Jordan probed for numbers instead of accepting vague answers. For example, when the buyer said, “Our response times have gotten slower,” he followed up with, “Roughly how long does it take today for a customer to get a meaningful update?” and “How has that changed over the last 6–12 months?” This yielded concrete data: average response time went from 6–8 hours up to 12–14 hours, and they’re now missing their internal SLA on about 25% of tickets.

3) Understanding internal dynamics: He asked who feels the pain most acutely and which teams complain the loudest. The buyer explained that dispatchers feel overwhelmed, customer support is frustrated by having to chase information, and management is worried about customer churn. Jordan acknowledged each group’s perspective, which helped the buyer feel that he understood not just the process but also the human side of the problem.

4) Empathy and validation: When the buyer described how their small team had to cover for two open headcount positions, Jordan responded with empathy rather than immediately jumping into a pitch: “It sounds like you’re being asked to do more with less while still hitting the same SLAs. That’s a tough spot to be in.” The buyer responded positively and shared more candid details, including internal pressure from their VP of Operations.

Active listening and rapport:
1) Reflective listening: Jordan frequently summarized what he heard: “So if I’m hearing you correctly, the main issue isn’t just the delay itself, but the fact that customers have to follow up multiple times to get an answer, and your team is stuck chasing information internally.” These reflections were accurate and helped the buyer confirm or clarify details.

2) Non-interruptive style: He consistently allowed the buyer to finish his thoughts, even when there were pauses. There were no instances of talking over the buyer. On two occasions when the buyer went off on a tangent about broader company initiatives, Jordan let him finish and then gently steered the conversation back with, “That’s helpful context. Bringing it back to your day-to-day workflow…”

3) Balance of talking vs. listening: While Jordan did listen carefully, he still spoke a bit more than the buyer overall, especially during the demo portion. During the discovery section, the balance was good, but in total call duration the rep likely talked around 60–65% of the time. This is acceptable given the need to demonstrate the product, but he could create more space for the buyer’s reactions during the demo by pausing more frequently and asking, “How does this compare to what you’re doing today?” or “What concerns does this raise for you?”

Action points / areas for improvement:
1) Dig deeper into decision criteria and timeline: Jordan asked when they would ideally like to have a solution in place, and the buyer said “Q1 next year,” but he did not fully unpack the internal decision milestones (e.g., security review duration, legal review, budget sign-off meeting dates). In future calls, he should ask more detailed questions like, “What has to happen internally between now and Q1 to make this a reality?” and “Have you gone through a similar software evaluation recently that we can model this process after?”

2) Explore status quo costs more explicitly: While he uncovered pain points and approximate response times, he could have gone further in quantifying the cost of inaction (e.g., how many customers complain per week, how many loads are at risk, how often they issue credits or refunds). This would strengthen the business case and help the buyer advocate internally.

3) Involve emotional drivers: Jordan did a good job acknowledging stress and workload, but he could also have asked more about what success would feel like for the buyer personally (e.g., “If this pilot is successful, what would that mean for you and your team?”). This can deepen rapport and align the solution with the buyer’s personal goals (less firefighting, more strategic work, better work–life balance).

4) Confirm understanding with more check-backs: He summarized at a few key points, but he could add more deliberate check-backs such as, “On a scale from 1–10, how well does this line up with what you’re looking for?” or “Is there anything important about your process that I haven’t asked about yet?” to ensure no critical context is missed.

Overall, Jordan showed strong discovery and rapport-building skills. The buyer was open, volunteered detailed information, and agreed to next steps, which indicates a high level of trust. With slightly deeper probing into decision criteria, cost of inaction, and personal success metrics, his information gathering could become even more strategic and impactful.

3) Document mutual action plan in writing: Jordan verbally walked through the steps required on both sides (security review, pilot scope, training session, go-live), but he did not summarize this as a mutual action plan during the call. Recommend he close future calls by confirming a 3–5 step written plan and emailing it to the buyer within 1 hour of the call.

4) Clarify decision-making roles: Jordan asked who else would need to be involved, and the buyer mentioned IT and finance, but he did not probe on who the ultimate economic buyer is vs. who is the technical approver. In future calls, he should explicitly ask: “Who signs off on the budget?” and “Who would say no even if everyone else said yes?” to avoid surprises later in the cycle.

answers.value:
Jordan followed the recommended discovery-led pitch structure closely: brief rapport, recap of previous email exchange, 10–12 minutes of needs discovery, then a tailored 15-minute demo focused specifically on the buyer’s top three pain points (manual dispatching, slow customer email response times, and lack of visibility into SLA breaches). He avoided a generic feature dump and instead anchored each feature in a specific problem the buyer had described.

Strengths observed:
1) Clear problem–solution mapping: When the buyer mentioned that their dispatch team manually updates spreadsheets every hour, Jordan immediately pivoted to show the automated status-sync workflows and used specific language from the buyer (“we lose track of trucks when drivers don’t update the sheet”) to describe the benefit. This made the pitch feel highly relevant and personalized.

2) Strong product knowledge: Jordan demonstrated confidence in explaining both high-level value and detailed functionality, including conditional triggers, API integrations, and role-based permissions. When the buyer asked about integration with their existing TMS (TransFleet Pro), Jordan explained the available REST API connectors and typical implementation timelines, and he correctly outlined which parts would require their IT team’s involvement.

3) Effective use of stories and social proof: He referenced two similar logistics clients (one regional, one national) and gave concrete before/after metrics (e.g., “They reduced average ticket response time from 14 hours to 5 hours within the first 45 days of using our automation flows”). These examples helped the buyer visualize tangible outcomes rather than just features.

4) Calm handling of objections: When the buyer raised concerns about their already stretched IT team, Jordan acknowledged the concern, explained how our onboarding team typically takes the lead on initial configuration, and described a low-IT-involvement pilot setup. He did not become defensive and instead reframed the pilot as a way to prove value with minimal internal disruption.

Action points / areas for improvement:
1) Sharpen the opening value statement: Jordan’s initial explanation of the product was a bit wordy and used too many internal terms (“playbooks,” “recipes,” etc.) without defining them. Suggest he develop a single, crisp 1–2 sentence value statement in plain language before going deeper: e.g., “We help logistics teams automate repetitive communication and tracking tasks so dispatchers and support reps can handle more loads with fewer errors.”

2) Use more quantifiable ROI framing: While he cited a few case study metrics, he could have gone further by calculating a rough ROI using the buyer’s own numbers. For example, the buyer shared their average ticket volume and average handle time; Jordan could have quickly estimated potential hours saved per week and connected that to cost or headcount impact.

3) Slow down the demo pacing during key moments: During the workflow builder demo, he clicked through steps a bit quickly, and the buyer asked him to “go back one screen” twice. Recommend he periodically pause and ask, “Does this pace work for you?” or “Should I slow down here?” especially when showing more technical configuration screens.

4) Close with a tighter summary: The closing recap was comprehensive but slightly scattered. He restated benefits, next steps, and a bit of technical detail all together. A clearer structure would help: (1) summarize top 3 pains, (2) summarize how the product addresses each, (3) restate agreed pilot objective and timeline, and (4) confirm next meeting details. This would leave the buyer with a sharper mental picture of why they should move forward.

Overall, Jordan delivered a strong, buyer-centric pitch that aligned well with our playbook. With minor refinements in clarity, pacing, and ROI framing, he can elevate from solid to top-tier performance.

answers.field_label: Feedback Action Points Information Gathering And Buyer Rapport
answers.value:
Jordan did a thorough job of using open-ended questions to draw out the buyer’s current process, pain points, and internal constraints. He began with some light rapport-building around the buyer’s location (Dallas–Fort Worth area) and recent weather issues impacting their trucking routes, which helped the conversation feel less transactional and more like a peer discussion.

Information gathering strengths:
1) Strong discovery questions: He asked layered questions such as, “Walk me through what happens from the moment a customer calls about a delayed shipment until the issue is fully resolved,” which led the buyer to describe multiple handoffs between dispatch, customer support, and billing. This surfaced three separate bottlenecks (manual spreadsheet updates, delayed email responses, and lack of visibility for the billing team) that Jordan later tied directly to specific product capabilities.

2) Quantifying impact: Jordan probed for numbers instead of accepting vague answers. For example, when the buyer said, “Our response times have gotten slower,” he followed up with, “Roughly how long does it take today for a customer to get a meaningful update?” and “How has that changed over the last 6–12 months?” This yielded concrete data: average response time went from 6–8 hours up to 12–14 hours, and they’re now missing their internal SLA on about 25% of tickets.

3) Understanding internal dynamics: He asked who feels the pain most acutely and which teams complain the loudest. The buyer explained that dispatchers feel overwhelmed, customer support is frustrated by having to chase information, and management is worried about customer churn. Jordan acknowledged each group’s perspective, which helped the buyer feel that he understood not just the process but also the human side of the problem.

4) Empathy and validation: When the buyer described how their small team had to cover for two open headcount positions, Jordan responded with empathy rather than immediately jumping into a pitch: “It sounds like you’re being asked to do more with less while still hitting the same SLAs. That’s a tough spot to be in.” The buyer responded positively and shared more candid details, including internal pressure from their VP of Operations.

Active listening and rapport:
1) Reflective listening: Jordan frequently summarized what he heard: “So if I’m hearing you correctly, the main issue isn’t just the delay itself, but the fact that customers have to follow up multiple times to get an answer, and your team is stuck chasing information internally.” These reflections were accurate and helped the buyer confirm or clarify details.

2) Non-interruptive style: He consistently allowed the buyer to finish his thoughts, even when there were pauses. There were no instances of talking over the buyer. On two occasions when the buyer went off on a tangent about broader company initiatives, Jordan let him finish and then gently steered the conversation back with, “That’s helpful context. Bringing it back to your day-to-day workflow…”

3) Balance of talking vs. listening: While Jordan did listen carefully, he still spoke a bit more than the buyer overall, especially during the demo portion. During the discovery section, the balance was good, but in total call duration the rep likely talked around 60–65% of the time. This is acceptable given the need to demonstrate the product, but he could create more space for the buyer’s reactions during the demo by pausing more frequently and asking, “How does this compare to what you’re doing today?” or “What concerns does this raise for you?”

Action points / areas for improvement:
1) Dig deeper into decision criteria and timeline: Jordan asked when they would ideally like to have a solution in place, and the buyer said “Q1 next year,” but he did not fully unpack the internal decision milestones (e.g., security review duration, legal review, budget sign-off meeting dates). In future calls, he should ask more detailed questions like, “What has to happen internally between now and Q1 to make this a reality?” and “Have you gone through a similar software evaluation recently that we can model this process after?”

2) Explore status quo costs more explicitly: While he uncovered pain points and approximate response times, he could have gone further in quantifying the cost of inaction (e.g., how many customers complain per week, how many loads are at risk, how often they issue credits or refunds). This would strengthen the business case and help the buyer advocate internally.

3) Involve emotional drivers: Jordan did a good job acknowledging stress and workload, but he could also have asked more about what success would feel like for the buyer personally (e.g., “If this pilot is successful, what would that mean for you and your team?”). This can deepen rapport and align the solution with the buyer’s personal goals (less firefighting, more strategic work, better work–life balance).

4) Confirm understanding with more check-backs: He summarized at a few key points, but he could add more deliberate check-backs such as, “On a scale from 1–10, how well does this line up with what you’re looking for?” or “Is there anything important about your process that I haven’t asked about yet?” to ensure no critical context is missed.

Overall, Jordan showed strong discovery and rapport-building skills. The buyer was open, volunteered detailed information, and agreed to next steps, which indicates a high level of trust. With slightly deeper probing into decision criteria, cost of inaction, and personal success metrics, his information gathering could become even more strategic and impactful.
